first_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day div q8-12h PO, max 3g/day
10 mg/kg Day 1 then 5 mg/kg Days 2-5, atypical organisms

second_line_drugs.notes: 45 mg/kg/day q12h, failure or mixed infection

notes: CXR/labs not needed for uncomplicated outpatient CAP. Duration 5 days.
counselling: Give all doses of antibiotic for full 5 days even if child feels better
Offer frequent small sips of water and ORS to keep child hydrated
Watch for fast breathing, chest indrawing, or inability to drink - go to hospital immediately
Keep child in well-ventilated room; avoid smoke and dust exposure
Vaccination (PCV, Hib) prevents many types of pneumonia - keep immunizations up to date
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if no improvement in 48-72 hours on antibiotics, recurrent pneumonia (>2 episodes/year), suspected foreign body aspiration, or underlying immunodeficiency
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, severe respiratory distress (grunting, nasal flaring, chest indrawing), inability to feed/drink, age <2 months, toxic appearance, failed outpatient therapy, or complicated pneumonia (effusion/empyema)

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Assess severity and differentiate bacterial vs viral

investigations.name: CRP or Procalcitonin
investigations.indication: Guide antibiotic decision in ambiguous cases

investigations.name: Blood culture
investigations.indication: Before starting IV antibiotics in hospitalised patients

investigations.name: Pulse oximetry
investigations.indication: Assess oxygenation in all suspected pneumonia

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg nebulised q20min x3, min 2.5mg max 5mg
Budesonide-formoterol as both maintenance AND reliever at Steps 3-5

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day (max 40mg) x3-5 days, moderate-severe
0.3-0.6 mg/kg single dose, alternative to prednisolone course

notes: GINA 2024: SABA alone without ICS not recommended for age 6yr+.
guideline_changes: ICS-formoterol preferred reliever at Steps 3-5. Tiotropium add-on for age 6yr+. No fixed FEV1/FVC cutoff.
counselling: Always carry the reliever inhaler (blue puffer) wherever the child goes
Use spacer device with mask for children under 5 years for better medicine delivery
Identify and avoid triggers: dust, smoke, pets, cold air, strong smells
Do not stop controller inhaler even when child is well - it prevents attacks
Seek emergency care if child cannot speak in full sentences, lips turn blue, or reliever not helping
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if uncontrolled asthma despite Step 3 therapy, life-threatening exacerbation, diagnostic uncertainty, or suspected occupational/allergic triggers needing immunotherapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe exacerbation not responding to 3 nebulizations in 1 hour, SpO2 <92% on room air, altered consciousness, silent chest, or need for IV magnesium/aminophylline

investigations.name: Spirometry (FEV1/FVC)
investigations.indication: Baseline assessment and monitoring in children >6 years

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: If first episode, atypical presentation, or suspected complication

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.6 mg/kg single dose PO/IM, max 16mg
0.5 mL/kg of 1:1000 (max 5mL), moderate-severe

second_line_drugs.notes: 2mg x1 dose, if oral not feasible

notes: Observe at least 2h post nebulised epinephrine before discharge.
counselling: Keep child calm - crying worsens stridor and breathing difficulty
Steam inhalation or cool mist humidifier may provide some comfort
Dexamethasone single dose usually gives relief within 2-4 hours
Watch for worsening stridor at rest, drooling, or high fever - return to hospital
Croup usually peaks on night 2-3 and resolves within 5-7 days
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if recurrent croup (>3 episodes), age <6 months, stridor at rest persisting beyond 48 hours of steroid, or suspected structural airway anomaly
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe stridor at rest, poor air entry, cyanosis, altered consciousness, need for nebulised epinephrine (observe 2-4 hours post-dose), or toxic appearance suggesting bacterial tracheitis

investigations.name: AP neck X-ray
investigations.indication: Steeple sign if diagnosis uncertain; not routinely needed

investigations.name: Lateral neck X-ray
investigations.indication: If epiglottitis or retropharyngeal abscess suspected

first_line_drugs.notes: Hydration, nasal suction, O2 if SpO2 below 90%
50mg IM under 5kg / 100mg IM 5kg+, single dose RSV prophylaxis

second_line_drugs.notes: q4-8h nebulised, hospitalised patients over 3 days

notes: Bronchodilators and corticosteroids NOT recommended routinely.
guideline_changes: Nirsevimab (Beyfortus) now recommended for ALL infants under 8 months, replacing palivizumab as standard prophylaxis.
counselling: Bronchiolitis is a viral illness - antibiotics will not help
Use saline nasal drops and gentle suction before feeds to clear blocked nose
Offer small frequent feeds; if baby cannot feed, seek hospital care
Watch for fast breathing, chest indrawing, or refusal to feed - these need emergency care
Recovery takes 7-14 days; cough may persist 2-3 weeks - this is normal
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if recurrent wheezing episodes, age <6 weeks, history of prematurity <32 weeks, or underlying chronic lung disease or congenital heart disease
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <90% on room air, apnea episodes, inability to maintain hydration orally, respiratory rate >70/min, age <12 weeks, or born premature <32 weeks gestation

investigations.name: RSV rapid antigen test
investigations.indication: For cohorting in hospital; not needed for outpatient management

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Only if diagnostic uncertainty or suspected complication

first_line_drugs.notes: 80-90 mg/kg/day q8-12h. Under 2yr: 10 days. Age 2yr+ mild: 5-7 days
Age 6mo+, mild unilateral AOM without otorrhoea, safety-net prescription

second_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day q12h, failure at 48-72h or concurrent conjunctivitis

notes: Azithromycin and TMP-SMX no longer recommended for AOM - inadequate coverage.
guideline_changes: Post-PCV: H. influenzae and M. catarrhalis now dominant, self-resolve more. Azithromycin/TMP-SMX NOT recommended (PIDS 2023).
counselling: Give paracetamol or ibuprofen for ear pain - pain relief is most important initial step
Complete the full course of antibiotic even if child feels better after 2-3 days
Keep water out of ears during bathing if there is ear discharge
Follow up in 48-72 hours if no improvement; antibiotic may need to be changed
Most ear infections in children resolve well; repeated infections may need ENT evaluation
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if >3 episodes in 6 months or >4 in 12 months, suspected cholesteatoma, mastoiditis signs (post-auricular swelling), or persistent perforation >3 months
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if signs of acute mastoiditis (post-auricular swelling, protrusion of pinna), suspected intracranial complication (meningitis, brain abscess), or toxic child with high fever unresponsive to oral antibiotics

investigations.name: Pneumatic otoscopy
investigations.indication: Primary diagnostic tool for all suspected AOM

investigations.name: Tympanometry
investigations.indication: If diagnosis uncertain or chronic effusion suspected

investigations.name: Culture of ear discharge
investigations.indication: If otorrhoea present and failed initial antibiotic

first_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg/day (max 1g) once daily x10 days
Children 250mg BID-TID, adolescents 500mg BID-TID x10 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 12 mg/kg/day (max 500mg) x5 days, for penicillin allergy

notes: 10-day course required to prevent acute rheumatic fever. Only treat confirmed positive RADT or culture.
guideline_changes: IDSA 2025: use Centor/McIsaac clinical scoring BEFORE testing - shift from 2012 universal testing approach.
counselling: Full 10-day course of antibiotic is essential to prevent rheumatic fever - do not stop early
Child can return to school after 24 hours on antibiotics and no fever
Offer warm fluids and soft foods; cold items like ice cream may soothe throat
Replace toothbrush after 48 hours of antibiotics to prevent re-infection
Watch for joint pain, skin rash, or chest pain in the weeks after - see doctor immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if recurrent strep pharyngitis (>=7 episodes/year, >=5/year for 2 years, or >=3/year for 3 years) meeting Paradise criteria for tonsillectomy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if peritonsillar abscess suspected (trismus, uvula deviation, drooling), retropharyngeal abscess, severe dehydration from inability to swallow, or Lemierre syndrome signs

investigations.name: Rapid Antigen Detection Test (RADT)
investigations.indication: First-line test for suspected strep pharyngitis

investigations.name: Throat culture
investigations.indication: If RADT negative but high clinical suspicion (McIsaac >=3)

investigations.name: ASO titre
investigations.indication: If suspected post-streptococcal complication (rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis)

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg over 3-4h, mild-moderate dehydration
0.15 mg/kg (max 8mg) single dose, for vomiting impairing ORS

second_line_drugs.notes: 5 mg/day for under 10yr - WHO 2024 reduced dose, x10-14 days
20 mL/kg bolus, severe dehydration or shock

notes: Antibiotics not indicated for watery diarrhoea. Avoid loperamide in children.
guideline_changes: WHO 2024: zinc dose reduced from 20mg to 5mg/day for children under 10yr. Guideline expanded to cover up to age 10yr.
counselling: Give ORS solution sip by sip every 1-2 minutes - do not give large quantities at once
Continue breastfeeding during diarrhoea; do not stop any feeds
Give zinc tablet daily for 14 days - it reduces duration and prevents recurrence
Danger signs: sunken eyes, no tears, very dry mouth, not passing urine - rush to hospital
Do not give anti-diarrhoeal medicines (loperamide) or unnecessary antibiotics to children
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if chronic diarrhoea >14 days, bloody diarrhoea not responding to treatment, suspected inflammatory bowel disease, or failure to thrive
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe dehydration (sunken eyes, skin pinch goes back very slowly), inability to drink or retain ORS due to persistent vomiting, bloody diarrhoea with toxicity, altered consciousness, or age <2 months

investigations.name: Stool routine and microscopy
investigations.indication: Bloody or mucoid diarrhoea to differentiate invasive vs non-invasive

investigations.name: Stool culture
investigations.indication: Bloody diarrhoea, dysentery, or outbreak investigation

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: Severe dehydration or IV fluid therapy

first_line_drugs.notes: Disimpaction 1-1.5 g/kg/day x3-6 days. Maintenance 0.4-0.8 g/kg/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-3 mL/kg/day divided q12h, alternative osmotic laxative

notes: PEG is best-evidenced agent. Dietary fibre and adequate fluid are essential adjuncts.
counselling: Give PEG laxative daily as prescribed - it is safe for long-term use and not habit-forming
Include more fruits, vegetables, and whole grains in diet; ensure adequate water intake
Establish a regular toilet routine - sit on toilet for 5-10 minutes after meals
Do not punish or scold the child for soiling; it is not intentional
Treatment takes 3-6 months; do not stop laxative suddenly when stools improve
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if no response to 3 months of PEG therapy, suspected Hirschsprung disease (delayed meconium passage, abdominal distension), or associated failure to thrive
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe fecal impaction not responding to oral disimpaction, signs of intestinal obstruction, or severe abdominal pain with vomiting

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray
investigations.indication: Assess fecal loading if uncertain clinical picture

investigations.name: Thyroid function tests
investigations.indication: If associated growth failure or other symptoms of hypothyroidism

investigations.name: Barium enema or anorectal manometry
investigations.indication: If Hirschsprung disease suspected (onset at birth, failure to thrive)

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply liberally multiple times daily after bathing, baseline for all severities
Hydrocortisone 1% face/folds, Triamcinolone 0.1% trunk/limbs BID x7-14 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.03% under 15yr, 0.1% adolescents, steroid-sparing calcineurin inhibitor
Age 6 months+ moderate-severe AD, Q4W subcutaneous, strongly endorsed
BID for age 12yr+, topical JAK inhibitor

notes: Bleach baths evidence-based for reducing secondary skin infections.
guideline_changes: Dupilumab approved from age 6 months for moderate-severe AD. Ruxolitinib cream (topical JAK inhibitor) added for age 12yr+. Replaces 2012 guideline.
counselling: Apply moisturiser generously at least 2-3 times daily and immediately after bathing
Use lukewarm water for bathing (not hot); keep bath time short (5-10 minutes)
Dress child in soft cotton clothing; avoid wool and synthetic fabrics
Cut fingernails short to prevent scratching and skin infection
Eczema is a long-term condition with flare-ups; regular moisturising is the most important step
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if moderate-severe eczema not controlled with mid-potency topical steroids, suspected secondary bacterial or herpetic infection (eczema herpeticum), or need for systemic therapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if eczema herpeticum (widespread vesicles with fever), severe secondary bacterial infection requiring IV antibiotics, or erythroderma

investigations.name: Skin swab culture
investigations.indication: If secondary bacterial infection suspected (crusting, weeping, fever)

investigations.name: Serum IgE
investigations.indication: If allergic triggers suspected; not diagnostic but supportive

investigations.name: Skin prick testing
investigations.indication: If food allergy trigger suspected (egg, milk, peanut in young children)

first_line_drugs.notes: TID x5-7 days, first-line for localised non-bullous impetigo

second_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mg/kg/day divided q6-8h x7 days, extensive or bullous impetigo
8-10 mg/kg/day TMP component q12h x7 days, if MRSA suspected

notes: Systemic antibiotics for extensive disease, bullous impetigo, or suspected MRSA.
counselling: Keep affected area clean; wash gently with soap and water before applying ointment
Child should not share towels, clothes, or bedding to prevent spread to siblings
Keep fingernails short and discourage scratching of the sores
Child can return to school after 24 hours of antibiotic treatment if lesions are covered
Complete the full course of antibiotics; do not stop early even if skin looks better
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if recurrent impetigo despite adequate treatment, suspected MRSA (culture-guided), or deep tissue involvement (ecthyma)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if extensive bullous impetigo with systemic toxicity, suspected staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, or post-streptococcal complications (glomerulonephritis signs)

investigations.name: Wound swab culture and sensitivity
investigations.indication: Recurrent impetigo or failure of first-line treatment

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: If GAS impetigo - screen for post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis 2-3 weeks later

first_line_drugs.notes: 8-10 mg/kg/day TMP component q12h x7-10 days, only if local resistance under 20%
5-7 mg/kg/day q6h x7 days, lower UTI/cystitis only, age 3 months+

second_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mg/kg/day q8h x7-10 days, preferred in India
50-75 mg/kg/day x10-14 days, febrile UTI/pyelonephritis

notes: ISPN 2023 India: first-line guided by local antibiogram. Renal ultrasound for all first febrile UTIs.
guideline_changes: ISPN 2023 India: TMP-SMX resistance over 40-50% in many centres. Cephalexin or cefixime preferred empirically.
counselling: Complete the full antibiotic course; stopping early can cause resistant infection
Encourage the child to drink plenty of water throughout the day
Teach proper wiping technique (front to back) for girls to prevent recurrence
Do not delay urination - encourage child to go to toilet when they feel the urge
Follow up urine test after treatment is important to confirm infection has cleared
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist/urologist if first febrile UTI in child <2 years with abnormal renal ultrasound, recurrent UTIs, vesicoureteral reflux grade III or higher, or renal scarring on DMSA scan
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if age <2 months, febrile UTI with toxicity, vomiting preventing oral antibiotics, urosepsis signs, or known obstructive uropathy

investigations.name: Urine routine and culture (midstream/catheter)
investigations.indication: Mandatory for all suspected UTIs; clean catch or catheter in non-toilet-trained

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: All first febrile UTIs to detect structural anomalies

investigations.name: DMSA renal scan
investigations.indication: 4-6 months after febrile UTI to detect renal scarring

investigations.name: MCU (voiding cystourethrogram)
investigations.indication: Recurrent febrile UTI, abnormal renal US, or family history of VUR

first_line_drugs.notes: Weight-based 6 doses over 3 days BID, take with food

second_line_drugs.notes: 2.4 mg/kg IV at 0h, 12h, 24h then daily, severe malaria first-line
Weight-based x3 days, new ACT added in WHO 2023

notes: Monitor for delayed haemolysis after IV artesunate.
guideline_changes: IV artesunate is now unambiguous first-line for severe malaria, replacing IV quinine. Artesunate-pyronaridine added as new ACT.
counselling: Complete the full 3-day course of ACT tablets even if fever goes away
Give the medicine with food or milk for better absorption
Use mosquito nets (treated with insecticide) every night and mosquito repellent
Return immediately if fever returns after treatment or child becomes very weak
Watch for danger signs: repeated vomiting, dark urine, jaundice, confusion - rush to hospital
referral_criteria: Refer to infectious disease specialist or higher centre if suspected severe/complicated malaria, mixed species infection, treatment failure after 72 hours, or underlying conditions (sickle cell, immunodeficiency)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if cerebral malaria (altered consciousness, seizures), severe anaemia (Hb <5 g/dL), respiratory distress, hypoglycaemia, renal failure, jaundice, parasitaemia >5%, or inability to tolerate oral medication

investigations.name: Peripheral blood smear (thick and thin)
investigations.indication: Gold standard for malaria diagnosis and species identification

investigations.name: Rapid diagnostic test (RDT)
investigations.indication: Point-of-care diagnosis when microscopy not available

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Screen for hypoglycaemia especially in severe malaria

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.2-0.3 mg/kg (max 10mg), preferred pre-hospital non-IV route
0.5 mg/kg (max 20mg), seizure over 5 minutes, pre-hospital

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4mg), hospital management
15-20 mg/kg IV loading, status epilepticus

notes: Antipyretics do NOT prevent recurrence. Long-term anticonvulsants not recommended for simple febrile seizures.
counselling: Febrile seizures look scary but are generally harmless and do not cause brain damage
During a seizure: lay child on side, do not put anything in mouth, time the seizure
If seizure lasts more than 5 minutes, call ambulance or rush to hospital
Paracetamol and ibuprofen do NOT prevent febrile seizures but help with fever comfort
Most children outgrow febrile seizures by age 5-6 years; long-term medicine is usually not needed
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist if complex febrile seizure (focal, >15 minutes, recurrent within 24 hours), seizure in child <6 months or >5 years, or developmental concerns
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if seizure duration >15 minutes (status epilepticus), post-ictal altered consciousness >1 hour, suspected meningitis (neck stiffness, bulging fontanelle), first seizure in infant <12 months, or complex febrile seizure for observation

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Rule out hypoglycaemia as seizure cause

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: If diarrhoea, vomiting, or abnormal fluid intake

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture
investigations.indication: Mandatory if <12 months or meningeal signs present; consider in 12-18 months if unimmunised

investigations.name: EEG
investigations.indication: Not indicated for simple febrile seizure; only if atypical features or developmental concerns

first_line_drugs.notes: Start 5mg BID for age 6yr+, titrate by 5mg/week, max 60mg/day
18-54mg once daily AM, Concerta or Ritalin LA

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5 mg/kg/day for 4 weeks then 1.2 mg/kg/day (max 100mg), non-stimulant
1mg/day HS, titrate to 1-4mg/day, non-stimulant alpha-2 agonist

notes: Behaviour therapy first-line for age 4-5yr. Stimulants first-line for age 6yr+. Monitor growth, BP, HR.
counselling: ADHD is a medical condition, not bad behaviour - the child needs support, not punishment
Medicine works best when combined with behavioural strategies and structured routines
Give methylphenidate in the morning; monitor appetite and sleep
Regular follow-up every 3 months to check weight, height, blood pressure and heart rate
Inform school teachers about the condition; structured classroom seating and shorter task durations help
referral_criteria: Refer to child psychiatrist/developmental pediatrician for initial diagnosis, comorbid conditions (anxiety, learning disability, autism), medication non-response, or significant side effects
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation rarely needed; consider if acute suicidal ideation, severe aggression with safety concerns, or need for inpatient behavioural assessment

investigations.name: Vanderbilt Assessment Scale (parent and teacher)
investigations.indication: Standardised ADHD rating for diagnosis and monitoring

investigations.name: Vision and hearing screening
investigations.indication: Rule out sensory deficits mimicking inattention

investigations.name: Thyroid function tests
investigations.indication: If clinical suspicion of thyroid disorder

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.indication: Before starting stimulants if family history of cardiac disease or sudden death

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.2-0.4 U/kg/day SC once daily at bedtime
0.05-0.1 U/kg/dose SC pre-meal, lispro or aspart

second_line_drugs.notes: Automated insulin delivery, preferred for all ages where available - ISPAD 2022

notes: HbA1c target under 7% for all ages. CGM strongly recommended. TIR 70-180 mg/dL over 70% is a co-primary target.
guideline_changes: Time-in-range over 70% added as co-primary target. Hybrid closed-loop systems now recommended as preferred delivery for all ages.
counselling: Insulin injections are lifelong - never stop without doctor advice even if child feels well
Check blood sugar at least 4 times daily; use CGM if available
Always carry fast-acting sugar (glucose tablets, juice) for low sugar episodes
Learn to count carbohydrates in food and adjust insulin dose accordingly
Regular follow-up every 3 months for HbA1c; annual eye and kidney screening from 5 years of diabetes
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric endocrinologist for all new diagnoses, recurrent DKA, HbA1c persistently >9%, suspected celiac or thyroid comorbidity, or transition to insulin pump/CGM
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if diabetic ketoacidosis (pH <7.3, bicarbonate <18), new-onset T1D for stabilisation and education, severe hypoglycaemia with seizure or loss of consciousness, or intercurrent illness with inability to manage at home

investigations.name: HbA1c
investigations.indication: Every 3 months to assess glycaemic control

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.3 mg/kg/day (max 5-10mg) once daily, long-acting CCB
0.07 mg/kg/day (max 5mg) QD initially, preferred for CKD or proteinuria

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.7 mg/kg/day (max 50mg), alternative to ACE inhibitor
1-3 mg/kg/day (max 50mg), volume-dependent hypertension

notes: Beta-blockers NOT recommended as first-line in children. Lifestyle modification is essential first step.
counselling: Reduce salt in cooking and avoid packaged/junk food which is high in sodium
Encourage daily physical activity for at least 60 minutes (walking, cycling, sports)
Maintain healthy weight - weight loss of even 5% can significantly lower blood pressure
Give medicine at the same time every day; do not skip doses
Regular blood pressure checks at home and at clinic visits are essential for monitoring
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist/cardiologist if stage 2 hypertension, secondary hypertension suspected (renal, endocrine), end-organ damage, or persistent hypertension despite 2 medications
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if hypertensive emergency (symptomatic: headache, seizures, visual changes, encephalopathy), BP >30 mmHg above 95th percentile, or evidence of end-organ damage (cardiac, renal, retinal)

investigations.name: Renal function tests (creatinine, BUN)
investigations.indication: Rule out renal cause of hypertension

investigations.name: Urinalysis with protein/creatinine ratio
investigations.indication: Screen for renal disease and proteinuria

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound with Doppler
investigations.indication: Screen for structural renal anomalies or renal artery stenosis

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Assess for left ventricular hypertrophy as end-organ damage

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: Baseline and monitor with ACEI/diuretic therapy

first_line_drugs.notes: 2 g/kg single infusion over 10-12h, within first 10 days of fever onset
30-50 mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses during acute phase, then 3-5 mg/kg/day once afebrile

second_line_drugs.notes: 5 mg/kg single dose, for IVIG-resistant Kawasaki disease
For high-risk IVIG-resistant cases per Kobayashi/Egami scoring

notes: Echocardiogram at diagnosis, 2 weeks and 6 weeks. Coronary artery aneurysm is the key complication.
counselling: This is a serious condition affecting blood vessels of the heart - treatment must start urgently
IVIG infusion may cause fever and headache; this is expected and managed in hospital
Give aspirin as prescribed - this prevents blood clots in heart vessels
Avoid live vaccines (MMR, varicella) for 11 months after IVIG treatment
Regular follow-up echocardiograms at 2 weeks and 6 weeks are essential to check heart arteries
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist immediately on diagnosis for echocardiography and co-management; ongoing follow-up for coronary artery monitoring
hospitalisation_criteria: All children with suspected Kawasaki disease should be hospitalised for IVIG infusion; emergent admission if coronary artery aneurysm, IVIG-resistant fever, shock/hemodynamic instability (Kawasaki shock syndrome)

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: At diagnosis, 2 weeks and 6 weeks; assess coronary arteries

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Thrombocytosis, leukocytosis, anaemia in Kawasaki disease

investigations.name: CRP and ESR
investigations.indication: Inflammatory markers to guide treatment response

investigations.name: Liver function tests
investigations.indication: Hepatic involvement and albumin level (low albumin = IVIG resistance risk)

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: Sterile pyuria common in Kawasaki disease

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 spray/nostril (50mcg) once daily, age 4yr+, most effective single agent
5mg (age 2-5yr), 10mg (age 6yr+) once daily, non-sedating antihistamine

second_line_drugs.notes: 5mg (age 2-5yr), 10mg (age 6yr+) once daily
4mg (2-5yr), 5mg (6-14yr) once daily. FDA 2020 Black Box Warning for neuropsychiatric effects

notes: FDA 2020 Black Box Warning for montelukast: mood changes, suicidal ideation. Weigh risk/benefit.
counselling: Use nasal spray daily even when symptoms are better - it works best with regular use
Close windows during high pollen season; use air conditioning if possible
Wash hands and face after playing outdoors; shower before bedtime to remove allergens
Keep bedroom free of dust - use allergen-proof pillow and mattress covers
Avoid first-generation antihistamines (Avil/Benadryl) as they cause drowsiness and affect school performance
referral_criteria: Refer to allergist/immunologist if symptoms uncontrolled despite intranasal steroids + antihistamines, suspected nasal polyps, or candidate for allergen immunotherapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not typically required; consider if severe epistaxis from nasal steroid use or complicated sinusitis

investigations.name: Skin prick test
investigations.indication: Identify specific allergen triggers for avoidance and immunotherapy

investigations.name: Serum specific IgE (ImmunoCAP)
investigations.indication: When skin testing not feasible or child on antihistamines

investigations.name: Nasal endoscopy
investigations.indication: If unilateral symptoms, suspected polyps, or structural abnormality

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.01 mg/kg anterolateral thigh (max 0.5mg). Auto-injector: 0.15mg under 30kg / 0.3mg 30kg+. Repeat q5-15min if needed

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mL/kg IV bolus, hypotension or shock
1 mg/kg (max 50mg), adjunct only - never delays epinephrine
1-2 mg/kg (max 125mg), adjunct to reduce biphasic reaction risk

notes: Epinephrine is the ONLY first-line treatment. Never delay for antihistamines. Observe 4-6h for biphasic anaphylaxis.
counselling: Epinephrine auto-injector must be carried by child at ALL times - in school bag and at home
Use auto-injector FIRST at any sign of anaphylaxis - do not wait for it to get worse
After using epinephrine, always call ambulance and go to hospital for 4-6 hour observation
Teach family, school staff, and caregivers how to use the auto-injector
Get a medical alert bracelet/card with the allergy information for the child
referral_criteria: Refer to allergist/immunologist for all cases of anaphylaxis for trigger identification (skin prick/IgE testing), anaphylaxis action plan, and consideration for oral immunotherapy
hospitalisation_criteria: All anaphylaxis cases require minimum 4-6 hours emergency department observation; admit for 24 hours if severe/refractory anaphylaxis, needed multiple epinephrine doses, biphasic reaction history, or respiratory compromise

investigations.name: Serum tryptase
investigations.indication: Draw within 1-2 hours of onset; confirms mast cell degranulation

investigations.name: Specific IgE testing
investigations.indication: 2-4 weeks after event to identify trigger allergen

investigations.name: Skin prick testing
investigations.indication: 4-6 weeks after event by allergist for trigger identification

diagnosis_name: Fever and mild to moderate pain

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg/dose q4-6h PO/PR, max 75 mg/kg/day, not over 4g/day, all ages
5-10 mg/kg/dose q6-8h PO, max 40 mg/kg/day or 2.4g/day, age 6 months+

second_line_drugs.notes: q4h alternating schedule, evidence-based for sustained fever control

notes: Avoid aspirin in all children under 18yr (Reye syndrome). Avoid ibuprofen in dehydration or under 6 months.
counselling: Fever itself is not dangerous - it is the body fighting infection; focus on child comfort
Give paracetamol or ibuprofen by weight, not age; use the measuring cup provided
Do not give aspirin to children - it can cause a serious liver condition (Reye syndrome)
Dress child in light clothing; sponging with lukewarm (not cold) water may help comfort
Seek medical attention if fever persists >3 days, child <3 months old, or child appears very sick
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatrician if fever without source >5 days (evaluate for Kawasaki, autoimmune), recurrent fevers (periodic fever syndromes), or fever with unexplained weight loss/night sweats
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if febrile neonate <28 days (full sepsis workup), toxic appearance, petechial rash with fever, suspected meningitis, or fever >5 days without identified source

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Fever without source to screen for bacterial infection

investigations.name: Urinalysis and urine culture
investigations.indication: Mandatory in febrile child <2 years without source

investigations.name: Blood culture
investigations.indication: If toxic appearance, fever >39C in young infant, or admission planned

first_line_drugs.notes: Percutaneous; indicated for critical valvar AS in neonates who are ductal-dependent regardless of gradient
0.01-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV continuous infusion to maintain ductal patency pre-intervention
Open surgical repair for neonates/infants not amenable to balloon valvuloplasty

second_line_drugs.notes: Autograft pulmonary valve replacement of aortic valve with pulmonary homograft; gold standard for AVR in children
For older children when repair not feasible; requires lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin
0.1 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; afterload reduction for aortic regurgitation

notes: Repair is preferred over replacement in pediatric patients; 10-year freedom from reoperation is ~51%. Bicuspid aortic valve is most common anomaly and requires lifelong surveillance for aortopathy.
guideline_changes: 2025 ACC/AHA/ISACHD guideline for congenital heart disease management released
counselling: Your child has a heart valve problem that needs regular cardiology follow-up
Watch for symptoms like difficulty breathing during feeds, poor weight gain, or bluish skin
Maintain good dental hygiene to prevent heart valve infections (endocarditis)
Ensure all routine vaccinations including influenza are up to date
Some children may need surgery or catheter procedures - your cardiologist will guide timing
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist at diagnosis; refer to pediatric cardiac surgery if gradient >50 mmHg, symptomatic stenosis, or progressive aortic regurgitation with LV dilation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if critical aortic stenosis in neonate (ductal-dependent), acute heart failure, syncope with exertion, or planned catheterisation/surgical intervention

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.indication: LV hypertrophy, ST changes in significant stenosis

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Assess cardiac silhouette and pulmonary vascularity

investigations.indication: Detailed aortopathy assessment in bicuspid aortic valve

first_line_drugs.notes: Small ASDs (<5mm) in children <2yr; many close spontaneously; serial echo monitoring q6-12mo
Percutaneous Amplatzer or GORE occluder for secundum ASD ≥5mm with RV volume overload; via femoral vein
Open repair via sternotomy with CPB for large, sinus venosus, or primum ASDs not amenable to device

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; for symptomatic volume overload pre-intervention
5-10 mcg/kg/day PO divided q12h; for RV failure symptoms if present pre-operatively

notes: Closure is recommended if significant L-to-R shunt (Qp:Qs >1.5) with RV dilation. Device closure has low morbidity and avoids sternotomy for eligible secundum defects.
guideline_changes: Transcatheter device closure increasingly preferred over surgery for secundum ASDs
counselling: Many small ASDs close on their own in the first 2 years - regular echo monitoring is needed
The child can usually participate in normal physical activities unless doctor advises otherwise
Watch for symptoms like easy tiredness, frequent respiratory infections, or poor weight gain
If device closure or surgery is needed, it has excellent outcomes with low risk
Keep all routine vaccinations up to date including annual influenza vaccine
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist for all detected ASDs; refer to cardiac surgery/interventional cardiology if significant shunt (Qp:Qs >1.5) with RV dilation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if heart failure symptoms, planned device closure or surgical repair, paradoxical embolism, or arrhythmia (atrial fibrillation/flutter)

investigations.indication: Confirm diagnosis, assess shunt size and RV dilation

investigations.name: ECG

investigations.name: Cardiac catheterisation

first_line_drugs.notes: Coarctectomy with extended end-to-end anastomosis; primary technique in neonates and infants
0.01-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV infusion to maintain ductal patency in neonates prior to intervention
Preferred for discrete coarctation in children 4mo-5yr (<25kg) without arch hypoplasia

second_line_drugs.notes: Preferred in older children >25kg; endovascular stenting for native or recurrent coarctation
0.1 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; for residual hypertension post-repair, titrate to effect
0.1-0.2 mg/kg/day PO once daily (max 5mg); adjunct antihypertensive post-repair
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO once daily; beta-blocker for post-repair hypertension

notes: Long-term hypertension monitoring is essential even after successful repair. SBE prophylaxis recommended indefinitely regardless of associated anomalies.
source: STS 2024 Neonatal CoA Guidelines, AHA 2024 Aortopathy Statement
guideline_changes: 2024 STS clinical practice guidelines published for neonatal/infant coarctation management
counselling: Your child has a narrowing of the main blood vessel (aorta) that needs treatment
After repair, lifelong blood pressure monitoring is essential - check BP in both arms
Regular cardiology follow-up is needed even after successful surgery
Report any headache, nosebleeds, or leg pain with exercise to the cardiologist
Maintain good dental hygiene for endocarditis prevention
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist at diagnosis; urgent referral to pediatric cardiac surgery if critical coarctation in neonate, BP gradient >20 mmHg, or persistent hypertension
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if neonatal critical coarctation (PGE1 needed), heart failure, planned surgical or catheter intervention, or hypertensive emergency

investigations.name: Four-limb blood pressure
investigations.indication: Upper-lower limb BP gradient >20 mmHg is diagnostic

diagnosis_name: Congenital malformation of cardiac chambers and connections, unspecified

first_line_drugs.notes: Diagnostic and interventional; for defect characterization and percutaneous repair of simple lesions
Open-heart surgery tailored to specific defect anatomy; timing based on hemodynamic significance

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; for CHF symptom management pre-intervention
0.1 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; afterload reduction for ventricular dysfunction
5-10 mcg/kg/day PO divided q12h; for symptomatic heart failure support

notes: This unspecified code requires further diagnostic workup with echocardiography and cardiac MRI to define anatomy. Lifelong cardiology follow-up is essential even after surgical correction.
source: AHA/ACC 2018 ACHD Guidelines, ACC 2025 CHD Guideline
guideline_changes: 2025 ACC/AHA/ISACHD guideline for congenital heart disease released
counselling: Your child has a heart condition that requires further testing to understand fully
Keep all cardiology appointments and scheduled echocardiograms
Watch for fast breathing, poor feeding, excessive sweating, or bluish discolouration
All routine vaccinations should be given on schedule unless specifically advised otherwise
Lifelong cardiology follow-up is important even after any corrective procedures
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist immediately at detection for definitive diagnosis with echocardiography and cardiac MRI; ongoing cardiology follow-up lifelong
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if symptomatic heart failure, cyanosis, arrhythmia, need for diagnostic cardiac catheterisation, or planned surgical/interventional procedure

first_line_drugs.notes: First-line for moderate-severe valvar PS (gradient >40mmHg); percutaneous via femoral vein; excellent outcomes
Mild PS (gradient <40mmHg) requires no intervention; serial echo q1-2yr to monitor progression

second_line_drugs.notes: Open surgical repair for dysplastic valves (e.g., Noonan syndrome) not responsive to balloon
Surgical patch for subvalvar or supravalvar obstruction combined with valve repair
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q8h; for infundibular spasm if dynamic RVOT obstruction present

notes: Balloon valvuloplasty is curative in most cases with excellent long-term outcomes. Patients with Noonan or Williams syndromes have lower success rates with balloon and may need surgical repair.
source: ACC 2023 PS Clinical Practice Algorithm, AHA 2011 Pediatric CHD
guideline_changes: ACC published updated clinical practice algorithm for PS follow-up in 2023
counselling: Mild pulmonary stenosis usually does not need treatment and is monitored with regular echo
If balloon procedure is needed, it is done through a small tube in the leg vein - very safe
Child can usually participate in normal activities; severe PS may need exercise restriction
Regular cardiology follow-up is needed even after successful balloon valvuloplasty
Watch for increasing tiredness, bluish discolouration, or fainting with exertion
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist at diagnosis; refer for intervention if gradient >40 mmHg, RV dysfunction, or symptomatic stenosis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if critical PS in neonate (PGE1 dependent), planned balloon valvuloplasty, right heart failure, or significant cyanosis

first_line_drugs.notes: VSD patch closure + RVOT reconstruction; typically performed at 3-6 months of age
0.01-0.05 mcg/kg/min IV infusion; maintains ductal patency in cyanotic neonates pre-surgery

second_line_drugs.notes: Palliative systemic-to-pulmonary shunt for small neonates not suitable for primary repair
Transcatheter palliation; stent placed across RVOT to improve pulmonary blood flow as bridge to repair
Transcatheter ductal stent as alternative to surgical shunt for palliation in selected neonates
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q8h; for hypercyanotic (tet) spell prevention and management

notes: Complete repair by 6 months is standard; earlier if symptomatic cyanosis. Knee-chest positioning and morphine 0.1mg/kg IM are acute tet spell measures before definitive repair.
source: ACC 2024 TOF Clinical Algorithm, AATS Expert Consensus
guideline_changes: ACC 2024 updated clinical practice algorithm for unrepaired and repaired TOF follow-up
counselling: Your child has a combination of 4 heart defects that will need surgery, usually around 3-6 months
Blue spells (tet spells) may happen - hold baby in knee-chest position and go to hospital immediately
Ensure adequate hydration and avoid prolonged crying which can trigger spells
Surgery has excellent outcomes; most children lead normal lives after repair
Lifelong cardiology follow-up is required even after successful repair
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiac surgeon at diagnosis for surgical planning; urgently if symptomatic cyanosis or hypercyanotic spells
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if hypercyanotic (tet) spell, oxygen saturation <75%, severe cyanosis, planned surgical repair, or need for palliative shunt

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Confirm anatomy: VSD, overriding aorta, RVOT obstruction, RVH

investigations.name: Cardiac catheterisation

first_line_drugs.notes: Anastomosis of common pulmonary venous confluence to left atrium with ligation of anomalous connection; CPB required
0.01-0.05 mcg/kg/min IV infusion; maintains PDA to allow oxygenated blood to reach systemic circulation

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 ppm via ventilator; for pulmonary hypertension management in obstructed TAPVC pre-operatively
Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation as bridge in critically ill neonates with refractory hypoxemia
1 mg/kg/dose IV q6-12h; for pulmonary edema management in obstructed TAPVC

notes: Obstructed TAPVC is a surgical emergency requiring immediate repair. Non-obstructed TAPVC should be repaired within the first month of life; lifelong follow-up for pulmonary vein stenosis.
source: AHA Pediatric CHD Guidelines, STS Congenital Database
counselling: This is a serious heart condition requiring urgent surgery - your baby will be in NICU/PICU
After surgery, regular cardiology follow-up is essential to monitor for vein narrowing
Watch for fast breathing, difficulty feeding, or bluish skin and report immediately
Ensure all vaccinations including RSV prophylaxis are given as recommended
Long-term outcomes are generally good after successful surgical repair
referral_criteria: Emergent referral to pediatric cardiac surgeon at diagnosis; ongoing follow-up with pediatric cardiologist for pulmonary vein stenosis surveillance
hospitalisation_criteria: All TAPVC cases require hospitalisation; emergency surgery for obstructed TAPVC; semi-urgent surgery for non-obstructed TAPVC within first month of life

investigations.name: Echocardiography

investigations.indication: Right axis deviation, RV hypertrophy, peaked P waves

investigations.indication: Delineate anatomy pre-surgery if echo insufficient

snomed_display: Total anomalous pulmonary venous connections of mixed type

first_line_drugs.notes: Jatene procedure; anatomic repair switching aorta and PA with coronary reimplantation; within first 2 weeks of life
0.01-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV continuous; maintains ductal patency for systemic-pulmonary mixing pre-surgery
Rashkind procedure; emergent catheter-based creation of ASD to improve mixing; for severe cyanosis

second_line_drugs.notes: Atrial switch operation; historical alternative now reserved for late-presenting or complex anatomy cases
For d-TGA with VSD and PS; LV-to-aorta baffle + RV-to-PA conduit

notes: Arterial switch must be performed within 2-4 weeks before LV deconditions in low-pressure pulmonary circuit. Lifelong follow-up required for coronary and neo-aortic root complications.
source: AHA/ACC CHD Guidelines, ACC 2025 ACHD Guideline
counselling: Your baby needs surgery within the first 2 weeks of life - this is urgent but has excellent outcomes
The baby will be in NICU/PICU before and after surgery
After arterial switch operation, most children lead completely normal active lives
Lifelong cardiology follow-up is required to monitor coronary arteries and heart valve function
Ensure all routine vaccinations are given on schedule after recovery
referral_criteria: Emergent referral to pediatric cardiac surgery centre at prenatal or postnatal diagnosis; lifelong cardiology follow-up
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require immediate NICU admission; PGE1 to maintain ductal patency; Rashkind septostomy if severely cyanotic; arterial switch surgery within 2 weeks

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Confirm diagnosis, assess coronary pattern, and associated lesions

first_line_drugs.notes: Small restrictive VSDs; many close spontaneously in first year; serial echo q6-12mo monitoring
For moderate-large VSDs with CHF, Qp:Qs >2, or PA hypertension; via sternotomy with CPB
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; first-line diuretic for CHF symptoms from large L-to-R shunt

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q8h; afterload reduction for symptomatic CHF management
1-3 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; potassium-sparing diuretic adjunct for CHF
Amplatzer muscular VSD occluder for large muscular VSDs; percutaneous or hybrid approach
5-10 mcg/kg/day PO divided q12h; for symptomatic CHF with large shunt pre-surgery

notes: Large VSDs with elevated PA pressure must be closed early to prevent irreversible pulmonary vascular obstructive disease. Perimembranous and outlet VSDs require surgical closure.
source: AHA/ACC 2024 CHD Management, ACC VSD Algorithm
guideline_changes: Transcatheter VSD closure devices gaining wider use for muscular defects
counselling: Many small VSDs close on their own during the first year of life
Watch for fast breathing during feeds, poor weight gain, or excessive sweating - report to doctor
If surgery is needed, it has excellent outcomes; most children recover fully
Extra calories may be needed as heart has to work harder - your doctor will guide feeding
Regular echo follow-up is important to track the size of the hole
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist for all VSDs; refer to cardiac surgery if large VSD with heart failure, Qp:Qs >2, or rising PA pressure
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if heart failure (poor feeding, tachypnea, hepatomegaly), planned surgical or device closure, or signs of pulmonary hypertension

investigations.name: Echocardiography with Doppler
investigations.indication: Confirm diagnosis, assess shunt size, and PA pressure

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; ACE inhibitor for LV dysfunction; titrate to 0.5 mg/kg/day
0.05 mg/kg/dose PO q12h; titrate to 0.3-0.5 mg/kg/dose; beta-blocker for symptomatic HF
1-2 mg/kg/dose IV/PO q6-12h; loop diuretic for fluid overload and pulmonary congestion
0.25-0.75 mcg/kg/min IV infusion; inodilator for cardiogenic shock with low cardiac output

second_line_drugs.notes: 2 g/kg IV over 10-12h single dose; immunomodulatory therapy; evidence mixed but commonly used
Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation; bridge to recovery or transplant for refractory cardiogenic shock
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; aldosterone antagonist for chronic HF management

notes: Supportive care is the mainstay; avoid NSAIDs and exercise restriction for 3-6 months. ECMO has shown good outcomes as bridge therapy in fulminant cases with recoverable myocardium.
source: AHA 2021 Pediatric Myocarditis Statement, ACC 2024 Myocarditis ECDP
guideline_changes: 2024 ACC Expert Consensus Decision Pathway for myocarditis diagnosis and management published
counselling: Your child has inflammation of the heart muscle - complete bed rest is essential
No sports or vigorous physical activity for at least 3-6 months after recovery
Give medicines regularly as prescribed - they help the heart recover
Watch for worsening breathlessness, chest pain, fast heartbeat, or swelling of feet
Regular follow-up echocardiograms are needed to monitor heart function recovery
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist immediately on suspicion; transfer to centre with ECMO capability if fulminant presentation
hospitalisation_criteria: All suspected myocarditis cases require hospitalisation; ICU admission if hemodynamically unstable, arrhythmia, or reduced ejection fraction <40%

investigations.name: Echocardiography

investigations.name: Cardiac MRI
investigations.indication: Gold standard for diagnosis; myocardial edema and late gadolinium enhancement

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.5 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; ACE inhibitor for DCM with systolic dysfunction; first-line GDMT
0.05-0.5 mg/kg/dose PO q12h; beta-blocker for DCM; titrate slowly over weeks
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; loop diuretic for congestive symptoms

second_line_drugs.notes: ARNI; FDA-approved for children >1yr; dose by weight; emerging first-line for pediatric HFrEF
Cardiac myosin inhibitor for obstructive HCM; dose per protocol; new class in 2024 HCM guideline
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; aldosterone antagonist adjunct for symptomatic HF
Loading 10-15 mg/kg/day PO x7-10d then 5 mg/kg/day; for ventricular arrhythmias in CM

notes: Treatment is guided by cardiomyopathy subtype: DCM needs afterload reduction, HCM needs negative inotropy. Sacubitril-valsartan is increasingly used in pediatric DCM based on FDA approval for age >1yr.
source: AHA 2023 Pediatric CM Statement, AHA/ACC 2024 HCM Guideline
guideline_changes: 2024 AHA/ACC HCM guideline adds cardiac myosin inhibitors for obstructive HCM
counselling: Cardiomyopathy is a chronic heart condition requiring lifelong medication and monitoring
Give all heart medicines at the same time every day; never skip doses
Restrict strenuous activity as advised; light activity may be allowed based on type
Monitor daily weight - sudden weight gain may indicate fluid retention
Genetic testing may be recommended for family screening in some types
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist at diagnosis; refer for transplant evaluation if refractory heart failure or EF <25% despite optimal GDMT
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if new-onset cardiomyopathy for workup, acute decompensated heart failure, ventricular arrhythmia, syncope, or need for inotropic support

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Assess chamber size, wall thickness, systolic and diastolic function

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; titrate to 0.5 mg/kg/day; ACE inhibitor cornerstone of HFrEF GDMT
0.05 mg/kg/dose PO q12h; titrate to 0.35 mg/kg/dose; preferred beta-blocker in pediatric HF
1-2 mg/kg/dose PO/IV q6-12h; loop diuretic for volume overload; adjust to euvolemia
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h; aldosterone antagonist for symptomatic HFrEF

second_line_drugs.notes: ARNI; FDA-approved age >1yr; replacing ACE-I as preferred neurohormonal blockade in pediatric HFrEF
0.25-0.75 mcg/kg/min IV infusion; inodilator for acute decompensated HF with low output
5-10 mcg/kg/day PO; target level 0.5-1.0 ng/mL; for symptom relief in HFrEF

notes: Pediatric HF GDMT mirrors adult pillars: ACE-I/ARNI + beta-blocker + MRA + diuretic. SGLT2 inhibitors show promise but lack pediatric data; sacubitril-valsartan is rapidly becoming first-line.
source: AHA 2022 HF Guideline, AHA 2024 Pediatric CHD-HF Statement
guideline_changes: 2024 AHA scientific statement on chronic HF in children with CHD published
counselling: Heart failure medicines must be taken daily without missing; they help the heart pump better
Monitor weight daily - sudden increase of 0.5-1 kg may mean fluid retention
Restrict salt intake; avoid packaged foods, pickles, and papad
Watch for increasing breathlessness, leg swelling, or inability to lie flat - see doctor urgently
Some activity is beneficial; follow doctor guidance on safe exercise levels
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist for all new heart failure; refer for advanced HF/transplant evaluation if refractory to optimal GDMT
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if acute decompensated heart failure, new-onset HF for workup, need for IV diuretics or inotropes, arrhythmia, or pre-syncope/syncope

investigations.indication: Baseline and monitoring with diuretic/ACEI therapy

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); first-line NSAID; taper over 2-4 weeks after symptom resolution
Children ≤5yr: 0.5mg/day PO; >5yr: 1-1.5mg/day PO divided q12h; no loading dose; 3 months duration

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 mg/kg/dose PO q12h; alternative NSAID if ibuprofen not tolerated
0.25-0.5 mg/kg/day PO; only if NSAID+colchicine fail or contraindicated; slow taper over weeks
2-4 mg/kg/day SC (max 100mg); IL-1 receptor antagonist for refractory recurrent pericarditis
Loading 4.4 mg/kg SC then 2.2 mg/kg/wk; FDA-approved for recurrent pericarditis age ≥12yr

notes: Colchicine added to NSAID halves recurrence rates. Avoid corticosteroids as first-line due to increased recurrence risk; exercise restriction until symptom and CRP resolution.
source: ACC 2025 Pericarditis Consensus, ESC 2015 Pericardial Diseases
guideline_changes: 2025 ACC Expert Consensus Statement on pericarditis diagnosis and management released
counselling: Take ibuprofen with food to protect the stomach; colchicine is given for 3 months to prevent recurrence
Complete bed rest initially; no sports until symptoms resolve and CRP is normal
Watch for increasing chest pain, breathlessness, or dizziness - these need urgent evaluation
Avoid corticosteroid treatment if possible as it increases risk of recurrence
Follow-up echocardiogram is needed to ensure no pericardial effusion or constriction
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist if moderate-large pericardial effusion, recurrent pericarditis (>2 episodes), suspected purulent or tubercular pericarditis, or tamponade features
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if large pericardial effusion with hemodynamic compromise, cardiac tamponade, suspected purulent pericarditis, fever with myopericarditis, or high-risk features (troponin elevation)

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Assess pericardial effusion size and hemodynamic impact

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.indication: Diffuse ST elevation, PR depression; low voltage if large effusion

investigations.indication: Inflammatory markers to guide duration of treatment

investigations.indication: Cardiomegaly if large effusion (water bottle sign)

first_line_drugs.notes: Ampicillin 200-300 mg/kg/day IV divided q4-6h + Gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day IV q8h; empiric for native valve
40-60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h; for MRSA or penicillin allergy; target trough 15-20 mcg/mL

second_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/day IV once daily (max 4g); for viridans streptococci; 4-week course with or without gentamicin
200 mg/kg/day IV divided q4-6h; for MSSA endocarditis; 6-week course
Valve repair or replacement for refractory infection, large vegetations >10mm, embolic events, or HF

notes: Treatment is 4-6 weeks IV antibiotics tailored to culture and sensitivity. Consult pediatric infectious disease; adjust doses based on renal function and drug levels.
source: AHA 2015 IE Update, ESC 2023 Endocarditis Guidelines
guideline_changes: 2023 ESC guidelines updated with refined surgical timing and diagnostic criteria
counselling: Endocarditis requires 4-6 weeks of intravenous antibiotics - compliance is critical
Maintain excellent dental hygiene to prevent future episodes
The child may need a PICC line for prolonged IV antibiotics at home
Report any new fever, joint pain, or red spots on skin during treatment
Children with valve disease need antibiotic prophylaxis before dental procedures lifelong
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist and infectious disease specialist at diagnosis; refer to cardiac surgery if valve destruction, large vegetations >10mm, recurrent emboli, or refractory infection
hospitalisation_criteria: All infective endocarditis cases require hospitalisation for IV antibiotics, blood cultures, and monitoring; ICU if hemodynamic instability, acute valve failure, or stroke

investigations.indication: Identify vegetations, assess valve function, abscess

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg/dose PO q8h (ages 1-17yr); FDA-approved for WHO Group 1 PAH; titrate based on response
2 mg/kg/dose PO q12h (ages ≥3yr); ERA approved for pediatric PAH; monitor LFTs monthly
10-20 ppm via ventilator; first-line for acute PH crisis and PPHN; wean gradually

1.25 ng/kg/min SC/IV; titrate slowly; prostacyclin analogue for advanced PAH
Dose per weight; dual ERA increasingly used in pediatric PAH; limited pediatric data
Oral IP receptor agonist; titrate from 200mcg q12h; emerging use in pediatric PAH

counselling: Pulmonary hypertension requires lifelong medication - never stop without cardiologist guidance
Avoid high altitudes and air travel without supplemental oxygen
Avoid dehydration and excessive salt intake
Annual influenza vaccination and RSV prophylaxis are important
Report any worsening breathlessness, fainting, or blue discolouration immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to pulmonary hypertension centre for all confirmed PAH; escalation to prostacyclin therapy if WHO FC III-IV
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if acute RV failure, syncope, new arrhythmia, PAH crisis (acute PH with hemodynamic instability), oxygen desaturation <85%, or planned catheterisation

investigations.indication: Estimate PA pressure, assess RV function

diagnosis_name: Acute rheumatic fever without mention of heart involvement

first_line_drugs.notes: 600,000 U IM x1 (<27kg) or 1.2 MU IM x1 (≥27kg); single dose for streptococcal eradication

guideline_changes: Naproxen now recommended as first-line over aspirin for rheumatic arthritis
counselling: Acute rheumatic fever is caused by untreated strep throat - always complete strep treatment
Monthly penicillin injection is needed for at least 5-10 years to prevent recurrence
Joint pain usually resolves in 2-4 weeks with treatment
Regular echocardiograms are needed to check if heart valves are affected
All sore throats in the family should be tested and treated for strep
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric cardiologist if any cardiac involvement (murmur, echo abnormality); refer to pediatric rheumatologist if atypical presentation or chorea
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if carditis with heart failure, severe chorea, high fever not responding to treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty requiring echocardiography and workup

counselling: Monthly penicillin injection must not be missed - it prevents further heart valve damage
Report any fever, joint pain, or new breathlessness immediately as it may indicate recurrence

counselling: Apply antifungal cream beyond the visible rash edges by 2 cm; continue for 1 week after rash clears
For tinea capitis (scalp ringworm), oral medicine is essential - cream alone will not work
Do not share combs, towels, caps, or pillows to prevent spread
Wash clothing and bedding in hot water during treatment
If family members or pets have similar patches, they need treatment too
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if tinea capitis not responding to 8 weeks of griseofulvin, suspected T. indotineae (resistant strain), extensive tinea corporis, or onychomycosis
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not needed; consider if kerion (inflammatory tinea capitis) with secondary bacterial infection requiring IV antibiotics

investigations.name: KOH preparation
investigations.indication: Confirm dermatophyte hyphae before starting treatment

investigations.name: Fungal culture
investigations.indication: Species identification; important if treatment failure

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to body/scalp as wash, leave 5 min, rinse daily for 2 weeks; for pityriasis versicolor
Apply to affected area, leave 10 min, rinse daily for 1-2 weeks; OTC antifungal
Apply to affected area BID for 2-4 weeks; topical azole for localized disease

second_line_drugs.notes: 6 mg/kg PO (max 400 mg) single dose or weekly x2-3 weeks; for extensive disease
3-5 mg/kg/day PO for 5-7 days; for recurrent or widespread pityriasis versicolor
Apply as body wash, leave 5 min, rinse; use 2-3x/week; OTC maintenance option

notes: Pityriasis versicolor has high recurrence; discuss monthly prophylactic antifungal wash. Dyspigmentation may persist for months after successful treatment.
counselling: Pityriasis versicolor is caused by a normal skin yeast - it is not contagious
Apply antifungal shampoo as body wash; leave on for 5-10 minutes before rinsing
The white/brown patches may take months to match normal skin colour even after treatment
Use antifungal shampoo once monthly as maintenance to prevent recurrence
The condition commonly recurs in hot humid weather - preventive treatment before summer helps
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if widespread recurrent disease not responding to topical treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not needed for superficial mycoses

investigations.name: KOH preparation
investigations.indication: Spaghetti and meatballs appearance confirms Malassezia

first_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 mg/kg/dose PO Q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); for pain and inflammation in EM minor
1.25 mg/kg/dose PO Q6h (max 50 mg/dose); for pruritus; can also use as oral rinse
Apply to skin lesions BID for 7-14 days; for symptomatic relief of EM minor

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/dose PO QID (max 400 mg/dose) for 5 days; if HSV-triggered EM confirmed
>=12 yr: 500 mg PO BID x 5 days; for HSV-associated recurrent EM; suppressive dosing
Apply to oral lesions Q3h PRN (max 4 doses/day); for painful oral mucosal involvement

counselling: Erythema multiforme usually heals on its own in 1-4 weeks
Give pain medicine as needed; soft cool foods if mouth sores are present
Avoid hot or spicy foods that may irritate mouth ulcers
If triggered by cold sores (herpes), antiviral medicine can prevent recurrence
Watch for spreading rash, blistering of skin, or difficulty eating/drinking - see doctor urgently

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if EM major with severe oral involvement preventing eating, suspected SJS/TEN (detachment >10% BSA), dehydration, or systemic toxicity

investigations.name: Skin biopsy

investigations.name: Mycoplasma IgM

Bed rest with leg elevation; compressive bandaging; reduces inflammation and pain

second_line_drugs.notes: 150-300 mg PO TID for 3-4 weeks; for refractory EN; monitor thyroid function
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO for 1-2 weeks then taper; only after infection excluded

counselling: Erythema nodosum usually resolves on its own in 2-6 weeks
Rest legs as much as possible; elevate legs when sitting
Take ibuprofen regularly with food for pain and inflammation
The bumps may leave bruise-like marks that fade gradually - this is normal
It is important to find the underlying cause - complete all prescribed blood tests
referral_criteria: Refer to rheumatologist or dermatologist if recurrent EN, suspected underlying systemic disease (IBD, sarcoidosis, TB), or not responding to NSAIDs
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed; admit if unable to walk due to pain, suspected underlying serious condition (TB, lymphoma), or systemic toxicity

investigations.indication: If GI symptoms present; screen for IBD

Apply to affected area BID for 7-14 days; for localized erythematous rash
Apply liberally BID-TID; soothe and protect inflamed skin

1.25 mg/kg/dose PO Q6h (max 50 mg/dose); for acute symptomatic relief
2 mg/kg/day PO divided Q6-8h (max 25 mg/dose); for persistent pruritus

counselling: Most erythematous rashes in children are self-limiting and resolve within 1-2 weeks
Apply moisturiser and mild steroid cream as directed for itch and redness
If caused by a medicine, stop the medicine and inform your doctor
Take photos of the rash progression to show the doctor at follow-up
Seek urgent care if rash spreads rapidly, blisters form, or child develops fever
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if diagnosis uncertain, rash persisting >2 weeks, suspected drug reaction, or mucosal involvement
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if suspected severe drug eruption (SJS/TEN), widespread blistering, mucosal involvement, or systemic toxicity

investigations.indication: Eosinophilia in drug reactions; atypical lymphocytes in viral

Apply to itchy lesions BID for 1-2 weeks; for moderate pruritus

3x/week for 2-4 weeks; for severe/widespread pruritic cases; accelerates clearance
25-40 mg/kg/day PO divided QID for 14 days; limited evidence for faster resolution

notes: Pityriasis rosea is self-limited (4-8 weeks); treatment is symptomatic. Avoid hot water and strenuous exercise which may exacerbate the rash.
counselling: Pityriasis rosea is harmless and heals on its own in 6-8 weeks
It is not contagious - child can attend school normally
Avoid hot baths and vigorous exercise which may make the rash worse
Use calamine lotion or mild steroid cream for itch relief
The rash follows a Christmas tree pattern on the back - this is characteristic and not worrying
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if atypical presentation (no herald patch, face involvement), persisting >12 weeks, or diagnostic uncertainty (rule out secondary syphilis in adolescents)
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not needed for pityriasis rosea

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (usually sufficient)

Apply BID to face/flexural psoriasis for 2-4 weeks; lower potency for sensitive areas

guideline_changes: Apremilast approved for pediatric psoriasis ages 6-17 in Aug 2024
counselling: Psoriasis is a chronic condition with flare-ups and remissions - it is manageable but not curable
Apply steroid cream or calcipotriene as directed; do not use strong steroids on face or groin
Keep skin well moisturised - this reduces scaling and itch
Stress and infections (especially strep throat) can trigger flares
Emotional support is important; psoriasis is not contagious - educate school and friends
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist for moderate-severe psoriasis (>10% BSA), psoriatic arthritis symptoms, failure of topical therapy, or need for systemic/biologic therapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if erythrodermic or pustular psoriasis, psoriasis with severe secondary infection, or metabolic derangement from extensive disease

counselling: Most congenital skin appendage anomalies are cosmetic and managed conservatively
Keep the area clean and watch for signs of infection (redness, warmth, pus)
For aplasia cutis: keep wound clean and covered; most small defects heal without surgery
Some skin anomalies may be associated with underlying conditions - follow-up blood tests may be needed
Surgical removal of accessory appendages can be done electively when child is older

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if large scalp aplasia cutis with sagittal sinus exposure, significant bleeding, secondary infection requiring IV antibiotics, or associated systemic anomalies

investigations.indication: If suspected syndromic association (e.g., Adams-Oliver syndrome)

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to depigmented areas BID for >=6 months; first-line for facial vitiligo; >=2 yr
Apply to affected area BID; first-line alternative TCI for vitiligo; >=2 yr

counselling: Vitiligo treatment takes 6-12 months to show results - be patient and consistent
Apply topical cream as directed; combine with sun exposure (10-15 min) for better results
Use sunscreen on depigmented areas to prevent sunburn and protect from further damage
Vitiligo is not contagious and does not cause physical harm
Emotional and psychological support for the child is very important; counselling may help

hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not needed for pigmentation disorders

Apply to pit/fissure or cavitated lesion as interim therapeutic restoration

Apply to high-risk surfaces q3mo as adjunct to fluoride
0.25mg/d age 6mo-3yr, 0.5mg/d 3-6yr, 1mg/d 6-16yr if water <0.3ppm F

counselling: Brush teeth twice daily with fluoride toothpaste; supervise brushing until age 8
Limit sugary drinks and snacks between meals; avoid bottle feeding at bedtime
Fluoride varnish applied by dentist every 3-6 months helps prevent new cavities
First dental visit should be by age 1 year or when first tooth appears
Silver diamine fluoride (SDF) can arrest cavities without drilling in young children

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if dental abscess with facial cellulitis, Ludwig angina (floor of mouth infection), or need for extensive dental rehabilitation under GA

Pulpotomy agent: mix and apply over pulp stumps, sets in 12min, restore same visit

counselling: If your child has a toothache, give paracetamol/ibuprofen and see the dentist urgently
Do not apply aspirin or home remedies directly on the gum - this can burn tissue
Tooth infection (abscess) with face swelling needs immediate dental treatment
Baby tooth infections must be treated to protect the permanent teeth growing underneath
Regular dental checkups every 6 months can catch problems before they become painful

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if facial cellulitis from dental abscess, spreading infection (Ludwig angina, orbital cellulitis), fever with trismus, or airway compromise

Bleeding gums during brushing is a sign of gingivitis - do not stop brushing, it will improve
Professional dental cleaning removes hardened plaque (tarite) that brushing cannot
Eat crunchy fruits and vegetables which naturally help clean teeth
Chlorhexidine mouthwash is for short-term use (2 weeks) only; it can stain teeth with prolonged use

Apply prescribed mouth paste directly on the ulcer after meals and at bedtime

If ulcers recur frequently, keep a food diary - some foods (citrus, tomato) may trigger them

first_line_drugs.notes: Fit bilateral behind-the-ear aids; trial 3-6mo; verify with real-ear measurements
For severe-profound bilateral SNHL (>70dB); FDA approved ≥9mo; implant ASAP ideally <12mo
Softband for conductive HL in infants; surgical implant (Baha/Ponto) at age ≥5yr

Surgical reconstruction of ossicular chain for congenital/acquired conductive HL
Classroom amplification system; transmitter to teacher, receiver to child's hearing aid
Begin by 6mo of age per EHDI guidelines; ongoing per developmental milestones

counselling: Early hearing aid fitting and speech therapy are critical - start by 6 months of age
Hearing aids should be worn during all waking hours for best speech development
Talk to your child face-to-face; reduce background noise during conversations
Regular audiological follow-up every 3-6 months is essential for adjustments
Cochlear implant can give hearing to profoundly deaf children if fitted early (before 12 months)

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for cochlear implant surgery, tympanoplasty, or ossiculoplasty as planned procedures

Enroll by 6mo per EHDI; speech therapy, family training, developmental support

notes: All newborns should be screened before 1 month; diagnosis by 3 months; intervention by 6 months (1-3-6 rule). Etiology workup includes genetics, CMV testing, imaging, and ophthalmology referral.
counselling: Do not delay evaluation - early diagnosis and intervention prevents speech delay
Follow the 1-3-6 rule: screen by 1 month, diagnose by 3 months, intervene by 6 months
Hearing aids and speech therapy should start as soon as hearing loss is confirmed
Talk, read, and sing to your child regularly; face-to-face communication is very important
Ensure family members learn to communicate effectively with the child

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for cochlear implant surgery or other surgical interventions (tympanoplasty, ossicular reconstruction)

investigations.indication: Screen for associated renal anomalies (branchio-oto-renal syndrome)

first_line_drugs.notes: Instill 5-10 drops in affected ear, wait 15-30min, then irrigate; age ≥3yr

Under direct visualization with otoscope; by trained provider; preferred in young children

Use ear drops as directed to soften wax before the removal appointment
If ear feels blocked after swimming, tilt head to drain water; do not insert objects
Come back if ear pain, discharge, or hearing difficulty persists after wax removal

3-4 drops in affected ear tid-qid x7-10d; only if TM intact (ototoxic risk)

No swimming until treatment is complete and ear is healed
Warm the ear drops in your hand before putting them in to reduce discomfort
Use ear drops for the full 7 days even if ear feels better

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if malignant otitis externa (diabetic/immunocompromised child), periauricular cellulitis spreading beyond canal, or suspected mastoiditis

investigations.indication: Assess canal edema, TM integrity, and debris

Reassess q3-6mo with pneumatic otoscopy until effusion resolves

Child inflates balloon via nostril bid-tid x4wk; age ≥3yr; modest benefit
Consider if age ≥4yr with persistent OME or getting 2nd set of tubes

notes: Steroids, antibiotics, antihistamines, and decongestants are NOT recommended for OME per AAO-HNS guidelines. Tympanostomy tubes indicated when OME causes significant hearing loss or speech delay.
counselling: Fluid behind the eardrum is very common after ear infections - it usually clears in 3 months
Antibiotics, decongestants, and antihistamines do NOT help clear this fluid
If your child seems to not hear well, speak face-to-face and reduce background noise
Hearing test is recommended if fluid persists for 3 months
Ear tubes may be needed if fluid persists and causes hearing loss or speech delay
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if OME persists >3 months, documented hearing loss >=20 dB, speech/language delay, or learning difficulties
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for tympanostomy tube insertion or adenoidectomy (day procedure under GA)

investigations.name: Pneumatic otoscopy
investigations.indication: Primary diagnostic tool; assess TM mobility

investigations.name: Tympanometry
investigations.indication: Type B tracing confirms middle ear effusion

investigations.name: Audiometry
investigations.indication: If OME >3 months or speech/language concerns

first_line_drugs.notes: 5 drops in affected ear bid x7-10d; safe with perforated TM (non-ototoxic)
3-4 drops in affected ear bid x7-10d; safe with perforated TM
Cotton ball with petroleum jelly for bathing; avoid swimming until healed

second_line_drugs.notes: Office-based paper/gelfoam patch for small acute perforations to aid healing
Formal surgical repair with fascia graft if perforation persists >3mo; under GA
10-15mg/kg PO q4-6h PRN pain, max 75mg/kg/day or 4g/day

notes: Most acute TM perforations heal spontaneously within 1-3 months; avoid aminoglycoside otic drops (ototoxic through perforation). Surgical repair indicated for persistent perforation with hearing loss or recurrent infection.
counselling: Keep the ear completely dry - use cotton with petroleum jelly during bathing, no swimming
Do not put any drops in the ear unless prescribed by doctor (some drops are harmful with perforation)
Most small perforations heal on their own within 1-3 months
Avoid blowing nose forcefully as air pressure can prevent healing
If hearing does not improve after healing, follow-up hearing test is needed
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if perforation not healed in 3 months, persistent hearing loss, recurrent ear infections through perforation, or cholesteatoma suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for tympanoplasty surgery (elective), or if acute perforation with vertigo, facial nerve palsy, or suspected inner ear injury

investigations.name: Otoscopy
investigations.indication: Document perforation size, location, and margin

investigations.name: Audiometry (pure tone)
investigations.indication: Assess hearing loss and conductive gap

investigations.name: CT temporal bone
investigations.indication: If cholesteatoma or ossicular erosion suspected

first_line_drugs.notes: 80-90mg/kg/day PO divided bid-tid x10d (<2yr) or 7d (2-5yr) or 5d (>5yr), max 3g/day
90mg/kg/day amox component PO divided bid x10d if failed amox or recent Abx use
5-10mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN pain/fever, max 40mg/kg/day
10-15mg/kg PO q4-6h PRN pain/fever, max 75mg/kg/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 50mg/kg IM x1-3 doses (1 dose for uncomplicated; 3 daily doses if failed oral)
14mg/kg/day PO once daily or divided bid x10d, max 600mg/day; PCN-allergic (non-severe)
10mg/kg PO day 1 then 5mg/kg/day x4d for severe PCN allergy, max 250mg/day
4 drops in ear bid x7d for AOM with otorrhea through tympanostomy tube

notes: High-dose amoxicillin is first-line; watchful waiting may be appropriate for mild AOM in children >2yr with unilateral symptoms. Pain management is essential and should be addressed regardless of antibiotic decision.
counselling: Give the antibiotic for the full prescribed course - stopping early causes resistant bacteria
Paracetamol or ibuprofen is very important for ear pain relief; do not wait for antibiotic to work
Follow up in 48-72 hours if no improvement; antibiotic may need changing
Clean any ear discharge gently with cotton; do not insert anything deep into ear canal
Completed pneumococcal and influenza vaccines reduce ear infection frequency
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if recurrent AOM (>=3 in 6 months or >=4 in 12 months), failed 2 courses of antibiotics, suspected mastoiditis, or persistent otorrhoea >2 weeks
