hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if acute mastoiditis, intracranial complication (meningitis, brain abscess, lateral sinus thrombosis), toxic appearance, or need for IV antibiotics

investigations.name: Pneumatic otoscopy
investigations.indication: Primary diagnostic assessment of middle ear

investigations.name: Tympanocentesis with culture
investigations.indication: If failed 2 antibiotic courses or immunocompromised

investigations.name: CT temporal bone
investigations.indication: If mastoiditis or intracranial complication suspected

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: If systemic toxicity or complicated AOM

first_line_drugs.notes: Comprehensive exam by low vision specialist; prescribe magnifiers, contrast aids, lighting
Maximize residual vision with best refraction; update q6-12mo in growing children
Certified O&M specialist; begin early; white cane training when ambulatory

second_line_drugs.notes: CCTV, tablet-based magnifiers, screen readers; prescribe per functional assessment
JAWS, NVDA, or VoiceOver for school-age children; training by TVI specialist
Adaptive daily living skills; sensory integration; handwriting alternatives
Enroll by 6mo; developmental support, family training, per state EI program

notes: Low vision rehabilitation should begin early as a parallel ongoing treatment, not a last resort. Multidisciplinary team approach including TVI, O&M specialist, OT, and family support is essential.
counselling: Start low vision rehabilitation early - it helps the child use remaining vision to the fullest
Ensure good lighting at home and school; reduce glare with tinted lenses if needed
Talk to the school about special seating (close to board) and learning aids
Teach the child orientation and mobility skills early for independence
Regular eye check-ups to detect any treatable causes and monitor progression
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric ophthalmologist for all cases of childhood visual impairment; refer to low vision centre for rehabilitation; refer to genetic counselling if hereditary cause
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed; admit if acute visual loss requiring urgent workup (retinal detachment, optic neuritis), or surgical intervention for treatable cause

investigations.name: Comprehensive eye examination
investigations.indication: Dilated fundus exam, refraction, visual field

investigations.name: MRI brain and orbits
investigations.indication: If optic nerve or neurological cause suspected

investigations.name: Genetic testing
investigations.indication: If hereditary cause suspected (Leber, retinitis pigmentosa)

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply 1cm ribbon to lower conjunctival sac qid x5-7d; all ages including neonates
1 drop in affected eye q3h while awake x7d; first-line for bacterial conjunctivitis ≥2mo
1-2 drops q2-4h PRN for viral/allergic conjunctivitis; supportive care

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 drop in affected eye tid x7d; age ≥1yr; broader coverage for severe cases
1 drop bid x2d then 1 drop daily x5d; age ≥1yr; good compliance profile
1 drop in each eye bid for allergic conjunctivitis; age ≥3yr
1 drop in each eye bid-tid for allergic conjunctivitis; age ≥3yr

notes: Bacterial conjunctivitis is most common in children; viral is self-limiting in 7-14d. Neonatal conjunctivitis (<30d) requires urgent evaluation to rule out gonococcal/chlamydial infection.
counselling: Wash hands frequently and do not touch or rub the eyes
Use separate towels and washcloths from other family members
Clean eye discharge with wet cotton, wiping from inner to outer corner
Do not share eye drops between family members; replace bottle after infection
Child can return to school after 24 hours on antibiotic drops or when discharge stops
referral_criteria: Refer to ophthalmologist if neonatal conjunctivitis (<30 days), conjunctivitis not improving after 1 week of treatment, suspected gonococcal (hyperacute, profuse), or associated corneal involvement
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if neonatal gonococcal conjunctivitis (IV ceftriaxone needed), orbital cellulitis, or corneal ulcer with conjunctivitis

investigations.name: Conjunctival swab culture
investigations.indication: If neonatal, severe, or not responding to empirical treatment

investigations.name: Gram stain
investigations.indication: If hyperacute purulent conjunctivitis (rule out gonococcal)

investigations.name: Chlamydia PCR
investigations.indication: Neonatal conjunctivitis at 5-14 days of life

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 firm downward strokes over lacrimal sac bid-tid; parents taught technique; age <12mo
Apply 1cm ribbon bid-tid for acute dacryocystitis/purulent discharge episodes
Apply warm moist compress to inner canthus for 5-10min tid-qid to relieve obstruction

second_line_drugs.notes: Office or OR procedure if CNLDO persists >12mo; 85-95% success rate; under brief GA
Probing with balloon dilation for failed initial probing; higher success in older children
For recurrent obstruction after probing; tube left in place 3-6mo then removed
Surgical bypass of NLD; reserved for refractory cases after failed less invasive procedures

notes: Congenital NLDO affects 6-20% of newborns; >90% resolve spontaneously by 12 months with massage. Probing should be deferred until after 12 months to allow spontaneous resolution.
counselling: Lacrimal massage technique is the most important treatment: press firmly downward over inner corner of eye 10 times, 2-3 times daily
Wipe away discharge with clean wet cotton; the blocked tear duct itself is not infected
More than 90% of blocked tear ducts open on their own by age 12 months
If there is no improvement by 12 months, a simple probing procedure can open the duct
Antibiotic eye drops are only needed during active discharge episodes, not continuously
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric ophthalmologist if CNLDO persists beyond 12 months, acute dacryocystitis (red swollen lump at inner corner), or suspected dacryocystocele in neonate
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if acute dacryocystitis with preseptal cellulitis, dacryocystocele with respiratory distress (bilateral, neonatal), or probing under GA

investigations.name: Clinical examination
investigations.indication: Diagnosis is clinical; fluorescein dye disappearance test

investigations.name: Dacryocystography
investigations.indication: If complex obstruction suspected before surgery

first_line_drugs.notes: Full cycloplegic refraction Rx; update q6-12mo; polycarbonate lenses for children
1 drop in each eye at bedtime daily for myopia control; age 4-12yr; monitor q6mo
For active children/sports; orthokeratology overnight lenses for myopia control option

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 drop each eye at bedtime daily for faster-progressing myopia; more side effects
Overnight rigid GP lenses reshape cornea; for myopia control; age ≥6yr; monitor closely
FDA-approved dual-focus soft lens for myopia control; age 8-12yr at initiation
For accommodative esotropia or high AC/A ratio; prescribed per binocular vision assessment

notes: Myopia control interventions (atropine, Ortho-K, MiSight) should be considered for progressive myopia in children. Ensure 120min outdoor time daily as modifiable protective factor against myopia progression.
guideline_changes: Low-dose atropine 0.01-0.05% now widely adopted for myopia control based on Phase 3 trial data
counselling: Wear glasses consistently as prescribed - the eyes need clear images for proper development
Encourage 2 hours of outdoor play daily - this slows myopia progression
Limit screen time; follow the 20-20-20 rule (every 20 min, look 20 feet away for 20 seconds)
Eye check-ups every 6 months are needed as power changes during growth
If prescribed atropine drops for myopia control, use at bedtime; minor pupil dilation and light sensitivity are normal
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric ophthalmologist if amblyopia detected, rapid myopia progression (>-0.75D/year), high myopia (>-6D), or associated with systemic conditions (Marfan, Down syndrome)
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not needed for refractive errors; consider if complication of contact lens use (keratitis)

investigations.name: Cycloplegic refraction
investigations.indication: Accurate measurement of refractive error in children

investigations.name: Axial length measurement
investigations.indication: Monitor myopia progression in children on myopia control

investigations.name: Fundus examination
investigations.indication: High myopes need retinal screening for degenerative changes

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 drop q1h around the clock x48h then taper to q2-4h based on response; age ≥1yr
Alternating q1h (each q2h) for severe/central ulcers; compounded drops
1 drop bid-tid for ciliary spasm/pain relief; adjunct to antibiotic therapy

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 drop q1h x48h then taper; alternative 4th-gen fluoroquinolone monotherapy; ≥1yr
1 drop q1-2h for fungal keratitis; first-line for filamentous fungi
1 drop q1h for severe fungal keratitis; compounded; for Candida/resistant fungi
Apply 5x/day x14-21d for HSV keratitis; add oral acyclovir 20mg/kg q5h if stromal

notes: Bacterial keratitis is an ocular emergency; obtain corneal cultures before starting empiric therapy if central/severe. Contact lens wearers need Pseudomonas coverage with fluoroquinolone monotherapy.
counselling: Keratitis is a serious eye infection that can cause vision loss if not treated promptly
Use eye drops exactly as prescribed - initially drops may be needed every hour
Do not wear contact lenses until completely healed and cleared by eye doctor
Attend all follow-up appointments; corneal healing needs close monitoring
If pain worsens or vision decreases further, return to eye doctor immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to ophthalmologist urgently for all suspected keratitis; refer to cornea specialist if central ulcer, fungal keratitis, or not responding to treatment
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe corneal ulcer threatening perforation, hypopyon, need for hourly fortified drops (compliance concern), or fungal/acanthamoeba keratitis

investigations.name: Corneal scraping for gram stain and culture
investigations.indication: Before starting antibiotics for all central/large ulcers

investigations.name: Fluorescein staining
investigations.indication: Assess corneal defect size and depth

investigations.name: Corneal confocal microscopy
investigations.indication: If Acanthamoeba keratitis suspected (contact lens wearer)

first_line_drugs.notes: Correct full cycloplegic refraction; accommodative esotropia may fully resolve with glasses
Patch sound eye 2h/day for moderate amblyopia, 6h/day for severe; with near activities
1 drop in sound eye daily or weekend-only for moderate amblyopia; age <10yr

second_line_drugs.notes: Recession/resection of extraocular muscles; under GA; for deviations unresponsive to optical Rx
Inject into extraocular muscle under EMG guidance; for small-angle deviations or pre-surgical
Translucent filter on spectacle lens over sound eye; for moderate amblyopia; better compliance
Fresnel or ground-in prism for small residual deviations; for diplopia relief

notes: Treat amblyopia first before surgical correction of strabismus. Visual plasticity is greatest before age 7-9; early detection and treatment yield best outcomes.
counselling: Wearing glasses as prescribed is essential - it may fully correct accommodative squint
Patching or atropine for amblyopia treatment requires patience; results take weeks to months
Encourage the child to do close-up activities (drawing, reading) during patching for better results
Surgery for squint has good results but may need to be repeated; it aligns the eyes, does not replace glasses
Early treatment gives best outcomes; visual plasticity decreases significantly after age 7-9 years
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric ophthalmologist/strabismus specialist at first detection of squint at any age; urgent if new onset in child >6 months or associated with neurological signs
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for strabismus surgery (day procedure under GA); urgent if acquired squint with suspected neurological cause (cranial nerve palsy, brain tumor)

investigations.name: Cycloplegic refraction
investigations.indication: Essential for all strabismus; accommodative component assessment

investigations.name: MRI brain
investigations.indication: If acquired non-accommodative esotropia, cranial nerve palsy, or neurological signs

first_line_drugs.notes: Full cycloplegic refraction, dilated fundus exam, visual field if cooperative; age-appropriate
Prescribe optimal refraction first; reassess 4-6wk; may resolve functional visual disturbance
For amblyopia: 2h/day moderate, 6h/day severe; combine with 1h near activities daily

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 drop in sound eye daily or weekend-only for amblyopia; equivalent to 2h patching
VR-based dichoptic therapy 1h/day x12wk for amblyopia; FDA-approved age 4-7yr
If unexplained visual loss, papilledema, optic atrophy, or neurologic signs present
Magnifiers, large-print materials, assistive tech if best-corrected VA remains impaired

notes: Visual disturbances in children require thorough evaluation to exclude retinal, optic nerve, or neurologic pathology. Functional/non-organic visual loss is a diagnosis of exclusion in the pediatric population.
guideline_changes: Digital therapeutic (Luminopia) FDA-approved 2021 as adjunct for amblyopia treatment in children 4-7yr
counselling: Any change in your child vision needs prompt evaluation - early treatment is essential
If patching is prescribed for lazy eye, consistent use gives the best chance of improvement
Ensure good lighting for reading and close work
Regular eye examinations are needed to track progress
Report any sudden vision loss, flashing lights, or new headaches urgently to the eye doctor
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric ophthalmologist for all children with visual disturbances; urgent referral if acute vision loss, papilledema, or neurological signs
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if acute vision loss requiring urgent workup (optic neuritis, papilledema with raised ICP, retinal detachment), or neuroimaging needed urgently

investigations.name: Comprehensive eye examination
investigations.indication: Visual acuity, refraction, fundoscopy, visual fields

investigations.name: MRI brain and orbits
investigations.indication: If optic neuritis, papilledema, or unexplained vision loss

investigations.name: Visual evoked potentials (VEP)
investigations.indication: Objective assessment of visual pathway function

first_line_drugs.notes: Under direct otoscopic visualization; first-line for visible non-spherical objects
Body-temp water via syringe; for non-organic, non-battery objects; intact TM required
Frazier suction tip applied to FB surface; for smooth/round objects in cooperative child

second_line_drugs.notes: Blunt probe tip with dried glue applied to FB surface; for round objects; cooperative patient
Operating microscope + microinstruments; for failed ED attempts, deep FB, or uncooperative child
4 drops bid x5-7d post-removal if canal abrasion or infection present

notes: Button batteries require emergent removal due to risk of liquefactive necrosis; do NOT irrigate batteries. Limit removal attempts to 1-2 tries in ED; failed attempts increase complication risk and warrant ENT referral.
counselling: Do not try to remove the object at home - this may push it deeper and damage the ear
If it is a button battery in the ear, go to hospital immediately - it is an emergency
Keep the child calm and do not insert water or any liquid into the ear
After removal, some ear discomfort for 1-2 days is normal
Teach children not to insert objects into ears, nose, or mouth
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if failed removal attempt in clinic, deep or posteriorly lodged FB, button battery in ear (emergent), or uncooperative child needing removal under GA
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if button battery in ear canal (emergent removal), suspected TM perforation from FB, or need for removal under GA

investigations.name: Otoscopy
investigations.indication: Visualize and identify the foreign body

investigations.name: X-ray temporal bone
investigations.indication: If metallic FB or button battery suspected

snomed_display: Foreign body in right external ear

diagnosis_name: Foreign body in eye and adnexa

first_line_drugs.notes: Irrigate affected eye with 500-1000mL NS; evert lids; sweep fornices with moist cotton swab
1-2 drops for anesthesia before examination/removal; single use; do not prescribe for home
Moisten tip, roll gently over superficial conjunctival/corneal FB under slit lamp

second_line_drugs.notes: For embedded corneal FB; under topical anesthesia; by trained provider; ophthalmology referral
Apply 1cm ribbon qid x5-7d post-removal to prevent infection of corneal defect
1 drop qid x5-7d post-removal for corneal abrasion prophylaxis; age ≥1yr
1 drop bid-tid for ciliary spasm/photophobia after corneal FB removal

notes: Check visual acuity before any manipulation; fluorescein exam mandatory to assess corneal defect and rule out penetration. Young children may require sedation or OR removal under GA; ophthalmology consultation for embedded or metallic FBs.
counselling: Do not rub the eye - this can push the object deeper and scratch the cornea
Flush the eye with clean water if a chemical or small particle is involved
Wear protective eyewear during sports and activities with flying debris
After removal, use prescribed antibiotic drops/ointment to prevent infection
If pain or vision problem continues after removal, return to doctor immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to ophthalmologist if embedded corneal FB, metallic FB with rust ring, suspected intraocular FB (penetrating injury), or corneal ulcer developing
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if suspected globe perforation/penetrating injury, intraocular FB, chemical burn, or corneal ulcer from FB

investigations.name: Slit lamp examination
investigations.indication: Detailed corneal assessment and FB localisation

investigations.name: Fluorescein staining
investigations.indication: Assess corneal abrasion and rule out perforation (Seidel test)

first_line_drugs.notes: Within 2h for esophageal button battery or sharp object; within 24h for esophageal coin/food bolus
10mL PO q10min (max 6 doses) while awaiting endoscopy for esophageal button battery
10mL PO q10min alternating with honey while awaiting endoscopy for esophageal battery
AP chest/abdomen; confirm location and track progression; q12-24h if in stomach

second_line_drugs.notes: For small blunt objects past esophagus; stool monitoring; repeat XR if not passed in 2wk
GoLYTELY 25mL/kg/h via NGT for multiple magnets or drug packets beyond endoscope reach
0.02-0.03mg/kg IV (max 0.5mg) for esophageal food bolus; smooth muscle relaxant

notes: Button batteries in esophagus are a surgical emergency (remove <2h); magnets require urgent removal if >1 ingested due to bowel perforation risk. Most blunt objects past the esophagus pass spontaneously within 4-6 days.
guideline_changes: Honey/sucralfate pre-endoscopy for button battery ingestion now recommended (NASPGHAN 2023)
counselling: If a button battery was swallowed and is in the food pipe (esophagus), it is a medical emergency
If a coin or small blunt object has passed into the stomach, most pass naturally in 4-6 days
Check the child stool for the object; report if not passed in 2 weeks
Do not try to make the child vomit - this can cause more damage
Childproof the home: keep small objects, batteries, and magnets away from young children
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric GI/surgeon immediately if esophageal FB, button battery ingestion, multiple magnet ingestion, or sharp object ingestion
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if esophageal FB of any type, button battery anywhere in GI tract, multiple magnets, sharp object, symptomatic FB (pain, drooling, vomiting), or large object unlikely to pass pylorus

investigations.name: AP and lateral chest/abdomen X-ray
investigations.indication: Confirm FB location, type, and number

investigations.name: Repeat X-ray at 12-24 hours
investigations.indication: If FB in stomach - confirm progression through GI tract

investigations.name: Metal detector screening
investigations.indication: For metallic objects as radiation-free monitoring

diagnosis_name: Chronic diseases of tonsils and adenoids

first_line_drugs.notes: For ≥7 episodes/1yr, ≥5/yr x2yr, or ≥3/yr x3yr of documented strep/tonsillitis; or OSA
50mg/kg/day PO divided bid-tid x10d for acute exacerbations of chronic tonsillitis
0.5mg/kg IV single dose intraop (max 10mg) for tonsillectomy; reduces PONV/pain

second_line_drugs.notes: 45mg/kg/day PO divided bid x10d for recurrent tonsillitis unresponsive to amoxicillin
20-30mg/kg/day PO divided tid x10d for chronic tonsillitis biofilm eradication; PCN allergy
5-10mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN x7-10d post-tonsillectomy; safe per AAO-HNS 2019 update
10-15mg/kg PO q4-6h PRN pain post-tonsillectomy; max 75mg/kg/day

notes: Watchful waiting recommended if infection frequency is below Paradise criteria thresholds. Post-tonsillectomy: codeine is CONTRAINDICATED in children <12yr; ibuprofen and acetaminophen are mainstays of pain management.
counselling: If surgery is recommended, it means your child meets criteria for frequent, severe infections
Post-surgery: soft foods and plenty of fluids for 10-14 days; avoid hard or crunchy foods
Pain after tonsillectomy peaks on day 3-5; give paracetamol and ibuprofen regularly, NOT codeine
Watch for post-operative bleeding (fresh blood from mouth/nose) - go to emergency immediately
The child may have bad breath and white patches in throat after surgery - this is normal healing
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if meeting Paradise criteria (>=7 episodes/1yr, >=5/yr x2yr, >=3/yr x3yr), sleep-disordered breathing/OSA, peritonsillar abscess, or suspected malignancy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for tonsillectomy (usually day surgery); emergency admission for peritonsillar abscess (drainage), post-tonsillectomy haemorrhage, or airway compromise from tonsillar hypertrophy

investigations.name: Polysomnography (sleep study)
investigations.indication: If sleep-disordered breathing/OSA suspected before surgery

snomed_display: [X]Anemia in other chronic diseases classified elsewhere

first_line_drugs.notes: Pinch lower soft nose firmly x10-15min; lean forward; do not release to check
2 sprays in affected nostril; age ≥3yr; may apply on gauze and pack; max 3-5 days use
Apply thin layer to anterior septum bid x2-4wk to moisturize and prevent recurrence

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply stick to visible bleeding vessel on anterior septum x5-10sec; unilateral only per session
Soak gauze in 100mg/mL solution and pack nostril x30min; or 500mg in 5mL nebulized
Merocel sponge or ribbon gauze with antibiotic ointment; remove in 24-48h
2 sprays per nostril bid-tid for humidification and crust prevention; prophylactic

notes: Anterior epistaxis from Little's area (Kiesselbach plexus) accounts for >90% of pediatric nosebleeds. Recurrent or posterior epistaxis warrants workup for coagulopathy, hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia, or nasal pathology.
counselling: Pinch the soft part of nose firmly for 10-15 minutes without releasing; lean forward
Do not tilt head back - blood may go into throat causing nausea
Apply petroleum jelly inside nostrils twice daily for 2-4 weeks to prevent recurrence
Keep fingernails short and discourage nose picking
Use saline nasal spray to keep nasal lining moist, especially in dry weather
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if recurrent epistaxis (>1/week), posterior epistaxis (bleeding from both nostrils or down throat), suspected coagulopathy, or nasal mass
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe epistaxis not controlled by 20 minutes of direct pressure, posterior epistaxis requiring packing, hemodynamic instability, or suspected coagulopathy with active bleeding

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: If recurrent or heavy epistaxis; assess for anaemia

investigations.name: Coagulation profile (PT/aPTT)
investigations.indication: If recurrent epistaxis or family history of bleeding disorder

investigations.name: Nasal endoscopy
investigations.indication: If recurrent posterior epistaxis or suspected nasal pathology

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 spray per nostril daily x6-12wk; age ≥2yr; 77% show improvement avoiding surgery
Isotonic saline 5-10mL per nostril bid via squeeze bottle or neti pot; all ages
Outpatient procedure under GA if medical therapy fails or severe obstruction/OSA/recurrent OME

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 spray per nostril daily x6-12wk; age ≥4yr; alternative intranasal steroid
4mg PO daily (2-5yr) or 5mg (6-14yr) as adjunct; FDA black box: neuropsychiatric risk
1.25mg PO daily (1-5yr), 2.5mg (6-11yr) if concurrent allergic rhinitis component
For mild symptoms without OSA; adenoid may involute with growth by age 7-10yr

notes: Trial of intranasal corticosteroids for 6-12 weeks is recommended before proceeding to adenoidectomy for uncomplicated hypertrophy. Surgery indicated for OSA, chronic OME with hearing loss, or chronic rhinosinusitis unresponsive to medical therapy.
guideline_changes: Intranasal mometasone now established as first-line medical trial before adenoidectomy (S2k 2023)
counselling: Nasal steroid spray is the first treatment to try - use for at least 6 weeks before deciding on surgery
Use correct spray technique: point nozzle towards outer wall of nose, not septum
Saline nasal wash helps clear mucus and reduces symptoms
Watch for mouth breathing, snoring, or disturbed sleep - these may indicate surgery is needed
Adenoids naturally shrink after age 7-10 years; some children may outgrow symptoms
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if nasal steroid spray fails after 6-12 weeks trial, sleep-disordered breathing, chronic sinusitis, recurrent OME with hearing loss, or significant nasal obstruction
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for adenoidectomy (day procedure under GA); urgent if severe OSA or acute upper airway obstruction

investigations.name: Lateral X-ray nasopharynx
investigations.indication: Assess adenoid size and airway obstruction

investigations.name: Sleep study (polysomnography)
investigations.indication: If OSA suspected before surgical decision

investigations.name: Tympanometry
investigations.indication: If recurrent OME associated with adenoid hypertrophy

first_line_drugs.notes: Indicated for OSA/SDB with tonsillar hypertrophy; PSG recommended if <2yr, obese, or comorbid
1 spray per nostril daily trial x6wk for mild SDB before surgical decision; age ≥2yr
0.5mg/kg IV single dose intraop (max 10mg); reduces PONV and improves oral intake

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-10mg/kg PO q6-8h PRN x7-10d post-tonsillectomy; AAO-HNS confirms safety
10-15mg/kg PO q4-6h PRN pain post-tonsillectomy; max 75mg/kg/day
4mg (2-5yr) or 5mg (6-14yr) PO daily for persistent OSA post-surgery; FDA black box warning
If snoring without OSA/apnea and no impact on growth, behavior, or school performance

notes: Adenotonsillectomy is first-line for pediatric OSA with tonsillar hypertrophy; PSG should be obtained before surgery in high-risk groups. Codeine is contraindicated post-tonsillectomy in children <12yr due to fatal respiratory depression risk.
counselling: If your child snores loudly and stops breathing during sleep, a sleep study may be needed
Tonsillectomy has excellent results for sleep apnoea caused by large tonsils
Post-surgery: soft foods for 10-14 days; regular pain medicines (paracetamol + ibuprofen); NO codeine
Watch for bleeding from mouth/nose after surgery - go to emergency immediately
Keep child well hydrated after surgery; ice lollies and cold drinks can soothe the throat
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if tonsillar hypertrophy causing OSA/sleep-disordered breathing, dysphagia, failure to thrive from poor eating, or asymmetric tonsillar enlargement (rule out malignancy)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for tonsillectomy (day surgery for most; overnight if <3 years, OSA, or comorbidities); emergency for acute airway compromise or post-tonsillectomy haemorrhage

investigations.name: Polysomnography (sleep study)
investigations.indication: Document OSA severity before surgery; recommended if <2 years, obese, or comorbid

investigations.name: Lateral neck X-ray
investigations.indication: If unable to visualize tonsils or adenoid assessment needed

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.01 mg/kg (0.1 mL/kg of 1:10,000) IV/IO q3-5min during cardiac arrest; max single dose 1 mg
100% FiO2 via bag-mask ventilation; titrate to SpO2 94-99% once ROSC achieved
20 mL/kg IV/IO bolus for volume resuscitation; repeat as needed up to 60 mL/kg

second_line_drugs.notes: Target 33-34°C for 72h in neonatal HIE; initiate within 6h of birth
2-5 mL/kg IV for hypoglycemia (glucose <60 mg/dL); recheck in 15 min
1 mEq/kg IV slow push if pH <7.0 and prolonged arrest; dilute 1:1 in neonates

notes: Prioritize airway management and ventilation; most pediatric arrests are respiratory in origin. Avoid hyperventilation; target normocapnia (ETCO2 35-40 mmHg) post-ROSC.
guideline_changes: 2025 AHA updated ventilation rates to 20-30 breaths/min for pediatric advanced airway
counselling: This is a medical emergency - follow the doctor team instructions carefully
After recovery, watch for any changes in behaviour, memory, or movement
Baby-proof the home: keep plastic bags, cords, and small objects away from children
Learn infant/child CPR - it can save a life in choking or drowning emergencies
Follow up with your pediatrician for developmental monitoring after the event
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist if post-hypoxic seizures, abnormal neurological exam, developmental regression; refer to PICU for ongoing care
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require emergency hospitalisation; NICU/PICU admission for ongoing resuscitation, therapeutic hypothermia (neonates), seizure management, and neurological monitoring

investigations.name: Blood gas (arterial)
investigations.indication: Assess pH, pCO2, lactate, base deficit

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Hypoglycaemia common in post-arrest; monitor frequently

investigations.name: EEG
investigations.indication: If seizures suspected or for prognostication in HIE

investigations.name: MRI brain
investigations.indication: Assess hypoxic-ischaemic injury severity; day 3-5 optimal timing

diagnosis_name: Burn and corrosion, unspecified body region

first_line_drugs.notes: 3 mL/kg/%TBSA over 24h; half in first 8h, rest over 16h; add D5 maintenance for <30 kg
0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN pain; max 0.2 mg/kg/dose; titrate to comfort
Apply topically to burn wound BID after cleansing; avoid on face/near eyes
TdaP 0.5 mL IM if not current; add TIG 250 units IM if incomplete primary series

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg IV for procedural sedation during debridement; 0.5 mg/kg for analgesia
Apply topically to deep burns BID; preferred for ear cartilage burns
0.5-1 g/kg IV if serum albumin <2 g/dL after 24h; adjunct to crystalloid

notes: Fluid resuscitation required for burns >10-15% TBSA in children. Monitor UOP target 0.5-1 mL/kg/hr; watch for fluid creep.
guideline_changes: Updated pediatric Parkland formula to 3 mL/kg/%TBSA with mandatory dextrose maintenance
counselling: Cool the burn immediately with running cool water for 20 minutes - NOT ice or butter
Do not break blisters; cover with clean cling film and go to hospital
Pain management is very important - give paracetamol/ibuprofen regularly
Keep burn dressings clean; follow wound care instructions from the burn team
Watch for signs of infection: increasing redness, swelling, pus, or fever - see doctor immediately
referral_criteria: Transfer to burn centre if >10% TBSA, full-thickness burn, face/hands/feet/perineum/genitalia/joint burns, electrical or chemical burns, circumferential burns, or inhalation injury suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if >10% TBSA (>5% in <2 years), full-thickness burns, face/airway/hands/feet/perineum, electrical or chemical burns, suspected inhalation injury, circumferential burns, or non-accidental injury suspected

investigations.name: Complete blood count
investigations.indication: Baseline and monitor for burn-related anaemia

investigations.name: Blood gas
investigations.indication: If inhalation injury suspected; monitor carboxyhaemoglobin

investigations.name: Wound swab culture
investigations.indication: If signs of burn wound infection

diagnosis_name: Burns classified according to extent of body surface involved

first_line_drugs.notes: 3 mL/kg/%TBSA for first 24h; half in first 8h from time of burn; D5LR maintenance for <30 kg
0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN; consider PCA for older children; titrate to pain score
Apply topically BID to partial/full thickness burns; reassess wound daily

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mcg/kg IV q1-2h PRN or 1-2 mcg/kg intranasal for procedural pain
Emergent surgical procedure for circumferential full-thickness burns with compartment syndrome
0.1 mg/kg PO BID for severe burns >20% TBSA to reduce catabolism; max 20 mg/day

notes: Transfer to burn center for >10% TBSA, full-thickness, face/hands/feet/perineum, or electrical/chemical burns. Use Lund-Browder chart for accurate TBSA in children.
guideline_changes: 2024 Utah PTN guideline emphasizes early enteral nutrition within 6h for >20% TBSA burns
counselling: Burns covering a large area are serious and need hospital treatment with IV fluids
The child will be in hospital for wound care, pain management, and monitoring
Nutrition is very important for burn healing - high-protein, high-calorie diet is needed
After healing, use sunscreen on burn scars and massage with moisturiser to reduce scarring
Compression garments and physiotherapy may be needed to prevent scar contracture
referral_criteria: Transfer to dedicated burn centre for all significant paediatric burns; refer to plastic surgery for scar management; refer to physiotherapy/OT for rehabilitation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all major burns (>10% TBSA, full-thickness, face/hands/feet/perineum); ICU admission if >20% TBSA, inhalation injury, hemodynamic instability, or need for intubation

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes, renal and liver function
investigations.indication: Guide ongoing fluid management and detect complications

investigations.name: Albumin and prealbumin
investigations.indication: Nutritional status monitoring in major burns

investigations.name: Wound swab culture
investigations.indication: Monitor for burn wound infection; targeted antibiotics

investigations.name: Urine output monitoring
investigations.indication: Key guide for fluid resuscitation adequacy

first_line_drugs.notes: Airway, breathing, circulation stabilization per ATLS; treat life-threatening injuries first
10 mg/kg PO q6-8h for pain; max 40 mg/kg/day; avoid if abdominal trauma suspected
20 mL/kg IV/IO bolus if signs of shock; repeat up to 60 mL/kg; assess after each bolus

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN for moderate-severe pain; monitor respiratory status
2-5 mg IV/SC if coagulopathy detected; evaluate for abusive head trauma

notes: Mandatory CPS reporting required. Perform skeletal survey in all children <2 years with suspected abuse; use TEN-4-FACESp screening for bruising.
guideline_changes: ACS Best Practices for Trauma Center Recognition of Child Abuse effective Jan 2024
counselling: Your child is safe now and will receive the best care possible
A social worker will help your family through this process
Follow-up appointments are very important for your child's recovery
Please report any new concerns about your child's safety immediately
Counselling services are available for the whole family at no extra cost
referral_criteria: All cases: mandatory referral to child protection services (CPS). Refer to pediatric surgery for fractures/internal injuries; ophthalmology for retinal hemorrhages; neurosurgery for intracranial injury
hospitalisation_criteria: Any suspected abuse requiring medical stabilization; all children <2 years with unexplained injuries; any child with altered mental status, fractures, or internal organ injury

investigations.name: Skeletal survey (full)
investigations.indication: All children <2 years with suspected abuse

investigations.name: CT head without contrast
investigations.indication: Suspected abusive head trauma, altered mental status

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: Baseline evaluation, assess for bleeding disorder

investigations.name: Coagulation profile (PT, aPTT, INR)
investigations.indication: Rule out coagulopathy before attributing bruising to abuse

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mL/kg IV bolus; then aggressive fluid resuscitation targeting UOP 1-2 mL/kg/hr to prevent rhabdomyolysis
12-lead ECG on arrival; continuous monitoring for 24h minimum for all high-voltage injuries
0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN for pain; titrate to comfort; assess for compartment syndrome

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mEq/kg IV bolus then 150 mEq/L infusion to alkalinize urine if myoglobinuria present
0.5-1 g/kg IV if myoglobinuria persists despite fluids; maintain UOP >2 mL/kg/hr
0.1 mg/kg IV for seizures secondary to electrical injury; max 5 mg/dose

notes: High-voltage injuries require C-spine immobilization and rhabdomyolysis monitoring with serial CK/myoglobin. Lightning-strike victims need neuro re-exams and ophthalmology referral.
counselling: Keep your child away from open electrical wiring and switchboards
Cover all electrical sockets with child-proof plugs at home
Follow-up ECG monitoring may be needed even after discharge
Watch for delayed symptoms like muscle pain, dark urine, or confusion and come back immediately
Electrical burns may look small outside but be deep inside — all follow-ups are important
referral_criteria: High-voltage injury (>1000V), lightning strike, burns crossing joints, compartment syndrome, cardiac arrhythmias persisting >24 hours, neurological deficits
hospitalisation_criteria: All high-voltage injuries; any ECG abnormality; visible burns; loss of consciousness; dark/cola-colored urine (myoglobinuria); cardiac arrest at scene

investigations.name: 12-lead ECG
investigations.indication: All electrical injuries — assess for arrhythmia

first_line_drugs.notes: 2-5 mL/kg IV over 10-20 min for raised ICP; may infuse 0.1-1 mL/kg/hr continuous
20-40 mg/kg IV load (max 1500 mg) for seizure prophylaxis in severe TBI (GCS <8)
1-2 mcg/kg IV for sedation/analgesia; continuous infusion 1-3 mcg/kg/hr in ventilated patients
For GCS <9: Rocuronium 1 mg/kg + Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV; avoid hypoxia and hypotension

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 g/kg IV over 15-20 min for acute herniation; hold if serum osm >320
5-15 mg/kg IV load then 1-3 mg/kg/hr infusion for refractory ICP; requires EEG monitoring
20 mg/kg IV load (max rate 1 mg/kg/min) if levetiracetam unavailable; monitor ECG

notes: Maintain CPP 40-50 mmHg; avoid hypotension, hypoxia, and hyperthermia. Place ICP monitor for GCS <8 after resuscitation; target PaCO2 35-39 mmHg.
source: Brain Trauma Foundation Peds TBI 3rd Ed, CHOP TBI Pathway 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 WVU guideline updated levetiracetam as preferred seizure prophylaxis over phenytoin
counselling: Head injuries in children need close monitoring — watch for excessive sleepiness, repeated vomiting, or seizures
Keep the child's head slightly elevated while resting
Do not give any sedating medicines without doctor's advice
Return immediately if child becomes confused, has a seizure, or clear fluid leaks from nose/ear
Follow-up with neurosurgery is mandatory — do not miss appointments
referral_criteria: GCS <13 or deteriorating GCS; focal neurological deficits; penetrating injury; skull fracture with depression; seizures; CSF leak; need for ICP monitoring or neurosurgical intervention
hospitalisation_criteria: GCS <15 persisting >2 hours; any abnormality on CT head; post-traumatic seizure; coagulopathy; vomiting >3 times; age <1 year with any mechanism of concern

investigations.name: CT head without contrast
investigations.indication: All moderate-severe TBI (GCS <13); apply PECARN rules for mild TBI

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid IV bolus; repeat up to 3 boluses then consider blood products
10-20 mL/kg IV if hemorrhagic shock unresponsive to 40-60 mL/kg crystalloid; use O-neg if uncrossmatched
15-20 mg/kg IV (max 1 g) over 10 min within 3h of injury; then 2 mg/kg/hr infusion for 8h
1-2 mcg/kg IV q30-60min PRN or intranasal for trauma pain; avoid sedation in unprotected airway

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg IV for RSI or procedural sedation; maintains hemodynamic stability
pRBC:FFP:Platelets in 1:1:1 ratio for life-threatening hemorrhage; activate early
0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV if vasopressor needed after adequate volume resuscitation

notes: Follow ATLS primary survey (ABCDE) adapted for pediatric anatomy. Hypotension is a late sign in children; tachycardia and poor perfusion are early indicators of shock.
source: ATLS 10th Ed, CHOP Trauma Resuscitation Pathway 2024
counselling: Your child has multiple injuries and needs careful monitoring in the hospital
Report immediately if the child becomes drowsy, has difficulty breathing, or has increasing pain
Follow all follow-up appointments with the trauma team
Rehabilitation and physiotherapy may be needed after discharge
Keep the child calm and comfortable — emotional support is important during recovery
referral_criteria: Trauma center referral for: hemodynamic instability despite resuscitation, suspected internal organ injury, traumatic brain injury, spinal injury, vascular injury, or need for surgical intervention
hospitalisation_criteria: All polytrauma patients requiring resuscitation; hemodynamic instability; altered mental status; suspected internal bleeding; any injury requiring surgical intervention

investigations.name: Trauma CT (head, chest, abdomen-pelvis)
investigations.indication: Identify internal injuries in significant mechanism

diagnosis_name: Open wound of unspecified body region

first_line_drugs.notes: Lidocaine 4%-Epinephrine 0.1%-Tetracaine 0.5% gel; apply to wound edges 20-30 min before repair
Local infiltration 1-2 mg/kg (max 4.5 mg/kg); buffer with NaHCO3 (1:10 ratio) to reduce pain
Copious NS irrigation under pressure (18G syringe); minimum 200 mL for contaminated wounds
TdaP 0.5 mL IM if not current; add TIG 250 units IM if dirty wound and incomplete series

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-octyl cyanoacrylate (Dermabond) for clean linear lacerations <5 cm; not for high-tension areas
25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided q6-8h for 5-7 days if high infection risk (bite, crush, contaminated)
25-45 mg/kg/day PO divided q12h for bite wounds; 5-7 day course

notes: Use absorbable sutures (chromic gut/poliglecaprone) in children to avoid traumatic removal. Assess tetanus status, neurovascular integrity, and tendon function before closure.
source: CHOP Laceration Pathway 2024, Texas Children's Wound Care 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 TCH guideline updated to prefer absorbable deep sutures (poliglecaprone 5-0/6-0)
counselling: Keep the wound clean and dry for 48 hours, then gently clean with soap and water daily
Watch for signs of infection: increasing redness, swelling, pus, or fever — come back immediately
Complete the full course of antibiotics if prescribed
If stitches were placed, come back on the date given for removal or checkup
Ensure your child's tetanus vaccination is up to date
referral_criteria: Wounds involving tendons, nerves, or vessels; wounds requiring specialist repair (facial lacerations near eyes/lips, hand injuries); bite wounds with deep tissue involvement; wounds with foreign body not removable in ED
hospitalisation_criteria: Wound with active hemorrhage not controlled by pressure; associated fracture or vascular compromise; extensive wound requiring operative repair under general anesthesia; wound infection with systemic signs (fever, cellulitis)

investigations.name: X-ray of affected area
investigations.indication: Rule out fracture or retained foreign body

investigations.name: Wound culture
investigations.indication: If signs of infection present, bite wounds

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: If significant blood loss or infection suspected

first_line_drugs.notes: 2-5 mL/kg IV bolus for hypoglycemia (glucose <60 mg/dL); recheck glucose in 15 min
50-100 mg/kg IV (max 4 g/day) if meningitis suspected; give within 1h of suspicion
20 mg/kg IV q8h for suspected HSV encephalitis; continue pending PCR results

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg IV q6h (max 60 mg/kg/day) added to ceftriaxone for suspected bacterial meningitis
0.1 mg/kg IV/IM/IN (max 2 mg) if opioid toxicity suspected; repeat q2-3min as needed
0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4 mg) if seizure activity suspected; repeat once after 5 min if needed
0.01 mg/kg IV (max 0.2 mg) over 15 sec if benzodiazepine ingestion; avoid if seizure hx

notes: Use AEIOU-TIPS mnemonic for differential. Check glucose on every pediatric AMS patient; obtain CT head, CBC, BMP, ammonia, toxicology screen, and blood culture early.
guideline_changes: 2024 Iberoamerican Academy published comprehensive pediatric AMS treatment guide
counselling: Your child's changed behavior needs urgent evaluation — please stay in the hospital until cleared
Tell doctors about any medicines, chemicals, or substances the child may have accidentally consumed
Watch for seizures, worsening drowsiness, or difficulty breathing and alert staff immediately
Do not feed the child until the doctor says it is safe
Follow-up with neurology may be needed after discharge
referral_criteria: Persistent altered sensorium >6 hours despite treatment; focal neurological signs; suspected intracranial pathology; metabolic cause requiring specialist management (inborn errors); suspected poisoning requiring toxicology consultation
hospitalisation_criteria: All children with altered mental status require admission until cause identified and resolved; GCS <15; any abnormality on CT/MRI; ongoing seizures; metabolic derangement; suspected CNS infection

investigations.name: Blood glucose (bedside)
investigations.indication: Immediate — hypoglycemia is a reversible cause

investigations.name: Serum ammonia
investigations.indication: Screen for hepatic encephalopathy, urea cycle defects

first_line_drugs.notes: For GCS <9: Rocuronium 1 mg/kg + Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV; use cuffed ETT; verify with ETCO2
5 mL/kg IV over 10-20 min if signs of herniation; monitor Na q4-6h, target Na <160
2-5 mL/kg IV if glucose <60 mg/dL; maintain normoglycemia 80-180 mg/dL

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 g/kg IV over 15-20 min for acute herniation; hold if serum osm >320 mOsm/L
20-40 mg/kg IV load (max 1500 mg) for seizure prophylaxis or treatment
20 mg/kg IV q8h if viral encephalitis suspected; pending LP and PCR
0.1 mg/kg IV/IM/IN (max 2 mg) if opioid toxicity suspected; may repeat q2-3min

notes: Secure airway immediately for GCS <9. Order CT head, full metabolic panel, ammonia, toxicology screen, and continuous EEG monitoring in PICU.
source: Brain Trauma Foundation Peds 3rd Ed, PALS/AHA 2025
guideline_changes: 2025 PALS updated to recommend cuffed ETT for all pediatric intubations
counselling: Your child is critically ill and needs intensive care unit monitoring
The medical team will keep you informed about every step of treatment
The child may need a breathing tube and ventilator support — this is to protect the airway
Please share any information about medicines, chemicals, or recent illnesses
Recovery may take time — the team will support you throughout
referral_criteria: All comatose children: immediate referral to PICU. Neurosurgery if structural lesion on CT. Toxicology consult if poisoning suspected. Metabolic/genetics if inborn error suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: All comatose children (GCS <=8) require PICU admission with continuous monitoring, airway protection, and ICP management as indicated

investigations.name: Complete metabolic panel
investigations.indication: Electrolytes, glucose, renal and liver function

first_line_drugs.notes: 2-5 mL/kg IV bolus over 10-20 min; then 0.1-1 mL/kg/hr continuous infusion; target Na 150-160
Elevate head of bed 30 degrees; keep head midline to optimize venous drainage
1-3 mcg/kg/hr IV continuous infusion in intubated patients; minimize ICP spikes from stimulation

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 g/kg IV over 15-20 min q4-6h; hold if serum osmolality >320 or signs of renal failure
5-15 mg/kg IV load then 1-3 mg/kg/hr for refractory ICP; requires continuous EEG and arterial line
Surgical option for refractory ICP not responsive to maximal medical therapy
Target ETCO2 30-35 mmHg as temporizing measure for acute herniation only; avoid routine use

notes: Maintain CPP >=40 mmHg (target 40-50 mmHg). Monitor serum Na closely with HTS; RCT showed HTS superior to mannitol with ICP reduction of -14.3 vs -5.4 mmHg.
source: Brain Trauma Foundation Peds 3rd Ed, Neurocritical Care Society 2020
guideline_changes: Hypertonic saline now preferred over mannitol; RCT shows superior ICP reduction and lower mortality
counselling: Your child has dangerously high pressure inside the brain and needs intensive care
The medical team will monitor the child continuously with special equipment
Some treatments may cause the child to be very sleepy — this is expected and necessary
Please stay available for updates and consent for procedures
Recovery depends on the underlying cause — the team will explain the plan at each stage
referral_criteria: All cases: neurosurgery consultation for ICP monitoring, possible EVD placement, or decompressive craniectomy. Transfer to higher center if PICU or neurosurgery not available
hospitalisation_criteria: All patients with raised ICP require PICU admission. Clinical signs: papilledema, Cushing triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular breathing), bulging fontanelle in infants, GCS deterioration

investigations.name: CT head without contrast
investigations.indication: Identify cause of raised ICP (mass, hydrocephalus, hemorrhage)

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mL/kg IV slow push over 3-5 min (max 30 mL); cardiac membrane stabilizer; onset 1-3 min
0.1 units/kg IV bolus (max 10 units) with Dextrose 10% 5 mL/kg; onset 15-30 min; monitor glucose
2.5-5 mg nebulized q20min x3 doses or 4 mcg/kg IV over 20 min; shifts K+ intracellularly
1-2 mEq/kg IV over 5-10 min if concurrent metabolic acidosis (pH <7.2); not first-line alone

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 g/kg PO or PR (max 15 g/dose in children); onset 1-2 hr; avoid in bowel obstruction
4-8.4 g PO daily in older children; newer potassium binder with better GI tolerability
1-2 mg/kg IV if adequate renal function; enhances renal K+ excretion; onset 15-30 min
For refractory hyperkalemia (K+ >7 with ECG changes) or renal failure; most definitive therapy

notes: Treat K+ >6.5 mEq/L or any level with ECG changes emergently. Give calcium first if ECG changes present; insulin-glucose is most reliable shift therapy.
guideline_changes: 2024 Janjua review updated pediatric hyperkalemia management algorithm
counselling: High potassium can cause dangerous heart rhythm problems — treatment must not be delayed
Your child may need IV medicines and continuous heart monitoring
If your child is on any medicines at home, please bring them — some medicines raise potassium
After discharge, a special diet low in potassium may be needed temporarily
Follow-up blood tests are important to make sure potassium stays normal
referral_criteria: Nephrology referral if renal failure is the cause. Cardiology if persistent arrhythmias. Dialysis team if refractory hyperkalemia (K+ >7 with ECG changes despite medical therapy)
hospitalisation_criteria: K+ >=6.5 mEq/L; any level with ECG changes (peaked T waves, widened QRS, arrhythmia); symptomatic hyperkalemia; renal failure; recurrent hyperkalemia requiring dialysis

investigations.name: 12-lead ECG
investigations.indication: Assess for hyperkalemic changes (peaked T waves, wide QRS)

investigations.name: Serum potassium (venous, non-hemolyzed)
investigations.indication: Confirm true hyperkalemia (rule out hemolyzed sample)

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mL/kg IV bolus over 10-20 min for severe symptomatic hyponatremia; raise Na 3-5 mEq/L acutely
Free water deficit replacement IV over 48-72h; correct Na no faster than 0.5 mEq/L/hr or 10-12 mEq/day
20 mL/kg IV bolus if hypovolemic; then switch to appropriate correction fluid

second_line_drugs.notes: Free water replacement for pure water deficit; infuse slowly to avoid cerebral edema
For euvolemic/hypervolemic hyponatremia (SIADH); restrict to 50-75% maintenance
1-2 mg/kg IV for hypervolemic hyponatremia; promotes free water excretion

notes: Maximum Na correction: 8 mEq/L in 24h for chronic hyponatremia to prevent osmotic demyelination. For hypernatremia, correct slowly over 48-72h to prevent cerebral edema.
guideline_changes: 2024 NEJM Evidence debate on safe correction limits; current consensus maintains 8 mEq/L/day max
counselling: Your child's sodium level is abnormal and needs careful correction in the hospital
Correction must be done slowly over 1-3 days to prevent brain swelling or damage
Blood tests will be done frequently — every few hours — to monitor progress
Once discharged, follow up to recheck sodium levels as advised
Make sure the child drinks appropriate fluids and avoid excess plain water
referral_criteria: Nephrology if renal cause suspected. Endocrinology if SIADH, diabetes insipidus, or adrenal insufficiency. Neurology if seizures from sodium derangement
hospitalisation_criteria: Symptomatic hypo/hypernatremia (seizures, altered mental status); Na <125 or >155 mEq/L; any level requiring IV correction; failure of outpatient oral rehydration

investigations.name: Serum sodium (serial q2-4h)
investigations.indication: Monitor correction rate — max 8 mEq/L per 24h for hyponatremia

investigations.name: Cortisol and TSH
investigations.indication: Rule out adrenal insufficiency and hypothyroidism in hyponatremia

first_line_drugs.notes: Identify and treat etiology: DKA, sepsis, renal failure, poisoning; acidosis often self-corrects
20 mL/kg IV bolus for dehydration/hypovolemia-related acidosis; reassess after each bolus
0.05-0.1 units/kg/hr IV continuous infusion; do NOT bolus in pediatric DKA; target glucose drop 50-100 mg/dL/hr

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mEq/kg IV slow infusion ONLY if pH <6.9-7.0; avoid in DKA unless pH <6.9; risk of hypokalemia
Alternative buffer if NaHCO3 contraindicated; 3-5 mL/kg IV; use with caution in neonates
For severe refractory acidosis or toxic alcohol ingestion; corrects acidosis and removes toxin

notes: Avoid routine bicarb in pediatric patients; evidence shows harm in most settings. Focus on treating the underlying cause; check anion gap, lactate, ketones, and toxicology.
guideline_changes: 2024 real-world study shows NaHCO3 benefit only when Cl >=113 mmol/L; harmful when Cl <107
counselling: Acidosis means your child's blood has become too acidic — we need to find and treat the cause
The child may need IV fluids and medicines to correct the acid level
Breathing faster is the body's way of compensating — it is expected initially
Please share if the child might have eaten or drunk anything unusual
Follow-up blood tests are important to make sure the problem is fully resolved
referral_criteria: Nephrology if renal tubular acidosis or renal failure. Endocrinology for DKA management. Toxicology if poisoning suspected. Metabolic genetics if inborn error of metabolism
hospitalisation_criteria: pH <7.2; bicarbonate <12 mEq/L; DKA (any severity); lactic acidosis with hemodynamic compromise; suspected toxic ingestion; altered mental status

investigations.name: Arterial blood gas
investigations.indication: Assess severity of acidosis, calculate anion gap

investigations.name: Basic metabolic panel
investigations.indication: Electrolytes, anion gap, renal function, glucose

investigations.name: Serum lactate
investigations.indication: Screen for lactic acidosis (sepsis, shock, tissue hypoperfusion)

investigations.name: Toxicology screen
investigations.indication: If ingestion suspected (salicylates, methanol, ethylene glycol)

first_line_drugs.notes: Vt 6-8 mL/kg IBW; plateau pressure <=28 cmH2O; driving pressure <=15 cmH2O; titrate PEEP per FiO2 table
16+ hr/day for severe PARDS (OI >16); initiate within first 3 days; monitor hemodynamics
0.05-0.1 mg/kg/hr IV continuous infusion; target RASS -1 to -2 for ventilator synchrony

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-20 ppm for refractory hypoxemia; assess response at 4h; bridge to ECMO if no improvement
1-2 mg/kg/day IV for >7 days in unresolving PARDS; extrapolated from adult ARDS data
Consider if failing conventional ventilation; mean airway pressure +5 above conventional
Cisatracurium 1-4 mcg/kg/min IV infusion for severe PARDS with ventilator dyssynchrony

notes: Use PALICC-2 criteria for diagnosis. Target Vt 6-8 mL/kg ideal body weight; consider ECMO referral for OI >40 refractory to optimal management.
guideline_changes: PALICC-2 (2023) updated PARDS diagnostic criteria and recommended lung-protective bundles
counselling: Your child has a severe lung condition and needs a breathing machine (ventilator) in the ICU
The medical team is doing everything possible — recovery from ARDS takes time
The child may be kept sedated to help the lungs heal — this is part of the treatment
You can visit during allowed hours — your presence and voice help the child
After recovery, follow-up with a lung specialist is needed to check breathing function
referral_criteria: PICU mandatory. Consider ECMO center referral if oxygenation index >40 despite optimal ventilation. Pulmonology consult for underlying etiology. Infectious disease if atypical pathogen suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: All PARDS patients require PICU admission with mechanical ventilation support. Criteria: OI >=4 (mild), >=8 (moderate), >=16 (severe) per PALICC-2

first_line_drugs.notes: Immediate BVM with 100% O2; rate 20-30 breaths/min with advanced airway; ensure chest rise
Cuffed ETT preferred; size (age/4)+3.5; RSI with Rocuronium 1 mg/kg + Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV
0.01 mg/kg IV/IO (1:10,000) q3-5min if progresses to pulseless arrest; max 1 mg/dose
0.02 mg/kg IV (min 0.1 mg, max 0.5 mg) for bradycardia-associated respiratory arrest

second_line_drugs.notes: LMA as rescue airway if intubation fails; size per weight-based chart
0.1 mg/kg IV/IM/IN (max 2 mg) if opioid-induced respiratory arrest; may repeat q2-3min
4 mL/kg ET for neonates with respiratory failure from surfactant deficiency

notes: Most pediatric cardiac arrests are secondary to respiratory failure. Verify ETT with waveform capnography; aim ETCO2 35-40 mmHg post-intubation.
guideline_changes: 2025 AHA updated pediatric ventilation rate to 20-30/min with advanced airway and recommends cuffed ETT
counselling: Your child stopped breathing and needed emergency treatment to restart breathing
The child will need to be monitored in the ICU on a breathing machine for some time
Please share any information about allergies, medicines, or events before this happened
The medical team will explain each step of treatment to you
After recovery, follow-up is essential to prevent recurrence
referral_criteria: PICU mandatory. Pulmonology for underlying respiratory disease. ENT if upper airway obstruction. Neurology if apnea of central origin. Consider ECMO center if refractory
hospitalisation_criteria: All respiratory arrests require PICU admission. Continued intubation and ventilation until underlying cause treated and respiratory drive adequate

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.01-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV continuous infusion; first-line inotrope; SUCRA 85% in 2024 meta-analysis
0.25-0.75 mcg/kg/min IV (load 50 mcg/kg over 15 min optional); reduces afterload + inotropy
1-2 mg/kg IV q6-12h for pulmonary edema/fluid overload; monitor electrolytes

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-20 mcg/kg/min IV continuous infusion; alternative inotrope if epinephrine unavailable
0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min IV if vasodilatory component present; SUCRA 80% in meta-analysis
0.01-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV for duct-dependent cardiac lesions in neonates
Consider for refractory cardiogenic shock unresponsive to maximal vasoactive support

notes: Avoid fluid boluses in cardiogenic shock unless clear evidence of hypovolemia. Milrinone preferred for post-surgical low cardiac output; dopamine no longer recommended first-line.
source: SSC Peds 2020, ACC Cardiogenic Shock 2025, PICU Network Meta-Analysis 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 network meta-analysis ranks epinephrine over dopamine; milrinone preferred for low CO states
counselling: Your child's heart is not pumping blood effectively — this is a serious condition needing ICU care
Medicines will be given through IV to help the heart pump better
The child will be on continuous heart monitoring — alarms may sound but the team is watching
Fluid intake will be strictly controlled — please do not give extra fluids without asking
Follow-up with a heart specialist (cardiologist) is essential after discharge
referral_criteria: Cardiology mandatory. ECMO center referral if refractory to maximal vasoactive support. Cardiac surgery if structural heart disease. Consider cardiac transplant evaluation for end-stage cardiomyopathy
hospitalisation_criteria: All cardiogenic shock patients require PICU admission. Signs: hypotension, tachycardia, poor perfusion, hepatomegaly, pulmonary edema, rising lactate

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 mL/kg IV/IO bolus over 5-20 min; repeat up to 40-60 mL/kg in first hour; reassess after each
10-20 mL/kg IV if hemorrhagic shock and no response to 40-60 mL/kg crystalloid; use O-neg if urgent
15-20 mg/kg IV (max 1 g) over 10 min within 3h of trauma; then 2 mg/kg/hr for 8h

second_line_drugs.notes: pRBC:FFP:Plt at 1:1:1 ratio for massive hemorrhage; activate when >40 mL/kg blood products predicted
0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV if persistent hypotension after adequate volume; bridge therapy only
0.5 mL/kg IV slow push during massive transfusion to correct citrate-induced hypocalcemia

notes: Tachycardia and poor perfusion are early signs; hypotension is a late and ominous finding in children. Delay intubation if possible until volume resuscitated to avoid hemodynamic collapse.
source: ATLS 10th Ed, SSC Peds 2020, Dutch Pediatric Society CPG
counselling: Your child has lost a significant amount of body fluid or blood and needs urgent replacement
IV fluids and possibly blood transfusion will be given to stabilize the child
The child will be monitored closely — heart rate, blood pressure, and urine output
Please tell us about any bleeding, vomiting, diarrhea, or injury before coming to hospital
Follow-up after discharge is important to ensure full recovery
referral_criteria: Surgical consultation if hemorrhagic cause. Gastroenterology if GI bleed. Hematology if coagulopathy. Transfer to trauma center if mechanism warrants
hospitalisation_criteria: All children with hypovolemic shock require admission. Signs: tachycardia, prolonged capillary refill >3 sec, altered mental status, hypotension (late sign), decreased urine output

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 mL/kg IV/IO bolus; repeat up to 40-60 mL/kg in first hour; assess for fluid overload
0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV if fluid-refractory shock; initiate within 1h if poor perfusion persists
50-100 mg/kg IV (max 4 g/day) as empiric antibiotic; give within 1h of sepsis recognition
15 mg/kg IV q6h if MRSA suspected; adjust for renal function; target trough 10-20 mcg/mL

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min IV as alternative first-line vasopressor; may be preferred in cold shock
2 mg/kg IV bolus (max 100 mg) then 1-2 mg/kg/day for catecholamine-resistant shock
20-40 mg/kg IV q8h (max 2 g/dose) for broad-spectrum coverage if resistant organisms suspected

notes: Use Phoenix Sepsis Score >=2 for diagnosis (replaces SIRS). Antibiotics within 1 hour; vasopressors after 40-60 mL/kg fluids; can give peripheral vasopressors if no central line.
guideline_changes: 2024 Phoenix Sepsis Score replaces SIRS-based criteria for pediatric sepsis diagnosis
counselling: Your child has a severe infection that has spread to the blood — this needs urgent ICU treatment
Strong antibiotics will be given through IV to fight the infection
The child may need medicines to maintain blood pressure and organ function
Recovery takes time — the team will keep you updated daily
After discharge, follow-up blood tests and check-ups are very important
referral_criteria: PICU mandatory. Infectious disease consult for resistant organisms or unusual infections. Surgery if source control needed (abscess drainage, debridement). Nephrology if acute kidney injury requiring dialysis
hospitalisation_criteria: All septic shock patients require PICU admission. Phoenix Sepsis Score >=2 with need for vasoactive support. Fluid-refractory shock. Any organ dysfunction from sepsis

investigations.name: Blood culture (2 sets)
investigations.indication: Identify causative organism — draw before antibiotics

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: WBC count, neutrophil count, platelet trend

diagnosis_name: Systemic inflammatory response syndrome of infectious origin with organ failure

first_line_drugs.notes: Ceftriaxone 50-100 mg/kg IV + Vancomycin 15 mg/kg IV within 1h; adjust per culture results
10-20 mL/kg IV bolus; up to 40-60 mL/kg in first hour; monitor for fluid overload signs
0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min IV for fluid-refractory shock; titrate to MAP appropriate for age

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min IV for cold shock with low cardiac output phenotype
2 mg/kg IV bolus (max 100 mg) then divided q6h for catecholamine-resistant shock
20-40 mg/kg IV q8h (max 2 g/dose) if ESBL or multidrug-resistant organisms suspected
1 g/kg IV over 12h; consider for toxic shock syndrome or streptococcal sepsis

notes: Phoenix Sepsis Score >=2 now defines pediatric sepsis with organ dysfunction. Prioritize early antibiotics, aggressive but monitored fluid resuscitation, and early vasoactive support.
source: SSC Peds 2020, Phoenix Sepsis Criteria 2024, SCCM PANDEM 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 Phoenix criteria replaced SIRS-based definitions; PANDEM guidelines published for PICU care
counselling: Your child has a serious whole-body infection response with organ involvement
Treatment in the ICU with strong antibiotics and supportive care is needed
Multiple IV medicines and close monitoring will be required
The medical team will update you regularly on your child's condition
Follow-up after discharge is essential to ensure complete recovery of all organ functions
referral_criteria: PICU mandatory. Infectious disease for resistant organisms. Subspecialty referral based on organ failure (nephrology for AKI, pulmonology for ARDS, cardiology for myocardial dysfunction)
hospitalisation_criteria: All patients with sepsis with organ failure (Phoenix Score >=2) require PICU admission. Any hemodynamic instability, respiratory failure, or end-organ damage

diagnosis_name: Poisoning by antiepileptic, sedative-hypnotic and antiparkinsonism drugs

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 g/kg PO (max 50 g) if within 1-2h of ingestion; multi-dose 0.5 g/kg q4h for carbamazepine/phenobarbital
Intubate for GCS <9 or loss of protective reflexes; RSI with Rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV
20 mL/kg bolus for hypotension; maintain euvolemia; monitor glucose and electrolytes

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.01 mg/kg IV (max 0.2 mg) over 15 sec for pure BZD overdose; repeat q60sec up to 0.05 mg/kg or 1 mg total
1-2 mEq/kg IV bolus for QRS widening from carbamazepine/TCA; target serum pH 7.45-7.55
For severe phenobarbital toxicity (level >100 mcg/mL) or refractory carbamazepine poisoning
1.5 mL/kg IV bolus then 0.25 mL/kg/min for lipophilic drug toxicity with cardiovascular collapse

notes: Avoid flumazenil in mixed overdose or chronic benzodiazepine use (seizure risk). Multi-dose activated charcoal is effective for phenobarbital, carbamazepine, and phenytoin.
counselling: Keep all medicines locked away and out of reach of children — even a small amount can be dangerous
Bring the medicine bottle or packaging to the hospital so doctors know exactly what was taken
The child will be monitored for several hours even if looking well — effects can be delayed
After discharge, child-proof your home and store all medicines safely
Call Poison Helpline (1800-11-6117) immediately if accidental ingestion happens again
referral_criteria: Toxicology consultation for severe poisoning. Nephrology if hemodialysis indicated (phenobarbital level >100 mcg/mL). PICU for intubated patients or hemodynamic instability
hospitalisation_criteria: GCS <12; respiratory depression requiring intervention; hemodynamic instability; QRS widening on ECG; seizures; need for multi-dose activated charcoal or hemodialysis

investigations.name: Serum drug levels (specific to ingested drug)
investigations.indication: Quantify exposure and guide antidote therapy

diagnosis_name: Poisoning by diuretics and other unspecified drugs, medicaments and biological substances

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mL/kg IV bolus for dehydration/hypotension; repeat as needed; correct electrolyte abnormalities
1 g/kg PO (max 50 g) if within 1-2h of ingestion and patient is alert with intact airway
KCl 0.5-1 mEq/kg IV over 1h for hypokalemia; MgSO4 25-50 mg/kg IV for hypomagnesemia

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5 mL/kg IV slow push if symptomatic hypocalcemia or hyperkalemia (from K-sparing diuretics)
Norepinephrine 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV for refractory hypotension despite fluid resuscitation

notes: Diuretic toxicity causes electrolyte derangements (hypoK, hypoNa, hypoMg) and dehydration. Monitor ECG for arrhythmias; effects may be delayed 2-4h until diuretic action begins.
source: AACT Position Statements, Poison Center Guidelines
counselling: Keep all medicines locked away from children in a high, secure place
Your child may lose a lot of water and salts — we will replace them through IV
Watch for dizziness, muscle cramps, or fast heartbeat even after discharge
Follow up for blood tests to make sure salts (electrolytes) return to normal
Call Poison Helpline (1800-11-6117) for any future accidental medicine intake
referral_criteria: Nephrology if acute kidney injury or persistent electrolyte derangement. Cardiology if arrhythmias from electrolyte imbalance. Toxicology for complex or massive ingestion
hospitalisation_criteria: Symptomatic electrolyte derangement; ECG abnormalities; hemodynamic instability; large ingested amount; persistent vomiting preventing oral replacement

investigations.name: Basic metabolic panel (serial q4-6h)
investigations.indication: Monitor K, Na, Mg, Ca, renal function

diagnosis_name: Poisoning by nonopioid analgesics, antipyretics and antirheumatics

first_line_drugs.notes: IV: 150 mg/kg over 1h, then 15 mg/kg/hr continuous; PO: 140 mg/kg load, then 70 mg/kg q4h x17 doses
1 g/kg PO (max 50 g) within 1-4h of acetaminophen ingestion; most effective within 1h
Maintenance fluids; correct dehydration; monitor hepatic and renal function q6-12h

second_line_drugs.notes: PEG 25 mL/kg/hr via NG for sustained-release preparations or massive NSAID ingestion
1-2 mEq/kg IV for salicylate poisoning; target urine pH 7.5-8.0 for enhanced elimination
For severe salicylate poisoning (level >100 mg/dL, renal failure, or refractory acidosis)

notes: Start NAC within 8h of acetaminophen ingestion for best efficacy; use Rumack-Matthew nomogram for risk assessment. For salicylate poisoning, alkalinize urine and monitor serial levels.
guideline_changes: New consensus guidelines released for acetaminophen poisoning management with updated NAC protocols
counselling: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) overdose can silently damage the liver — timely treatment is crucial
Keep all painkillers locked away and out of children's reach
The child needs blood tests every 6-12 hours to monitor liver function
Complete the full course of the antidote (NAC) even if the child seems well
After discharge, follow up in 1 week for repeat liver function tests
referral_criteria: Hepatology/liver transplant center if ALT >10,000 or INR >2 or signs of liver failure. Toxicology for complex mixed ingestion. Nephrology if salicylate poisoning with renal failure or severe acidosis requiring hemodialysis
hospitalisation_criteria: Acetaminophen level above treatment line on Rumack-Matthew nomogram; any elevation of ALT/AST; salicylate level >40 mg/dL; metabolic acidosis; altered mental status; intentional ingestion in adolescents

investigations.name: Serum acetaminophen level (4h post-ingestion)
investigations.indication: Plot on Rumack-Matthew nomogram for treatment decision

investigations.name: Basic metabolic panel with blood gas
investigations.indication: Acid-base status, renal function, anion gap

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg IV loading dose, then 10 mg/kg IV q12h until alcohol level <30 mg/dL; safe in pediatrics
2-5 mL/kg IV bolus for hypoglycemia (common in pediatric ethanol ingestion); monitor glucose q1h
20 mL/kg IV bolus for dehydration; maintain euvolemia; correct metabolic acidosis

second_line_drugs.notes: 7.6 mL/kg loading dose IV, then 0.8-1.3 mL/kg/hr infusion; if fomepizole unavailable
For methanol/ethylene glycol with severe acidosis, visual changes, renal failure, or level >50 mg/dL
1-2 mg/kg IV q4-6h (max 50 mg) for methanol poisoning; enhances formate metabolism
Thiamine 100 mg IV + Pyridoxine 50 mg IV for ethylene glycol; cofactors for detoxification

notes: Fomepizole blocks alcohol dehydrogenase, preventing toxic metabolite formation. Start before lab confirmation if high suspicion; early treatment may obviate need for hemodialysis.
guideline_changes: Fomepizole now preferred over ethanol infusion as antidote for toxic alcohols in pediatrics
counselling: Alcohol and similar liquids (sanitizer, antifreeze) are extremely dangerous for children
Your child's blood sugar can drop dangerously — this is being monitored
Store all household chemicals and alcoholic beverages securely away from children
If the child consumed sanitizer or antifreeze, the antidote must be given for the full prescribed duration
Follow up for blood tests after discharge — kidney and eye checks may be needed
referral_criteria: Nephrology if methanol/ethylene glycol poisoning requiring dialysis. Ophthalmology for methanol exposure (visual assessment). Toxicology for all toxic alcohol ingestions. Psychiatry if intentional in adolescents
hospitalisation_criteria: Any toxic alcohol ingestion (methanol, ethylene glycol); ethanol with hypoglycemia, altered mental status, or glucose <60 mg/dL; any patient requiring fomepizole or hemodialysis

investigations.name: Serum ethanol/methanol/ethylene glycol levels
investigations.indication: Identify toxic alcohol and guide treatment

investigations.name: Blood glucose (serial q1h)
investigations.indication: Hypoglycemia common in pediatric ethanol ingestion

first_line_drugs.notes: Nothing by mouth; assess airway for edema/stridor; intubate early if oropharyngeal burns or stridor
20 mL/kg IV bolus for shock; maintain euvolemia; correct acid-base and electrolyte abnormalities
Omeprazole 1 mg/kg IV q12-24h (max 40 mg) to reduce acid-mediated injury to damaged mucosa

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg IV q2-4h PRN for pain; avoid oral medications until endoscopy completed
Esophagogastroduodenoscopy for all symptomatic patients to grade injury; guides further management
50-100 mg/kg IV if evidence of perforation or mediastinitis; broad-spectrum coverage

notes: Do NOT induce vomiting, give activated charcoal, or attempt neutralization. Endoscopy within 12-24h for symptomatic children; asymptomatic patients may be observed 2-4h and discharged.
counselling: Your child has swallowed a burning/corrosive substance — do NOT try to make the child vomit
Do not give milk or water to dilute unless the doctor says so
The child will need a camera test (endoscopy) to check for damage inside
Keep all cleaning products, acids, and alkalis locked away from children
Long-term follow-up may be needed to check for narrowing of the food pipe
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery/GI for endoscopy within 24h. ENT if laryngeal involvement. Transfer to burns center if extensive oral/pharyngeal burns. Pediatric surgery if perforation suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: All symptomatic corrosive ingestions (drooling, dysphagia, vomiting, oral burns, stridor). Intentional ingestion in adolescents. Any patient requiring endoscopy or NPO status

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy (EGD)
investigations.indication: Grade mucosal injury — within 12-24h of ingestion

investigations.name: Chest X-ray (PA)
investigations.indication: Rule out mediastinal air (perforation) and aspiration

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray
investigations.indication: Free air under diaphragm if perforation suspected

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Baseline; watch for infection if severe burns

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.02-0.05 mg/kg IV q5-10min; double dose until secretions dry; may need very large cumulative doses
25-50 mg/kg IV over 30 min (max 1 g); then 10-20 mg/kg/hr infusion; give within 24-36h of exposure
Remove clothing, wash skin with soap and water; activated charcoal 1 g/kg PO if oral ingestion within 1h

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.3 mg/kg IV (max 10 mg) for seizures from organophosphate poisoning; repeat q5-10min PRN
0.01-0.02 mg/kg IV as alternative to atropine; does not cross BBB so won't treat CNS symptoms
4-8 mg/kg IV load then 0.5-1 mg/kg/hr; alternative oxime used in some regions

notes: Organophosphate poisoning causes cholinergic crisis (SLUDGE/DUMBELS). Titrate atropine to secretion drying, not pupil size; pralidoxime must be given early before aging occurs.
source: WHO Organophosphate Management, CDC ATSDR Guidelines
counselling: Pesticide poisoning is very serious — your child needs continuous monitoring in the hospital
Remove and discard all contaminated clothing; bathe the child thoroughly
Tell the doctor the exact name of the pesticide and how the child was exposed
Store all pesticides in original containers, locked away from children
Watch for muscle twitching, excessive secretions, or breathing difficulty even after treatment
referral_criteria: Toxicology/poison center consultation for all organophosphate poisonings. PICU if respiratory failure or seizures. Neurology for seizure management. Transfer to higher center if ventilator not available
hospitalisation_criteria: All symptomatic organophosphate/carbamate poisoning (SLUDGE symptoms). Any patient requiring atropine. Respiratory distress. Altered mental status. All intentional exposures

investigations.name: Serum cholinesterase (RBC and plasma)
investigations.indication: Confirm organophosphate poisoning — low levels diagnostic

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.indication: Cardiac arrhythmia from cholinergic excess or atropine

diagnosis_name: Toxic effect of venom of contact with venomous animals

first_line_drugs.notes: 4-6 vials IV initial dose (same as adult); 8-12 vials for severe; repeat 4-6 vials q6h x3 for control
20 mL/kg IV bolus for hypotension; aggressive fluid resuscitation for anaphylaxis or venom shock
0.01 mg/kg IM (1:1,000) max 0.3 mg for antivenom anaphylaxis; repeat q5-15min PRN

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 vials IV initial dose for pit viper envenomation; longer half-life, fewer recurrent symptoms
10-15 mL/kg IV for severe coagulopathy (fibrinogen <100, platelets <50K) unresponsive to antivenom
