1 mg/kg IV (max 50 mg) premedication or for mild antivenom reactions; give with H2 blocker
TdaP 0.5 mL IM if not current; recommended for all envenomation wounds

notes: Children receive larger venom load relative to body size and are at higher morbidity risk. Antivenom dose is based on venom amount (not patient weight); serial limb measurements q15-60 min.
source: ACS Snakebite Guidelines, Texas Children's Envenomation Protocol
counselling: Your child was bitten by a venomous animal — antivenom is the most important treatment
Keep the affected limb still and at heart level — do not apply tourniquet or ice
Do not try to suck out venom or apply any local remedies
Watch for swelling, bleeding, difficulty breathing, or allergic reactions after treatment
Follow up in 1-2 weeks as venom effects can recur (recurrent coagulopathy)
referral_criteria: Toxicology/envenomation specialist. Surgical consultation if compartment syndrome suspected. Hematology if severe coagulopathy. Transfer to antivenom-equipped center if antivenom unavailable
hospitalisation_criteria: All children with signs of envenomation (progressive swelling, coagulopathy, neurotoxicity). Antivenom administration. Observation for minimum 8-12h even for dry bites. Anaphylaxis to antivenom

investigations.name: Type and crossmatch
investigations.indication: Prepare for transfusion if severe coagulopathy

investigations.name: Serial limb circumference measurements
investigations.indication: Monitor progression of envenomation q15-30 min

first_line_drugs.notes: 8 mg/m2/day PO divided TID; stress dose: triple during illness; max 20 mg/m2/day
0.05-0.2 mg PO daily for mineralocorticoid replacement; monitor renin and electrolytes

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-3 mg/m2/day PO daily; not preferred in children due to growth suppression
50-100 mg/m2 IV bolus for adrenal crisis then 50-100 mg/m2/24h continuous infusion

notes: Avoid long-acting glucocorticoids in growing children; use lowest dose allowing adequate growth. All patients need emergency injection training and steroid sick-day rules.
guideline_changes: NICE 2024 guideline published; Khindivi oral HC solution FDA-approved 2025
counselling: Your child needs to take steroid medicine every day for life — never miss a dose
During fever, vomiting, or any illness, TRIPLE the steroid dose (sick-day rules)
Always carry an emergency steroid injection kit and a medical alert bracelet
Teach all caregivers (school, grandparents) the sick-day rules and how to give emergency injection
Follow up every 3-6 months with the endocrinologist for dose adjustment as the child grows
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology for all cases. Emergency referral if adrenal crisis (hypotension, hypoglycemia, hyperkalemia not responding to stress dose steroids)
hospitalisation_criteria: Adrenal crisis: shock, severe dehydration, altered mental status, refractory hypoglycemia, hyperkalemia with ECG changes. Any patient unable to tolerate oral steroids during illness

investigations.name: ACTH stimulation test (Synacthen test)
investigations.indication: Gold standard for diagnosis — cortisol <500 nmol/L post-stimulation is diagnostic

investigations.name: Serum ACTH
investigations.indication: Differentiate primary (high ACTH) vs secondary (low ACTH)

diagnosis_name: Congenital adrenogenital disorders associated with enzyme deficiency

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/m2/day PO divided TID; titrate to lowest dose controlling androgens and allowing growth
0.1-0.2 mg PO daily for salt-wasting forms; add NaCl 2-4 g/day in infants

second_line_drugs.notes: 25-100 mg PO BID based on weight (>=4y); adjunct to GC to reduce steroid dose
2-4 mg/m2/day PO; only in post-pubertal patients when HC is insufficient

notes: Hydrocortisone is first-line in growing children; avoid dexamethasone pre-puberty. Crinecerfont (2024) enables GC dose reduction by blocking excess ACTH-driven androgen production.
guideline_changes: Crinecerfont (Crenessity) FDA-approved Dec 2024 as first-in-class CRF1 antagonist adjunct for CAH age >=4y
counselling: Your child has CAH — lifelong steroid medicine is needed to replace missing hormones
Give hydrocortisone 3 times a day at the same times every day
During illness or fever, TRIPLE the dose — this is very important to prevent crisis
Salt supplements may be needed in infancy — continue as directed
Regular blood tests (17-OHP, electrolytes) are needed every 3-4 months to adjust doses
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology mandatory for all cases. Pediatric surgery/urology for genital surgery timing in affected females. Genetics for family counselling and prenatal diagnosis in future pregnancies
hospitalisation_criteria: Salt-wasting crisis (hyponatremia <125, hyperkalemia, dehydration, shock). Any infant with ambiguous genitalia pending workup. Adrenal crisis with vomiting preventing oral medication

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes (Na, K)
investigations.indication: Hyponatremia and hyperkalemia in salt-wasting form

investigations.name: Karyotype
investigations.indication: Determine genetic sex in ambiguous genitalia

first_line_drugs.notes: 2 mL/kg IV bolus (0.2 g/kg); then infusion 2-5 mg/kg/min to maintain glucose >70 mg/dL
5-15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for hyperinsulinism; max 15 mg/kg/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-20 mcg/kg/day SC divided TID-QID; second-line if diazoxide-unresponsive
0.02-0.03 mg/kg IV/IM/SC (max 1 mg); for emergency use or continuous IV 5-10 mcg/kg/hr

notes: Identify and treat underlying cause; diazoxide is first-line medical therapy for congenital hyperinsulinism. Monitor for diazoxide side effects including fluid retention and hypertrichosis.
guideline_changes: Low-dose diazoxide protocols (2-5 mg/kg/day) supported for transient hyperinsulinism (2024 data)
counselling: Low blood sugar can cause brain damage if not treated quickly — always keep glucose/sugar ready
Feed the child frequently — never let the child go without food for more than 3-4 hours
Learn to recognize signs of low sugar: shakiness, sweating, irritability, drowsiness
If the child becomes unconscious, rub sugar or honey inside the cheek and rush to hospital
Follow up regularly with the endocrinologist for medicine adjustment
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology for recurrent or persistent hypoglycemia. Metabolic genetics if inborn error suspected. Pediatric surgery if focal hyperinsulinism (for pancreatectomy evaluation)
hospitalisation_criteria: Glucose <40 mg/dL with symptoms; recurrent hypoglycemia requiring IV dextrose; new diagnosis of hyperinsulinism; seizures from hypoglycemia; need for critical sample during hypoglycemia

investigations.name: Critical sample during hypoglycemia (insulin, cortisol, GH, beta-hydroxybutyrate, free fatty acids, lactate)
investigations.indication: Essential for diagnosis — draw when glucose <50 mg/dL

investigations.name: Urine organic acids
investigations.indication: Screen for fatty acid oxidation defects, organic acidemias

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.1 U/kg/hr IV; start after 1h fluids and K+ >3.0; do NOT bolus
10-20 mL/kg IV bolus over 20-30 min; then replace deficit over 36h with added KCl 40 mmol/L

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.15 U/kg SC q1-2h; for mild DKA without IV access in resource-limited settings
0.5-1 mmol/kg IV over 6h; if phosphate <1 mg/dL; use with KCl to provide total K+ replacement

notes: Start insulin only after initial fluid resuscitation and confirmed K+ >3.0 mmol/L. Monitor for cerebral edema especially in children <5y; avoid rapid glucose drops >5 mmol/L/hr.
guideline_changes: ISPAD 2024 updated DKA chapter; low-dose insulin 0.05 U/kg/hr now preferred initial rate
counselling: Your child has diabetes with a complication called DKA — this is treatable but serious
Insulin and fluids will be given through IV to correct the problem slowly over 24-48 hours
After recovery, your child will need insulin injections every day for life
You will be trained on insulin injection, blood sugar monitoring, and diet management
Never stop insulin on your own — even during illness, insulin must continue (may need to adjust dose)
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology/diabetology mandatory. PICU if severe DKA (pH <7.1) or altered mental status. Neurosurgery immediately if signs of cerebral edema (sudden headache, vomiting, altered consciousness during treatment)
hospitalisation_criteria: All DKA episodes require hospitalisation. Severe DKA (pH <7.1, bicarbonate <5) requires PICU. New-onset T1DM for stabilization and diabetes education. Any DKA in children <5 years (higher cerebral edema risk)

investigations.name: Venous blood gas (q2-4h)
investigations.indication: Monitor pH and bicarbonate for resolution

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes (q2-4h)
investigations.indication: Monitor potassium — must be >3.0 before starting insulin

investigations.name: HbA1c
investigations.indication: Assess pre-existing glycemic control; distinguishes new vs established T1DM

diagnosis_name: Disorders of branched-chain amino-acid metabolism and fatty-acid metabolism

first_line_drugs.notes: Restrict leucine to age-appropriate tolerance; use MSUD-specific formula to maintain Leu <200 umol/L (<5y)
High-calorie IV glucose (8-10 mg/kg/min) during acute decompensation to promote anabolism

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-300 mg/day PO trial for thiamine-responsive MSUD variants; assess response over 3 weeks
50-100 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for fatty acid oxidation defects; max 3 g/day
1-2 g/kg/day PO for long-chain FAO defects; avoid fasting >4-6h in infants

notes: Acute metabolic crises require emergency IV glucose and protein restriction; hemodialysis if leucine >1500 umol/L. Liver transplant is curative for classic MSUD.
counselling: Your child has a metabolic disorder that requires a special diet for life
Follow the dietary restrictions strictly — even small deviations can cause crisis
During fever, vomiting, or illness, take the child to hospital immediately — do not wait
Never let the child fast for more than 4-6 hours (infants) or 8 hours (older children)
Regular blood tests to check amino acid levels are needed every 1-3 months
referral_criteria: Metabolic genetics/biochemical genetics specialist mandatory. Liver transplant center for classic MSUD if eligible. Dietitian specializing in metabolic disorders. Neurology for developmental monitoring
hospitalisation_criteria: Acute metabolic crisis (encephalopathy, vomiting, metabolic acidosis). Leucine >750 umol/L. Intercurrent illness with poor oral intake. New diagnosis requiring stabilization

investigations.name: Plasma amino acids (quantitative)
investigations.indication: Diagnosis and monitoring — leucine, isoleucine, valine levels

investigations.name: Urine organic acids
investigations.indication: Characteristic pattern for MSUD and FAO disorders

investigations.name: Acylcarnitine profile
investigations.indication: Specific pattern for fatty acid oxidation defects

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.58 mg/kg (100 U/kg) IV weekly over 3-4h; premedicate with antipyretic/antihistamine
0.5 mg/kg IV weekly over 1-3h; monitor for infusion reactions in ~20% patients

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg IV weekly over 4h; for Maroteaux-Lamy syndrome
Hematopoietic stem cell transplant; preferred for severe MPS I (Hurler) if <2.5y with DQ >70

notes: ERT does not cross the blood-brain barrier; HSCT is preferred for MPS I Hurler phenotype if done early. Early diagnosis via newborn screening improves outcomes substantially.
guideline_changes: Gene therapy trials advancing for MPS I/II; intrathecal ERT for CNS involvement under study
counselling: Your child has a storage disorder that causes substances to build up in the body over time
Enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) is given as a weekly IV infusion — do not miss sessions
The child may have a mild reaction during infusion (fever, rash) — premedication is given to prevent this
Regular eye, ear, and heart checkups are needed as the condition can affect multiple organs
Early treatment gives the best results — please keep all follow-up appointments
referral_criteria: Metabolic genetics mandatory. HSCT team referral for MPS I Hurler (age <2.5y). Orthopedics for skeletal complications. ENT for airway management. Cardiology for valve disease. Ophthalmology for corneal clouding
hospitalisation_criteria: Initiation of ERT (first dose monitoring). Acute airway compromise. Cardiac decompensation. HSCT admission. Spinal cord compression (odontoid hypoplasia). Hydrocephalus

investigations.name: Enzyme activity assay (specific to MPS type)
investigations.indication: Confirmatory — alpha-L-iduronidase for MPS I, iduronate-2-sulfatase for MPS II

first_line_drugs.notes: 100-500 mg/day PO trial; ~50% of CBS-deficient HCU patients are B6-responsive
Use met-free amino acid formula; target plasma homocysteine <50 umol/L

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-125 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for B6-nonresponsive HCU; max 6 g/day in children
Folate 1-5 mg/day PO + B12 1 mg/day PO as adjunct to lower homocysteine

notes: Test all patients for pyridoxine responsiveness before committing to lifelong dietary restriction. Early treatment prevents intellectual disability, thromboembolism, and lens dislocation.
guideline_changes: Novel enzyme replacement therapies (pegtibatinase, pegtarviliase) in clinical trials for CBS-deficient HCU
counselling: Your child needs a special diet low in methionine and may need lifelong vitamin supplements
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) responsiveness will be tested — if responsive, it greatly simplifies treatment
Blood tests for homocysteine level are needed every 3-6 months
Report any sudden vision changes, leg pain, or swelling immediately — these are emergencies
Ensure the child takes betaine and folate as prescribed — these help lower homocysteine
referral_criteria: Metabolic genetics mandatory. Ophthalmology for lens dislocation screening. Hematology if thromboembolic event. Cardiology for cardiovascular risk monitoring
hospitalisation_criteria: Thromboembolic event (stroke, DVT, PE). Lens dislocation requiring surgery. Acute metabolic decompensation. New diagnosis requiring stabilization and dietary initiation

investigations.name: Pyridoxine responsiveness test
investigations.indication: 50% of CBS-deficient patients respond — determines treatment approach

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 g/kg PO q4-6h (older children); maintains euglycemia in GSD I/III; avoid in <1y
Complex carbs q2-4h daytime; continuous NG glucose infusion overnight for infants

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for hyperuricemia in GSD I; max 800 mg/day
1-2 g/kg PO at bedtime; provides longer glucose release than raw cornstarch; age >5y

notes: Frequent feeding and cornstarch are the mainstay for hepatic GSD; avoid fasting >4h in infants. Monitor lactate, uric acid, lipids, and liver size regularly.
counselling: Your child must eat regularly and never skip meals — fasting can cause dangerous low blood sugar
Cornstarch mixed in cool water is a key medicine — give at prescribed times without fail
Night-time feeds are critical for infants — set alarms to avoid long gaps
Carry glucose tablets or sugar whenever travelling in case of emergency
Regular blood tests for sugar, lactate, uric acid, and liver size checks are needed every 3-6 months
referral_criteria: Metabolic genetics mandatory. Hepatology for liver adenoma surveillance in GSD I. Nephrology for renal complications. Dietitian specializing in metabolic disorders for cornstarch regimen
hospitalisation_criteria: Hypoglycemic episode requiring IV dextrose. Lactic acidosis. Intercurrent illness with poor oral intake. New diagnosis requiring initiation of feeding regimen

investigations.name: Serum lactate
investigations.indication: Elevated in GSD I during fasting

first_line_drugs.notes: Limit phenylalanine intake using Phe-free amino acid formula; target blood Phe 120-360 umol/L
10-20 mg/kg/day PO once daily; for BH4-responsive patients (~25-50%); trial response required

second_line_drugs.notes: SC injection; approved for adults/adolescents >=16y; titrate from 2.5 mg to 20-40 mg daily; anaphylaxis risk
Supplement PO to compete with Phe transport across BBB; adjunct in patients with poor dietary compliance

notes: Newborn screening enables early dietary treatment preventing intellectual disability. All patients should undergo BH4 responsiveness testing; diet remains the cornerstone of therapy.
guideline_changes: ACMG 2024 evidence-based guideline published Aug 2024; sapropterin conditionally recommended in pregnancy
counselling: Your child needs a special low-protein diet for life — this prevents brain damage
Use the special PKU formula as the main source of nutrition — it provides essential nutrients without phenylalanine
Blood phenylalanine levels must be checked weekly (infants) to monthly (older children)
Avoid high-protein foods: meat, fish, eggs, dairy, nuts, soy — your dietitian will give you a detailed list
If the child is unwell or not eating, contact the metabolic team immediately
referral_criteria: Metabolic genetics/biochemical genetics mandatory. Developmental pediatrics for cognitive monitoring. Dietitian specializing in PKU. Maternal-fetal medicine for females with PKU planning pregnancy
hospitalisation_criteria: Phenylalanine level >900 umol/L with neurological symptoms. New diagnosis in neonate for initiation of diet. Metabolic crisis during intercurrent illness. Non-compliance with dangerously high levels

investigations.name: BH4 loading test
investigations.indication: Determine BH4 responsiveness — 25-50% of patients respond

investigations.name: Plasma amino acids (full profile)
investigations.indication: Ensure adequate tyrosine and other amino acid levels

investigations.name: MRI brain
investigations.indication: If developmental regression or poor metabolic control

diagnosis_name: Sphingolipidosis and other lipid storage disorders

first_line_drugs.notes: 30-60 U/kg IV every 2 weeks; for Gaucher type 1; adjust based on clinical response
Gaucher: 100 mg PO TID (adults); NPC: substrate reduction therapy per weight-based dosing

second_line_drugs.notes: PO per weight-based dosing for Niemann-Pick type C; first FDA-approved NPC therapy (2024)
PO weight-based dosing for NPC; second FDA-approved NPC therapy (Sep 2024)
60 U/kg IV every 2 weeks for Gaucher type 1 (age >=4y); alternative to imiglucerase

notes: ERT is effective for Gaucher type 1 but not for neuronopathic forms. Two new NPC therapies approved in 2024 represent major advances for this previously untreatable condition.
guideline_changes: Arimoclomol (Miplyffa) and levacetylleucine (Aqneursa) both FDA-approved Sep 2024 for Niemann-Pick C
counselling: Your child has a rare storage disorder — treatment depends on the specific type
Enzyme infusions (if applicable) must be given regularly — usually every 2 weeks
Watch for bone pain, easy bruising, enlarged belly, or difficulty walking and report immediately
Some types affect the brain — developmental monitoring is important
Genetic counselling for the family is recommended to understand the inheritance pattern
referral_criteria: Metabolic genetics mandatory. Hematology for Gaucher disease. Neurology for neuronopathic forms. Hepatology for liver involvement. HSCT team for eligible patients. Psychiatry for NPC (behavioral changes)
hospitalisation_criteria: First dose of ERT (monitoring for infusion reactions). Acute neurological deterioration. Splenic crisis (Gaucher). Severe anemia or thrombocytopenia requiring transfusion

investigations.name: Enzyme activity assay (specific to disorder)
investigations.indication: Confirmatory diagnosis — glucocerebrosidase for Gaucher, etc.

investigations.name: Genetic testing
investigations.indication: Confirm mutation, family screening, prenatal diagnosis

first_line_drugs.notes: Intensive health behavior and lifestyle treatment (26+ contact hours); diet, physical activity, behavioral therapy
SC weekly; titrate 0.25mg to 2.4mg over 16wk; age >=12y with BMI >=95th percentile
SC daily; titrate 0.6mg to 3.0mg over 4wk; age >=12y (6-12y per recent trial data)

second_line_drugs.notes: 120 mg PO TID with meals; age >=12y; add multivitamin due to fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption
Per weight-based dosing PO daily; age >=12y; monitor HR and mood

notes: Semaglutide achieves up to 17% BMI reduction, the most potent of approved agents. Pharmacotherapy is adjunct to, not replacement for, intensive lifestyle modification.
guideline_changes: Semaglutide FDA-approved Dec 2022 for age >=12y; liraglutide expanded to ages 6-12y (2024 trial data)
counselling: Your child's weight needs to be managed with diet changes, more physical activity, and less screen time
Limit sugar-sweetened drinks (cola, juice, packaged drinks) — switch to water and buttermilk
Ensure at least 60 minutes of active play/exercise every day — whole family should participate
Reduce screen time to less than 2 hours per day for children above 5 years
Regular follow-up every 3 months to track weight, blood sugar, and cholesterol is important
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology if secondary cause suspected or pharmacotherapy considered (age >=12). Dietitian for structured meal planning. Psychology for binge eating or emotional eating. Bariatric surgery team if BMI >=40 or >=35 with comorbidities (age >=13)
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider admission for: severe obstructive sleep apnea, hypertensive emergency from obesity, bariatric surgery, or psychiatric crisis (eating disorder with medical instability)

investigations.name: Fasting lipid profile
investigations.indication: Dyslipidemia screening — all obese children >=2 years

investigations.name: Liver function tests (ALT)
investigations.indication: NAFLD/MASLD screening — screen all obese children

investigations.name: TSH
investigations.indication: Rule out hypothyroidism as secondary cause

diagnosis_name: Protein-energy malnutrition of moderate and mild degree

first_line_drugs.notes: Energy-dense supplementary food 250-500 kcal/day added to usual diet; attend checkup q2wk for 4-6 wk
Multivitamin/mineral supplement per WHO; include iron, zinc, folate, vitamin A per local protocol

second_line_drugs.notes: 92 g/day sachet (~500 kcal) for moderate acute malnutrition in 6-59 month olds
1-1.5 kcal/mL enteral formula for older children; increase by 10-20% q2-3d as tolerated

notes: Counsel on age-appropriate feeding practices and complete immunizations. Monitor weight-for-height z-score every 2 weeks; refer if no improvement after 4-6 weeks.
guideline_changes: WHO 2023 guideline expanded to include moderate wasting management in 6-59 month age group
counselling: Your child is underweight — more nutritious food is needed every day
Give energy-rich foods like dal, ghee, peanuts, eggs, banana, and milk at every meal
Feed the child 5-6 times a day — 3 meals and 2-3 snacks
Continue breastfeeding if the child is under 2 years — it is the best nutrition
Come for weight check-up every 2 weeks until the child's weight is back on track
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology if no improvement after 4-6 weeks of supplementary feeding. Dietitian for detailed meal planning. Social services if food insecurity identified. Investigate for underlying disease if weight not improving
hospitalisation_criteria: Failure to respond to outpatient supplementary feeding after 4-6 weeks. Development of complications (severe dehydration, electrolyte imbalance). Coexisting severe illness requiring treatment. Weight-for-height z-score dropping to <-3 SD (now SAM)

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: Screen for UTI as underlying cause

first_line_drugs.notes: 75 kcal/100 mL; stabilization phase days 1-7; feed q2-3h; low protein/Na to prevent refeeding syndrome
150-200 kcal/kg/day in rehab phase; 10-15 kg total over 6-8 wk; for age >=6mo without complications
40 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for 5-7 days; routine antibiotic per WHO SAM protocol

second_line_drugs.notes: 100 kcal/100 mL; transition/rehabilitation phase; increase feeds by 10 mL/feed until desired intake
5 mL/kg IV bolus for hypoglycemia (glucose <54 mg/dL); then feed immediately

notes: Start refeeding cautiously with F-75 to avoid refeeding syndrome; monitor electrolytes (K, Mg, PO4) BID for 4 days. Treat hypothermia, hypoglycemia, and dehydration before addressing nutrition.
guideline_changes: WHO 2023 guideline consolidated wasting and oedema management; community-based RUTF approach strengthened
counselling: Your child is severely malnourished and needs careful treatment in the hospital first, then at home
Give the therapeutic food (RUTF) as prescribed — it has all the nutrients your child needs
Feed small amounts frequently — do not force-feed large quantities at once
Continue breastfeeding along with therapeutic food if the child is under 2 years
Come for weekly follow-up until the child's weight reaches the target — missing visits is dangerous
referral_criteria: Nutrition rehabilitation center if available. Pediatric gastroenterology if underlying GI disease. Social services for food insecurity. Infectious disease if TB or HIV suspected. Metabolic evaluation if malnutrition unexplained by dietary history
hospitalisation_criteria: All complicated SAM (with medical complications: dehydration, sepsis, hypothermia, hypoglycemia, severe edema, poor appetite). WHZ <-3 SD or MUAC <115mm. Bilateral pitting edema. Failed appetite test (RUTF refusal)

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Hypoglycemia screening — common and life-threatening in SAM

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes (Na, K, Mg, PO4)
investigations.indication: Refeeding syndrome prevention — check BID for 4 days

investigations.name: Blood culture
investigations.indication: Occult sepsis is common in SAM children

investigations.name: HIV test
investigations.indication: All SAM children should be tested per WHO

snomed_display: Severe protein-calorie malnutrition (Gomez: less than 60 percent of standard weight)

first_line_drugs.notes: Start at 50-80% estimated caloric needs for current weight; advance 10-20%/day if electrolytes stable
100-300 mg IV/PO daily before and during refeeding to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
Check K, Mg, PO4 BID for >=4 days; replace aggressively; do not advance feeds if abnormal

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mmol/kg/day IV or PO if PO4 low; critical to prevent refeeding syndrome cardiac complications
Polymeric 1 kcal/mL formula via NG if unable to eat; advance slowly per refeeding protocol

notes: High refeeding syndrome risk in any child malnourished >10 days; start calories low and advance slowly. Correct electrolytes before advancing nutritional support.
counselling: Your child needs gradual refeeding — starting with small amounts and increasing slowly is safer
Do not try to feed large amounts quickly — it can cause a dangerous condition called refeeding syndrome
The child will need frequent blood tests during the first few days to monitor salt levels
Once stable, follow the nutritional plan given by the doctor and dietitian
Follow up weekly for weight monitoring until nutritional goals are met
referral_criteria: Dietitian for refeeding protocol. Pediatric gastroenterology for underlying malabsorption. Psychiatry if eating disorder suspected. Social services for food insecurity or neglect
hospitalisation_criteria: Any malnourished patient at refeeding risk (>10 days poor intake). Electrolyte instability (low phosphate, potassium, magnesium). Cardiac symptoms during refeeding. BMI <14 kg/m2 in adolescents

investigations.name: Serum phosphate, potassium, magnesium
investigations.indication: Refeeding syndrome monitoring — check BID for 4 days

investigations.name: ECG
investigations.indication: Cardiac monitoring during refeeding (QTc prolongation risk)

first_line_drugs.notes: Age 6-11y <30kg: ELX50/TEZ25/IVA37.5 AM + IVA75 PM; >=30kg or >=12y: ELX100/TEZ50/IVA75 AM + IVA150 PM; with fat
2.5 mg nebulized daily; for age >=6y to improve mucus clearance and lung function

second_line_drugs.notes: For gating mutations; age 1mo-5y: 25-75 mg PO BID by weight; >=6y: 150 mg PO q12h with fat
500-2500 lipase U/kg/meal PO; max 10,000 U/kg/day; for pancreatic insufficiency
4 mL nebulized BID; for age >=6y; improves mucociliary clearance

notes: Trikafta has transformed CF care with significant lung function improvement and reduced mortality. Start CFTR modulators at earliest eligible age to attenuate disease progression.
guideline_changes: Trikafta approved down to age 2y (2023); Canadian consensus recommends starting CFTR modulators at youngest age possible
counselling: Your child has cystic fibrosis — daily chest physiotherapy and medicines are essential for life
Give the CFTR modulator (Trikafta) with fatty food every day at the same time
Do airway clearance (chest physiotherapy) twice a day — even when the child seems well
Pancreatic enzyme capsules must be taken with every meal and snack — before eating
Call the CF team immediately if the child has fever, increased cough, or colored sputum
referral_criteria: Pediatric pulmonology (CF center) mandatory for all cases. Gastroenterology for pancreatic insufficiency. Endocrinology for CF-related diabetes. ENT for nasal polyps. Lung transplant evaluation if FEV1 <30% predicted
hospitalisation_criteria: Pulmonary exacerbation requiring IV antibiotics. Newly diagnosed CF for initiation of care. Hemoptysis. Pneumothorax. Meconium ileus equivalent. Distal intestinal obstruction syndrome

investigations.name: Sweat chloride test
investigations.indication: Gold standard for CF diagnosis — Cl >60 mmol/L diagnostic

investigations.name: CFTR genetic testing
investigations.indication: Identify mutation — guides CFTR modulator eligibility

investigations.name: Pulmonary function tests (spirometry)
investigations.indication: FEV1 monitoring — baseline and q3-6 months (age >=6)

investigations.name: Sputum culture
investigations.indication: Identify respiratory pathogens — Pseudomonas, Staph aureus

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for mitochondrial disorders; higher doses not necessarily more effective
50-100 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for carnitine deficiency or secondary deficiency; max 3 g/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-400 mg/day PO for Complex I/II defects and MADD; assess response over 3 months
5-10 mg/kg/day PO as mitochondrial antioxidant adjunct

notes: Metabolic disorders require individualized cocktail therapy targeting specific enzyme defects. Evidence for mitochondrial supplements is limited but CoQ10 and carnitine are widely used.
guideline_changes: FDA granted Rare Pediatric Disease designation for BPM31510IV for primary CoQ10 deficiency (2024)
counselling: Your child has a metabolic disorder that affects energy production in the body's cells
Give the vitamin and supplement cocktail prescribed (CoQ10, carnitine, riboflavin) daily without fail
Avoid prolonged fasting and strenuous exercise — both can trigger a crisis
During illness or fever, bring the child to hospital early — do not wait for things to worsen
Regular follow-up every 3-6 months with the metabolic team is essential
referral_criteria: Metabolic genetics specialist mandatory. Neurology for seizures or regression. Cardiology for cardiomyopathy. Ophthalmology for retinopathy. Audiology for sensorineural hearing loss
hospitalisation_criteria: Acute metabolic crisis (lactic acidosis, seizures, stroke-like episodes). Cardiac decompensation. Intercurrent illness with poor oral intake. New diagnosis requiring evaluation

investigations.name: Serum lactate and pyruvate
investigations.indication: Elevated lactate:pyruvate ratio suggests mitochondrial disease

investigations.name: Plasma acylcarnitine profile
investigations.indication: Screen for fatty acid oxidation defects

investigations.name: Genetic testing (mitochondrial panel/WES)
investigations.indication: Identify specific mutation for diagnosis and prognosis

investigations.name: Echocardiogram
investigations.indication: Screen for cardiomyopathy in mitochondrial disease

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg IM monthly for 3-6 months for pubertal induction; start at chronological age >=14y and BA >=12y
0.25-0.5 mg PO daily (or 5 mcg/kg/day) for pubertal induction; start at chronological age >=13y and BA >=11y

second_line_drugs.notes: Start 6.25 mcg (1/8 of 50 mcg patch) nightly; increase q6mo; more physiologic than oral
2.5 mg PO daily for 6 months in boys; aromatase inhibitor to boost growth and pubertal tempo

notes: Distinguish constitutional delay from permanent hypogonadism with clinical follow-up and labs. Low-dose sex steroid trial for 3-6 months can help differentiate and provide psychological benefit.
guideline_changes: 2024 JCRPE review updated delayed puberty management perspectives including aromatase inhibitor use
counselling: Your child's puberty is delayed — in most cases, this is a normal variant and nothing to worry about
A short course of hormone medicine may be given to help start puberty
The child may feel different from peers — provide emotional support and reassurance
Height and development will be monitored every 3-6 months
Follow up with the endocrinologist to determine if further treatment is needed
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology for all cases of delayed puberty. Genetics if Turner syndrome or Klinefelter syndrome suspected. Ophthalmology/neurosurgery if pituitary pathology on MRI
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Admit if: adrenal crisis in undiagnosed panhypopituitarism, severe psychological crisis, or need for dynamic hormone testing not available outpatient

investigations.name: Bone age (left wrist X-ray)
investigations.indication: Assess skeletal maturity — delayed bone age suggests constitutional delay

investigations.name: LH, FSH, estradiol/testosterone
investigations.indication: Distinguish hypogonadotropic (low LH/FSH) vs hypergonadotropic (high LH/FSH)

investigations.name: Karyotype
investigations.indication: Turner syndrome (45,X) in girls; Klinefelter (47,XXY) in boys

investigations.name: MRI brain with pituitary focus
investigations.indication: Rule out CNS pathology if hypogonadotropic

investigations.name: TSH, free T4, IGF-1
investigations.indication: Screen for hypothyroidism and growth hormone deficiency

first_line_drugs.notes: 7.5-15 mg IM q28d (1-mo depot) or 11.25-30 mg IM q3mo; US doses 200-300 mcg/kg/28d
50 mg SC implant replaced annually; avoids repeat injections; minor procedure under local anesthesia

second_line_drugs.notes: 22.5 mg IM q24wk (6-mo depot, Triptodur); or 3.75 mg IM q28d
45 mg SC q6mo; small-volume injection; FDA-approved for CPP

notes: GnRH agonists are standard of care for central precocious puberty to preserve adult height potential. Monitor for adequate LH suppression and growth velocity deceleration.
guideline_changes: 6-month depot formulations (Fensolvi SC, Triptodur IM) now widely used reducing injection burden
counselling: Your child is showing signs of puberty earlier than expected — treatment can help preserve adult height
The monthly/3-monthly injection stops early puberty and allows normal growth to continue
The child may initially have a brief increase in symptoms after the first injection — this is temporary
Provide emotional support — the child may feel different from same-age peers
Regular follow-up every 3-6 months with bone age X-ray is important to track progress
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology mandatory. MRI brain if onset before age 6 (girls) or 9 (boys) to rule out CNS pathology. Genetics if associated with NF1 or other syndromes
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Admit only if: CNS tumor discovered on MRI, acute complication of underlying condition, or need for GnRH stimulation test not available outpatient

investigations.name: Bone age (left wrist X-ray)
investigations.indication: Advanced bone age confirms premature maturation

investigations.name: LH, FSH (basal and GnRH-stimulated)
investigations.indication: Stimulated LH >5 IU/L confirms central precocious puberty

investigations.name: MRI brain with pituitary focus
investigations.indication: Mandatory to rule out CNS lesion (especially in boys and girls <6 years)

investigations.name: Pelvic ultrasound (girls)
investigations.indication: Uterine enlargement, ovarian cysts — supportive of CPP

first_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mcg/kg/day SC daily for GHD; up to 67 mcg/kg/day for ISS; titrate by IGF-1 levels

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.16 mg/kg SC once weekly; long-acting GH approved for pediatric GHD; reduces injection burden
0.66 mg/kg SC once weekly; alternative long-acting GH for pediatric GHD

notes: Individualize dosing based on growth response and IGF-1 levels; stop GH when growth velocity falls below 2.5 cm/year. Once-weekly formulations (2024) improve adherence.
guideline_changes: Somapacitan (Sogroya) once-weekly GH approved for new pediatric indications (2024)
counselling: Your child is shorter than expected — growth hormone injections may be needed to help the child grow taller
The injection is given every day (or once weekly with newer medicine) under the skin — usually at bedtime
Growth will be monitored every 3-6 months — bring the child for regular height and weight measurements
Good nutrition, adequate sleep (8-10 hours), and regular physical activity also help growth
Treatment works best when started early — continue until the doctor says growth plates have closed
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology mandatory. Genetics if syndromic short stature (Turner, Noonan, Prader-Willi). GI if celiac or IBD contributing. Psychology if significant psychological impact
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Admit only for: GH stimulation testing if not available outpatient, newly diagnosed pituitary tumor, or severe GH deficiency with hypoglycemia in infants

investigations.name: Bone age (left wrist X-ray)
investigations.indication: Assess growth potential — compare bone age to chronological age

investigations.name: Thyroid function (TSH, free T4)
investigations.indication: Hypothyroidism is a treatable cause of short stature

first_line_drugs.notes: Neonates: 10-15 mcg/kg/day PO; 1y: 4-6 mcg/kg/day; adolescents: 2-4 mcg/kg/day; take on empty stomach

second_line_drugs.notes: Same mcg/kg dosing as tablet; use liquid formulation in neonates/infants unable to swallow tablets

notes: Start treatment within 2 weeks of life for congenital hypothyroidism to prevent cognitive impairment. Recheck TSH/FT4 at 4-6 weeks after any dose change.
counselling: Your child needs thyroid medicine (levothyroxine) every day — give it on an empty stomach in the morning
Give the tablet crushed in a small amount of water for infants — avoid mixing with soy formula or iron
Never stop the medicine without asking the doctor — stopping can cause developmental delays
Blood tests (TSH, T4) are needed 4-6 weeks after each dose change, then every 3-6 months
For congenital hypothyroidism, early treatment within 2 weeks of birth prevents intellectual disability
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology for all congenital hypothyroidism. Re-evaluation at age 3 (trial off levothyroxine to confirm permanent vs transient). Genetics if syndromic. Developmental pediatrics if developmental concerns
hospitalisation_criteria: Myxedema coma (extremely rare in children) — IV levothyroxine, ICU admission. Severe hypothyroidism with pericardial effusion or cardiac compromise. Newborn with very high TSH >100 requiring urgent treatment

investigations.name: TSH and free T4
investigations.indication: Primary diagnosis — high TSH, low FT4 confirms hypothyroidism

investigations.name: Thyroid scintigraphy or ultrasound
investigations.indication: Determine etiology in congenital hypothyroidism (ectopic, aplastic, dyshormonogenesis)

investigations.name: Anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies
investigations.indication: Hashimoto thyroiditis — most common cause in older children

investigations.name: Bone age
investigations.indication: Assess degree of hypothyroidism and delayed skeletal maturation

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1.0 mg/kg/day PO divided TID initially; maintenance 0.2 mg/kg/day; treat >=3 years
0.5-2 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for symptom control (tachycardia, tremor) while awaiting MMI effect

second_line_drugs.notes: Dose calculated per thyroid size; for children >10y failing ATD or with significant adverse effects
Near-total thyroidectomy by experienced surgeon; for large goiter, non-compliance, or ATD failure

notes: Methimazole is first-line; never use propylthiouracil (PTU) in children due to hepatotoxicity risk. Treatment duration of 3-5+ years recommended; euthyroid usually achieved in 4-6 weeks.
counselling: Your child has an overactive thyroid — medicine (methimazole) will be given for at least 3 years
Never use propylthiouracil (PTU) in children — it can cause liver damage
Report immediately if the child develops: fever with sore throat, jaundice, or rash — stop methimazole and come to hospital
Blood tests (TSH, T3, T4, CBC) are needed every 4-6 weeks initially, then every 3 months
Propranolol for fast heart rate is temporary — it will be stopped once thyroid levels normalize
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology mandatory. Nuclear medicine if RAI therapy considered (age >10). Experienced thyroid surgeon if surgery needed. Ophthalmology if Graves ophthalmopathy present
hospitalisation_criteria: Thyroid storm (fever, tachycardia, altered mental status, severe thyrotoxicosis). New-onset atrial fibrillation. Severe heart failure from thyrotoxicosis. Agranulocytosis from methimazole (fever + sore throat + neutrophils <500)

investigations.name: TSH, free T4, free T3
investigations.indication: Diagnosis and monitoring — TSH suppressed, FT4/FT3 elevated

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: Baseline before methimazole — monitor for agranulocytosis

first_line_drugs.notes: Neonates: 0.5-1 mg IM at birth; VKDB treatment: 1-5 mg IV/SC; preterm: 0.3-0.5 mg/kg IM
1000 mcg PO daily x4mo for nutritional deficiency; or 1000 mcg IM weekly x4wk then monthly

second_line_drugs.notes: 100-300 mg PO daily for scurvy treatment; continue for 1-3 months until stores replete
25-200 IU/day PO for deficiency; higher doses in cholestasis or fat malabsorption

notes: Vitamin K prophylaxis at birth prevents hemorrhagic disease of newborn. Identify and treat underlying cause of deficiency (dietary, malabsorption, or chronic disease).
counselling: Your child has a vitamin deficiency that can be corrected with proper supplementation
Give vitamin supplements as prescribed — complete the full course even if the child looks better
Feed the child a balanced diet with fruits, vegetables, eggs, milk, and green leafy vegetables
All newborns must receive Vitamin K injection at birth — never refuse it
Follow up for blood tests after completing the treatment course to confirm recovery
referral_criteria: Hematology if severe anemia from B12/folate deficiency. Neurology if neurological symptoms from B12 deficiency. GI if malabsorption suspected as cause. Dietitian for dietary counselling
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe bleeding from vitamin K deficiency (VKDB). Severe megaloblastic anemia (Hb <5 g/dL) from B12 deficiency. Scurvy with hemorrhagic complications. Neurological symptoms from B12 deficiency requiring urgent replacement

investigations.name: CBC with peripheral smear
investigations.indication: Macrocytic anemia in B12/folate deficiency; assess bleeding risk

investigations.name: Serum homocysteine and methylmalonic acid
investigations.indication: Elevated in B12 deficiency — more sensitive than serum B12

first_line_drugs.notes: <6mo: 50,000 IU PO; 6-12mo: 100,000 IU PO; >12mo: 200,000 IU PO; day 1, day 2, then repeat at 2 wk

second_line_drugs.notes: 100,000 IU PO q4-6mo for ages 6-11mo; 200,000 IU PO q4-6mo for 12-59mo in endemic areas

notes: Active corneal xerophthalmia is a medical emergency requiring immediate high-dose vitamin A. Also give vitamin A with measles treatment per WHO protocol regardless of deficiency status.
counselling: Your child has vitamin A deficiency — high-dose vitamin A drops will be given on 3 occasions
Eye involvement (night blindness, dry eyes) is a sign of severe deficiency — report immediately
Feed the child orange/yellow fruits and vegetables (carrot, papaya, mango), green leafy vegetables, and eggs
Vitamin A supplementation every 6 months is recommended for all children 6-59 months in India
Come back if the child develops eye redness, whiteness on the eye, or difficulty seeing at night
referral_criteria: Ophthalmology urgently if corneal involvement (xerophthalmia stage X2-X3B — corneal ulcer/keratomalacia). Pediatric nutrition for severe malnutrition associated with VAD. GI if malabsorption identified
hospitalisation_criteria: Corneal xerophthalmia (X2 or higher — corneal xerosis, ulceration, keratomalacia). Measles with vitamin A deficiency. Severe malnutrition with VAD. Corneal perforation requiring emergency treatment

investigations.name: Ophthalmologic examination
investigations.indication: Bitot spots, corneal xerosis, keratomalacia grading

investigations.name: Serum albumin
investigations.indication: Protein status — VAD often coexists with PEM

first_line_drugs.notes: <1y: 2000 IU/day PO x12wk; >1y: 3000-6000 IU/day PO x8-12wk; recheck 25-OH-D after course
30-75 mg/kg/day elemental calcium PO divided TID for rickets with low Ca or inadequate dietary intake

second_line_drugs.notes: 50,000 IU PO weekly x6-8wk for adolescents; then maintenance 600-1000 IU/day
0.01-0.05 mcg/kg/day PO for vitamin D-dependent rickets type I or renal disease

notes: All breastfed infants should receive 400 IU/day vitamin D prophylaxis from birth. Double treatment dose in patients with obesity, malabsorption, or dark skin pigmentation.
source: AAP 2008, Indian Academy of Pediatrics 2021, Endocrine Society 2024
counselling: Your child has vitamin D deficiency — this can cause weak bones and rickets in children
Give vitamin D drops/tablets as prescribed every day — complete the full 8-12 week course
All breastfed babies should get 400 IU vitamin D daily from birth until weaning to fortified milk
Encourage 15-20 minutes of sunlight exposure daily (morning sun is best)
Include calcium-rich foods: milk, curd, paneer, ragi, sesame seeds in the child's diet
referral_criteria: Pediatric endocrinology if vitamin D-resistant or dependent rickets suspected. Orthopedics if severe skeletal deformities (bowing, fractures). Nephrology for renal rickets. GI if malabsorption
hospitalisation_criteria: Hypocalcemic seizures from severe vitamin D deficiency. Symptomatic hypocalcemia (tetany, laryngospasm). Pathological fractures. Severe rickets requiring IV calcium supplementation

investigations.name: Serum 25-OH vitamin D
investigations.indication: Primary diagnostic test — <20 ng/mL is deficiency, <12 is severe

investigations.name: Serum calcium, phosphorus, ALP
investigations.indication: Low Ca, low PO4, high ALP in nutritional rickets

investigations.name: Parathyroid hormone (PTH)
investigations.indication: Secondary hyperparathyroidism from vitamin D deficiency

investigations.name: Wrist X-ray
investigations.indication: Rachitic changes: cupping, fraying, widening of metaphysis

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg PO over 3-4h; 5 mL q5min initially, increase as tolerated
20 mL/kg IV bolus for severe dehydration; repeat up to 3 boluses if needed
20 mL/kg IV bolus for severe dehydration; alternative to NS

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg PO/IV single dose (max 4 mg) to facilitate oral rehydration
Nasogastric ORS at 20 mL/kg/h if PO fails and IV not available

notes: Oral rehydration is first-line for mild-moderate dehydration; IV reserved for severe or failed ORT. Continue breastfeeding; avoid sugary fluids.
guideline_changes: Emphasis on hypoosmolar ORS and early refeeding confirmed in 2023 updates
counselling: Your child is dehydrated — give ORS (oral rehydration solution) in small sips every few minutes
Do not give fruit juice, cola, or sweetened drinks — they can worsen diarrhea
Continue breastfeeding — it is the best fluid for a dehydrated baby
If the child vomits, wait 10 minutes and try again with smaller sips
Come back immediately if: child becomes very drowsy, eyes look sunken, no tears when crying, or no urine for 6 hours
referral_criteria: Pediatric emergency if severe dehydration with shock. GI referral if chronic/recurrent dehydration suggesting underlying disease. Endocrinology if diabetes insipidus or adrenal insufficiency suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration (>10% weight loss, altered mental status, hemodynamic instability). Failed oral rehydration. Persistent vomiting preventing ORS intake. Infant <3 months with moderate-severe dehydration

investigations.name: Blood urea and creatinine
investigations.indication: Assess renal function, severity of dehydration

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Hypoglycemia risk especially in young children

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg PO over 3-4h for mild-mod dehydration
10 mg/day PO (<6mo) or 20 mg/day PO (>6mo) for 10-14 days
0.15 mg/kg PO/IV single dose (max 4 mg) for persistent vomiting

second_line_drugs.notes: 1.5 mg/kg PO TID for up to 7 days; antisecretory
250-750 mg/day PO for 5-7 days; adjunctive probiotic
10 billion CFU/day PO for 5-7 days; adjunctive probiotic

notes: Antibiotics not routinely indicated; reserve for specific pathogens or toxic/invasive presentations. Early refeeding with regular diet improves outcomes.
guideline_changes: 2023 WHO update reinforces zinc supplementation and reduced-osmolarity ORS
counselling: Your child has a stomach infection causing loose stools — ORS and zinc are the main treatment
Give ORS after every loose stool: under 2 years = 50-100mL, over 2 years = 100-200mL
Continue normal feeding — do not starve the child. Breast milk is the best food during illness
Give zinc syrup for 10-14 days as prescribed — it reduces the duration and severity of diarrhea
Wash hands with soap before feeding and after toilet — this prevents spread of infection
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI if bloody diarrhea persisting >5 days, or diarrhea >14 days (persistent diarrhea). Infectious disease if unusual pathogen or immunocompromised child. Surgical consultation if toxic megacolon suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration requiring IV fluids. Failed oral rehydration. Bloody diarrhea with systemic toxicity (high fever, altered mental status). Age <3 months with moderate-severe dehydration. Suspected surgical abdomen

investigations.name: Stool routine and microscopy
investigations.indication: WBCs, RBCs, parasites — differentiate inflammatory vs non-inflammatory

investigations.name: Stool culture
investigations.indication: If bloody diarrhea, high fever, or antibiotic use planned

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: If systemic infection or dehydration suspected

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg PO over 3-4h for dehydration management
Identify and eliminate offending allergens; trial of hypoallergenic formula in infants

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg PO/IV single dose (max 4 mg) for vomiting
1.5 mg/kg PO TID for up to 7 days as antisecretory agent
10 billion CFU/day PO for 5-7 days as adjunctive therapy

notes: Focus on identifying underlying cause (food allergy, eosinophilic GI disease). Supportive care with rehydration and dietary modification is mainstay.
counselling: Your child has inflammation in the gut that is not from infection — we need to find the cause
Elimination of suspected food allergens may be tried under medical guidance
In infants, switching to a hypoallergenic formula may help
Keep a food diary to identify triggers — note what the child eats and any symptoms
Follow up in 2 weeks to see if symptoms are improving with dietary changes
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology if symptoms persist >2 weeks or recur. Allergy/immunology if food allergy suspected. Dietitian for elimination diet guidance
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration from vomiting/diarrhea. Bloody stools with anemia. Failure to thrive. Suspected eosinophilic GI disease requiring endoscopy

investigations.name: CBC with differential and eosinophil count
Serum IgE and specific IgE/skin prick tests

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg/day PO daily x5 days (max 500 mg/day) for Campylobacter/Shigella
50-75 mg/kg/day IV/IM q24h (max 2 g) for invasive Salmonella or severe cases
Rehydration first-line; antibiotics only for invasive/toxic presentations

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg PO BID (max 500 mg/dose) x5-7 days if susceptible
8 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided BID x5 days if susceptible
30 mg/kg/day PO divided TID x10 days for C. difficile

notes: Most bacterial gastroenteritis is self-limited; antibiotics reserved for invasive disease, immunocompromised, or specific pathogens. Always check local resistance patterns.
guideline_changes: 2024 ESPGHAN/NASPGHAN H. pylori update emphasizes susceptibility-guided therapy
counselling: Your child has a bacterial gut infection — antibiotics are given only if the infection is serious or specific bacteria are found
Give ORS after every loose stool to prevent dehydration
Continue normal feeding — stopping food does not help and slows recovery
Practice strict hand hygiene — wash hands with soap before meals and after toilet
Ensure safe drinking water — boil or filter water before giving to the child
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI if diarrhea persists >14 days. Infectious disease for resistant organisms or immunocompromised patients. Surgery if toxic megacolon or perforation suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration. Systemic toxicity (high fever, altered mental status). Bloody diarrhea with hemodynamic instability. Immunocompromised child with invasive disease. Suspected HUS (after STEC infection)

investigations.name: Renal function and CBC
investigations.indication: Screen for HUS if STEC suspected (Hb, platelets, creatinine)

first_line_drugs.notes: 12 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg), then 6 mg/kg/day PO days 2-5 (max 250 mg)
50 mg/kg/day IV/IM q24h (max 2 g) x5 days for resistant or severe cases

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO BID (max 500 mg/dose) x3 days if susceptible
8 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided BID x5 days if susceptible
20 mg/kg PO QID x5 days (max 400 mg/dose); alternative per WHO

notes: Always obtain stool culture and susceptibility before or concurrent with empiric therapy. XDR strains increasing; ceftriaxone preferred if resistance suspected.
guideline_changes: CDC 2023 alert on increasing XDR Shigella strains; azithromycin resistance rising
counselling: Your child has Shigella infection — this is a serious bacterial diarrhea that needs antibiotics
Give the full course of antibiotics even if the child improves before finishing
Maintain hydration with ORS — the child may have bloody stools which is common in this infection
Strict hand hygiene is essential — Shigella spreads very easily from person to person
Come back if: blood in stools increases, child develops high fever, or stops urinating
referral_criteria: Infectious disease for XDR Shigella or treatment failure. Nephrology if HUS develops. GI if chronic carrier state. ICU if septic shock or toxic megacolon
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration. Toxic appearance (high fever, altered mental status, lethargy). Bloody diarrhea with hemodynamic instability. Age <6 months. Suspected HUS or toxic megacolon. Immunocompromised child

investigations.name: Stool culture and sensitivity
investigations.indication: Confirm Shigella and guide antibiotic therapy — resistance is common

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg PO over 3-4h for mild-mod dehydration
10 mg/day PO (<6mo) or 20 mg/day PO (>6mo) x10-14 days
0.15 mg/kg PO/IV single dose (max 4 mg) for persistent vomiting

second_line_drugs.notes: 1.5 mg/kg PO TID x5-7 days; antisecretory adjunct
250-750 mg/day PO x5-7 days; adjunctive probiotic

notes: No antiviral therapy available; treatment is entirely supportive. Rotavirus vaccination remains the most effective prevention strategy.
counselling: Your child has a viral stomach infection — it usually gets better on its own in 3-5 days
No antibiotics are needed — viruses do not respond to antibiotics
Give ORS sip by sip and continue feeding — this is the most important treatment
Wash hands thoroughly and keep the child's utensils separate to prevent spread to other family members
Rotavirus vaccine can prevent the most severe forms of viral diarrhea — ask about it at next visit
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI if diarrhea persists >14 days (consider post-infectious lactose intolerance). Immunology if recurrent or severe viral GI infections suggesting immunodeficiency
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration requiring IV fluids. Failed oral rehydration despite ondansetron. Age <3 months with moderate-severe dehydration. Seizures from electrolyte imbalance

investigations.name: Stool rotavirus/norovirus rapid test
investigations.indication: Identify specific virus — helps epidemiological tracking

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: If moderate-severe dehydration — guide fluid therapy

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Hypoglycemia risk in young children with poor intake

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/dose (pip) IV q8h (max 4.5 g/dose) preop and for complicated cases
Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV q24h (max 2g) + Metronidazole 10 mg/kg IV q8h (max 500 mg)
Laparoscopic appendectomy; standard of care for confirmed appendicitis

second_line_drugs.notes: IV amoxicillin-clavulanate then PO x7-10 days for uncomplicated; 58-75% 1yr success
20 mg/kg IV q8h (max 1 g) for severe allergy or resistant organisms
15 mg/kg IV q12h (max 1 g/day) for complicated cases with penicillin allergy

notes: Uncomplicated appendicitis may be managed non-operatively with antibiotics in select cases, but surgery remains standard. Complicated appendicitis with abscess may benefit from initial antibiotics and interval appendectomy.
guideline_changes: 2024 pathways increasingly support non-operative antibiotic-first for uncomplicated cases
counselling: Your child has appendicitis — surgery (removal of the appendix) is usually the best treatment
The operation is done through small holes in the belly (laparoscopic) — recovery is faster
After surgery, the child can usually eat within 24 hours and go home in 1-2 days
Watch for fever, increasing belly pain, or vomiting after discharge — come back immediately
Appendicitis is not preventable — there is nothing you did wrong
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery mandatory. Interventional radiology for percutaneous drainage of appendiceal abscess. PICU if perforated with sepsis
hospitalisation_criteria: All acute appendicitis requires hospitalisation for surgery or antibiotic-first management. Peritonitis. Appendiceal abscess or phlegmon. Perforation with sepsis

investigations.name: CRP
investigations.indication: Elevated in complicated appendicitis — helps differentiate

investigations.name: CT abdomen (if US inconclusive)
investigations.indication: Higher sensitivity — use low-dose protocol in children

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/dose (pip) IV q8h (max 4.5 g/dose) perioperative
Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV q24h (max 2g) + Metronidazole 10 mg/kg IV q8h (max 500 mg)
Laparoscopic preferred; open if needed. Postop abx based on intraop findings

second_line_drugs.notes: 45 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 875 mg/dose) for step-down oral therapy
Cipro 10 mg/kg PO BID + Metro 10 mg/kg PO TID for oral step-down if pen-allergic

notes: If unspecified, obtain imaging (US or CT) to classify. Gangrenous/perforated appendicitis requires 7-10 days of antibiotics; uncomplicated needs only perioperative doses.
counselling: We suspect appendicitis but need more tests to be sure — imaging will help confirm
Until confirmed, the child should not eat or drink (nil by mouth) in case surgery is needed
If appendicitis is confirmed, surgery will be recommended
After surgery, recovery is usually quick — 2-3 days in most cases
Come back immediately if pain worsens, child develops high fever, or starts vomiting
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery for evaluation and possible appendectomy. Interventional radiology if abscess needs drainage
hospitalisation_criteria: All suspected appendicitis for observation, imaging, and possible surgery. Peritonitis on examination. Hemodynamic instability. Need for NPO status and IV fluids

investigations.name: Abdominal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Initial imaging of choice in children — appendix visualization

first_line_drugs.notes: Soave, Swenson, or Duhamel; single-stage preferred. Laparoscopic or transanal approach
20 mL/kg NS via rectal catheter BID-TID preoperatively to decompress

second_line_drugs.notes: Staged approach for severe enterocolitis or total colonic aganglionosis
30 mg/kg/day IV/PO divided TID x7-14 days for Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis
Intrasphincteric injection for postop obstructive symptoms; 1-4 units/kg per session

notes: Definitive treatment is surgical. Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (HAEC) is a life-threatening complication requiring urgent rectal irrigations and IV antibiotics.
guideline_changes: 2023 NASPGHAN guidance on postsurgical defecation issues; trend toward single-stage pull-through
counselling: Your child has Hirschsprung disease — a section of the bowel lacks nerves and cannot push stool through
Surgery is needed to remove the abnormal part and connect the healthy bowel
Rectal irrigations (washing) will be done daily until surgery to prevent dangerous bowel swelling
After surgery, the child may have loose or frequent stools initially — this usually improves over months
Come back urgently if the child develops: fever with distended belly (enterocolitis), vomiting, or bloody stools
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery mandatory for definitive surgical management. GI for post-surgical bowel management. Genetics counselling (RET gene testing in family). Neonatology if diagnosed in newborn period
hospitalisation_criteria: New diagnosis for stabilization and rectal irrigations. Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (HAEC) — medical emergency. Pre-operative preparation. Post-operative care

investigations.name: Rectal suction biopsy
investigations.indication: Gold standard — absence of ganglion cells confirms diagnosis

investigations.name: Anorectal manometry
investigations.indication: Absent rectoanal inhibitory reflex supports diagnosis

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray
investigations.indication: Dilated proximal bowel, assess for enterocolitis

first_line_drugs.notes: Insert NG tube for suction; strict NPO. Fluid resuscitate with NS/LR 20 mL/kg boluses
NS or LR 20 mL/kg bolus; correct electrolytes. Maintenance 1.5x calculated rate
Emergency laparotomy/laparoscopy for strangulation, volvulus, or failed conservative Rx

second_line_drugs.notes: Therapeutic contrast enema for meconium ileus or adhesive partial obstruction
100 mg/kg/dose IV q8h (max 4.5 g) if peritonitis or perforation suspected

notes: Etiology must be urgently identified (volvulus, hernia, adhesions, intussusception). Midgut volvulus requires emergent surgery without delay for imaging.
counselling: Your child has a bowel blockage — this is serious and needs hospital treatment
The child must not eat or drink until the blockage is cleared
A tube through the nose (NG tube) will help remove trapped air and fluid from the stomach
Surgery may be needed if the blockage does not clear with non-surgical treatment
Come back immediately after discharge if the child develops vomiting, abdominal distension, or stops passing stool
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery mandatory for all intestinal obstructions. Emergency surgery for: volvulus, strangulation, perforation, or failed conservative management
hospitalisation_criteria: All intestinal obstructions require hospitalisation. Signs: vomiting (especially bilious in children), abdominal distension, absent bowel sounds, abdominal pain. Emergency for complete obstruction or peritonitis

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray (erect and supine)
investigations.indication: Air-fluid levels, dilated loops, free air if perforation

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: Dehydration and electrolyte imbalance from vomiting/third-spacing

investigations.name: Upper GI contrast study
investigations.indication: Malrotation with volvulus — upper GI series is diagnostic

first_line_drugs.notes: Pneumatic reduction under fluoroscopy; 83-97% success rate. Max pressure 120 mmHg
NS 20 mL/kg bolus; correct dehydration before reduction attempt

second_line_drugs.notes: US-guided saline enema reduction; 73-92% success. No radiation exposure
Operative manual reduction or resection if enema fails, peritonitis, or perforation
0.5 mg/kg IV single dose (max 10 mg); may reduce recurrence rate (adjunctive)

notes: Air enema is first-line for ileocolic intussusception without peritonitis. Recurrence rate is 5-10%; lead points more common in children >2 years requiring surgical evaluation.
guideline_changes: 2024 meta-analysis confirms air enema superiority over hydrostatic reduction in success rates
counselling: Your child has a condition where part of the intestine slides into itself like a telescope — this is called intussusception
An air enema (non-surgical procedure) can fix this in most cases — success rate is very high
The child may cry during the procedure but it avoids surgery in most cases
There is a 5-10% chance of it happening again — come back immediately if symptoms return (pain, bloody stool, vomiting)
Monitor the child closely for 24 hours after the procedure — watch for recurrence of symptoms
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery if: enema reduction fails, peritonitis, perforation, recurrent intussusception (>3 episodes — evaluate for lead point), or age >2 years (higher chance of pathological lead point)
hospitalisation_criteria: All intussusception cases require hospitalisation for reduction and 24-hour observation. Peritonitis or perforation require emergency surgery. Recurrent episodes require admission for surgical evaluation

investigations.name: Abdominal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Target sign (doughnut sign) — sensitivity >95% for ileocolic intussusception

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray
investigations.indication: Obstruction pattern, free air if perforation

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided QD-BID (max 40 mg/day) x8-12 weeks
1 mg/kg/day PO QD (max 40 mg); FDA-approved for pediatric erosive esophagitis
88-440 mcg swallowed BID (age-based); for eosinophilic esophagitis

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5-1 mg PO BID (mixed with sucralose); for eosinophilic esophagitis
SC q2-4wk weight-based dosing; for EoE refractory to PPI/steroids, age >=1yr
2-food or 6-food empiric elimination diet for EoE; dietitian-guided

notes: Distinguish reflux esophagitis (PPI-responsive) from eosinophilic esophagitis (may need topical steroids or biologics). EGD with biopsy required for EoE diagnosis.
guideline_changes: PPI-nonresponsive EoE no longer a diagnostic category; dupilumab approved for EoE age 1+ (2022)
counselling: Your child has inflammation of the food pipe (esophagus) — medicine will help it heal
Give the PPI (omeprazole/esomeprazole) 30 minutes before breakfast on an empty stomach
If food allergy is suspected, an elimination diet may be tried under doctor's guidance
Avoid spicy, acidic, and fried foods that may worsen symptoms
An endoscopy (camera test) may be needed to check the cause — it is safe in children
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology for endoscopy and biopsy. Allergy/immunology if eosinophilic esophagitis diagnosed. Dietitian for elimination diet guidance. Surgery for refractory strictures
hospitalisation_criteria: Dysphagia with food impaction (emergency endoscopy needed). Severe dehydration from inability to swallow. GI bleeding. Esophageal stricture requiring dilation

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy with biopsy
investigations.indication: Diagnose cause — reflux esophagitis vs EoE (>15 eos/HPF)

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 40 mg/day) x4-8 weeks for acid suppression
50 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1 g/dose) x14 days; part of H. pylori triple therapy
15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 500 mg/dose) x14 days; if susceptible

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 500 mg/dose) x14 days; if clarithromycin-resistant
Quadruple therapy component; age >12yr, 262 mg PO QID x14 days
40-80 mg/kg/day PO divided QID (max 1 g/dose); mucosal protectant

notes: H. pylori testing is indicated for ulcers/erosions. Susceptibility testing is mandatory per 2024 guidelines before initiating eradication therapy. Non-H. pylori gastritis managed with PPI alone.
guideline_changes: 2024 update mandates susceptibility-guided therapy; high-dose PPI + amoxicillin-based triple therapy x14 days
counselling: Your child has stomach inflammation — acid-reducing medicine (PPI) will help heal it
If H. pylori bacteria is found, a combination of antibiotics for 14 days is needed
Complete the full course of antibiotics — stopping early can cause the bacteria to become resistant
Avoid giving the child spicy foods, fizzy drinks, and painkillers (NSAIDs) without doctor's advice
A repeat test after treatment is done to confirm the bacteria is gone
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology for endoscopy if red flags present (GI bleeding, weight loss, anemia). Infectious disease for resistant H. pylori. Surgery for perforated ulcer
hospitalisation_criteria: GI bleeding (hematemesis, melena). Perforation (acute abdomen). Severe dehydration from persistent vomiting. Severe anemia requiring transfusion

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy with biopsy
investigations.indication: H. pylori detection, histology, gastritis grading

investigations.name: H. pylori stool antigen or urea breath test
investigations.indication: Non-invasive H. pylori testing; confirm eradication 4 weeks post-treatment

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Anemia from chronic GI blood loss

investigations.name: Serum iron, ferritin
investigations.indication: Iron deficiency from occult blood loss

first_line_drugs.notes: Upright positioning post-feed; smaller/frequent feeds; thickened feeds in infants
1 mg/kg/day PO QD-BID (max 20 mg BID) x4-8 weeks; if lifestyle changes fail
0.7-1 mg/kg/day PO QD (max 40 mg); for erosive GERD

second_line_drugs.notes: Famotidine 0.5 mg/kg PO BID (max 20 mg BID); H2RA if PPI not tolerated
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO QD (max 30 mg); alternative PPI
Laparoscopic antireflux surgery for refractory GERD with complications

notes: Avoid acid suppression in infants with extraesophageal symptoms only. PPI trial of 4-8 weeks recommended for typical GERD symptoms before further workup.
counselling: Your child has acid reflux — this is very common in infants and often resolves by 12-18 months
Keep the baby upright for 20-30 minutes after feeding — avoid lying flat immediately
Give smaller, more frequent feeds rather than large feeds
For older children: avoid eating 2-3 hours before bedtime, reduce fatty and spicy foods
Do not use acid-blocking medicine in infants who are spitting up but growing well — this is normal
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI if: red flags (failure to thrive, GI bleeding, respiratory complications), failed 4-8 week PPI trial, or suspected complications. Surgery referral for severe refractory GERD or complications
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe failure to thrive from GERD. Aspiration pneumonia. Apparent life-threatening event (ALTE/BRUE) from reflux. Esophageal stricture. Severe esophagitis with bleeding

investigations.name: Trial of PPI therapy (4-8 weeks)
investigations.indication: Empiric approach — symptom response supports diagnosis

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy with biopsy
investigations.indication: If red flags or failed PPI trial — assess esophagitis, rule out EoE

investigations.name: pH-impedance monitoring (24h)
investigations.indication: Gold standard for quantifying reflux — before surgery or if diagnosis unclear

investigations.name: Upper GI barium study
investigations.indication: Rule out anatomic abnormalities (malrotation, hiatal hernia) — not for diagnosing GERD

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO QD-BID (max 40 mg/day) x4-8 weeks for ulcer healing
50 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1 g/dose) x14 days as part of triple therapy
15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 500 mg/dose) x14 days; susceptibility-guided

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg/day PO QD (max 40 mg) x8 weeks; alternative PPI
0.5 mg/kg PO BID (max 20 mg BID) if PPI contraindicated/not tolerated
40-80 mg/kg/day PO divided QID (max 1 g/dose); cytoprotective adjunct

notes: Test for H. pylori in all pediatric peptic ulcers; eradication confirmed 4-8 weeks post-treatment. NSAID-related ulcers require NSAID cessation and PPI therapy.
guideline_changes: 2024 H. pylori guidelines emphasize 14-day susceptibility-guided triple therapy
counselling: Your child has an ulcer in the stomach or intestine — acid-reducing medicine will help heal it
If H. pylori bacteria is the cause, a 14-day antibiotic course is needed — do not skip doses
Avoid giving painkillers (ibuprofen, aspirin) without doctor's advice — they can worsen ulcers
Feed the child regular balanced meals — no need for bland diet unless it helps with symptoms
Follow up for a confirmation test to make sure the bacteria is completely gone
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI for endoscopy and H. pylori management. Surgery for perforation or uncontrolled GI bleeding. Hematology if severe anemia from chronic blood loss
hospitalisation_criteria: Upper GI bleeding (hematemesis, melena, hemodynamic instability). Perforation (acute abdomen with free air). Severe anemia (Hb <7 g/dL). Intractable vomiting

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy with biopsy
investigations.indication: Visualize ulcer, H. pylori testing, rule out malignancy in older children

investigations.name: H. pylori testing (biopsy CLO test, stool antigen, or UBT)
investigations.indication: All peptic ulcers must be tested for H. pylori

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Assess for anemia from blood loss

investigations.name: Serum iron and ferritin
investigations.indication: Iron deficiency from chronic blood loss

first_line_drugs.notes: Rest, adequate hydration, balanced nutrition. No specific antiviral therapy available
10-15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day) for fever/pain; AVOID if hepatotoxicity concern
NS/D5NS maintenance if unable to tolerate PO; correct dehydration from vomiting

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO q8h (max 4 mg/dose) for nausea/vomiting
240 mg/kg/day PO divided TID (max 4 g/dose) for severe pruritus

notes: Self-limited in children with excellent prognosis; fulminant hepatitis rare (<1%). Avoid hepatotoxic medications; no dietary restrictions needed. HAV vaccination prevents disease.
counselling: Your child has hepatitis A — this is a viral infection of the liver that usually heals on its own
Give plenty of fluids and a balanced diet — no special dietary restrictions are needed
Avoid giving paracetamol in high doses — it can stress the liver. Use only as directed by the doctor
The child is contagious for about 2 weeks — maintain strict hand hygiene and do not share utensils
Hepatitis A vaccine can protect other family members — ask about vaccination
referral_criteria: Hepatology/pediatric GI if signs of liver failure (coagulopathy, encephalopathy, persistent jaundice >6 weeks). Liver transplant center if fulminant hepatitis develops
hospitalisation_criteria: Persistent vomiting with dehydration. INR >1.5 (coagulopathy). Hepatic encephalopathy (altered behavior, confusion). Severe jaundice with liver synthetic failure

investigations.name: PT/INR
investigations.indication: Liver synthetic function — coagulopathy suggests severe disease

first_line_drugs.notes: Rest, hydration, nutrition. Most acute HBV is self-limited in older children
NS maintenance for dehydration; monitor glucose, coagulation, LFTs serially

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.015 mg/kg PO QD (max 0.5 mg) for severe/protracted acute HBV or fulminant hepatitis
8 mg/kg PO QD (max 300 mg) age >=2yr; for severe acute HBV (off-label)

notes: Acute HBV rarely needs antivirals; interferon is contraindicated in acute phase. Neonates require HBIG + HBV vaccine within 12h of birth to prevent vertical transmission.
guideline_changes: 2024 WHO expanded treatment criteria for chronic HBV; EASL 2025 updated management guidance
counselling: Your child has hepatitis B — most older children recover fully, but monitoring is important
Blood tests will be done over the next 6 months to make sure the virus clears completely
If the virus does not clear, long-term antiviral treatment may be needed
All family members should be tested and vaccinated against hepatitis B
For newborns: HBIG and HBV vaccine within 12 hours of birth prevents mother-to-child transmission
referral_criteria: Pediatric hepatology/GI for all cases. Infectious disease for chronic HBV requiring antivirals. Liver transplant center if fulminant hepatitis. Obstetrics for pregnant mothers (HBV-positive) for prevention of vertical transmission
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe acute HBV with coagulopathy (INR >1.5). Hepatic encephalopathy. Fulminant hepatic failure. Severe dehydration from persistent vomiting. Newborn of HBV-positive mother for HBIG + vaccine

investigations.name: HBsAg, HBV DNA, HBeAg
investigations.indication: Confirm acute HBV; viral load for severity

investigations.name: Liver function tests
investigations.indication: AST, ALT, bilirubin for severity assessment

first_line_drugs.notes: Weight-based: <17kg 150/37.5mg, 17-<30kg 200/50mg, >=30kg 400/100mg PO QD x12wk
Weight-based granules or tablets PO QD x8wk; pangenotypic, age >=3yr

second_line_drugs.notes: Weight-based PO QD x12wk; for genotypes 1,4,5,6; age >=3yr
No DAAs approved <3yr; observe for spontaneous clearance (high in infants)

notes: Treat all children >=3yr with chronic HCV; spontaneous clearance high in infants so defer treatment. SVR rates >95% with current DAA regimens in children.
guideline_changes: Pan-genotypic DAAs now FDA-approved for children >=3yr; child-friendly formulations available
counselling: Your child has hepatitis C — new oral medicines can cure this infection in 8-12 weeks
The medicines must be taken every day at the same time — do not miss doses
Treatment is available for children aged 3 years and above with very high cure rates (>95%)
Hepatitis C spreads through blood — avoid sharing razors, toothbrushes, or needles
After completing treatment, a blood test at 12 weeks will confirm if the virus is cured
referral_criteria: Pediatric hepatology/GI mandatory for DAA therapy initiation. Infectious disease for complex cases. Hematology if acquired through blood products. Liver transplant if cirrhosis develops
hospitalisation_criteria: Fulminant hepatitis (rare). Decompensated cirrhosis. Severe liver failure. Generally DAA treatment is outpatient

investigations.name: HCV RNA (quantitative PCR)
investigations.indication: Confirm viremia and measure viral load

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Baseline; rule out cytopenias from chronic disease

first_line_drugs.notes: Etiology-specific: antivirals for HBV/HCV, chelation for Wilson, UDCA for cholestasis
10-20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for cholestatic liver diseases
