High-calorie diet 120-150% RDA; MCT-enriched formula; fat-soluble vitamin supplementation

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-3 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 100 mg) for ascites management
1-2 mg/kg/day PO/IV (max 6 mg/kg/day) for ascites; combine with spironolactone
Definitive treatment for decompensated cirrhosis or liver failure

notes: Management is etiology-directed. Monitor for complications: variceal bleeding, ascites, hepatorenal syndrome. Liver transplant evaluation should be early for decompensation.
guideline_changes: Resmetirom (THR-beta agonist) approved 2024 for MASH in adults; pediatric trials pending
counselling: Your child has liver scarring (cirrhosis) — lifelong monitoring and treatment of the cause is needed
Feed the child high-calorie nutritious food — liver disease increases nutritional needs by 20-50%
Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) supplements must be taken as prescribed
Come back urgently if: child vomits blood, has black stools, develops a swollen belly, or becomes confused
Regular ultrasound and blood tests every 3-6 months are needed to monitor the liver
referral_criteria: Pediatric hepatology mandatory. Liver transplant evaluation early for decompensated cirrhosis. GI for variceal screening/banding. Nutrition/dietitian. Genetics if inherited liver disease
hospitalisation_criteria: Variceal bleeding (hematemesis/melena). Ascites not responding to diuretics. Hepatic encephalopathy. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. Hepatorenal syndrome. Acute decompensation

investigations.name: Liver function tests (comprehensive)
investigations.indication: AST, ALT, bilirubin, albumin, GGT — monitor disease progression

investigations.name: Liver ultrasound with Doppler
investigations.indication: Liver size, nodularity, portal flow, ascites

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy
investigations.indication: Variceal screening — start at diagnosis of cirrhosis

first_line_drugs.notes: 150 mg/kg IV over 1h, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h for APAP toxicity
Continuous infusion to maintain glucose >70 mg/dL; 6-8 mg/kg/min glucose infusion rate
2-10 mg IV single dose for coagulopathy; repeat if INR remains elevated

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg IV q8h x14-21 days if HSV hepatitis suspected (neonates especially)
2 mg/kg/day PO/IV (max 60 mg) for autoimmune hepatitis-related ALF
10-15 mL/kg IV for active bleeding with coagulopathy; avoid routine use
Emergency listing for ALF meeting King's College or PELD criteria

notes: Transfer to pediatric liver transplant center urgently. NAC beneficial for APAP-induced ALF; role in non-APAP PALF remains uncertain. Treat identifiable causes (HSV, Wilson, AIH).
guideline_changes: 2024 ISPGHAN consensus on pediatric ALF management; NAC role debated for non-APAP PALF
counselling: Your child's liver is failing and needs emergency treatment — this is life-threatening
The child may need transfer to a liver transplant center if the liver does not recover
Multiple medicines and close monitoring are needed — the child will be in the ICU
Please share all information about medicines taken, illnesses, or toxin exposure
The medical team will keep you informed at every step — liver transplant may be discussed
referral_criteria: Pediatric hepatology and liver transplant center URGENTLY. PICU mandatory. Nephrology if hepatorenal syndrome. Neurosurgery if intracranial hypertension from hepatic encephalopathy
hospitalisation_criteria: All pediatric acute liver failure (PALF) requires PICU admission. Criteria: coagulopathy (INR >=1.5 with encephalopathy or INR >=2.0 without encephalopathy) in acute liver disease without pre-existing chronic liver disease

investigations.name: PT/INR (serial q6-12h)
investigations.indication: Key prognostic marker — rising INR indicates worsening

investigations.name: Viral hepatitis panel (HAV IgM, HBsAg, HCV RNA)

first_line_drugs.notes: Ceftriaxone 50-75 mg/kg IV q24h (max 2g) + Metronidazole 30 mg/kg/day IV divided TID
US-guided needle aspiration (<5cm) or catheter drainage (>5cm)

second_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/dose (pip) IV q8h (max 4.5 g) for polymicrobial pyogenic abscess
35-50 mg/kg/day PO/IV divided TID x7-10 days for amoebic liver abscess
20 mg/kg IV q8h (max 1 g) for resistant organisms or treatment failure
Open drainage for ruptured abscess, peritonitis, or failed percutaneous approach

notes: Pyogenic abscesses require both antibiotics and drainage; amoebic abscesses often resolve with metronidazole alone. Antibiotic duration 2-6 weeks depending on response.
source: Pediatric Surgery 2023, Johns Hopkins ABX Guide 2024
counselling: Your child has an infection inside the liver (abscess) — antibiotics and possibly drainage are needed
IV antibiotics will be given for several days, followed by oral antibiotics for weeks
If the abscess is large, a needle will be used to drain the pus under ultrasound guidance
Complete the full course of antibiotics (usually 2-6 weeks) even after feeling better
Follow-up ultrasound is needed to confirm the abscess has resolved
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery/interventional radiology for drainage (abscess >5 cm or not responding to antibiotics alone). GI if underlying liver disease. Immunology if recurrent liver abscesses
hospitalisation_criteria: All liver abscesses require hospitalisation for IV antibiotics. Drainage needed for: abscess >5 cm, no clinical improvement in 48-72h of antibiotics, or risk of rupture. Sepsis or hemodynamic instability

investigations.name: Abdominal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Identify abscess location, size, and guide drainage

investigations.name: Amoebic serology (IHA/ELISA)
investigations.indication: Differentiate amoebic from pyogenic abscess — common in India

first_line_drugs.notes: Structured diet + exercise program; target 7-10% weight loss if overweight/obese
Reduce sugar-sweetened beverages, processed foods; increase fiber and whole foods
400-800 IU/day PO for biopsy-proven NASH in children without diabetes

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for cholestatic liver disorders
500 mg PO BID (age >=10yr) for NAFLD with insulin resistance; limited evidence

notes: NAFLD/MASLD is the most common pediatric chronic liver disease. Screen overweight children with ALT; liver biopsy for indeterminate cases. Lifestyle modification is primary treatment.
guideline_changes: NAFLD renamed MASLD/MASH; resmetirom approved 2024 for adult MASH; pediatric guidance forthcoming
counselling: Your child has fatty liver disease (NAFLD/MASLD) — this is caused by excess weight and poor diet
The most important treatment is lifestyle change: healthier food and more physical activity
Cut down sugar-sweetened drinks (cola, juice, packaged drinks) — switch to water and buttermilk
60 minutes of active play/exercise every day is the best medicine for this condition
Regular blood tests every 6 months to check liver enzymes and follow-up with the doctor
referral_criteria: Pediatric hepatology/GI if ALT persistently elevated despite 6 months of lifestyle changes. Liver biopsy if NASH suspected. Dietitian for structured meal planning. Endocrinology if insulin resistance/prediabetes
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider if: acute liver failure from NASH, liver biopsy under sedation, severe metabolic complications (diabetes, hypertension crisis)

investigations.name: ALT (serial q3-6 months)
investigations.indication: Primary monitoring marker — >2x ULN warrants further evaluation

investigations.name: Fasting glucose and HbA1c
investigations.indication: Screen for insulin resistance and diabetes

investigations.name: Liver biopsy
investigations.indication: If persistent elevation despite lifestyle changes — stage fibrosis/NASH

first_line_drugs.notes: 2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) x2-4wk, then taper over 6-8wk to 2.5-5 mg/day maintenance
1-2.5 mg/kg/day PO QD (added after initial steroid response); steroid-sparing agent

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1 g BID) if azathioprine intolerant/refractory
3-5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; bridge therapy for severe AIH or steroid-refractory
0.05-0.1 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; target trough 3-5 ng/mL for refractory AIH

notes: Autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) is the most common inflammatory liver disease requiring treatment. Relapse rate 60-80% after withdrawal; many patients need lifelong immunosuppression.
guideline_changes: EASL 2025 updated AIH guidelines; mycophenolate increasingly used as steroid-sparing agent
counselling: Your child has autoimmune liver disease — the body's immune system is attacking the liver
Steroid medicine and an immunosuppressant will be needed, possibly for life
Do not stop the medicines without asking the doctor — disease flares are very common when stopped
Regular blood tests every 2-4 weeks initially, then every 3 months, are needed to monitor response
Report immediately if the child becomes yellow (jaundiced), has dark urine, or develops a swollen belly
referral_criteria: Pediatric hepatology mandatory. Liver transplant evaluation if steroid-refractory or decompensated. Immunology for atypical cases. Genetics if overlap with other autoimmune diseases
hospitalisation_criteria: New diagnosis with significant coagulopathy or jaundice. Acute liver failure from autoimmune hepatitis. Disease flare with hepatic decompensation. Initiation of immunosuppression requiring close monitoring

investigations.name: Liver function tests
investigations.indication: AST, ALT markedly elevated; bilirubin, albumin for severity

investigations.name: Liver biopsy
investigations.indication: Interface hepatitis — confirms diagnosis and grades severity

first_line_drugs.notes: For esophageal varices with bleeding or high-risk stigmata; repeat q2-4wk until obliteration
1-2 mcg/kg IV bolus then 1-2 mcg/kg/h infusion (max 50 mcg/h) for acute variceal bleed
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID; titrate to 25% HR reduction. Limited pediatric evidence

second_line_drugs.notes: Endoscopic injection for variceal bleeding if banding not feasible (younger children)
1-3 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 100 mg) for ascites
Transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt for refractory bleeding/ascites
Surgical bypass for extrahepatic portal vein obstruction in eligible children

notes: Beta-blockers for primary prophylaxis lack robust pediatric evidence; band ligation preferred for secondary prophylaxis. Evaluate for liver transplant if decompensated cirrhosis.
guideline_changes: Baveno VII criteria for non-invasive assessment of portal hypertension; beta-blockers debated in children
counselling: Your child has high blood pressure in the liver blood vessels — this can cause the veins in the food pipe to swell and bleed
An endoscopy (camera test) is needed to check for swollen veins and may need treatment (banding)
If the child vomits blood or has black tarry stools — rush to the hospital immediately
Salt restriction may be needed if the child has a swollen belly (fluid accumulation)
Regular follow-up every 3-6 months with endoscopy is essential to prevent bleeding
referral_criteria: Pediatric hepatology/GI mandatory. GI endoscopy for variceal surveillance and banding. Interventional radiology for TIPS. Pediatric surgery for shunt procedures (Meso-Rex). Liver transplant if decompensated cirrhosis
hospitalisation_criteria: Acute variceal bleeding (hematemesis/melena) — emergency. New onset ascites. Hepatic encephalopathy. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. Scheduled endoscopic band ligation

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy
investigations.indication: Variceal grading and banding — first screening at diagnosis

investigations.name: Liver ultrasound with Doppler
investigations.indication: Portal vein flow direction, spleen size, ascites

investigations.name: CT/MR angiography
investigations.indication: Map portal venous anatomy for surgical planning

first_line_drugs.notes: Lifelong elimination of wheat, barley, rye. Dietitian-guided. Labeled GF oats permitted
Iron, folate, calcium, vitamin D, zinc as needed based on deficiency labs

second_line_drugs.notes: Short-term lactose avoidance if secondary lactase deficiency present at diagnosis
1-2 mg/kg/day PO x2-4wk for celiac crisis with severe malabsorption (rare)

notes: GFD is the only proven treatment. Monitor tTG-IgA for adherence. Follow-up with dietitian and serology at 3-6 month intervals initially, then annually.
guideline_changes: 2024 ESPGHAN position paper on GFD implementation; oats allowed if labeled gluten-free
counselling: Your child has celiac disease — a lifelong strict gluten-free diet is the only treatment
Avoid wheat (roti, bread, pasta), barley, and rye completely — even tiny amounts cause damage
Rice, corn, jowar, bajra, and certified gluten-free oats are safe
Read food labels carefully — gluten is hidden in many processed foods, sauces, and medicines
Join a celiac support group — they can help with diet planning and recipes for Indian meals
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology for diagnosis confirmation. Dietitian specializing in celiac disease MANDATORY. Endocrinology if associated thyroid disease. Dermatology for dermatitis herpetiformis. Re-evaluate non-responders for refractory celiac or wrong diagnosis
hospitalisation_criteria: Celiac crisis (severe diarrhea, dehydration, metabolic derangement — rare). Severe anemia requiring transfusion. Failure to thrive not responding to GFD. Suspected refractory celiac disease

investigations.name: Total serum IgA
investigations.indication: Rule out IgA deficiency (false negative tTG-IgA)

investigations.name: Upper GI endoscopy with duodenal biopsy
investigations.indication: Villous atrophy — Marsh classification confirms diagnosis

investigations.name: CBC, iron, ferritin, folate, vitamin D, calcium
investigations.indication: Nutritional deficiency screening — common at diagnosis

investigations.name: HLA DQ2/DQ8 typing
investigations.indication: Negative result rules out celiac disease (high NPV)

first_line_drugs.notes: High-calorie diet 120-150% RDA; oral supplements or enteral nutrition as needed
Iron, calcium, zinc, magnesium, fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) based on labs
Gluten-free diet for celiac, PERT for pancreatic insufficiency, antibiotics for SIBO

second_line_drugs.notes: TPN for severe malabsorption/intestinal failure; individualized caloric and micronutrient goals
0.05-0.1 mg/kg/dose PO (max 2 mg/dose) for symptomatic diarrhea; age >2yr only
240 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for bile acid malabsorption

notes: Thorough workup essential to identify cause (celiac, IBD, EPI, SIBO, cow milk protein allergy). Prompt referral to pediatric GI for growth failure associated with malabsorption.
counselling: Your child's body is not absorbing food properly — we need to find and treat the cause
Extra calories, vitamins, and minerals may be needed to help the child grow
Follow the dietitian's meal plan — the child may need special formulas or supplements
Weight and height will be monitored closely — bring the child for regular follow-ups
Some causes of malabsorption need lifelong dietary changes — the doctor will explain based on the diagnosis
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology mandatory for diagnostic workup. Dietitian for nutritional rehabilitation. Allergist if food allergy causing malabsorption. Genetics if inherited cause (CF, congenital enteropathy)
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe failure to thrive (weight loss >10%). Severe dehydration. Malnutrition requiring parenteral nutrition. Electrolyte imbalance. Diagnostic workup requiring endoscopy

investigations.name: tTG-IgA
investigations.indication: Screen for celiac disease — most common cause in India

investigations.name: Stool for reducing substances and pH
CBC, iron, ferritin, vitamin D, calcium, zinc
Upper GI endoscopy with biopsy
investigations.indication: If celiac serology positive or etiology unclear

first_line_drugs.notes: 1.5-2x maintenance with D5 0.9% NS; bolus 20 mL/kg if hemodynamically unstable
15 mg/kg PO/IV q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day); first-line analgesia
0.5 mg/kg IV q6h (max 30 mg/dose) x5 days for pain; NSAID adjunct
Begin PO/NG feeds within 24-48h if tolerated; low-fat diet not required

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.2 mg/kg IV q2-4h (max 10 mg) for severe pain unresponsive to non-opioids
20 mg/kg IV q8h (max 1 g) ONLY for infected necrosis; prophylactic abx NOT recommended
For biliary pancreatitis with choledocholithiasis or ductal pathology

notes: Aggressive IV hydration and early enteral feeding are cornerstones of treatment. Prophylactic antibiotics not indicated. Opioids are safe and should not be withheld for pain.
guideline_changes: 2024 BSPGHAN guidance published; liberal IV fluids associated with improved outcomes in pediatric AP
counselling: Your child has inflammation of the pancreas — rest, IV fluids, and pain management are the main treatment
The child can start eating within 24-48 hours when tolerated — no need for prolonged fasting
Low-fat diet is NOT mandatory in mild cases — a regular diet is fine when tolerated
Report immediately if pain returns, the child develops fever, or the belly becomes more swollen
Follow up to check for recurrent pancreatitis — some children need long-term monitoring
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology for all cases. Surgery/IR if infected pancreatic necrosis or pseudocyst. ERCP for biliary pancreatitis with choledocholithiasis. Genetics if recurrent pancreatitis (PRSS1, SPINK1, CFTR)
hospitalisation_criteria: All acute pancreatitis should be hospitalised for IV fluids and pain management. Severe pancreatitis (organ failure, necrosis on CT). Intractable pain or vomiting. Unable to tolerate oral intake

investigations.name: Serum lipase
investigations.indication: Primary diagnostic test — >3x upper limit of normal confirms pancreatitis

investigations.name: Serum amylase
investigations.indication: Supportive — may be normal in 15-20% of cases

investigations.name: Liver function tests
investigations.indication: Screen for biliary cause (elevated bilirubin, ALP)

first_line_drugs.notes: 500-2500 lipase units/kg/meal PO; 250-500 LU/kg/snack. Enteric-coated capsules
Vitamins A, D, E, K at 1-2x RDA PO daily; monitor levels q6-12 months

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg/day PO QD to enhance PERT efficacy by reducing gastric acid inactivation
Medium-chain triglycerides 1-2 mL/kg/day PO for improved fat absorption
ERCP for chronic pancreatitis with duct stones, strictures, or pseudocysts

notes: PERT is the mainstay for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency. Do not exceed 10,000 LU/kg/day to avoid fibrosing colonopathy. Monitor growth and nutritional status regularly.
guideline_changes: 2024 European guidelines on EPI diagnosis and PERT dosing published (UEG/EPC/ESPGHAN)
counselling: Your child's pancreas is not producing enough enzymes to digest food properly
Pancreatic enzyme capsules must be taken WITH every meal and snack — never after the meal
Do not crush or chew the enzyme capsules — swallow whole or sprinkle contents on soft acidic food
Extra fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are needed because the body cannot absorb them well
Watch for greasy, foul-smelling stools — this means the enzyme dose may need to be increased
referral_criteria: Pediatric gastroenterology mandatory. CF team if cystic fibrosis is the cause. Endocrinology if CF-related diabetes develops. Dietitian for nutritional optimization. Surgery/ERCP for chronic pancreatitis with duct pathology
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe malnutrition from exocrine insufficiency. Acute pancreatitis flare. Distal intestinal obstruction syndrome (CF patients). ERCP for duct intervention

investigations.name: Fecal elastase
investigations.indication: Primary test for exocrine insufficiency — <200 mcg/g is diagnostic

investigations.name: 72-hour fecal fat (coefficient of fat absorption)
investigations.indication: Gold standard for steatorrhea — <93% absorption in children >6 months

investigations.name: Sweat chloride test
investigations.indication: Rule out cystic fibrosis as underlying cause

first_line_drugs.notes: Gentle digital reduction with lubrication; apply sugar/salt to edematous mucosa before reduction
0.4-0.8 g/kg/day PO QD (max 17 g) to prevent straining
Screen for cystic fibrosis, treat constipation, parasites, malnutrition

second_line_drugs.notes: Submucosal injection of 30% saline or 5% phenol above dentate line; outpatient procedure
For recurrent prolapse after failed conservative Rx; age >4yr or chronic predisposition
Anal encirclement suture for recurrent prolapse in select patients

notes: Self-limiting in 90% of children <3 years with conservative management. All children with rectal prolapse should be tested for cystic fibrosis (sweat chloride test).
counselling: Your child's rectum is coming out — this usually gets better on its own with treatment of the underlying cause
Keep the child's stools soft with stool softeners — hard stools and straining worsen the problem
If the rectum comes out, gently push it back with a lubricated finger — do not panic
Apply sugar or salt to the swollen tissue to reduce swelling before pushing back if needed
All children with rectal prolapse must be tested for cystic fibrosis — ask your doctor about the sweat test
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery if: recurrent prolapse despite conservative management, incarceration, or need for surgical fixation. CF team if sweat test positive. GI for underlying cause workup
hospitalisation_criteria: Incarcerated prolapse that cannot be manually reduced. Strangulated prolapse (ischemic tissue). Post-surgical care after rectopexy. Newly diagnosed cystic fibrosis

investigations.name: Sweat chloride test
investigations.indication: Rule out cystic fibrosis — MANDATORY in all children with rectal prolapse

investigations.name: Stool examination for parasites
investigations.indication: Parasitic infections (Trichuris, Ascaris) cause prolapse in endemic areas

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Anemia from chronic disease or parasites

first_line_drugs.notes: Magic mouthwash (diphenhydramine + Maalox +/- lidocaine) swish-spit q4-6h for pain
15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day) for pain and fever
10 mg/kg PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day) for pain and inflammation; age >6mo

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/dose PO 4x/day (max 800 mg) x7 days for herpetic gingivostomatitis
15 mL swish-spit BID x10 days; prevent secondary infection of ulcers
Apply to ulcers TID-QID after meals; for recurrent aphthous stomatitis
1-5 mL PO swish-spit QID x7-14 days for oral candidiasis

notes: Identify etiology: viral (HSV, enterovirus), aphthous, fungal, or trauma-related. Maintain hydration; soft/bland diet. Herpetic stomatitis most common in children 1-5 years.
counselling: Your child has mouth sores — this is usually from a virus and heals in 7-10 days
Give cold/soft foods: ice cream, yogurt, cold milk — avoid spicy, salty, or acidic foods
Keep the child hydrated — use a syringe or cup if the child refuses to drink due to pain
Give pain medicine (paracetamol/ibuprofen) 30 minutes before meals so the child can eat
The child is contagious — wash hands frequently and keep separate utensils for a few days
referral_criteria: ENT/oral surgery if large ulcers not healing >3 weeks. Dermatology for recurrent aphthous stomatitis. Immunology if recurrent severe oral infections (immunodeficiency screening). Oncology if non-healing ulcer suspicious for malignancy
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration from inability to drink. Herpetic gingivostomatitis with refusal of all oral intake. Oral candidiasis with systemic spread (immunocompromised). Airway compromise from severe oral/pharyngeal swelling

investigations.name: Clinical examination
investigations.indication: Usually sufficient — diagnose HSV, HFM, aphthous clinically

investigations.name: HSV PCR or DFA from lesion
investigations.indication: If herpetic gingivostomatitis suspected and immunocompromised

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: Rule out neutropenia if recurrent ulcers

first_line_drugs.notes: 6-12 sessions over 3 months; strongest evidence for functional abdominal pain disorders
8-12 sessions; addresses pain catastrophizing, improves coping and functioning
187 mg PO TID (age >8yr) for IBS-related pain; smooth muscle relaxant

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.5 mg/kg PO QHS (max 25 mg); neuromodulator for chronic functional pain
0.25-0.5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID (max 12 mg); for functional dyspepsia/abdominal migraine
10 billion CFU/day PO; modest evidence for functional abdominal pain

notes: Most pediatric chronic abdominal pain is functional (Rome IV criteria). Red flags (weight loss, GI bleeding, fever) require workup. Gut-brain psychotherapy is first-line per 2025 guidelines.
guideline_changes: 2025 ESPGHAN/NASPGHAN IBS/FAP guidelines published; gut-brain psychotherapies have strongest evidence
counselling: Chronic belly pain in children is very common and usually not from a serious disease
Stress, anxiety, and school pressure are common triggers — addressing these often helps the most
Gut-directed therapy (hypnotherapy/CBT) has the strongest evidence — consider it as first treatment
Keep a pain diary: when, where, how severe, and what the child ate — bring it to follow-ups
Red flags that need urgent evaluation: weight loss, blood in stool, fever, night-time pain waking the child
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI if red flags present or no improvement after 4-8 weeks. Psychology/hypnotherapy for gut-brain axis therapy. Dietitian if dietary triggers identified. Surgery urgently if acute abdomen suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Acute abdomen (appendicitis, volvulus, obstruction). Significant weight loss with abdominal pain. GI bleeding. Intractable pain requiring IV pain management

investigations.name: Stool calprotectin
investigations.indication: Distinguish organic from functional cause — <50 mcg/g suggests functional

investigations.name: Celiac screen (tTG-IgA + total IgA)
investigations.indication: Celiac disease screening — recommended in all chronic abdominal pain

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: UTI can present as abdominal pain in children

investigations.name: Abdominal ultrasound
investigations.indication: If atypical features or to reassure family

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mL/kg PO over 3-4h for dehydration; ad libitum for maintenance
10 mg/day PO (<6mo) or 20 mg/day PO (>6mo) x10-14 days per WHO guidelines
Continue breastfeeding; regular diet. Reduce juice/sugary drinks for toddler diarrhea

second_line_drugs.notes: 1.5 mg/kg PO TID x5-7 days; antisecretory for acute watery diarrhea
0.05-0.1 mg/kg PO TID (max 2 mg/dose); chronic diarrhea only, age >2yr
250-750 mg/day PO x5-7 days as adjunct; evidence for duration reduction

notes: Investigate persistent (>14 days) or chronic (>4 weeks) diarrhea for underlying cause. Toddler diarrhea is most common chronic cause in ages 1-3yr and is managed with dietary changes.
counselling: Your child has loose stools — ORS and zinc are the most important treatment
Give ORS after every loose stool — it replaces the water and salts lost
Continue feeding normally — stopping food makes diarrhea worse, not better
If diarrhea lasts more than 2 weeks, further investigation is needed — come for follow-up
Reduce fruit juice and sugary drinks — too much juice is a common cause of loose stools in toddlers
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI if diarrhea persists >14 days (persistent) or >4 weeks (chronic). Allergy/immunology if food allergy or immunodeficiency suspected. Dietitian for chronic diarrhea dietary management
hospitalisation_criteria: Severe dehydration. Failed oral rehydration. Bloody diarrhea with systemic toxicity. Infant <3 months with significant diarrhea. Chronic diarrhea with failure to thrive

investigations.name: Stool culture
investigations.indication: If bloody diarrhea or persistent diarrhea

investigations.name: Stool calprotectin
investigations.indication: If chronic diarrhea — distinguish inflammatory from functional

first_line_drugs.notes: NS 20 mL/kg bolus; crossmatch blood. Transfuse pRBC 10-15 mL/kg if Hb <7 or unstable
<20kg: 10 mg IV daily; 20-55kg: 20 mg IV daily; >55kg: 40 mg IV daily; for upper GI bleed
1-2 mcg/kg IV bolus then 1-2 mcg/kg/h infusion (max 50 mcg/h) for variceal hemorrhage

second_line_drugs.notes: EGD within 12h for variceal bleed or 24h for non-variceal; band ligation or injection
15-25 mg/kg IV q6-8h (max 1 g/dose) as adjunct for severe hemorrhage
40-80 mg/kg/day PO divided QID (max 1 g/dose) for mucosal protection

notes: Stabilize hemodynamics first. Upper GI bleed (hematemesis, melena) requires IV PPI and early endoscopy. Lower GI bleed: consider Meckel scan in young children, colonoscopy in older children.
counselling: Your child is bleeding from the stomach or intestines — this needs urgent hospital treatment
The child may need blood transfusion if a lot of blood has been lost
A camera test (endoscopy) will be done to find the source of bleeding and treat it
Do not give any painkillers (especially ibuprofen/aspirin) without doctor's advice
After discharge, follow up for repeat blood tests to make sure bleeding has stopped
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI urgently for endoscopy. Surgery if massive hemorrhage not controlled endoscopically. Interventional radiology for angiographic embolization. Hematology if coagulopathy
hospitalisation_criteria: All significant GI bleeding requires hospitalisation. Hemodynamic instability. Hemoglobin drop >2 g/dL. Active hematemesis or melena. Need for endoscopic intervention or blood transfusion

investigations.name: Meckel scan (Tc-99m pertechnetate)
investigations.indication: Young children with painless rectal bleeding — Meckel diverticulum

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg PO/IV single dose (max 4 mg); may repeat q8h. For AGE-related vomiting
5 mL PO q2-5min after vomiting; increase as tolerated to prevent dehydration

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.25-0.5 mg/kg PO/IM/IV q4-6h (max 25 mg); age >2yr. Sedation risk
1.25 mg/kg PO/IV q6h (max 50 mg/dose); for motion sickness or vestibular vomiting
0.1-0.15 mg/kg PO/IV q6-8h (max 10 mg); limited use due to EPS risk
0.15 mg/kg IV q6h (max 10 mg) for CINV or cyclic vomiting prophylaxis

notes: Ondansetron is first-line for AGE-related vomiting; single dose usually sufficient. Rule out surgical emergencies (pyloric stenosis, malrotation, obstruction) before attributing to benign cause.
counselling: Your child is vomiting — small frequent sips of ORS are more effective than large amounts
Wait 10 minutes after vomiting, then restart ORS in small sips (5 mL every 2-5 minutes)
Once vomiting stops, gradually return to a normal diet — do not keep the child on liquids only
Watch for: vomiting green/yellow fluid (bile), blood in vomit, or severe belly pain — come back immediately
For young infants (under 2 months), any persistent vomiting needs urgent evaluation
referral_criteria: Pediatric surgery urgently if bilious vomiting in infant (rule out malrotation/volvulus). GI for chronic/recurrent vomiting. Neurology if vomiting with headache or neurological signs (raised ICP). Endocrinology for cyclic vomiting syndrome
hospitalisation_criteria: Bilious vomiting in any infant (surgical emergency until proven otherwise). Severe dehydration. Projectile vomiting in infant <3 months (pyloric stenosis). Hematemesis. Altered mental status. Intractable vomiting >24 hours

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: Metabolic alkalosis (hypokalemic, hypochloremic) from persistent vomiting

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: UTI can present with vomiting in young children

first_line_drugs.notes: Structured behavioral therapy; weekly sessions for 12-16 weeks; caregiver-focused
Individual or group CBT; weekly sessions targeting anger management and problem-solving skills
0.25-0.5 mg/day initial, titrate to 0.5-3 mg/day in 2 divided doses; ages 5-17

second_line_drugs.notes: 300 mg BID-TID; target serum level 0.6-1.2 mEq/L; monitor renal/thyroid; ages 7+
2 mg/day initial, titrate to 5-15 mg/day; ages 6-17; monitor metabolic
Intensive community-based family therapy; 3-5 months duration; for severe cases

notes: Always treat comorbid ADHD/depression first. Pharmacotherapy only after psychosocial interventions fail; short-term antipsychotic use recommended.
guideline_changes: Network meta-analysis confirms SGAs most efficacious for disruptive behaviors
counselling: Your child has behavior problems that need structured treatment — this is a medical condition, not just bad behavior
Parent management training (PMT) is the most effective first step — learning consistent strategies at home
Set clear rules and consistent consequences — positive reinforcement works better than punishment
Medicine may be needed if behavioral therapy alone is not enough — your doctor will guide you
Screen time, peer group, and family stress all affect behavior — address these alongside treatment
referral_criteria: Child psychiatrist for medication management. Clinical psychologist for CBT/PMT. School counselor for academic support. Social services if family dysfunction or abuse contributing. Developmental pediatrics to screen for comorbid ADHD/learning disability
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider if: risk of self-harm or harm to others, acute psychotic features, failed outpatient treatment with escalating aggression, need for psychiatric stabilization

investigations.name: Standardized behavioral rating scales (CBCL, Conners)
investigations.indication: Quantify behavior problems, screen for ADHD, monitor treatment response

investigations.name: Psychoeducational testing
investigations.indication: Screen for comorbid learning disability (50% comorbidity)

investigations.name: Lead level
investigations.indication: Lead exposure associated with behavioral problems in Indian children

diagnosis_name: Disorders of social functioning with onset specific to childhood and adolescence

first_line_drugs.notes: Graduated exposure-based CBT; weekly sessions 12-20 weeks; for selective mutism and social anxiety
For reactive attachment disorder (F94.1); 12-25 sessions; caregiver involvement essential
Primary intervention for RAD/DSED; establish safe consistent caregiving environment

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 mg/day initial, titrate to 10-40 mg/day; off-label for selective mutism; ages 5+
12.5-25 mg/day initial, titrate to 50-200 mg/day; off-label for social anxiety; ages 6+
Child-centered or directive play therapy; weekly sessions; for younger children unable to do CBT

notes: No FDA-approved medications for RAD; stable caregiving is cornerstone. For selective mutism, CBT with graduated exposure is first-line; SSRIs adjunctive.
guideline_changes: Updated AACAP anxiety guideline now covers selective mutism under anxiety spectrum
counselling: Your child has difficulty with social interactions — this responds best to therapy, not medicine
A stable, consistent, and loving home environment is the most important factor for improvement
CBT with gradual exposure helps children with selective mutism speak in social situations
Be patient — progress may be slow but steady. Avoid forcing the child to speak
School coordination is important — teachers should understand the condition and provide accommodations
referral_criteria: Child psychiatrist if medication needed (SSRI for selective mutism). Clinical psychologist for CBT/play therapy. School coordination for accommodations. Social services for foster care/placement stability in RAD
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider if: severe self-harm risk, acute attachment crisis with danger, comorbid psychiatric emergency (suicidality, psychosis)

investigations.name: Developmental assessment
investigations.indication: Comprehensive evaluation to rule out ASD, intellectual disability

investigations.name: Hearing assessment (audiometry)
investigations.indication: Rule out hearing loss as cause of social withdrawal

investigations.name: Standardized anxiety scales
investigations.indication: Screen for anxiety (comorbid in selective mutism)

investigations.name: Thyroid function
investigations.indication: Rule out thyroid disorder affecting behavior

diagnosis_name: Emotional disorders with onset specific to childhood

first_line_drugs.notes: Individual or group CBT with exposure; 12-16 weekly sessions; ages 6-18; CAMS-supported
10 mg/day initial, titrate to max 20 mg/day; ages 7+; for separation/generalized anxiety
25 mg/day initial (6-12y), 50 mg/day (12+); titrate to max 200 mg/day; off-label for anxiety

second_line_drugs.notes: 25 mg/day initial, titrate to 50-200 mg/day; FDA-approved for pediatric OCD ages 8+
30 mg/day initial, max 60-120 mg/day; FDA-approved for GAD ages 7+
5 mg/day initial, titrate to 10-20 mg/day; ages 12+; off-label for anxiety
1-3 mg/kg/day in divided doses; last-line for refractory separation anxiety; ECG monitoring

notes: Combination CBT+SSRI yields ~81% improvement vs 55% SSRI alone (CAMS trial). Benzodiazepines not recommended for pediatric anxiety.
guideline_changes: 2020 AACAP guideline recommends combined CBT+SSRI for moderate-severe anxiety
counselling: Your child has anxiety that is stronger than normal — this is very treatable with therapy and sometimes medicine
CBT (cognitive behavioral therapy) is the gold standard — it teaches the child to manage anxiety
Do not force the child into feared situations suddenly — gradual exposure works much better
Avoid excessive reassurance — teach the child to cope rather than avoiding all anxious situations
Combination of therapy + medicine (if needed) gives the best results — up to 80% improvement
referral_criteria: Child psychiatrist if SSRI needed. Clinical psychologist for CBT. School counselor for school-based accommodations. Developmental pediatrics for comorbid ADHD or learning issues
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider if: school refusal >4 weeks not responding to outpatient treatment, suicidality, severe self-harm, panic disorder with agoraphobia causing inability to function, comorbid depression

investigations.name: Standardized anxiety scales (SCARED, GAD-7)
investigations.indication: Quantify anxiety severity and monitor treatment response

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Rule out anemia (fatigue mistaken for anxiety/depression)

first_line_drugs.notes: Habit reversal + relaxation training; 8 sessions over 10 weeks; ages 9+; AAN Level B
0.05 mg/day initial, titrate to 0.1-0.4 mg/day in divided doses; monitor BP; ages 6+
0.5 mg/day initial, titrate to 1-4 mg/day; extended-release preferred; monitor BP

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/day initial, titrate to 2-20 mg/day; for moderate-severe tics; monitor metabolic
0.5-1 mg/day initial, titrate to 1.5-10 mg/day; typical antipsychotic; monitor for EPS
25 mg/day initial, titrate to 50-200 mg/day; AAN Level C evidence; monitor cognition
12.5 mg/day initial, titrate to 25-75 mg/day; for refractory tics; monitor for depression

notes: Treat comorbid ADHD/OCD first as they often cause more impairment than tics. Stimulants generally safe and do not worsen tics.
guideline_changes: 2025 reaffirmation emphasizes CBIT as first-line behavioral intervention for ages 9+
counselling: Tics are involuntary movements or sounds — your child cannot control them, so do not scold or punish
Tics tend to come and go — they may increase with stress and decrease during focused activities
CBIT (behavioral therapy) is the first treatment — it teaches the child techniques to manage tics
Treat comorbid ADHD or OCD first — these usually cause more problems than the tics themselves
Most children with tics improve significantly by adulthood — reassurance is important
referral_criteria: Pediatric neurologist for diagnosis and medication management. Clinical psychologist for CBIT. Psychiatry if comorbid OCD/ADHD needs treatment. School for accommodations (extra time, breaks)
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider only for: tic status (severe continuous tics causing injury or exhaustion), malignant Tourette syndrome unresponsive to all treatments, deep brain stimulation evaluation (refractory severe cases)

investigations.name: Clinical assessment with Yale Global Tic Severity Scale (YGTSS)
investigations.indication: Quantify tic severity and track treatment response

investigations.name: Screening for ADHD and OCD (rating scales)
investigations.indication: Comorbid conditions — present in >50% of Tourette patients

investigations.name: Throat culture (RADT/ASO titer)
investigations.indication: If sudden onset tics — rule out PANDAS/PANS

investigations.name: TSH
investigations.indication: Thyroid disease can cause movement abnormalities

first_line_drugs.notes: Multidisciplinary evaluation + therapy; birth-3 years; federally mandated; individualized IFSP
1-3 sessions/week; targeting expressive/receptive language milestones; ages 0-5
1-3 sessions/week; fine motor, sensory processing, ADL skills; individualized goals
1-3 sessions/week; gross motor milestones (sitting, crawling, walking); ages 0-5

second_line_drugs.notes: Comprehensive evaluation for underlying etiology; genetic/metabolic workup as indicated
10-25 hrs/week; for children with concurrent ASD features; evidence-based behavioral approach
3-6 mg/kg/day elemental iron if deficient; iron deficiency linked to developmental delays

notes: Critical intervention window is before age 3. Most delays resolve with early intervention; refer immediately upon identification.
source: AAP 2022 Milestone Update, IDEA Part C
guideline_changes: CDC/AAP 2022 updated milestone checklists to improve early detection sensitivity
counselling: Your child is not reaching milestones at the expected age — early therapy gives the best results
Start therapy as soon as possible — the brain develops fastest in the first 3 years of life
Spend quality time playing, talking, and reading with your child every day — this is also therapy
Be patient with progress — every child develops at their own pace, and therapy helps them reach their potential
Make sure the child gets proper nutrition, sleep, and a stimulating environment at home
referral_criteria: Developmental pediatrics for comprehensive evaluation. Speech therapy, occupational therapy, physical therapy based on specific delays. Genetics/metabolic workup if global developmental delay. Audiology for speech delay. Ophthalmology if visual concerns
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed for developmental delay itself. Admit if: acute regression (loss of milestones — investigate for metabolic, neurologic cause), seizures, failure to thrive requiring nutritional rehabilitation

investigations.name: Developmental screening (ASQ, Denver II, DASII in India)
Thyroid function (TSH, FT4)
investigations.indication: Hypothyroidism is a treatable cause of developmental delay

investigations.name: Iron studies (ferritin)
investigations.indication: Iron deficiency associated with developmental delays

investigations.name: MRI brain
investigations.indication: If global developmental delay, microcephaly, or regression present

diagnosis_name: Pervasive developmental disorders / autism spectrum disorders

first_line_drugs.notes: 25-40 hrs/week intensive or 10-25 hrs/week focused; individualized; ages 2+; gold standard
1-5 sessions/week; targeting functional communication; may include AAC devices
1-3 sessions/week; sensory integration, fine motor, self-care skills; individualized
0.25 mg/day initial (<20kg) or 0.5 mg/day (>20kg); titrate to 0.5-3 mg/day; FDA-approved 5-16y for irritability

second_line_drugs.notes: 2 mg/day initial, titrate to 5-15 mg/day; FDA-approved ages 6-17 for irritability in ASD
1 mg/day initial, titrate to 1-4 mg/day; for hyperactivity/impulsivity in ASD; off-label
0.3 mg/kg/dose initial, titrate to max 1 mg/kg/dose; for comorbid ADHD symptoms; lower doses in ASD
2.5-5 mg/day initial, titrate to 10-40 mg/day; for anxiety/repetitive behaviors in ASD; off-label

notes: Behavioral and developmental therapies are primary treatment; medications target co-occurring symptoms, not core ASD. Monitor weight at baseline and quarterly on antipsychotics.
guideline_changes: 2024 guidelines expand pharmacologic options for co-occurring irritability and emphasize early ABA
counselling: Your child has autism spectrum disorder — early intensive behavioral therapy is the most important treatment
ABA therapy (applied behavior analysis) for 25-40 hours per week gives the best outcomes
Speech therapy will help your child communicate — even non-verbal children can learn alternative communication
Create a structured routine at home — children with ASD thrive with predictability
Medicines do not cure autism but can help with associated problems like irritability, hyperactivity, or anxiety
referral_criteria: Developmental pediatrics/child psychiatrist for diagnosis and management. ABA therapy provider. Speech-language pathologist. Occupational therapist. School for IEP/special education placement. Genetics (chromosomal microarray, Fragile X) for all newly diagnosed children
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider if: severe self-injurious behavior not manageable at home, acute behavioral crisis with risk of harm, medication adjustment requiring close monitoring, comorbid medical condition requiring admission

investigations.name: Standardized autism assessment (ADOS-2, CARS-2, ISAA in India)
Hearing assessment (OAE/BERA)
investigations.indication: Mandatory — rule out hearing loss mimicking ASD

investigations.name: Chromosomal microarray
investigations.indication: Genetic cause found in 10-20% — recommended for all newly diagnosed

investigations.name: Fragile X testing
investigations.indication: Most common inherited cause of intellectual disability with ASD features

investigations.name: Lead level
investigations.indication: Pica behavior in ASD children increases lead exposure risk

diagnosis_name: Specific developmental disorders of scholastic skills

first_line_drugs.notes: Orton-Gillingham-based phonics instruction; 4-5 sessions/week; 30-60 min; individualized
School-based specialized instruction; legally mandated accommodations under IDEA; annual review
1-3 sessions/week; phonological awareness, reading fluency; for language-based learning disorders

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 sessions/week; handwriting, visual-motor integration; for dysgraphia component
Text-to-speech, audiobooks, speech-to-text software; classroom accommodations
Structured math tutoring for dyscalculia; multisensory approaches; 3-5 sessions/week

notes: No pharmacologic treatment for learning disabilities; medications only for comorbid ADHD/anxiety. Early identification before age 7 yields best outcomes.
guideline_changes: Emphasis on structured literacy and Orton-Gillingham approaches for dyslexia intervention
counselling: Your child has a learning disability — this is NOT due to low intelligence or laziness
Structured reading programs (like Orton-Gillingham) are the most effective treatment for dyslexia
The child has a legal right to accommodations in school — an IEP (individualized education program) should be set up
Extra time on tests, use of audiobooks, and typing instead of handwriting can all help
Early identification and intervention (before age 7) gives the best outcomes — start therapy now
referral_criteria: Educational psychologist for formal psychoeducational testing. Learning disability specialist for structured literacy intervention. School for IEP evaluation. Speech-language pathologist for language-based learning issues. Psychiatry if comorbid ADHD/anxiety
hospitalisation_criteria: Not applicable — learning disabilities do not require hospitalisation. Admit only for severe comorbid psychiatric crisis (suicidality from academic stress/bullying)

investigations.name: Psychoeducational assessment (IQ + achievement testing)
investigations.indication: Diagnose specific learning disability — discrepancy between ability and achievement

investigations.name: Hearing assessment
investigations.indication: Rule out hearing loss affecting reading/language development

investigations.name: Vision assessment
investigations.indication: Rule out visual problems affecting reading

investigations.name: ADHD screening (Conners/Vanderbilt)
investigations.indication: ADHD comorbid in 30-50% of children with learning disabilities

diagnosis_name: Encopresis not due to a substance or known physiological condition

first_line_drugs.notes: Disimpaction: 1-1.5 g/kg/day PO x3-6 days; Maintenance: 0.4-0.8 g/kg/day PO; mix in 6-8 oz fluid
Scheduled sitting 5-10 min after meals; positive reinforcement; stool diary; ongoing

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-3 mL/kg/day PO in divided doses; osmotic laxative; for PEG-intolerant patients
Ages 2-6: 2.5-5 mL/day PO; Ages 6-12: 5-10 mL/day PO; stimulant laxative; short-term
1-3 mL/kg/day PO; max 240 mL/day; ages 1+; not for children at aspiration risk
1 pediatric suppository PR daily as needed; for acute relief; ages 2+

notes: Disimpaction must precede maintenance therapy. Taper laxatives by 25% every 2-3 months over 6-12 months once behavioral modification established.
guideline_changes: PEG 3350 dosing optimized in 2024 studies confirming 0.4-0.8 g/kg/day maintenance range
counselling: Encopresis (soiling) is usually caused by chronic constipation — it is NOT the child's fault
First, the impacted stool must be cleared (disimpaction) — then daily stool softener prevents re-impaction
The child should sit on the toilet for 5-10 minutes after each main meal — make it a relaxed routine
Praise and reward the child for sitting on the toilet — never punish for accidents
Treatment takes 6-12 months — do not stop the stool softener too early or the problem will return
referral_criteria: Pediatric GI if treatment failure after 6 months or red flags (blood in stool, failure to thrive, delayed meconium at birth). Psychology if significant behavioral component. Surgery if Hirschsprung suspected (delayed meconium, refractory to treatment)
hospitalisation_criteria: Rarely needed. Consider if: massive fecal impaction requiring inpatient disimpaction. Suspected Hirschsprung disease needing biopsy. Severe associated behavioral/psychological crisis

investigations.name: Abdominal X-ray
investigations.indication: Assess fecal loading and response to disimpaction

investigations.name: Celiac screen (tTG-IgA)
investigations.indication: Celiac disease can present as constipation

first_line_drugs.notes: Moisture-sensing alarm; use nightly for 12-16 weeks minimum; best sustained response; 66% cure rate
120 mcg melt tablet SL 30-60 min before bed; may increase to 240-360 mcg; restrict fluids 8 hrs

second_line_drugs.notes: For refractory cases; alarm nightly + desmopressin 120-240 mcg SL at bedtime; 12-16 weeks
5 mg PO at bedtime; for enuresis with daytime urgency/frequency; anticholinergic; ages 5+
25 mg PO at bedtime (ages 6-12); max 50 mg (ages 12+); last-line due to cardiac risk; ECG required

notes: Active treatment from age 6. Treat constipation and daytime incontinence first. Structured desmopressin withdrawal over weeks reduces relapse.
guideline_changes: 2025 IPSA consensus recommends structured desmopressin withdrawal to improve cure rates
counselling: Bedwetting is not the child's fault — never punish or shame them
Restrict fluids 2 hours before bedtime and ensure the child urinates before sleeping
Use a waterproof mattress cover and keep spare sheets ready
Be patient — alarm therapy takes 12-16 weeks to show full effect
Reward dry nights with a star chart to motivate the child
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist if daytime wetting persists, recurrent UTIs, abnormal voiding pattern, or no response after 6 months of alarm + desmopressin therapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation rarely needed; admit only if secondary to spinal cord pathology or if invasive urodynamic studies are required

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: If daytime symptoms or recurrent UTI

first_line_drugs.notes: Dietitian-led caloric needs assessment; age-appropriate dietary plan; weekly weight monitoring
High-calorie diet 120-150% of RDA; fortified foods; frequent small meals; adjust to age
Age-appropriate daily multivitamin with iron and zinc; correct specific deficiencies identified on labs

second_line_drugs.notes: PediaSure/Ensure 1-3 servings/day as caloric supplement; 240 kcal per 8 oz; ages 1-13
2 mg PO BID-TID (ages 2-6) or 4 mg PO BID-TID (ages 7+); appetite stimulant; off-label
For severe malnutrition or oral intake failure; continuous or bolus feeds; inpatient or home

notes: Investigate underlying etiology (malignancy, celiac, IBD, eating disorders, psychosocial). Weight loss >5% in children warrants urgent workup.
counselling: Offer 5-6 small energy-dense meals per day instead of 3 large meals
Add ghee, butter, or oil to dal and roti to increase calories
Track weight weekly at the same time on the same scale
Do not force-feed — create a calm, distraction-free mealtime environment
Follow up every 2 weeks until weight gain trend is established

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe dehydration, hemodynamic instability, weight loss >10% with inability to tolerate oral intake, or suspected malignancy

investigations.indication: Screen for inflammatory or infectious cause

investigations.indication: Rule out parasitic infection or malabsorption

For infants: assess latch, increase feeds; concentrate formula to 24-30 kcal/oz if needed
Registered dietitian evaluation; structured meal schedule; eliminate juice/low-calorie fillers
Daily supplementation; iron 2-6 mg/kg/day if deficient; zinc 1 mg/kg/day if deficient; PO

second_line_drugs.notes: PediaSure 1.0-1.5 kcal/mL; 1-3 servings/day between meals; ages 1+
2 mg PO BID (ages 2-6); 4 mg PO BID-TID (ages 7+); appetite stimulant; off-label
For severe FTT unresponsive to oral; continuous or bolus enteral feeds; multidisciplinary decision

guideline_changes: UCSF consensus now uses term 'inadequate growth' replacing 'failure to thrive'
counselling: Increase calorie density of foods — add ghee, cream, or powdered milk to regular meals
Feed every 2-3 hours; do not let the child fill up on juice or water before meals
Maintain a food diary to track what and how much the child actually eats
Growth catch-up takes weeks — do not expect overnight improvement
Bring the child for weight checks every 1-2 weeks initially
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist if no weight gain after 3 months of nutritional intervention, or if organic cause (celiac, GERD, metabolic) suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe malnutrition (weight <60% of expected), dehydration, hypothermia, hypoglycemia, or signs of neglect/abuse

investigations.indication: Rule out UTI or renal tubular acidosis

investigations.indication: Rule out celiac disease if chronic

Structured mealtimes; positive reinforcement; graduated food exposure; parent training

Specialized seating, adapted utensils, modified textures per OT/SLP assessment

counselling: Do not force-feed — it worsens food aversion in children
Offer new foods alongside familiar favorites, without pressure
Keep mealtimes under 30 minutes and maintain a regular schedule
Eat together as a family; children learn by watching others eat
Small improvements in feeding are meaningful — be patient with therapy progress

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe failure to thrive with dehydration, aspiration pneumonia, or need for nasogastric/gastrostomy tube placement

first_line_drugs.notes: Individualized education program; modified curriculum; resource room or inclusion support; IDEA-mandated
1-3 sessions/week; targeting functional communication, social language, and literacy
Positive behavior support; functional communication training; social skills groups; ongoing
1-2 sessions/week; adaptive daily living skills, fine motor, vocational readiness

counselling: Your child can learn and grow — focus on their strengths and what they can do
Enroll in special education or inclusive school with resource room support

Join a parent support group for guidance and emotional support
Regular follow-up every 6 months to reassess developmental progress and therapies

investigations.indication: Rule out inborn errors of metabolism

No screens (TV, phone, tablet) for at least 1 hour before bedtime
Keep the bedroom cool, dark, and quiet — use a nightlight if needed

Include green leafy vegetables (palak, methi), dal, and fortified cereals in diet
Improvement in energy and appetite expected within 2-3 weeks
Complete the full 4-month course even if the child feels better

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if Hb <7 g/dL with symptoms, active bleeding, or need for urgent transfusion or bone marrow biopsy

Include a variety of foods: fruits, vegetables, milk, eggs, and whole grains
If the child is a strict vegetarian, supplementation is especially important
Improvement in appetite and energy expected within 2-4 weeks
Complete the full course and get a follow-up blood test as advised

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe anemia (Hb <5 g/dL), neurological symptoms from B12/copper deficiency, or need for parenteral supplementation

Consider if no MSD/MUD; post-transplant cyclophosphamide-based GvHD prophylaxis

Avoid contact sports, crowded places, and anyone with infections

first_line_drugs.notes: Ara-C 100-200 mg/m2/day CI x7 days + daunorubicin 50 mg/m2 IV x3 days (7+3); 2 cycles
100-150 mg/m2/day IV x5 days; added to induction as ADE regimen
3 mg/m2 IV (max 4.5 mg) on days 1,4,7 of induction; for CD33+ AML
Age-adjusted IT Ara-C for CNS prophylaxis; given during each chemotherapy cycle

second_line_drugs.notes: First unit dose on days 1,3,5 IV; COG AAML1421 showed 81% response; for high-risk AML
Under investigation in APAL2020D trial for R/R AML; promising early results in children
For high-risk AML or poor response after induction; MSD or MUD donor preferred

notes: Over 85% of children enter remission after induction. Risk stratification by cytogenetics and MRD guides consolidation and HSCT decisions.
guideline_changes: Venetoclax being evaluated in frontline pediatric AML (APAL2020D trial). CPX-351 showed 81% ORR in COG study
counselling: AML treatment is intensive but most children enter remission after the first phase
The child will need to stay in the hospital for weeks during each chemotherapy cycle
Infections are the biggest risk — strict hand hygiene and avoiding sick contacts is critical
Report fever immediately — even 38C needs urgent medical attention during treatment
Emotional and psychological support for the child and family is very important
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric oncology center with AML protocol capability and HSCT access
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit at diagnosis for induction chemotherapy; admit for neutropenic fever, bleeding, or any complication during treatment

investigations.name: Bone marrow aspirate with flow cytometry

first_line_drugs.notes: Doxorubicin 25 mg/m2, bleomycin 10 U/m2, vinblastine 6 mg/m2, dacarbazine 375 mg/m2 IV q2wk; 4-6 cycles
BV 1.8 mg/kg IV d1 + doxorubicin, vincristine, etoposide, pred, cyclophosphamide q3wk x5 cycles
Low-dose 21 Gy ISRT for slow early responders or bulky disease; response-adapted approach

second_line_drugs.notes: 3 mg/kg IV q3wk for R/R HL; used with brentuximab +/- bendamustine
1.8 mg/kg IV q3wk for up to 16 cycles for R/R HL
Ifosfamide, carboplatin, etoposide for relapsed HL prior to autologous HSCT

notes: Cure rate 90-95%. BV+AVEPC achieved 92% 3-year EFS vs 82.5% with standard chemo. Minimize radiation to reduce late effects in children.
guideline_changes: Brentuximab vedotin + AVEPC is new standard for high-risk pediatric HL (FDA approved 2022)
counselling: Hodgkin lymphoma has a cure rate above 90% in children with proper treatment
Treatment involves chemotherapy cycles given every few weeks for several months
Side effects like nausea, hair loss, and tiredness are temporary and manageable
Maintain good nutrition and report fever or breathing difficulty immediately
Long-term follow-up after cure is important to monitor for late effects of treatment
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric oncology center; urgent if compressive symptoms (SVC syndrome, airway compromise)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for initiation of chemotherapy, SVC syndrome, airway compromise, neutropenic fever, or severe treatment toxicity

first_line_drugs.notes: Rituximab 375 mg/m2 IV + cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisone per BFM/LMB protocol
ALL-like regimen: induction, consolidation, maintenance x2 years total with VCR, pred, Asp, MTX, 6-MP

second_line_drugs.notes: Short intensive pulses q3-4wk x6-8 months; French LMB protocol with rituximab for B-NHL
1.8 mg/kg IV q3wk for CD30+ anaplastic large cell lymphoma; up to 16 cycles

notes: NHL subtype determines protocol: Burkitt/DLBCL use short intensive chemo, LBL uses ALL-like 2-year regimen. Cure rate exceeds 80% overall.
guideline_changes: Rituximab now routinely added to chemotherapy for mature B-cell NHL in children
counselling: NHL in children responds well to chemotherapy — cure rates exceed 80%
Treatment is intensive and requires close hospital follow-up
Watch for rapid swelling, breathing difficulty, or abdominal pain and seek emergency care
Good nutrition and hydration are critical during treatment
The child may need to miss school during intensive phases — coordinate with the school
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric oncology center; emergency if airway/bowel obstruction or tumor lysis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit at diagnosis for chemotherapy initiation, emergency admission for tumor lysis syndrome, bowel obstruction, or respiratory compromise

first_line_drugs.notes: Watchful waiting 4-6 weeks for low-risk nodes (<2 cm, mobile, no systemic symptoms); most self-resolve
25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided QID x10 days for suspected bacterial lymphadenitis (S. aureus/GAS)

second_line_drugs.notes: 40-45 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID x10 days; broader coverage for lymphadenitis
30-40 mg/kg/day PO divided TID x10 days; if MRSA suspected or penicillin allergy

notes: 90% of children aged 4-8 have palpable nodes. Red flags: supraclavicular location, >2 cm, systemic symptoms, persistent >6 weeks; biopsy if concerning.
counselling: Swollen lymph nodes are very common in children and usually due to infections
Most enlarged nodes will shrink on their own within 4-6 weeks
Give the full course of antibiotics if prescribed — do not stop early
Return if the node keeps growing, becomes very hard, or your child develops fever, weight loss, or night sweats
No need to worry if the node is small, soft, movable, and the child is otherwise well
referral_criteria: Refer for biopsy if node >2 cm persisting >6 weeks, supraclavicular location, associated systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss, night sweats), or hard/fixed node
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if suspected malignancy, abscess needing drainage, or systemic illness with lymphadenopathy

investigations.name: Mantoux test/IGRA
investigations.indication: Rule out tuberculosis in endemic area

first_line_drugs.notes: Directed therapy based on etiology: antibiotics for infection, chemo for malignancy, ERT for storage disease
Monitor CBC, LFTs, coagulation; imaging with ultrasound; avoid contact sports if splenomegaly

second_line_drugs.notes: 60 U/kg IV q2wk for Gaucher disease; reduces hepatosplenomegaly and improves cytopenias
Age-adjusted dosing PO TID for Gaucher type 1 if ERT unavailable; substrate reduction therapy

notes: Hepatosplenomegaly is a sign, not a diagnosis; urgent workup to identify underlying cause. Common etiologies: infections, hemolytic anemias, storage diseases, malignancies.
counselling: Enlarged liver and spleen is a sign of an underlying condition — investigations are needed to find the cause
Avoid contact sports and rough play to prevent splenic injury while the spleen is enlarged
Report fever, increasing abdominal distension, or unusual bleeding immediately
Follow all scheduled investigations and follow-up visits
Treatment depends on the underlying diagnosis — once identified, targeted therapy will be started
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hematologist/oncologist or gastroenterologist based on suspected etiology; urgent if rapidly progressive or malignancy suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe cytopenias, suspected malignancy, splenic rupture risk, or need for liver/bone marrow biopsy

diagnosis_name: Malignant neoplasm of bone and articular cartilage

first_line_drugs.notes: High-dose methotrexate 12 g/m2 IV + doxorubicin 37.5 mg/m2 IV x2d + cisplatin 60 mg/m2 IV x2d; 8-10wk
Limb-salvage surgery preferred if feasible; wide excision with negative margins after neoadjuvant chemo
Continue MAP regimen post-op x12-29 weeks; modify if <90% tumor necrosis

second_line_drugs.notes: Ifosfamide 1.8 g/m2 IV x5d + etoposide 100 mg/m2 IV x5d; add for poor histologic response
MTX 12 g/m2 IV over 4h with leucovorin rescue 15 mg/m2 q6h x10 doses starting at 24h post-MTX

notes: Osteosarcoma is most common; 5-year OS 62-65% for localized disease. Assess histologic response (>90% necrosis = good) to guide adjuvant therapy.
counselling: Bone cancer (osteosarcoma) treatment involves chemotherapy and surgery — limb-salvage is usually possible
Treatment is long (9-12 months) but cure rates are around 65% for localized disease
Report new or worsening pain, fever, or swelling immediately
Physiotherapy after surgery is essential for limb function recovery
Emotional support and counselling for the child and family is strongly recommended
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric orthopedic oncology center with chemotherapy and limb-salvage surgery capability
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for neoadjuvant chemotherapy, surgical resection, and management of chemotherapy complications

investigations.name: MRI of primary tumor
investigations.indication: Assess local extent and skip lesions

first_line_drugs.notes: Neurosurgical resection targeting gross total removal; residual <1 cm x 1 cm if possible
CSI 23.4 Gy (std risk) or 36 Gy (high risk) + tumor bed boost to 54-55.8 Gy for medulloblastoma
Post-radiation chemo for MB: cisplatin 75 mg/m2, VCR 1.5 mg/m2 weekly, cyclophosphamide 1.5 g/m2

second_line_drugs.notes: Carboplatin 175 mg/m2 IV wkly x4 + VCR 1.5 mg/m2 IV wkly for low-grade glioma; 10-wk cycles
150-200 mg/m2 PO x5 days q28 days for recurrent/progressive high-grade glioma
10 mg/kg IV q2wk for recurrent high-grade glioma; anti-VEGF targeted therapy

notes: Treatment is tumor-type specific (medulloblastoma, glioma, ependymoma). Avoid CSI in children <3y; use chemotherapy-only protocols for infants.
guideline_changes: TTFields (tumor-treating fields) emerging for pediatric brain tumors. WNT-subtype MB trials de-escalating therapy
counselling: Brain tumors in children often have good outcomes with proper treatment at a specialized center
Surgery is usually the first step; chemotherapy and/or radiation may follow depending on tumor type
Watch for headaches, vomiting (especially morning), balance problems, or vision changes
The child may need rehabilitation (physio, OT, speech) after surgery
Long-term follow-up is essential — regular MRI scans will be needed for years
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric neurosurgery and neuro-oncology center
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit urgently for raised intracranial pressure, new neurological deficits, posterior fossa syndrome, or for surgical planning

investigations.name: CT head (if MRI not immediately available)

diagnosis_name: Malignant neoplasm of connective and soft tissue

first_line_drugs.notes: Vincristine 1.5 mg/m2 IV wkly, actinomycin D 0.045 mg/kg IV, cyclophosphamide 2.2 g/m2 IV q3wk
Complete excision if feasible; delayed surgery after chemo for initially unresectable tumors
40-50 Gy to tumor bed; for microscopic or gross residual disease after surgery

second_line_drugs.notes: VAC alternating with vincristine + irinotecan (VCR 1.5 mg/m2 + irinotecan 50 mg/m2 x5d); less toxicity
Ifosfamide 3 g/m2 IV x3d + doxorubicin 30 mg/m2 IV x2d for non-rhabdo soft tissue sarcomas

notes: Rhabdomyosarcoma is the most common pediatric STS. Low-risk RMS achieves >90% FFS with VAC. Multimodal therapy (chemo, surgery, RT) is standard.
guideline_changes: EpSSG 2025 released European standard care recommendations for childhood rhabdomyosarcoma
counselling: Rhabdomyosarcoma is the most common soft tissue cancer in children — it responds well to treatment
Treatment involves chemotherapy, surgery, and sometimes radiation over many months
Report any new lumps, swelling, or worsening pain immediately
Maintain good nutrition during treatment to support the child's recovery
Regular follow-up imaging is needed even after treatment completion
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric oncology center with multidisciplinary sarcoma team
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for chemotherapy initiation, surgical resection, neutropenic fever, or tumor-related emergencies (obstruction, compression)

diagnosis_name: Malignant neoplasm of kidney, except renal pelvis

first_line_drugs.notes: Radical nephrectomy with lymph node sampling; COG approach is surgery first then chemo
Actinomycin D 0.045 mg/kg IV + VCR 1.5 mg/m2 IV wkly x18 weeks for stage I-II FH
Add doxorubicin 1.5 mg/kg IV to VA for stage III-IV FH; regimen DD-4A per COG protocol

second_line_drugs.notes: Actinomycin D + VCR x4-6 weeks pre-op; SIOP approach for large/bilateral tumors
Cyclophosphamide, etoposide + vincristine, doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide alternating; for anaplastic histology

notes: Wilms tumor has >90% overall survival for favorable histology. LOH at 1p/16q indicates worse prognosis and need for more intensive chemotherapy.
counselling: Wilms tumor (kidney cancer) has an excellent cure rate — over 90% for early-stage disease
Surgery to remove the kidney is the main treatment, followed by chemotherapy
The child can live a normal life with one kidney — protect the remaining kidney from injury
Report abdominal swelling, blood in urine, or fever immediately
Follow-up imaging (ultrasound/CT) will continue for several years after treatment
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric oncology center with pediatric surgical capability
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for nephrectomy and chemotherapy initiation; admit urgently for tumor rupture, hemorrhage, or bilateral tumor management

investigations.name: CT or MRI abdomen with contrast

first_line_drugs.notes: Congenital: 5-10 mcg/kg/day SC; cyclic/idiopathic: 1-5 mcg/kg/day SC; adjust to ANC >1000
Cefepime 50 mg/kg IV q8h for neutropenic fever (ANC <500 + temp >=38.3C); start empirically

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg SC single dose (max 6 mg) for chemotherapy-induced neutropenia; one dose per cycle
0.4 g/kg IV monthly for autoimmune neutropenia with recurrent severe infections

notes: Monitor CBC twice weekly during G-CSF initiation. Congenital severe neutropenia (Kostmann) requires long-term G-CSF; watch for MDS/AML transformation.
counselling: Low neutrophil count makes the child vulnerable to infections — avoid crowded places
Wash hands frequently and maintain good oral hygiene
Seek emergency care immediately for any fever above 38C — do not wait
Avoid raw/uncooked food and unpasteurized milk during severe neutropenia
G-CSF injection helps the body make more white blood cells — give as directed
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric hematologist if congenital neutropenia suspected, ANC persistently <500, or recurrent serious infections
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for neutropenic fever (ANC <500 + temp >=38.3C), severe oral/perianal infection, or suspected serious bacterial infection

investigations.name: Bone marrow aspirate
investigations.indication: If congenital or persistent severe neutropenia

first_line_drugs.notes: 150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 6g/day) for 3-4 weeks
40 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 2.7g/day); use if MRSA prevalence >=10%
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 12g/day); low MRSA areas

second_line_drugs.notes: 60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 4g/day); for confirmed MRSA
100-150 mg/kg/day PO divided q8h (max 4g/day); oral switch

notes: S. aureus is most common pathogen; Kingella kingae predominates in children <3yr. Transition to oral therapy guided by clinical response, typically after 3-5 days IV.
guideline_changes: 4-month shorter regimens now considered for non-severe pediatric cases
counselling: Your child has a bone infection that needs several weeks of antibiotics to clear completely
Do not stop antibiotics early even if the child feels better — incomplete treatment causes relapse
Keep the affected limb rested and elevated initially
Return if fever returns, swelling worsens, or the child refuses to use the limb
Follow-up blood tests (CRP) will be done to confirm the infection is clearing
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric orthopedic surgeon if abscess needs drainage, if septic arthritis coexists, or if chronic osteomyelitis with sequestrum
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for IV antibiotics (initial 3-5 days minimum), surgical drainage if abscess present, sepsis, or inability to tolerate oral antibiotics

investigations.indication: Gold standard for extent of infection

10-20 mg/kg IV q8h for 7-10 days; for immunocompromised

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg PO tid (max 1g/dose) for 7 days; age >=12yr
Limited pediatric data; 500 mg PO tid for adolescents >=12yr

notes: Immunocompromised children require IV acyclovir. Treat within 72h of rash onset for best efficacy.
source: AAP Red Book 2024, NIH OI Guidelines
counselling: Herpes zoster (shingles) in children is usually milder than in adults
Give acyclovir as prescribed — best results when started within 72 hours of rash
Keep the rash clean and dry; do not apply any home remedies
The child is contagious to those who have not had chickenpox — keep away from pregnant women and newborns
Pain and itching usually resolve within 2-3 weeks as the rash heals
referral_criteria: Refer to ophthalmologist if eye involvement (herpes zoster ophthalmicus), or to infectious disease specialist if immunocompromised
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if immunocompromised child, disseminated zoster, CNS involvement, or severe pain requiring IV therapy

20 mg/kg IV q8h for 14-21 days; neonatal HSV or encephalitis

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg PO bid (max 1g/dose); children >=12yr, recurrent HSV
40-60 mg/kg IV q8h; acyclovir-resistant HSV in immunocompromised

notes: Neonatal HSV requires high-dose IV acyclovir for 14-21 days. Ensure adequate hydration to prevent acyclovir crystalluria.
counselling: HSV infection can recur — triggers include fever, sun exposure, and stress
Keep the sores clean; wash hands after touching them to prevent spread
Complete the full course of acyclovir even if sores start healing
Do not kiss the child on the mouth or share utensils when sores are active
For neonatal herpes, hospital treatment is essential — it can be life-threatening in newborns
referral_criteria: Refer to infectious disease specialist for neonatal HSV, HSV encephalitis, or recurrent severe episodes; ophthalmologist for eye involvement

30-40 mg/kg/day PO/IV divided tid (max 1.8g/day); penicillin allergy

notes: Narrow antibiotic spectrum once culture and sensitivity results are available. Always obtain cultures before starting empiric therapy when feasible.
counselling: Complete the full course of antibiotics even if the child feels better after 2-3 days
Give probiotics (curd/yogurt) alongside antibiotics to prevent loose stools
Ensure adequate fluid intake and nutrition during the illness
Return if fever does not improve within 48-72 hours of starting antibiotics
Wash hands frequently and maintain hygiene to prevent spread
referral_criteria: Refer if no improvement after 48-72 hours of appropriate antibiotics, suspected deep-seated infection, or immunocompromised child
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for sepsis signs, toxic appearance, inability to tolerate oral medications, or immunocompromised status

first_line_drugs.notes: 20,000-120,000 IU IV based on severity; administer ASAP on clinical suspicion

counselling: Diphtheria is a serious but treatable infection — early treatment is critical
The child must complete the full 14-day antibiotic course
All family members and close contacts need prophylactic antibiotics and vaccination check
Watch for breathing difficulty, drooling, or neck swelling and seek emergency care immediately
After recovery, the child needs to complete DPT vaccination as per schedule
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to hospital with ICU facility; ENT referral if airway compromise
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all suspected diphtheria cases; ICU if airway compromise, myocarditis, or neurological involvement

notes: Begin empiric therapy immediately; meningococcal disease progresses rapidly. Close contacts require chemoprophylaxis with rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone.
counselling: Meningococcal disease is a medical emergency — the child is receiving urgent treatment
All close contacts (household, school) need prophylactic antibiotics within 24 hours
Watch for rash that does not fade on pressing (glass test) — this is a danger sign
After recovery, the child should receive meningococcal vaccine
Report the case to local public health authorities
referral_criteria: Refer immediately to pediatric ICU if septic shock, DIC, or meningitis with altered sensorium
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all cases — meningococcal disease is a medical emergency requiring IV antibiotics and close monitoring

The child can return to school 24 hours after starting antibiotics
Give soft, cool foods if the throat is sore; avoid spicy and acidic foods

Watch for joint pain, chest pain, or dark urine in the weeks after recovery and report immediately

hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if unable to swallow, severe dehydration, peritonsillar abscess, or signs of rheumatic fever

Sterilize bottle nipples and pacifiers daily by boiling

Get regular blood tests (CBC) as the medicine can lower blood counts temporarily
Hearing tests should be done every 6 months until age 3 years even after treatment

Report any rash, unusual bleeding, or feeding difficulty during treatment

counselling: ART medicines must be given every day at the same time — never skip doses
The child can live a normal, healthy life with consistent treatment

Keep the child's HIV status confidential — disclosure should be age-appropriate and gradual
Ensure all routine vaccinations are given (avoid live vaccines if severely immunosuppressed)
