first_line_drugs.notes: 35-50 mg/kg/day PO divided tid (max 2.25g/day) for 10 days; invasive disease
25-35 mg/kg/day PO divided tid for 7 days; follow metronidazole to clear cysts

second_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg/day PO once daily (max 2g) for 3-5 days; age >=3yr
30-40 mg/kg/day PO divided tid (max 1.95g/day) for 20 days; luminal agent

notes: Always follow tissue-active agent (metronidazole/tinidazole) with a luminal agent to eradicate intraluminal cysts. Tinidazole has better tolerability and shorter course.
counselling: Complete the full course of metronidazole AND the follow-up luminal agent (paromomycin)
The child may feel nauseous with metronidazole — give with food
Ensure safe drinking water — boil or filter water at home
Wash hands thoroughly before eating and after using the toilet
Follow up with stool test after completing treatment to confirm parasite clearance
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric gastroenterologist or surgeon if liver abscess detected, or if fulminant colitis suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for amoebic liver abscess, toxic megacolon, intestinal perforation, severe dehydration, or dysentery with systemic toxicity

investigations.name: Stool antigen test for E. histolytica

first_line_drugs.notes: 400 mg PO single dose (200 mg if <2yr); ~100% cure rate
100 mg PO bid for 3 days OR 500 mg PO single dose

second_line_drugs.notes: 150-200 mcg/kg PO single dose (max 12 mg); age >=5yr and >=15 kg
11 mg/kg PO single dose (max 1g)

notes: Single-dose albendazole is highly effective. Safe for children >=1 year per WHO MDA guidelines; same dose for adults and children.
counselling: Single dose of albendazole is usually enough — no need for repeat unless instructed
The child may pass worms in stool after treatment — this means the medicine is working
Wash hands before eating and after toilet; trim nails short
Wash all vegetables and fruits thoroughly before eating
Deworm all family members together; repeat deworming every 6 months in endemic areas
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric surgeon if biliary or intestinal obstruction suspected from heavy worm burden
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if bowel obstruction, biliary ascariasis, pancreatitis from worm migration, or heavy burden with respiratory symptoms (Loeffler syndrome)

first_line_drugs.notes: 400 mg PO single dose, repeat in 2 weeks (200 mg if <2yr)
100 mg PO single dose, repeat in 2 weeks

second_line_drugs.notes: 11 mg/kg PO single dose (max 1g), repeat in 2 weeks

notes: Repeat dose in 2 weeks is essential as drugs do not kill eggs. Treat all household members simultaneously to prevent reinfection.
counselling: Give the medicine now and repeat the same dose in exactly 2 weeks
Treat all family members at the same time to prevent reinfection
Wash all bed sheets, towels, and undergarments in hot water on the day of treatment
Have the child wear tight-fitting underwear at night to prevent scratching
Trim fingernails short and encourage handwashing before meals and after toilet
referral_criteria: Referral rarely needed; consider pediatric GI if persistent despite repeated treatment of entire household
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not required for pinworm infection

investigations.name: Scotch tape test (perianal)
investigations.indication: Detect eggs on perianal skin (early morning)

investigations.name: Stool examination
investigations.indication: Low sensitivity for pinworm (eggs rarely in stool)

first_line_drugs.notes: 400 mg PO single dose (200 mg if <2yr); 78-92% cure rate
100 mg PO bid for 3 days OR 500 mg PO single dose

second_line_drugs.notes: 11 mg/kg PO daily (max 1g) for 3 days
3-6 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO divided bid-tid; for anemia

notes: Hookworm causes iron-deficiency anemia; check hemoglobin and supplement iron as needed. Albendazole 3-day course improves cure rate for heavy infections.
counselling: Give albendazole as prescribed; a 3-day course may be needed for heavy infection
Iron supplement will also be given if the child is anemic — complete the full iron course
Always wear footwear outdoors — hookworm enters through bare feet
Wash hands and feet after playing in soil or mud
Repeat deworming every 6 months in areas where hookworm is common
referral_criteria: Refer to hematologist if severe anemia (Hb <5 g/dL) not responding to iron therapy, or if diagnosis is uncertain
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe anemia with cardiac symptoms, need for blood transfusion, or protein-losing enteropathy

investigations.name: Serum ferritin and iron studies
investigations.indication: Assess iron deficiency from chronic blood loss

first_line_drugs.notes: 400 mg PO single dose (200 mg if <2yr); broad-spectrum antihelminthic
100 mg PO bid for 3 days; broad-spectrum antihelminthic

second_line_drugs.notes: 35-50 mg/kg/day PO divided tid (max 2.25g/day) for 7-10 days; if protozoal suspected
200 mcg/kg PO single dose; if Strongyloides suspected, age >=5yr

notes: Obtain stool ova and parasites to guide targeted therapy. Empiric albendazole covers most helminth infections pending diagnosis.
counselling: Give the deworming medicine as directed — usually for 1-3 days
If worms are seen in stool after treatment, this is expected and normal
Ensure clean drinking water and thoroughly wash all fruits and vegetables
Practice good hand hygiene before eating and after using the toilet
Bring a stool sample for repeat testing 2 weeks after completing treatment
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric GI if parasitic infection persists despite two rounds of treatment, or if Strongyloides hyperinfection suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe anemia, bowel obstruction, Strongyloides hyperinfection, or significant malnutrition from chronic parasitism

investigations.name: Stool for ova and parasites (3 samples)

first_line_drugs.notes: 400 mg PO daily for 3 days (200 mg if <2yr); covers most helminths
100 mg PO bid for 3 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 200 mcg/kg PO single dose; Strongyloides, age >=5yr and >=15 kg
25 mg/kg PO tid for 1-2 days; for tapeworms and flukes

notes: Species-specific therapy is preferred once stool microscopy identifies the parasite. Extended 3-day courses improve efficacy for heavy infections.
counselling: Complete the full 3-day deworming course for best results
Different types of worms need different medicines — the stool test helps identify the right one
Wash hands before eating and after toilet; do not eat raw/undercooked meat
Keep the living area clean and ensure safe disposal of human waste
Repeat deworming every 6 months in endemic areas as reinfection is common
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric GI if Strongyloides suspected (immunocompromised risk), or if refractory to standard treatment
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for Strongyloides hyperinfection syndrome, bowel obstruction, or severe malnutrition

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg/day PO divided tid (max 750 mg/day) for 5-7 days; giardiasis
50 mg/kg PO single dose (max 2g); giardiasis, age >=3yr

second_line_drugs.notes: Age 1-3yr: 100 mg PO bid; 4-11yr: 200 mg PO bid; for 3 days
25-35 mg/kg/day PO divided tid for 7 days; pregnancy-safe option

notes: Tinidazole single-dose has 80-100% cure rate for giardiasis with better tolerability than metronidazole. Nitazoxanide also covers Cryptosporidium.
counselling: Give metronidazole with food to reduce stomach upset
The medicine may cause a metallic taste — this is normal and temporary
Ensure the child drinks only boiled or filtered water
Wash hands thoroughly before eating and after toilet use
Follow up with stool test after treatment to confirm the parasite is cleared
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric GI if recurrent giardiasis despite treatment (consider IgA deficiency), or if chronic diarrhea with malabsorption
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if severe dehydration from diarrhea, significant malnutrition, or Cryptosporidium in immunocompromised child

first_line_drugs.notes: 300/100 mg PO bid for 5 days; EUA age >=12yr and >=40 kg, within 5 days of onset
5 mg/kg IV day 1 (max 200 mg), then 2.5 mg/kg/day (max 100 mg) for 3 days; >=3 kg

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO daily (max 6 mg) for up to 10 days; hospitalized on O2/ventilator
2-4 mg PO daily based on age; hospitalized, EUA >=2yr
Weight-based IV single dose; MIS-C or rapid deterioration despite steroids

notes: Paxlovid is preferred for outpatient high-risk children >=12yr. Remdesivir 3-day outpatient course is second-line; most beneficial within 7 days of symptom onset.
guideline_changes: Remdesivir FDA-approved for children >=28 days and >=3 kg; Paxlovid EUA for >=12yr
counselling: Most children recover from COVID-19 with mild symptoms in 5-7 days
Give paracetamol for fever and ensure plenty of fluids
Watch for breathing difficulty, persistent chest pain, or inability to stay awake — seek emergency care
Monitor for MIS-C symptoms 2-6 weeks after infection: high fever, rash, red eyes, abdominal pain
Isolate the child for 5 days from symptom onset; masks for all household contacts
referral_criteria: Refer urgently if MIS-C suspected, oxygen saturation <94%, respiratory distress, or high-risk child (immunocompromised, cardiac disease)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for hypoxia (SpO2 <94%), respiratory distress, MIS-C, severe dehydration, or high-risk comorbidities

diagnosis_name: Influenza due to identified influenza virus

first_line_drugs.notes: <1yr: 3 mg/kg PO bid x5 days; 1-12yr weight-based PO bid x5 days (max 75 mg bid)
Single PO dose: 20-<80 kg 40 mg; >=80 kg 80 mg; age >=5yr, within 48h

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg (2 inhalations) bid for 5 days; age >=7yr
12 mg/kg IV single dose (max 600 mg); age >=6mo, unable to take PO

notes: Start antivirals within 48h of symptom onset; still consider beyond 48h in high-risk children. Oseltamivir is preferred due to widest age range and formulation options.
counselling: Give oseltamivir (Tamiflu) within 48 hours of symptom onset for best results
The child should rest at home and drink plenty of fluids
Keep the child away from school for at least 24 hours after fever resolves
Wash hands frequently and cover coughs/sneezes with elbow
Annual influenza vaccination is the best prevention — get it every year before monsoon/winter season
referral_criteria: Refer to hospital if respiratory distress, dehydration, altered consciousness, or high-risk child (asthma, immunocompromised, cardiac disease)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for respiratory distress, hypoxia, encephalopathy, myocarditis, or severe dehydration

investigations.name: Influenza PCR (nasopharyngeal)
investigations.indication: Confirm if rapid test negative but clinical suspicion high

first_line_drugs.notes: <1yr: 3 mg/kg PO bid x5 days; 1-12yr weight-based PO bid x5 days (max 75 mg bid)
Single PO dose: 20-<80 kg 40 mg; >=80 kg 80 mg; age >=5yr, within 48h

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg (2 inhalations) bid for 5 days; age >=7yr
12 mg/kg IV single dose (max 600 mg); age >=6mo, unable to take PO

notes: Treat clinically suspected influenza empirically when testing is pending or unavailable. Same antiviral approach regardless of virus identification status.
counselling: Start antiviral medicine (Tamiflu) as early as possible — even before test results return
Keep the child home, hydrated, and resting
Paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever and body aches
Do not give aspirin to children with viral illness (risk of Reye syndrome)
Annual flu vaccination recommended for all children above 6 months
referral_criteria: Refer to hospital if respiratory distress, high-risk child, or no improvement after 48 hours of antiviral therapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for respiratory distress, hypoxia (SpO2 <92%), dehydration, seizures, or immunocompromised status

investigations.name: Respiratory viral panel PCR
investigations.indication: If influenza test negative but viral illness suspected

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO/PR q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4g/day); first-line antipyretic
10 mg/kg PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day or 1.2g/day); age >=6mo

second_line_drugs.notes: Acetaminophen q4h + ibuprofen q6h staggered; refractory fever only

notes: Goal is child comfort, not normothermia. Investigate underlying cause; fever alone does not worsen disease course or cause neurological damage.
counselling: Fever itself is not dangerous — it is the body's way of fighting infection
Give paracetamol or ibuprofen only if the child is uncomfortable, not just for the number on the thermometer
Dress the child in light clothing; do not over-bundle
Encourage plenty of fluids — ORS, water, coconut water, dal water
Seek immediate care if fever persists beyond 3 days, child becomes drowsy, or rash appears
referral_criteria: Refer if fever persists >5 days without source, infant <3 months with fever >38C, or suspected serious bacterial infection
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit febrile infant <28 days (all), infant 29-60 days with ill appearance, fever with toxic look at any age, or fever with neutropenia

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: Screen for infection type (bacterial vs viral)

investigations.name: Urinalysis and urine culture
investigations.indication: Rule out UTI (common occult source in infants)

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg IV day 1 (max 4g), then 50-100 mg/kg/day; community-acquired
50 mg/kg IV q8h (max 2g/dose); if Pseudomonas concern or nosocomial
60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 4g/day); add if MRSA suspected

second_line_drugs.notes: 60-120 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 6g/day); ESBL or resistant organisms
300 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 16g/day pip component)

notes: Administer antibiotics within 1 hour for septic shock, within 3 hours for sepsis without shock. De-escalate based on cultures at 48 hours.
counselling: Sepsis is a life-threatening condition — the child is receiving emergency treatment
IV antibiotics must be given on time every time — do not delay or miss doses
Watch for any deterioration: increased drowsiness, poor feeding, fast breathing
Once the child improves, antibiotics may be switched to oral form to complete the course at home
Follow-up blood cultures will confirm the infection has cleared
referral_criteria: Refer immediately to pediatric ICU if septic shock, multiorgan dysfunction, or need for vasopressors/ventilation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all cases of sepsis; ICU admission for septic shock, respiratory failure, or multiorgan dysfunction

investigations.name: Lactate
investigations.indication: Tissue perfusion marker; elevated in shock

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg IV day 1 (max 4g), then 50-100 mg/kg/day; broad empiric coverage
60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 4g/day); add for MRSA coverage

second_line_drugs.notes: 60-120 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 6g/day); multidrug-resistant concern
7.5 mg/kg IV once daily; neonatal sepsis empiric with ampicillin
200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h; neonatal sepsis with gentamicin

notes: Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics when possible without delaying therapy. Fluid resuscitation with 10-20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid boluses for shock.
counselling: Your child has a serious bloodstream infection requiring hospital treatment
Antibiotics will be adjusted once blood test results identify the exact germ
Report any worsening: high fever spikes, cold hands/feet, drowsiness, or rash
The child needs to complete the full antibiotic course even after feeling better
After discharge, follow up within 1 week for repeat blood tests
referral_criteria: Refer to PICU if hemodynamic instability, need for vasopressors, respiratory support, or multiorgan failure
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all cases; PICU for septic shock, altered consciousness, respiratory failure, or DIC

first_line_drugs.notes: 250,000-400,000 U/kg/day IV divided q4-6h (max 24MU/day)
100 mg/kg/day IV divided q12h (max 4g/day)

second_line_drugs.notes: 40 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 2.7g/day); add for toxin suppression in GAS
60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 4g/day); severe penicillin allergy

notes: Add clindamycin to penicillin for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome to suppress toxin production. Group A strep remains universally penicillin-susceptible.
counselling: Streptococcal sepsis is a serious infection requiring IV antibiotics in hospital
The child will be closely monitored — report any worsening symptoms to the nurses immediately
All prescribed antibiotics must be completed — incomplete treatment risks relapse
After discharge, watch for recurrent fever, joint pain, or skin changes
Good wound care and hygiene prevent reinfection
referral_criteria: Refer to PICU for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome, necrotizing fasciitis, or hemodynamic instability
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all cases of streptococcal sepsis; ICU for toxic shock syndrome, necrotizing fasciitis, or multiorgan dysfunction

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg/kg/day PO divided tid (max 1.5g/dose); outpatient, non-purulent
30-40 mg/kg/day PO divided tid (max 1.8g/day); MRSA suspected or PCN allergy

second_line_drugs.notes: 8-12 mg/kg/day TMP PO divided bid (max 320 mg TMP bid); MRSA, purulent
100 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 6g/day); moderate-severe, inpatient
2-4 mg/kg/day PO divided bid (max 200 mg/day); age >=8yr, MRSA

notes: Mark and monitor erythema borders. Elevate affected extremity. Hospitalize if systemic toxicity, rapid spread, or failed outpatient therapy.
counselling: Give antibiotics exactly as prescribed — complete the full course
Mark the border of the redness with a pen and watch if it spreads beyond the mark
Keep the affected area elevated to reduce swelling
Apply warm compresses for comfort if advised by the doctor
Return if redness spreads rapidly, fever develops, or streaking appears — this needs urgent review
referral_criteria: Refer to surgeon if abscess formation needing incision and drainage, or if necrotizing fasciitis suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if rapid spread, systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia), facial cellulitis, periorbital/orbital cellulitis, or failed outpatient antibiotics

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO (max 300 mg); for 9-12 months total
15-20 mg/kg/day PO (max 600 mg); for 9-12 months total
30-40 mg/kg/day PO (max 2g); intensive phase 2 months
15-25 mg/kg/day PO (max 2.5g); intensive phase 2 months

second_line_drugs.notes: 15-20 mg/kg/day PO (max 750 mg); MDR-TB or ethambutol intolerance
15-30 mg/kg/day IV/IM (max 1g); MDR-TB intensive phase

notes: Miliary TB requires 9-12 months of therapy (2HRZE + 7-10HR). Add pyridoxine 1-2 mg/kg/day to prevent isoniazid neuropathy.
guideline_changes: ATS/CDC/ERS/IDSA 2024 update supports fluoroquinolone-containing regimens for drug-resistant TB
counselling: Miliary TB is a serious form of TB requiring 9-12 months of daily medicine — never skip doses
Give all 4 medicines together on empty stomach in the morning
Give pyridoxine (vitamin B6) daily to prevent nerve damage from isoniazid
Watch for jaundice (yellow eyes), rash, or vision changes and report immediately
The child is not infectious after 2 weeks of effective treatment — can return to school
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist or TB specialist; urgent referral if CNS involvement or drug resistance suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe respiratory distress, CNS involvement (TB meningitis), initiation of therapy in sick child, or drug-resistant TB

investigations.name: Sputum/gastric aspirate for AFB and GeneXpert
investigations.indication: Confirm TB and check rifampicin resistance

investigations.name: Mantoux test/IGRA
investigations.indication: Supportive evidence (may be negative in miliary TB)

investigations.name: Fundoscopy
investigations.indication: Choroidal tubercles pathognomonic for miliary TB

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO (max 300 mg); for 12 months total
20-30 mg/kg/day PO (max 600 mg); higher dose for CNS penetration, 12 months
30-40 mg/kg/day PO (max 2g); intensive phase 2 months
15-25 mg/kg/day PO (max 2.5g); intensive phase 2 months

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.6 mg/kg/day IV for 2-4 wk, then taper over 4 wk; adjunctive for TBM
15-20 mg/kg/day PO (max 1g); good CNS penetration, for resistant TB

notes: TB meningitis requires 12 months therapy and adjunctive corticosteroids to reduce mortality. Higher-dose rifampicin improves CNS penetration.
guideline_changes: ATS/CDC/ERS/IDSA 2024 update on drug-resistant TB regimens
counselling: TB meningitis needs 12 months of treatment — stopping early can be fatal
The child will also receive steroids initially to reduce brain swelling
Give pyridoxine daily with isoniazid
Watch for worsening headache, vomiting, drowsiness, or seizures and rush to hospital
Hearing and vision tests are recommended during and after treatment
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to pediatric neurologist and TB specialist; neurosurgery if hydrocephalus requiring VP shunt
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all cases of TB meningitis; ICU for altered consciousness, seizures, or raised ICP

investigations.name: CSF analysis
investigations.indication: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, high protein, low glucose

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO (max 300 mg)
15-20 mg/kg/day PO (max 600 mg)
30-40 mg/kg/day PO (max 2g); intensive phase 2 months
15-25 mg/kg/day PO (max 2.5g); intensive phase 2 months

second_line_drugs.notes: 15-20 mg/kg/day PO (max 750 mg); MDR-TB or drug intolerance
10 mg/kg PO/IV bid (max 600 mg bid); MDR/XDR-TB

notes: Standard 2HRZE/4HR for 6 months; non-severe TB in children 3mo-16yr may use 4-month regimen per WHO 2022. Add pyridoxine supplementation with isoniazid.
guideline_changes: 4-month regimen (2HRZ(E)/2HR) for non-severe TB in children 3mo-16yr per WHO 2022
counselling: Complete the full 6-month TB treatment course — even if the child feels better after 2 months
Give all medicines together on empty stomach in the morning
Give pyridoxine (vitamin B6) daily to prevent side effects of isoniazid
Report jaundice, persistent vomiting, vision changes, or joint pain immediately
The child can attend school after 2 weeks of treatment if feeling well
referral_criteria: Refer to TB specialist if drug resistance suspected, extrapulmonary sites requiring surgical intervention (spine, lymph node), or treatment failure
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for spinal TB with cord compression, pericardial TB, renal TB with obstructive uropathy, or severe systemic illness

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4g/day); first-line for fever/pain
10 mg/kg PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); ONLY after dengue is ruled out

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-7 mg/kg PO bid (max 1g/day); persistent arthralgia after excluding dengue
ORS ad libitum; prevent dehydration during febrile phase

notes: Use only acetaminophen until dengue is ruled out (co-endemic areas). NSAIDs may be added for persistent arthralgia once dengue is excluded.
guideline_changes: FDA approved Ixchiq (live attenuated vaccine) Nov 2023 for adults >=18yr; no pediatric approval yet
counselling: Chikungunya is a viral illness — antibiotics will not help
Give only paracetamol for fever until dengue is ruled out (no ibuprofen/aspirin initially)
Joint pain may persist for weeks to months — this is common in chikungunya
Ensure adequate fluid intake; ORS, coconut water, and soups are helpful
Use mosquito nets and repellents to prevent spread to other family members
referral_criteria: Refer if severe joint involvement causing immobility, persistent arthritis >3 months, encephalitis, or neonatal chikungunya
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for encephalitis, myocarditis, severe dehydration, neonatal chikungunya, or hemorrhagic manifestations

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4g/day); fever and pain control
ORS or isotonic fluids; maintain hydration, monitor daily from day 3

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-7 mL/kg/hr for 1-2h, then reduce; for dehydration or rising hematocrit
5-7 mL/kg/hr for 1-2h, then reduce; alternative crystalloid

notes: Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin due to bleeding risk. Monitor platelet count and hematocrit daily from day 3 until 1-2 days after defervescence.
guideline_changes: Dengvaxia and TAK-003 (Qdenga) vaccines available; treatment remains supportive
counselling: Give ONLY paracetamol for fever — do NOT give ibuprofen, aspirin, or nimesulide
Ensure the child drinks plenty of fluids — ORS, coconut water, fruit juices
Monitor daily from day 3 to day 7 — this is the critical period
Watch for warning signs: severe belly pain, persistent vomiting, bleeding, drowsiness
Use mosquito nets even during the day — dengue mosquito bites in daytime
referral_criteria: Refer urgently if warning signs: persistent vomiting, abdominal pain, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, hematocrit rising rapidly
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for warning signs, hematocrit rise >20%, platelet <20,000 with bleeding, severe dehydration, or altered consciousness

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 mL/kg bolus over 15-30 min; repeat as needed for shock resuscitation
15 mg/kg PO/PR q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day); avoid NSAIDs/aspirin

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 mL/kg IV; if no response to crystalloid after 2 boluses
10 mL/kg IV; only for severe bleeding with platelet <10,000 or active hemorrhage
5-10 mL/kg IV; for significant hemorrhage with Hgb drop

notes: Severe dengue requires ICU admission. Judicious fluid management is critical: overhydration causes fluid overload and pulmonary edema during recovery phase.
counselling: Severe dengue is a medical emergency — the child is receiving critical care
No ibuprofen or aspirin — only paracetamol for fever
IV fluids are carefully monitored — both too little and too much fluid are dangerous
The critical phase (day 3-7) requires close monitoring of blood pressure and blood counts
After recovery, do not skip the follow-up visit — platelet count and recovery need to be confirmed
referral_criteria: Refer immediately to PICU if dengue shock syndrome, severe hemorrhage, organ failure, or massive plasma leakage
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all cases of dengue hemorrhagic fever; ICU for shock, massive hemorrhage, organ impairment, or rapid hematocrit rise

investigations.name: Chest X-ray/USG abdomen
investigations.indication: Pleural effusion, ascites in plasma leakage

first_line_drugs.notes: 250,000 U/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 24MU/day) for 7 days; severe disease
2-4 mg/kg/day PO divided bid (max 200 mg/day) for 7 days; mild-moderate

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-75 mg/kg IV once daily (max 2g) for 7 days; severe disease alternative
10 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg), then 5 mg/kg/day for 2 more days; age <8yr

notes: Early antibiotic therapy reduces severity and duration. Avoid doxycycline in children <8yr due to dental staining; use azithromycin or amoxicillin instead.
counselling: Complete the full antibiotic course even if the child starts feeling better
Leptospirosis comes from contaminated water — avoid playing in stagnant water or floods
Watch for jaundice, dark urine, reduced urine output, or bleeding and seek immediate care
The child should rest until fever resolves completely
After flooding or heavy rains, wear footwear and avoid wading in dirty water
referral_criteria: Refer to hospital with ICU if Weil disease (jaundice + renal failure + hemorrhage), pulmonary hemorrhage, or myocarditis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe leptospirosis (Weil disease), acute kidney injury, pulmonary hemorrhage, hepatitis, or hemodynamic instability

first_line_drugs.notes: Weight-based PO bid for 3 days (5-14 kg: 1 tab; 15-24 kg: 2 tabs per dose)
Weight-based PO daily for 3 days; WHO-recommended ACT

second_line_drugs.notes: 2.4 mg/kg IV at 0, 12, 24h, then daily; severe malaria, then switch to oral ACT
10 mg/kg PO q8h for 7 days + clindamycin 7 mg/kg bid; if ACTs unavailable

notes: ACTs are first-line for all uncomplicated malaria. IV artesunate is mandatory for severe malaria; switch to oral ACT once patient can tolerate PO.
guideline_changes: WHO 2025 guidelines reinforce ACTs as first-line; triple ACT (artemether-lumefantrine-amodiaquine) under evaluation
counselling: Complete the full 3-day antimalarial course even if fever resolves on day 1
Ensure the child eats before taking the medicine — antimalarials should be taken with food
Use mosquito nets (treated with insecticide) every night
Watch for return of fever within 28 days — this may indicate treatment failure or relapse
If P. vivax diagnosed, an additional 14-day course of primaquine will be needed to prevent relapse
referral_criteria: Refer urgently if severe malaria: altered consciousness, seizures, severe anemia (Hb <5), respiratory distress, or jaundice
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe malaria (any danger sign), parasitemia >5%, age <6 months, inability to tolerate oral meds, or vomiting all doses

investigations.name: Peripheral blood smear (thick and thin)
investigations.indication: Gold standard for species identification and parasitemia

first_line_drugs.notes: Weight-based PO bid for 3 days per WHO dosing table
2.4 mg/kg IV at 0, 12, 24h, then daily until oral tolerated (min 24h)

second_line_drugs.notes: DHA 2.5 mg/kg + PPQ 20 mg/kg PO daily for 3 days (<25 kg)
Weight-based PO daily for 3 days; WHO-recommended ACT

notes: P. falciparum is the most lethal species; always assess for severity. Children <25 kg need minimum DHA 2.5 mg/kg/day per WHO 2025 guidelines.
guideline_changes: Triple ACT combinations under Phase 3 trials for areas with partial artemisinin resistance
counselling: P. falciparum is the most dangerous type of malaria — take all medicines exactly as prescribed
Take ACT (artemether-lumefantrine) with fatty food for better absorption
Watch for danger signs: confusion, seizures, very dark urine, jaundice, severe weakness
Return if fever comes back within 28 days — blood test will need to be repeated
Prevent future malaria: use insecticide-treated bed nets every night
referral_criteria: Refer immediately if severe falciparum malaria: cerebral malaria, severe anemia, acidosis, renal failure, pulmonary edema, or hyperparasitemia
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe malaria, hyperparasitemia >5%, any danger sign (prostration, seizures, respiratory distress, bleeding), or age <6 months

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO day 1-2, then 5 mg/kg day 3 (total 25 mg/kg base); if CQ-sensitive
0.25-0.5 mg/kg/day PO for 14 days (max 30 mg/day); after G6PD testing

second_line_drugs.notes: Weight-based PO bid for 3 days; if CQ-resistant P. vivax area
Single-dose radical cure; approved >=16yr only, requires G6PD normal

notes: Radical cure with primaquine (14 days) is essential to prevent relapse. Always test G6PD before primaquine; weekly dosing is alternative for G6PD-deficient patients.
guideline_changes: WHO 2025 supports single low-dose primaquine for gametocyte clearance where G6PD test unavailable
counselling: P. vivax malaria requires 14 days of primaquine to prevent relapse — this is critical
G6PD test must be done before starting primaquine to avoid a serious side effect
Give primaquine with food to reduce stomach upset
Watch for dark urine or sudden pallor during primaquine course — stop and see doctor immediately
Use bed nets and repellents consistently to prevent reinfection
referral_criteria: Refer if severe vivax malaria (rare), chloroquine-resistant vivax area, G6PD deficiency needing alternative radical cure, or splenic complications
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe anemia, splenic rupture, respiratory distress, or severe vivax malaria with organ dysfunction

investigations.name: Peripheral blood smear
investigations.indication: Confirm P. vivax (enlarged RBCs, Schuffner dots)

snomed_display: Plasmodium vivax malaria with rupture of spleen

first_line_drugs.notes: 2.2 mg/kg PO/IV bid (max 100 mg/dose) for 7 days
10 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg), then 5 mg/kg/day for 4 more days; age <8yr

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-75 mg/kg/day PO/IV divided q6h (max 4g/day) for 7 days; if resistant
10-20 mg/kg/day PO (max 600 mg); resistant scrub typhus

notes: Doxycycline is first-line for all ages; AAP supports short courses in children <8yr. Fever typically resolves within 48h of appropriate therapy.
guideline_changes: NEJM 2023 trial showed IV doxycycline + IV azithromycin combination superior for severe scrub typhus
counselling: Scrub typhus is spread by mite bites — fever usually improves within 48 hours of starting doxycycline
Look for a painless black scab (eschar) on the body — this helps confirm the diagnosis
Complete the full antibiotic course even after fever resolves
Wear full-sleeve clothes and use insect repellent when going into bushy or grassy areas
Watch for severe headache, confusion, or breathing difficulty and seek immediate care
referral_criteria: Refer to ICU if ARDS, multiorgan dysfunction, myocarditis, meningoencephalitis, or no response to doxycycline within 48 hours
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for organ involvement (hepatitis, AKI, pneumonitis), altered consciousness, hemodynamic instability, or infant with suspected scrub typhus

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/day PO (max 1g) for 5-7 days; preferred for uncomplicated
75-80 mg/kg/day IV (max 2.5g) for 10-14 days; severe or inpatient

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO bid (max 500 mg/dose) for 7-10 days; if susceptible, age >=1yr
60 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 3g/day); XDR typhoid

notes: Azithromycin is preferred empirically due to rising multidrug resistance. For travelers from Pakistan/Iraq, suspect XDR and use azithromycin or carbapenem.
guideline_changes: XDR typhoid (Pakistan, Iraq) requires azithromycin or carbapenems empirically
counselling: Complete the full antibiotic course — stopping early leads to relapse and drug resistance
Give plenty of fluids and a soft, easily digestible diet during fever
Wash hands thoroughly and ensure safe drinking water to prevent spread to family
The child can pass typhoid to others through stool — maintain strict hand hygiene after toilet
Typhoid vaccination (typhoid conjugate vaccine) is recommended for prevention
referral_criteria: Refer if suspected intestinal perforation, GI hemorrhage, encephalopathy, XDR typhoid, or no defervescence after 5 days of appropriate antibiotics
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for persistent high fever not responding to oral antibiotics, suspected complications (perforation, hemorrhage, encephalopathy), XDR typhoid requiring IV therapy

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to damp hair for 10 min, rinse; repeat in 7-10 days
Apply to dry hair, leave 8h or overnight, wash out; repeat in 7 days

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply to dry hair for 10 min, rinse; single application, age >=6mo
200 mcg/kg PO, repeat in 7-10 days; >=15 kg, refractory cases
Apply to dry hair for 10 min, rinse; single treatment, age >=6mo

notes: Permethrin resistance is increasing. Wet combing with nit comb every 3-4 days for 2 weeks is adjunctive. Treat all affected household contacts.
counselling: Apply the lotion exactly as directed — leave for the correct time then wash out
Repeat treatment in 7-10 days to kill newly hatched lice from surviving eggs
Comb hair with a fine-tooth nit comb every 3-4 days for 2 weeks after treatment
Wash bed sheets, pillowcases, and towels in hot water on treatment day
Check all family members and treat everyone who is affected at the same time
referral_criteria: Referral rarely needed; consider dermatology if refractory to two rounds of permethrin + manual removal
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalisation not needed for head lice

investigations.name: Dermoscopy
investigations.indication: If diagnosis uncertain — visualize lice and nits

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 mL IM on days 0, 3, 7, 14 (deltoid or anterolateral thigh in children)
20 IU/kg infiltrated into wound site; remainder IM at site distant from vaccine

second_line_drugs.notes: 1 mL IM on days 0, 3, 7, 14, 28; immunocompromised patients
40 IU/kg infiltrated into wound; where HRIG unavailable (skin test first)

notes: Post-exposure prophylaxis is nearly 100% effective if administered promptly. HRIG is given only once with the first vaccine dose; never repeat HRIG.
counselling: Rabies is nearly 100% fatal once symptoms appear — post-exposure vaccination is the ONLY lifesaving treatment
Wash the wound thoroughly with soap and running water for 15 minutes immediately after the bite
Get the first vaccine dose on the same day as the bite — do not delay
Complete all 4 vaccine doses on the scheduled days (0, 3, 7, 14)
Do not apply turmeric, chili, or any home remedies to the wound — only soap, water, and povidone-iodine
referral_criteria: Refer urgently to anti-rabies clinic or hospital for category III exposure (deep bite, multiple bites, bites on head/face/neck) — RIG is mandatory
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if wound needs surgical debridement, category III exposure requiring RIG infiltration, bite on face/neck, or symptomatic rabies (palliative care)

investigations.name: Clinical assessment of wound category
investigations.indication: WHO category I/II/III determines PEP regimen

investigations.name: No pre-treatment diagnostic test needed
investigations.indication: Treatment is based on exposure, not lab confirmation

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply neck to toes (include scalp in infants), wash off after 8-14h; repeat in 7 days
200 mcg/kg PO, repeat in 7-14 days; >=15 kg (emerging data for <15 kg)

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply in petrolatum nightly for 3 nights; infants <2mo
Apply daily for 2 consecutive days, wash off 48h after last application

notes: Treat all household members and close contacts simultaneously. Launder clothing/bedding at >=60C. Itching may persist 2-4 weeks post-treatment (not treatment failure).
guideline_changes: Oral ivermectin safety data expanding for children <15 kg per Lancet 2024 trial
counselling: Apply permethrin cream from neck to toes (include scalp in infants) and wash off after 8-14 hours
Repeat the application in 7 days — one application is not enough
Treat ALL family members and close contacts at the same time even if they have no symptoms
Wash all clothes, bed sheets, and towels in hot water (>60C) on treatment day
Itching may continue for 2-4 weeks after treatment — this does not mean treatment failed
referral_criteria: Refer to dermatologist if crusted (Norwegian) scabies, refractory to two rounds of treatment, or immunocompromised child
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for crusted (Norwegian) scabies requiring isolation and intensive topical + oral treatment, or secondary bacterial infection with sepsis

investigations.name: Skin scraping with KOH mount
investigations.indication: Visualize mites, eggs, or fecal pellets

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4g/day); fever and oral pain
10 mg/kg PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); age >=6mo, fever and pain

second_line_drugs.notes: Equal parts Maalox/diphenhydramine/lidocaine 2%; swish-and-spit for oral pain
ORS ad libitum; prevent dehydration from poor intake due to oral ulcers

notes: Self-limiting illness (7-10 days). Focus on maintaining hydration as oral ulcers may cause feeding refusal. No antiviral therapy is indicated.
counselling: HFMD is a viral illness that resolves on its own in 7-10 days — no antibiotics needed
Give cold fluids, ice pops, and soft foods — avoid spicy, salty, or acidic foods that sting the mouth sores
Watch for dehydration: fewer wet diapers, dry mouth, or no tears when crying
Keep the child home from school/daycare until fever resolves and sores are healing
Wash hands frequently — the virus spreads through saliva, blister fluid, and stool
referral_criteria: Refer if signs of neurological involvement (myoclonus, ataxia, encephalitis), cardiopulmonary compromise, or dehydration not responding to oral rehydration
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe dehydration, encephalitis, pulmonary edema (EV-A71 complication), myocarditis, or inability to maintain oral intake

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (usually sufficient)
investigations.indication: Characteristic oral ulcers + vesicles on hands/feet

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg PO q4-6h (max 75 mg/kg/day); fever management

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); age >=6mo, antipyretic/analgesic
Amoxicillin 45-90 mg/kg/day PO divided bid for bacterial pneumonia/otitis

notes: No specific antiviral therapy. Vitamin A reduces mortality and complication rates, especially in malnourished or hospitalized children. Isolate for 4 days after rash onset.
guideline_changes: CDC 2025 updated guidance on vitamin A: recommended for hospitalized children
counselling: There is no cure for measles — treatment focuses on managing symptoms and preventing complications
Give Vitamin A as prescribed — it reduces complications and death from measles
Keep the child isolated from others for 4 days after the rash appears
Watch for fast breathing, ear discharge, or drowsiness — these suggest complications
Ensure the child stays hydrated with ORS and nutritious foods
referral_criteria: Refer if pneumonia, encephalitis, severe malnutrition, or immunocompromised child with measles
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for measles pneumonia, encephalitis, severe dehydration, croup, severe malnutrition, or immunocompromised status

10 mg/kg PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); age >=6mo, pain and swelling

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply to swollen parotid glands for symptomatic relief
Maintenance rate isotonic crystalloid; if unable to tolerate PO due to trismus

notes: No specific antiviral treatment. Supportive care with hydration and soft diet. Orchitis complication in post-pubertal males requires bed rest and scrotal support.
counselling: Mumps is a viral infection — antibiotics are not needed
Give soft foods and plenty of fluids; avoid sour/acidic foods that stimulate saliva and worsen pain
Apply warm or cold compresses to swollen cheeks for pain relief
The child is contagious from 2 days before to 5 days after swelling appears — keep home from school
Watch for severe headache, stiff neck, or scrotal swelling in boys and seek medical care immediately
referral_criteria: Refer if meningitis (severe headache, neck stiffness), orchitis in post-pubertal boy, pancreatitis, or hearing loss
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for meningitis/encephalitis, severe pancreatitis, dehydration from inability to eat, or bilateral orchitis

10 mg/kg PO q6-8h (max 40 mg/kg/day); age >=6mo

notes: Self-limiting illness caused by HHV-6. High fever (up to 40.5C) resolves in 3-4 days followed by characteristic rash. No antiviral therapy needed in immunocompetent children.
counselling: Roseola causes high fever for 3-4 days followed by a rash once fever breaks — the rash is harmless
Give paracetamol for fever and ensure plenty of fluids
Febrile seizures can occur due to high fever — if it happens, place child on side and seek emergency care
The rash means the illness is almost over — no treatment needed for the rash
The child can return to daycare/school once fever-free for 24 hours
referral_criteria: Refer if febrile seizures occur, encephalitis suspected, or immunocompromised child with HHV-6 infection
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for prolonged febrile seizure, encephalitis, hepatitis, or immunocompromised child with severe HHV-6 disease

second_line_drugs.notes: ORS to maintain hydration during febrile illness

notes: No specific therapy for rubella or congenital rubella syndrome. Postnatal rubella is usually mild and self-limiting. Isolate for 7 days after rash onset.
counselling: Rubella in children is usually mild and self-limiting — no specific treatment needed
Give paracetamol if the child has fever or joint pain
Keep the child away from pregnant women for 7 days after rash onset — rubella can harm the unborn baby
The rash typically lasts 3 days and resolves without scarring
Ensure the child has received MMR vaccination to prevent future episodes
referral_criteria: Refer if congenital rubella syndrome suspected (cataracts, heart defects, deafness), thrombocytopenia, or encephalitis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for congenital rubella syndrome management, thrombocytopenic purpura, or encephalitis

Apply topically prn for pruritus relief

notes: AAP does not recommend routine acyclovir for uncomplicated varicella in healthy children. Reserve antiviral for immunocompromised, adolescents, or complicated disease.
counselling: Chickenpox usually resolves in 7-10 days — keep the child comfortable and hydrated
Do not use aspirin for fever — use paracetamol only
Trim fingernails short and keep sores clean to prevent bacterial superinfection
Calamine lotion can help with itching; oatmeal baths are soothing
The child is contagious until all blisters have crusted over (usually 5-7 days after rash onset) — keep home from school
referral_criteria: Refer if immunocompromised, neonatal varicella, encephalitis, pneumonitis, or severe skin superinfection
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit immunocompromised child with varicella, varicella pneumonia, encephalitis, neonatal varicella, or severe bacterial superinfection

notes: Treat only hemodynamically significant PDA; echo-guided assessment essential. Early closure <14 days not shown to improve outcomes per AAP 2025.
guideline_changes: AAP 2025 report: prophylactic treatment no longer recommended; acetaminophen now recognized option
counselling: PDA is a heart condition where a blood vessel that should close after birth stays open
Medicine (ibuprofen or paracetamol) is tried first to close the PDA
If medicine does not work, a small procedure or surgery may be needed
Watch for fast breathing, poor feeding, or excessive sweating during feeds — these suggest the PDA is significant
Many small PDAs close on their own and may just need monitoring

McGovern nipple or oral airway as emergency bridge to surgery

second_line_drugs.notes: Controversial; if used, stents left 2-6 weeks post-repair
Combined antibiotic-steroid drops x2 weeks post-op to reduce granulation

notes: Bilateral atresia is a neonatal emergency as newborns are obligate nasal breathers. Unilateral cases may be repaired electively.

counselling: Bilateral choanal atresia is a breathing emergency in newborns — the baby cannot breathe through the nose
An oral airway will be used temporarily until surgery can be done
After surgery, nasal drops and suctioning will be needed to keep the airway open
Watch for noisy breathing, feeding difficulty, or blue spells and report immediately
Follow-up endoscopy will be needed to check for re-narrowing of the nasal passages

counselling: CDH is a serious condition where the baby's organs are in the wrong position affecting breathing
Surgery will be done once the baby is stabilized — typically 2-3 days after birth

Surgery is safe with excellent outcomes — feeding resumes within hours after surgery
Before surgery, IV fluids will correct the baby's dehydration and electrolyte imbalance

Watch for vomiting after discharge — if persistent projectile vomiting returns, contact the surgeon

The baby will have a nasogastric tube to drain the stomach before surgery
After surgery, feeding starts slowly through a tube, then orally as the baby tolerates
Watch for vomiting, abdominal distension, or feeding intolerance after surgery and report
Screen for Down syndrome (30% association) and cardiac anomalies is recommended

Long-term follow-up with a pediatric surgeon and GI specialist is needed for years

investigations.indication: Cell count, glucose, protein, gram stain, culture

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: WBC count and markers of infection

investigations.name: CRP
investigations.indication: Inflammatory marker for monitoring treatment response

first_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg IV q8-12h (EOS); covers GBS and Listeria
5 mg/kg IV q24h (term >7d) or q36-48h (preterm); synergy with ampicillin

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg IV q8-12h for late-onset or suspected MRSA; monitor troughs
50 mg/kg IV q8-12h for late-onset suspected gram-negative sepsis
6 mg/kg IV q72h (<30 wk) for fungal prophylaxis in ELBW; or 12 mg/kg for treatment

notes: EOS: ampicillin + gentamicin first-line. LOS: vancomycin + gentamicin or cefotaxime. Obtain blood culture before antibiotics; stop at 36-48h if culture negative.
counselling: Baby has a blood infection that needs strong IV antibiotics
Blood test results will guide how long treatment continues
If blood cultures are negative at 36-48 hours, antibiotics may be stopped early
Continue breastfeeding; mother's milk has protective antibodies
Watch for warning signs after discharge: fever, poor feeding, excessive sleepiness, fast breathing
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU if preterm, critically ill, or not responding to antibiotics within 48 hours
hospitalisation_criteria: All suspected neonatal sepsis cases require admission for IV antibiotics and monitoring

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: WBC count, I:T ratio, platelet count

investigations.name: Procalcitonin
investigations.indication: Better early marker for sepsis than CRP

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture
investigations.indication: Rule out meningitis if clinical suspicion

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-20 mL/kg IV over 15-30 min; repeat up to 40-60 mL/kg total if responsive
0.05-0.3 mcg/kg/min IV continuous infusion for fluid-refractory shock
Empiric antibiotics within 1h of recognition; meningitis dosing if suspected

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-20 mcg/kg/min IV; alternative first-line vasopressor to epinephrine
1-2 mg/kg IV q8h for catecholamine-resistant shock; stress dose steroids
0.05-0.5 mcg/kg/min IV for warm shock with low SVR

notes: Start vasopressors if still shocked after 40-60 mL/kg fluid. Monitor lactate, UOP, and perfusion. Hydrocortisone for refractory shock.
guideline_changes: Epinephrine now favored equally with dopamine as first-line vasopressor per recent RCTs
counselling: Baby is critically ill with a severe blood infection causing shock; intensive care is needed
Multiple medicines through IV are needed to support blood pressure and fight infection
Baby may need a breathing machine and close monitoring of heart and blood pressure
Blood transfusions and other treatments may be needed
Recovery depends on how quickly treatment started; the NICU team is doing everything possible
referral_criteria: Immediate transfer to tertiary NICU with vasopressor and ventilator capability
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require immediate NICU/PICU admission; this is a life-threatening emergency

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Assess cardiac function and guide vasopressor choice

first_line_drugs.notes: 500 IU IM as single dose (2 separate sites); neutralizes unbound toxin
15 mg/kg IV load, then 7.5 mg/kg IV q12h x7-10 days; preferred over penicillin
0.1-0.3 mg/kg IV q1-4h PRN for spasms; or infusion 5-15 mg/kg/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO/IV divided q6h as adjunct for spasm control
50 mg/kg IV loading then 30-50 mg/kg/day infusion for autonomic dysfunction

notes: Neonatal tetanus is preventable with maternal TT vaccination. Monitor respiratory function closely; intubation may be needed for severe spasms.
counselling: Baby has tetanus infection from the umbilical cord; this is a serious illness
Treatment involves antitoxin, antibiotics, and medicines to control muscle spasms
Baby will need to be in a quiet, dark room as noise and light trigger spasms
Baby may need help with breathing during the illness
This is preventable with maternal tetanus vaccination; ensure all future pregnancies are covered
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU/PICU immediately; may need mechanical ventilation for severe spasms
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require ICU admission; life-threatening condition

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis
investigations.indication: Tetanus is diagnosed clinically; no specific lab test

first_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg IV q8-12h; covers streptococci and gram-positive organisms
5 mg/kg IV q24h; covers gram-negative organisms; synergy with ampicillin

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg IV q8-12h if MRSA suspected; adjust based on culture results
5-7.5 mg/kg IV q8h if anaerobic or necrotizing fasciitis concern
Rarely needed; only for necrotizing fasciitis or abscess formation

notes: Obtain wound and blood cultures before antibiotics. Full sepsis workup if systemic signs present. S. aureus and E. coli are most common pathogens.
counselling: Baby has an infection of the umbilical cord stump that needs IV antibiotics
Keep the cord area clean and dry; do not apply any home remedies or substances
Watch for spreading redness, foul smell, or pus around the umbilicus
If baby develops fever, poor feeding, or becomes lethargic, come immediately
Clean the cord stump with plain water; do not use antiseptics unless doctor advises
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric surgeon if necrotizing fasciitis suspected or abscess formation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if cellulitis extending >2 cm from umbilicus, systemic signs of sepsis, or any periumbilical crepitus

diagnosis_name: Disturbance of temperature regulation of newborn

first_line_drugs.notes: Servo-controlled to maintain axillary temp 36.5-37.4C; for hypothermia rewarming
Kangaroo mother care for stable infants; effective thermoregulation method
Apply at birth for preterms <32 wk without drying; reduces heat loss

second_line_drugs.notes: Warmed NS/D10W for severely hypothermic infants; slow rewarming 0.5C/h
Obtain cultures and start empiric antibiotics if hypothermia unexplained

notes: Hypothermia (<36.5C) increases mortality in neonates. Rewarm slowly at 0.5C/h. Unexplained temperature instability warrants sepsis workup.
counselling: Baby is unable to maintain normal body temperature; needs help staying warm
Kangaroo mother care (skin-to-skin) is one of the best ways to warm baby
Keep baby well wrapped; avoid bathing until temperature is stable
Hypothermia (low temperature) can be a sign of infection; doctor will investigate
At home, ensure room temperature is warm and baby is well dressed, especially at night
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU if severe hypothermia (<32C), not responding to rewarming, or suspected sepsis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if temperature <36C despite rewarming measures or if associated with other danger signs

investigations.name: Blood culture and CBC
investigations.indication: Sepsis screening if temperature instability is unexplained

investigations.name: Blood gas
investigations.indication: Assess for metabolic acidosis in severe hypothermia

investigations.name: Thyroid function test
investigations.indication: Rule out congenital hypothyroidism if persistent hypothermia

first_line_drugs.notes: NS 10-20 mL/kg over 15-30 min for shock; repeat as needed up to 40-60 mL/kg
D10W + 0.45% NaCl at maintenance rate; adjust Na+ by <=10-12 mEq/L/day
Assess latch, frequency, and adequacy; supplement if weight loss >10%

second_line_drugs.notes: Small frequent breastmilk/formula feeds if mild and tolerated orally
Check Na, K, BUN, Cr q8-12h during correction; slow correction for hypernatremia

notes: Hypernatremic dehydration common in breastfed neonates with poor intake. Correct sodium slowly (<=10-12 mEq/L/day) to prevent cerebral edema.
counselling: Baby is dehydrated, usually from inadequate feeding; fluids will be given through IV
Breastfeeding support will be provided to improve feeding technique
Monitor baby's urine output - at least 6-8 wet diapers per day means adequate feeding
Weight will be checked daily; expect steady weight gain after rehydration
If weight loss >10% from birth weight, bring baby to hospital immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist if hypernatremic dehydration (Na >150), seizures, or AKI
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if moderate-severe dehydration, weight loss >10%, hypernatremic, or hemodynamically unstable

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes (Na, K)
investigations.indication: Hypernatremic dehydration common in breastfed neonates

first_line_drugs.notes: D10W 2 mL/kg (200 mg/kg) IV push for BG <25 or symptomatic; then infusion
D10W at GIR 4-6 mg/kg/min; increase by 2 mg/kg/min q30min to max 12-15
Breastfeed or formula within 1h of birth for at-risk infants; q2-3h

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.5 mL/kg buccal massage for mild asymptomatic hypoglycemia; recheck in 30 min
0.02-0.03 mg/kg IM/IV (max 1 mg) for refractory hypoglycemia; temporary bridge
5-15 mg/kg/day PO divided q8h for persistent hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia

notes: Screen at-risk groups: IDM, SGA, LGA, preterm. Target BG >45 mg/dL after 4h of life. Max peripheral dextrose D12.5%; higher needs central line.
guideline_changes: Dextrose gel 40% increasingly adopted as first intervention for mild asymptomatic hypoglycemia
counselling: Baby has low blood sugar which needs to be corrected quickly
Feed baby frequently - every 2-3 hours - breastmilk is preferred
Blood sugar will be checked frequently by heel prick; this is necessary
If blood sugar stays low despite feeding, IV sugar water (dextrose) will be given
Watch for shakiness, poor feeding, or excessive sleepiness at home after discharge
referral_criteria: Refer to endocrinologist if hypoglycemia persists beyond 48-72 hours or requires GIR >12 mg/kg/min
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if BG <25 mg/dL, symptomatic, or requiring IV dextrose infusion

investigations.name: Blood glucose (serial)
investigations.indication: Bedside and lab glucose monitoring q30min-1h during treatment

investigations.name: Insulin level (critical sample)
investigations.indication: Collect during hypoglycemia episode to evaluate for hyperinsulinism

investigations.name: Cortisol and growth hormone
investigations.indication: Collect during hypoglycemia if persistent; rule out hormonal causes

investigations.name: Urine ketones
investigations.indication: Absence of ketones during hypoglycemia suggests hyperinsulinism

investigations.name: Blood gas
investigations.indication: Metabolic acidosis if associated organic acidemia

first_line_drugs.notes: Intensive: irradiance >=30 mcW/cm2/nm; use hour-specific nomogram for thresholds
D10W + electrolytes at 1.5x maintenance if dehydrated or TSB near exchange level

second_line_drugs.notes: Double-volume 160 mL/kg; if TSB exceeds exchange threshold despite phototherapy
0.5-1 g/kg IV over 2h for isoimmune hemolytic disease approaching exchange level

notes: Use AAP hour-specific bilirubin nomogram with risk factors. Measure TSB q4-8h during phototherapy. Rebound check 12-24h after stopping.
guideline_changes: AAP 2022 raised phototherapy thresholds by ~2 mg/dL for healthy term infants
counselling: Baby has jaundice (yellow skin) which is common; phototherapy (blue light treatment) will help
Continue breastfeeding frequently during phototherapy; do not stop breastfeeding
Baby's eyes will be covered with patches to protect them during light treatment
Jaundice usually resolves within a few days of treatment
After going home, watch for return of yellow color and bring baby for a check if it worsens
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist if TSB approaching exchange transfusion level, rapidly rising (>0.5 mg/dL/h), or onset within first 24 hours
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for phototherapy when TSB exceeds hour-specific treatment threshold per AAP nomogram

investigations.name: Total serum bilirubin
investigations.indication: Confirm level and guide treatment per AAP nomogram

diagnosis_name: Transitory neonatal disorders of calcium and magnesium metabolism

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mL/kg (10-20 mg/kg elemental Ca) IV slow push over 10-30 min with cardiac monitoring
50-75 mg/kg/day elemental Ca IV continuous or 80 mg/kg/day PO divided q6h

second_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mg/kg IV/IM q8-12h for hypomagnesemia; monitor for bradycardia
0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/day PO for persistent hypocalcemia from hypoparathyroidism

notes: IV calcium must be given slowly under cardiac monitoring to prevent bradycardia. Correct hypomagnesemia first as it impairs Ca correction.
counselling: Baby has low calcium or magnesium in the blood causing jitteriness or seizures
Calcium medicine will be given through IV slowly with heart monitoring
Oral calcium supplement will continue after IV treatment
Breastmilk or low-phosphorus formula helps maintain calcium levels
Follow up for repeat blood tests to ensure levels remain normal
referral_criteria: Refer to endocrinologist if hypocalcemia persists >1 week or hypoparathyroidism suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if symptomatic (seizures, tetany) or ionized calcium <4 mg/dL

investigations.name: Serum calcium (total and ionized)
investigations.indication: Confirm hypocalcemia; ionized Ca more accurate

investigations.name: PTH level
investigations.indication: Low in hypoparathyroidism, high in vitamin D deficiency

diagnosis_name: Neonatal withdrawal symptoms from maternal drug use

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.04-0.08 mg/kg PO q3-4h; titrate by 0.04 mg/kg per Finnegan score; wean 10-20%/day
Eat-Sleep-Console model: swaddling, low-stimulation, rooming-in, breastfeeding

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.1 mg/kg PO q6h; longer acting; may reduce treatment duration
0.5-1 mcg/kg PO q4-6h as adjunct for refractory symptoms
Loading 20 mg/kg, maintenance 5 mg/kg/day; for polysubstance or non-opioid withdrawal

notes: Eat-Sleep-Console approach reduces pharmacotherapy use by 50%. Prioritize rooming-in and breastfeeding (if not contraindicated).
guideline_changes: Eat-Sleep-Console (ESC) approach replacing Finnegan scoring at many centers; reduces pharmacotherapy need
counselling: Baby is showing withdrawal symptoms from medicines/substances mother took during pregnancy
Keeping baby with mother (rooming-in) and breastfeeding help reduce withdrawal symptoms
Swaddling, quiet environment, and gentle handling comfort the baby
Medicine may be needed if baby cannot eat or sleep well despite comfort measures
This is not the baby's fault; the goal is to make baby comfortable while symptoms resolve
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist if high Finnegan scores requiring pharmacotherapy; social work involvement for family support
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for observation minimum 3-5 days; longer if pharmacotherapy needed (ESC model)

investigations.name: Meconium drug screen
investigations.indication: Detects exposure over longer window than urine

investigations.name: Electrolytes
investigations.indication: Dehydration from poor feeding and excessive losses

first_line_drugs.notes: NS 10-20 mL/kg IV over 15-30 min via UVC; repeat up to 40-60 mL/kg total
0.01-0.03 mg/kg IV (0.1-0.3 mL/kg of 1:10,000) via UVC; repeat q3-5 min
Effective PPV is the most critical step; initiate within 60 seconds of birth

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-20 mcg/kg/min IV infusion for fluid-refractory hypotension
O-negative 10-20 mL/kg for hemorrhagic shock; uncrossmatched if emergent
1-2 mg/kg IV for vasopressor-resistant shock; adrenal insufficiency

notes: Identify shock etiology: hypovolemic, cardiogenic, or distributive. Effective ventilation is the single most important intervention in neonatal resuscitation.
counselling: Baby was very sick at birth and needed emergency resuscitation
Baby will be monitored closely in the NICU for several days
Multiple medicines and fluids through IV are needed to support blood pressure and circulation
Cause of the shock will be investigated and treated specifically
Recovery depends on the underlying cause; doctor will update you regularly
referral_criteria: Immediate NICU/PICU care; cardiology if cardiogenic shock suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require immediate NICU admission; life-threatening emergency

investigations.name: Blood gas (arterial)
investigations.indication: Lactate, pH, and base deficit to assess shock severity

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: Hemoglobin for hemorrhagic shock; WBC for sepsis

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Evaluate cardiac function and etiology of shock

snomed_display: Fetal and/or neonatal disorder of respiratory system

first_line_drugs.notes: 21-30% FiO2 for term, increase to 100% if no response; initiate within 60 sec
3:1 ratio with PPV; 90 compressions + 30 breaths/min if HR <60 despite PPV
0.01-0.03 mg/kg IV (1:10,000) via UVC if HR <60 after 60s of CC + PPV

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.1 mg/kg via ETT if IV access delayed; less reliable absorption
NS or O-neg blood 10 mL/kg IV over 5-10 min if hypovolemia suspected

notes: Effective ventilation is the single most important step. Evaluate for therapeutic hypothermia if moderate-severe encephalopathy develops.
guideline_changes: NRP 2025 updates: establish alternate airway before compressions; epinephrine after 60s of CC
counselling: Baby did not breathe well at birth and needed help; this can affect the brain
Baby will be closely monitored in the NICU for at least a few days
If brain injury is moderate-severe, cooling therapy may be offered within 6 hours of birth
Long-term follow-up with developmental specialist is very important
Most babies with mild birth asphyxia recover completely; outcome depends on severity
referral_criteria: Refer to tertiary NICU with cooling capability if moderate-severe encephalopathy; neurology follow-up
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require NICU admission; cooling facility needed for moderate-severe HIE

investigations.name: Blood gas (cord or early arterial)
investigations.indication: pH <7.0 and base deficit >12 indicate significant asphyxia

investigations.name: aEEG monitoring
investigations.indication: Continuous seizure monitoring; guides cooling decision

first_line_drugs.notes: Monitor size, watch for jaundice; resolves spontaneously in 2 weeks to 3 months
Serial TSB if jaundice develops; phototherapy per AAP thresholds if indicated

second_line_drugs.notes: If hyperbilirubinemia develops from hematoma reabsorption; per hour-specific nomogram
Monitor hemoglobin if large cephalohematoma; transfuse only if symptomatic anemia

notes: Do NOT aspirate cephalohematoma — risk of infection. Resolves spontaneously. Calcified rim may persist for months but is benign.
counselling: Baby has a blood collection under the scalp bone from birth; this is common and not dangerous
Do NOT squeeze or try to drain the swelling; it will absorb on its own in weeks to months
The swelling may feel hard at the edges as it heals; this is normal
Watch for increasing yellowness of skin (jaundice) and bring baby for bilirubin check if concerned
No special care is needed; handle baby normally
referral_criteria: Refer if rapidly enlarging, associated skull fracture, or coagulopathy suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if associated significant jaundice requiring phototherapy or symptomatic anemia from large hematoma

investigations.name: Serum bilirubin (serial)
investigations.indication: Monitor for hyperbilirubinemia from hematoma reabsorption

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Large hematomas may cause drop in hemoglobin

investigations.name: Skull X-ray
investigations.indication: Only if underlying skull fracture suspected

first_line_drugs.notes: Cool to 33.5-34.5C within 6h of birth; maintain x72h then rewarm 0.5C/h
Correct acidosis, maintain euglycemia, avoid hyperthermia, seizure monitoring

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg IV loading for seizures; additional 10 mg/kg if seizures persist
1000 IU/kg IV q24h x5d; neuroprotective adjunct under study; not yet standard
Cooling at 6-24h may provide small benefit if missed <6h window

notes: Eligible: >=36 wk GA with moderate-severe HIE. Requires continuous neuromonitoring (aEEG) and MRI at 4-7 days. Mild HIE not cooled outside trials.
guideline_changes: AAP 2026 clinical report: hypothermia may benefit if started 6-24h post-birth; mild HIE still not indicated outside trials
counselling: Baby's brain was injured due to lack of oxygen at birth; cooling therapy protects the brain
Baby will be cooled for 72 hours then slowly rewarmed; this is a proven treatment
Baby may have seizures which will be treated with medicines
Brain MRI at 4-7 days will help understand how much the brain was affected
Long-term developmental follow-up is essential; early intervention can greatly help
referral_criteria: Refer to tertiary NICU with therapeutic hypothermia capability within 6 hours of birth; neurology and developmental follow-up
hospitalisation_criteria: All moderate-severe HIE require NICU admission; therapeutic hypothermia must start within 6 hours

investigations.name: Blood gas
investigations.indication: pH, lactate, base deficit for severity assessment

diagnosis_name: Intracranial nontraumatic hemorrhage of fetus and newborn

first_line_drugs.notes: Maintain hemodynamic stability, correct coagulopathy, minimize handling
Head US at Day 3-7, repeat at 14d and 36 wk PMA; monitor for hydrocephalus

second_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mL/kg for symptomatic anemia from hemorrhage; maintain Hb >10 in acute phase
Neurosurgery consult for progressive post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus
25 mg/kg/day PO for temporizing slowly progressive hydrocephalus; limited evidence

notes: Prevention key: antenatal steroids, delayed cord clamping, minimal handling. Most grade I-II IVH resolve; grade III-IV carry higher neurodevelopmental risk.
counselling: Baby has bleeding inside the brain; this is more common in premature babies
Handle baby gently; minimize disturbances and loud noises
Head size will be measured regularly to check for fluid buildup (hydrocephalus)
Mild bleeding (grade 1-2) usually resolves on its own
Regular follow-up with brain scans and developmental assessments is important after discharge
referral_criteria: Refer to neurosurgery if progressive hydrocephalus; neurology for developmental follow-up
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require NICU monitoring; neurosurgery consultation for grade III-IV IVH

investigations.name: Cranial ultrasound (serial)
investigations.indication: Grade IVH; repeat at day 3, 7, 14, and 36 weeks PMA

investigations.name: Coagulation profile
investigations.indication: Rule out coagulopathy contributing to hemorrhage

investigations.name: Brain MRI
investigations.indication: At term-equivalent age for detailed assessment

snomed_display: Intracranial nontraumatic haemorrhage of foetus and newborn

first_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg IV loading over 10-15 min; additional 10 mg/kg if seizures persist (max 40)
Check and correct glucose, calcium, magnesium, sodium before/with anticonvulsants

second_line_drugs.notes: 40-60 mg/kg IV loading; maintenance 10 mg/kg IV q8h; fewer sedation side effects
20 mg/kg IV PE loading; second-line per ILAE if phenobarbital fails
0.05-0.15 mg/kg IV bolus then 0.1-0.4 mg/kg/h infusion for refractory seizures

notes: Phenobarbital is first-line regardless of etiology (ILAE 2023). Confirm seizures with aEEG/EEG when possible. Treat underlying cause concurrently.
guideline_changes: ILAE 2023 guidelines confirm phenobarbital as first-line; levetiracetam has lower efficacy (28% vs 80%)
counselling: Baby is having seizures which need immediate treatment to protect the brain
Seizures in newborns can look different from older children - subtle movements, lip smacking, or cycling movements
Blood tests will identify if low sugar, calcium, or infection is causing the seizures
Medicine (phenobarbital) will be given to stop and prevent seizures
Follow-up with a brain specialist (neurologist) and EEG testing is important
referral_criteria: Refer to neonatologist/neurologist; tertiary NICU for continuous EEG monitoring
hospitalisation_criteria: All neonatal seizures require NICU admission for monitoring and treatment

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Hypoglycemia is most common treatable cause

investigations.name: EEG or aEEG
investigations.indication: Confirm electrical seizure activity; guide treatment

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg/kg IV q8-12h; covers GBS, Listeria, and common pathogens
5 mg/kg IV q24-48h based on GA; synergistic with ampicillin for gram-negatives
Supplemental O2, CPAP, or mechanical ventilation based on severity

second_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg IV q8-12h; alternative to gentamicin if renal concerns or CSF coverage needed
Consider if concurrent RDS; poractant alfa 200 mg/kg via ETT

notes: Treat for 7-10 days; shorter courses (<=5 days) may suffice for culture-negative cases. Obtain blood culture before antibiotics.
counselling: Baby has a lung infection that started before or during birth
IV antibiotics will be given for 7-10 days; full course is important
Baby may need oxygen or breathing support during treatment
Blood cultures will guide the specific antibiotic choice
Baby will recover fully in most cases; follow up if breathing problems persist after discharge
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU if requiring mechanical ventilation or not responding to first-line antibiotics
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require admission for IV antibiotics; NICU if respiratory support needed

investigations.name: CBC with differential
investigations.indication: WBC count, neutrophil ratio for infection

investigations.name: Blood gas
investigations.indication: Assess respiratory status and oxygen needs

first_line_drugs.notes: Supplemental O2, CPAP, or MV; PEEP 6-8 cmH2O; rate 40-60; Ti 0.5-0.6s
100-200 mg/kg via ETT for severe MAS with high FiO2 requirement; may reduce ECMO need
20 ppm for PPHN complicating MAS (OI >15-25); wean as oxygenation improves

second_line_drugs.notes: High-frequency oscillatory ventilation for refractory hypoxemia or air trapping
Ampicillin + gentamicin empirically; MAS mimics pneumonia and culture often sent
For severe refractory respiratory failure; last resort after maximal medical therapy

notes: Supportive care is the mainstay; no specific therapy. Do not routinely suction non-vigorous infants. Most resolve in 3-5 days.
guideline_changes: Routine endotracheal suctioning of non-vigorous meconium-stained infants no longer recommended (since 2015 NRP)
counselling: Baby breathed in meconium (first stool) during birth, causing lung irritation
Baby needs oxygen and breathing support; most cases resolve in 3-5 days
In severe cases, special gas (nitric oxide) may be used to help the lungs
Baby will be in NICU until breathing normalizes
Full recovery is expected in most cases; follow up if coughing or fast breathing continues
referral_criteria: Refer to tertiary NICU if requiring iNO, HFOV, or consideration for ECMO
hospitalisation_criteria: All MAS cases require admission; NICU if supplemental O2 needed

investigations.name: Blood gas (arterial)
investigations.indication: Assess oxygenation and calculate oxygenation index

investigations.name: Blood culture
investigations.indication: MAS mimics pneumonia; rule out infection

first_line_drugs.notes: Loading 20 mg/kg IV/PO, maintenance 5-10 mg/kg/day; may increase to 10 if persistent
Nasal CPAP 5-7 cmH2O to splint airway and reduce apnea frequency

second_line_drugs.notes: Nasal intermittent PPV; may be more effective than CPAP for severe apnea
0.5-2 mg/kg/h IV infusion; for caffeine-refractory apnea; limited use

notes: Caffeine citrate (CAP trial) reduces apnea, BPD, and improves neurodevelopment. Rule out sepsis, anemia, and metabolic causes for new-onset apnea.
counselling: Baby stops breathing for short periods; this is common in premature babies
Caffeine medicine will help baby remember to breathe; it is safe
Baby will be on a monitor that alerts nurses when breathing pauses occur
Baby can go home when there have been no apnea episodes for 5-7 days
If baby turns blue or stops breathing at home, stimulate gently and call emergency
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU; investigate for underlying causes if apnea is new-onset in a previously well neonate
hospitalisation_criteria: All significant apnea episodes require hospital monitoring until event-free for 5-7 days

investigations.name: CBC, CRP
investigations.indication: Rule out sepsis as trigger for new-onset apnea

investigations.name: Cranial ultrasound
investigations.indication: Rule out IVH or CNS cause in preterm

investigations.name: EEG
investigations.indication: If seizures suspected as cause of apnea events

first_line_drugs.notes: 23-25G needle or 18-24G angiocath; 2nd-3rd ICS MCL; for tension pneumothorax emergently
Pigtail catheter (6-8 Fr) or trocar; connect to underwater seal -10 to -20 cmH2O suction

second_line_drugs.notes: For small asymptomatic pneumothorax; high-flow or 100% O2 may hasten reabsorption
Transition to HFOV if pneumothorax recurs on conventional ventilation

notes: Tension pneumothorax is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate needle decompression. Transillumination is rapid bedside diagnostic tool.
counselling: Air has leaked from baby's lung into the chest space, causing breathing difficulty
Needle or tube drainage may be needed to remove the trapped air
Baby will be on continuous monitoring and may need oxygen support
Most cases resolve within a few days after drainage
In rare cases, the air leak may recur; doctor will monitor closely
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU; pediatric surgeon if recurrent or complicated pneumothorax
hospitalisation_criteria: All symptomatic pneumothorax require NICU admission; tension pneumothorax is a life-threatening emergency

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Confirm pneumothorax; assess size and tension

first_line_drugs.notes: Nasal CPAP 5-8 cmH2O from birth; first-line respiratory support for preterm
Titrate to SpO2 90-95% (preterm) or 92-97% (term); avoid hyperoxia
Poractant alfa 200 mg/kg if FiO2 >0.3 on CPAP for RDS; via LISA if available

second_line_drugs.notes: Intubate if FiO2 >0.5-0.6 on CPAP, recurrent apnea, or respiratory failure
1-8 L/min for mild-moderate respiratory distress; heated humidified

notes: Identify underlying cause: RDS, TTN, pneumonia, pneumothorax, or CDH. Chest X-ray and blood gas guide management. Most TTN resolves by 72h.
counselling: Baby is having difficulty breathing which is common in newborns, especially premature babies
Oxygen or CPAP (gentle air pressure through the nose) will help baby breathe
If needed, surfactant medicine can be given directly into the lungs
Most cases of breathing difficulty improve within 2-3 days
Baby will remain in NICU until breathing comfortably on room air
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU; tertiary center if requiring advanced respiratory support
hospitalisation_criteria: All neonates with respiratory distress require admission for monitoring and support

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Identify cause: RDS, TTN, pneumonia, pneumothorax, CDH

investigations.name: Pre and post-ductal SpO2
investigations.indication: Screen for cyanotic heart disease or PPHN

snomed_display: Respiratory distress in early neonatal period

first_line_drugs.notes: Start 20 ppm when OI >15-25; wean by 5 ppm increments; avoid abrupt stop
Maintain preductal SpO2 >95%; target PaO2 60-80 mmHg; avoid hyperoxia
Optimize lung recruitment; target pH 7.35-7.45; avoid overventilation

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg PO q6h or 0.4 mg/kg IV over 3h; for iNO-refractory PPHN
0.33-0.99 mcg/kg/min IV; inodilator for PPHN with cardiac dysfunction
1-20 ng/kg/min IV; prostacyclin analog for refractory PPHN
For severe refractory PPHN (OI >40) failing maximal medical therapy

notes: iNO is the only FDA-approved pulmonary vasodilator for neonatal PPHN. 30% are iNO non-responders; sildenafil is best alternative.
guideline_changes: Sildenafil IV formulation and milrinone increasingly used for iNO-refractory PPHN
