counselling: Baby's lung blood vessels are not opening properly, making it hard to get oxygen
Special gas (nitric oxide) breathed through a tube helps open these blood vessels
This is a serious condition requiring intensive care
Most babies improve within 5-7 days with treatment
Follow-up after discharge for lung and heart health is important
referral_criteria: Refer to tertiary NICU with iNO capability; refer for ECMO if not responding to maximal therapy
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require NICU admission with mechanical ventilation and hemodynamic monitoring

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Confirm PPHN; estimate PA pressure; rule out structural heart disease

investigations.name: Blood gas (arterial)
investigations.indication: Calculate oxygenation index to guide therapy

diagnosis_name: Pulmonary hemorrhage originating in perinatal period

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.5 mL of 1:10,000 via ETT for vasoconstriction; may repeat x1
Raise PEEP to 8-10 cmH2O or switch to HFOV to tamponade bleeding
10-15 mL/kg to replace blood loss; maintain Hb >12 during acute phase

second_line_drugs.notes: 100-200 mg/kg after bleeding stops; restores surfactant inactivated by blood/protein
10-15 mL/kg FFP for coagulopathy; vitamin K 1 mg IV if not already given
50-100 mcg/kg IV for refractory hemorrhage; case reports only

notes: Life-threatening emergency. Increase MAP to tamponade bleeding, correct coagulopathy, and transfuse aggressively. Surfactant after bleeding stops.
counselling: Baby has bleeding from the lungs which is a serious emergency
Baby will need a breathing tube and ventilator to support breathing
Blood transfusion may be needed to replace lost blood
This is more common in very premature and sick babies
Treatment is aggressive but many babies recover; doctor will keep you updated
referral_criteria: Immediate NICU with ventilator support; may need tertiary transfer
hospitalisation_criteria: Life-threatening emergency requiring immediate NICU admission and mechanical ventilation

first_line_drugs.notes: Poractant alfa 200 mg/kg via thin catheter on CPAP; within 2h of birth preferred
Start 6-8 cmH2O immediately after birth; primary respiratory support
Betamethasone 12 mg IM x2 to mother 24h apart before preterm delivery

second_line_drugs.notes: Intubate-Surfactant-Extubate technique if LISA not available
Volume-targeted; TV 4-6 mL/kg if intubation required; minimize duration
20 mg/kg loading then 5-10 mg/kg/day; facilitates extubation and prevents apnea

notes: LISA reduces need for mechanical ventilation and BPD compared to INSURE. Poractant alfa 200 mg/kg superior to lower doses and beractant.
guideline_changes: LISA (less invasive surfactant administration) recommended over INSURE where expertise available
counselling: Baby's lungs lack a substance called surfactant needed for breathing; this is common in premature babies
Surfactant medicine will be given directly into the lungs through a thin tube
CPAP or ventilator support will help baby breathe until lungs mature
Antenatal steroid injection (if given before birth) has already helped lung development
Most babies improve significantly after surfactant treatment and gradually need less breathing support
referral_criteria: Deliver at center with NICU and surfactant availability; refer for antenatal steroids before preterm delivery
hospitalisation_criteria: All RDS cases require NICU admission until weaned off respiratory support

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Ground glass appearance with air bronchograms confirms RDS

investigations.name: Blood gas (arterial/capillary)
investigations.indication: Assess oxygenation and guide FiO2/surfactant decision

first_line_drugs.notes: FiO2 to maintain SpO2 92-97%; usually <40% FiO2 sufficient
5-7 cmH2O nasal CPAP if moderate respiratory distress; aids fluid clearance
D10W + electrolytes at maintenance; NPO until RR <60-70 to reduce aspiration risk

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-8 L/min heated humidified; alternative to CPAP for mild TTN
OG/NG feeds if RR 60-80 and unable to safely PO feed

notes: TTN is self-limiting; resolves within 24-72h. No proven pharmacotherapy (diuretics, salbutamol, steroids not recommended). Rule out other causes.
counselling: Baby is breathing fast because of fluid left in the lungs from birth; this is temporary
This condition resolves on its own within 1-3 days; no specific medicine is needed
Baby may need oxygen support and IV fluids until breathing normalizes
Once breathing rate comes down, breastfeeding can start
This is common after cesarean delivery and is not dangerous
referral_criteria: Refer to NICU if requiring >40% FiO2 or not improving within 72 hours
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if tachypnea (RR >60) with increased work of breathing or requiring supplemental oxygen

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Perihilar streaking, fluid in fissures; hyperinflation

investigations.name: CBC, CRP
investigations.indication: Rule out pneumonia if not improving

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO BID-TID; anticholinergic for detrusor overactivity; max 5 mg TID
CIC 4-6x daily from birth; cornerstone of neurogenic bladder management
0.05-0.1 mg/kg PO BID; antimuscarinic alternative if oxybutynin not tolerated

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.4-0.8 mg/kg PO daily; beta-3 agonist for refractory detrusor overactivity
6-12 units/kg intradetrusor injection; max 200 units; for refractory cases
Surgical bladder augmentation for patients refractory to medical therapy

notes: Proactive CIC and antimuscarinics from early life preserve upper tract function. Urodynamic studies guide therapy; monitor renal function and bladder compliance regularly.
guideline_changes: Beta-3 agonists (mirabegron) now approved for pediatric use
counselling: Child's bladder does not empty properly due to nerve problems; regular catheterization is needed
Clean intermittent catheterization (CIC) is the most important treatment; learn the proper technique
Medicine will help the bladder relax and hold more urine
Regular kidney ultrasounds are needed to protect kidneys from damage
Proper bowel management is also important; constipation worsens bladder problems
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist and nephrologist at diagnosis; urodynamics needed
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for febrile UTI, obstructive uropathy, or acute kidney injury

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Screen for hydronephrosis and renal damage

investigations.name: Urine culture
investigations.indication: Baseline and if symptomatic UTI suspected

first_line_drugs.notes: Timed voiding, bladder training, pelvic floor exercises; first-line for all pediatric bladder dysfunction
0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO BID-TID; anticholinergic for overactive bladder; max 5 mg TID

second_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mg PO daily (>5 yr); beta-3 agonist for refractory OAB
Intradetrusor injection 50-200 units for refractory detrusor overactivity
0.2-0.6 mg PO at bedtime for associated nocturnal enuresis; restrict fluids

notes: Behavioral modifications are first-line before pharmacotherapy. Screen for bladder-bowel dysfunction as combined management improves outcomes.
guideline_changes: FDA approval of beta-3 agonists for pediatric bladder dysfunction
counselling: Child has bladder problems that can be improved with regular toilet habits
Timed voiding (going to the toilet every 2-3 hours) is the most important step
Treat constipation as it makes bladder problems worse
Medicine helps the bladder relax; give it regularly as prescribed
Be patient; improvement takes 3-6 months of consistent effort
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist if not responding to behavioral therapy and antimuscarinics after 3 months
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for febrile UTI or acute urinary retention

investigations.name: Voiding diary
investigations.indication: Record frequency, volume, and incontinence episodes

diagnosis_name: Congenital obstructive defects of renal pelvis and congenital malformations of ureter

first_line_drugs.notes: Serial renal US q3-6mo for mild-moderate hydronephrosis; majority resolve spontaneously
Open or laparoscopic surgical repair of UPJ obstruction if worsening function or SFU grade 3-4

second_line_drugs.notes: Amoxicillin 10-15 mg/kg PO daily or TMP-SMX 2 mg/kg PO daily for high-grade HN
Robotic-assisted laparoscopic approach; emerging in older children with UPJ obstruction
Surgical correction for obstructive megaureter or ureteral malposition if symptomatic

notes: Many cases resolve spontaneously; surgery reserved for declining renal function or recurrent infections. Early diagnosis via prenatal US enables timely monitoring.
guideline_changes: Robotic-assisted laparoscopic pyeloplasty gaining wider pediatric use
counselling: Baby has a blockage or narrowing in the kidney drainage system found on ultrasound
Many cases resolve on their own with time; regular ultrasound monitoring is needed
Surgery is only needed if the kidney function is declining or infections occur
Antibiotic prophylaxis may be given to prevent urinary infections
Keep all follow-up appointments for ultrasound scans every 3-6 months
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist at diagnosis; nephrology if renal function declining
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for febrile UTI, worsening hydronephrosis with declining function, or surgical intervention

investigations.name: MAG3 diuretic renogram
investigations.indication: Assess drainage and differential renal function

investigations.name: Serum creatinine
investigations.indication: Monitor renal function in bilateral disease

first_line_drugs.notes: Target BP <50th-75th percentile; ACEi (enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO) or ARB preferred
Serial renal US, eGFR, urine albumin-creatinine ratio q6-12mo; Mayo Imaging Classification for risk

second_line_drugs.notes: V2-receptor antagonist; slows cyst growth in ADPKD; pediatric trials ongoing; adult dose 45-90 mg/d
Shown to slow total kidney volume growth in children with ADPKD; dose per age-based statin protocols
Emerging cyst-modifying agent; IMPEDE-PKD trial ongoing; not yet standard pediatric use

notes: BP control with RAS blockade is cornerstone of renoprotection. Novel disease-modifying agents (tolvaptan, metformin) are under active investigation in pediatric ADPKD.
guideline_changes: KDIGO 2025 first guideline on ADPKD; tolvaptan and metformin trials ongoing in children
counselling: Child has cysts (fluid-filled sacs) in the kidneys; this is a lifelong condition
Blood pressure control is the most important treatment to protect kidneys
Regular check-ups with blood tests and ultrasound are essential
Adequate water intake is important; encourage child to drink plenty of fluids
Genetic counseling is recommended as this condition can be inherited
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist at diagnosis; genetics counseling for family
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for hypertensive emergency, acute kidney injury, or infected cyst

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Monitor cyst burden and kidney size

first_line_drugs.notes: Urethroplasty at 6-18 months of age; technique based on severity (TIP, Mathieu, or staged)
Topical DHT cream or IM testosterone 2 mg/kg q4wk x2-3 doses preop for small glans/proximal hypospadias

second_line_drugs.notes: 250-1000 IU IM 1-2x/wk for 5-6 wk preop; stimulates local testosterone for penile growth
Bupivacaine 0.25% 1 mL/kg caudal block periop for analgesia; reduces opioid need

notes: Optimal surgical age is 6-18 months. Long-term follow-up is essential to detect stricture, voiding dysfunction, or recurrent curvature.
guideline_changes: 2024 EAU/ESPU Part II update on hypospadias surgical outcomes and follow-up
counselling: Child's urinary opening is not at the normal position; surgery will correct this
Surgery is usually done between 6-18 months of age when the baby is healthy
Do NOT get the child circumcised before surgery; the foreskin may be used for repair
After surgery, a catheter (tube) will drain urine for a few days
Follow-up visits are important to check for complications like narrowing of the new opening
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist by 6 months of age for surgical planning
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for day-care or short-stay surgery; overnight observation for proximal hypospadias repair

investigations.name: Karyotype
investigations.indication: Only if severe hypospadias with undescended testes (DSD evaluation)

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Screen for associated urinary tract anomalies in severe cases

first_line_drugs.notes: Cystoscopic incision of valves within 48h of birth once metabolically stable; gold standard
Urethral catheter or suprapubic catheter as initial decompression in unstable neonates

second_line_drugs.notes: Cutaneous vesicostomy if infant too small or unstable for primary ablation; temporary diversion
Oxybutynin 0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO BID for secondary bladder dysfunction post-ablation
Clean intermittent catheterization for persistent bladder dysfunction post valve ablation

notes: Prenatal diagnosis enables early intervention. Multidisciplinary follow-up (urology + nephrology) is essential to monitor bladder and renal function long-term.
guideline_changes: Miniaturized endoscopes (<2mm) now enable valve ablation in neonates >2 kg
counselling: Baby boy has valve-like flaps blocking urine flow; this needs urgent treatment
A catheter will be placed to drain the bladder immediately
Surgery (endoscopic) to remove the valves will be done once baby is stable
Kidney function needs lifelong monitoring as some damage may already have occurred
Child may need regular catheterization and medicines for bladder function long-term
referral_criteria: Immediate referral to pediatric urologist; nephrology co-management for renal function
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require admission at diagnosis for catheter drainage and stabilization before surgery

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Bilateral hydronephrosis, thick bladder wall, dilated posterior urethra

investigations.name: VCUG
investigations.indication: Confirms posterior urethral valves; shows dilated posterior urethra

investigations.name: Serum creatinine (nadir)
investigations.indication: Best predictor of long-term renal outcome; check after drainage

investigations.name: MAG3 renogram
investigations.indication: Assess differential renal function after stabilization

diagnosis_name: Renal agenesis and other reduction defects of kidney

first_line_drugs.notes: Yearly BP, urine albumin-creatinine ratio; serial renal US to confirm compensatory hypertrophy
Enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO if hypertension or proteinuria develops; target BP <75th percentile

second_line_drugs.notes: Losartan 0.7-1.4 mg/kg/day PO once daily if ACEi not tolerated; renoprotective
Moderate protein intake, adequate hydration, avoid nephrotoxins; no routine sports restriction per AAP

notes: Solitary kidney predisposes to hypertension and proteinuria over time. Ambulatory BP monitoring preferred; multidisciplinary follow-up for complex CAKUT.
guideline_changes: 2022 Italian consensus on congenital solitary kidney management published
counselling: Child has only one kidney (or a very small kidney); most people live normal lives with one kidney
Regular blood pressure checks and urine tests are important to protect the remaining kidney
Encourage adequate water intake; avoid dehydration
Contact sports with risk of abdominal injury should be discussed with the doctor
Medicine may be needed if blood pressure rises or protein appears in urine
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist for long-term monitoring; genetics if bilateral or syndromic
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for hypertensive emergency, acute kidney injury, or severe UTI

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Confirm solitary kidney; assess compensatory hypertrophy

investigations.name: Blood pressure (ambulatory)
investigations.indication: Annual ABPM for early hypertension detection

investigations.name: VCUG
investigations.indication: Screen for VUR in contralateral kidney if UTI history

first_line_drugs.notes: Surgical fixation by 6-12 months corrected age; scrotal or inguinal approach for palpable testes
For nonpalpable testes; EUA then laparoscopic exploration; may be staged (Fowler-Stephens)

second_line_drugs.notes: 250-1500 IU IM 2x/wk for 4 wk; limited role; not recommended by AUA for unilateral UDT
Nasal spray 1.2 mg/day for 4 wk; limited efficacy; not widely recommended

notes: Refer by 6 months if no spontaneous descent. Imaging is not recommended pre-referral; orchidopexy before 12 months optimizes fertility and reduces malignancy risk.
guideline_changes: AUA 2025 guideline update reinforces early orchidopexy by 12 months
counselling: Baby's testicle has not come down to its normal position in the scrotum
Surgery (orchidopexy) should be done by 12 months of age for best outcomes
Ultrasound or other imaging is usually NOT needed before surgery
Early surgery protects fertility and reduces cancer risk
After surgery, follow up to ensure the testicle remains in proper position
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist/surgeon by 6 months if testicle has not descended spontaneously
hospitalisation_criteria: Day-care surgery admission for orchidopexy

investigations.name: Clinical examination
investigations.indication: Primary diagnostic method; imaging not recommended pre-referral

investigations.name: Karyotype
investigations.indication: Only for bilateral nonpalpable testes to rule out DSD

investigations.name: Hormonal workup (LH, FSH, testosterone, AMH)
investigations.indication: Only for bilateral nonpalpable testes to assess testicular function

first_line_drugs.notes: Warm water soaks 2-3x daily; gentle retraction and hygiene; supportive care for mild balanitis
Apply to foreskin BID for 4-6 wk; for associated phimosis in children >3 yr

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply topically BID for 7-14 days if candidal balanitis suspected
Apply topically TID for 5-7 days for suspected bacterial infection; avoid neosporin in children
Cephalexin 25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided TID-QID for 7 days if refractory or strep-positive

notes: Most cases resolve with hygiene and topical therapy. Refer to urology if recurrent or refractory to exclude balanitis xerotica obliterans.
counselling: Child has an infection/inflammation of the foreskin/penis tip
Warm water soaks 2-3 times daily will help reduce swelling and pain
Do not forcefully retract the foreskin; gentle cleaning is sufficient
Apply the prescribed cream/ointment as directed
If symptoms return frequently, the doctor may recommend steroid cream or circumcision
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist if recurrent episodes or suspected balanitis xerotica obliterans
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if unable to pass urine (acute retention), spreading cellulitis, or systemic signs of infection

investigations.name: Blood glucose
investigations.indication: Screen for diabetes in recurrent balanitis in older children

first_line_drugs.notes: Apply to foreskin tip BID with gentle retraction for 4-8 wk; ~65% success rate
Apply to foreskin BID for 4-8 wk; alternative topical steroid with good efficacy

second_line_drugs.notes: Apply to foreskin BID for 4-6 wk; potent steroid for resistant physiologic phimosis
Dorsal slit or limited preputioplasty for failed steroid therapy; foreskin-sparing alternative
Definitive surgical option for pathologic phimosis, BXO, recurrent UTIs, or failed steroid therapy

notes: Topical steroids are first-line; treatment can be repeated if phimosis recurs. BXO is an absolute indication for circumcision at any age.
counselling: Tight foreskin is normal in young boys and usually loosens by age 3-4 years
Steroid cream applied to the foreskin tip twice daily for 4-8 weeks works in most cases
Gently retract the foreskin as far as it goes without force while applying cream
Surgery (circumcision) is only needed if cream does not work or skin disease (BXO) is present
Good hygiene with gentle retraction during bathing is important once foreskin is retractable
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist if steroid cream fails after 2 courses or pathologic phimosis suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for paraphimosis (emergency) or circumcision under general anesthesia

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: Screen for UTI if history of recurrent infections

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg IV/PO q6-12h for edema and fluid overload; avoid high doses if oliguric
0.06-0.3 mg/kg/day PO once daily for hypertension; max 5-10 mg/day based on age
Restrict fluid to insensible losses + urine output during acute phase; low-salt diet

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.25-0.5 mg/kg PO q6-8h for hypertensive urgency; short-acting for acute BP control
250-500 mg PO BID-TID for 10 days if active strep infection; eradication only, does not alter GN course
IV pulse 30 mg/kg (max 1g) x3 days for rapidly progressive/crescentic disease only

notes: APSGN is self-limiting; supportive care with diuretics and antihypertensives is mainstay. Immunosuppression reserved for crescentic disease on biopsy.
guideline_changes: Updated CDC clinical guidelines for post-streptococcal GN published
counselling: Child's kidneys are inflamed, usually after a throat or skin infection
Restrict salt and water intake during the swelling phase
Blood pressure medicine and water pills will help control swelling and BP
This condition usually heals completely in 2-4 weeks
Follow up with urine and blood tests; protein in urine should disappear within months
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist if rapidly progressive course, persistent low complement >8 weeks, or nephrotic-range proteinuria
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if hypertensive emergency, significant fluid overload, oliguria, or rapidly deteriorating renal function

investigations.name: Urinalysis with microscopy
investigations.indication: Dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts confirm glomerulonephritis

investigations.name: Serum complement C3 and C4
investigations.indication: Low C3 with normal C4 typical of APSGN

first_line_drugs.notes: 2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) daily x4 wk, then 1.5 mg/kg (max 50 mg) alt-day x4 wk
Albumin 1 g/kg IV over 2-4h with furosemide 1 mg/kg IV for severe edema/anasarca

second_line_drugs.notes: 2 mg/kg/day PO for 8-12 wk for frequent relapsers; cumulative max 168 mg/kg
4-6 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; for steroid-dependent or steroid-resistant NS; monitor trough levels
1200 mg/m2/day PO divided BID (max 2 g/day); for SDNS/FRNS; continue >=12 months
375 mg/m2 IV x1-2 doses; for SDNS refractory to standard steroid-sparing agents

notes: 85-90% of children achieve remission with steroids. Steroid-sparing agents selected based on toxicity profile, adherence, and relapse pattern.
guideline_changes: KDIGO 2025 first dedicated guideline for nephrotic syndrome in children; rituximab now recommended
counselling: Child's kidneys are leaking protein into urine causing body swelling
Steroid medicine (prednisolone) is the main treatment; follow the dose schedule carefully
Check urine with dipstick daily at home; record results in a diary
Restrict salt in diet during active disease; no need to restrict protein
Relapses are common (60-70% of children relapse); come back if swelling returns or urine shows protein
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist if steroid-resistant (no remission by 4 weeks), frequent relapses (>2/6 months), or steroid-dependent
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe edema/anasarca, respiratory distress from pleural effusion, peritonitis, or thrombosis

investigations.name: Complement C3, C4
investigations.indication: Normal in MCNS; low suggests other glomerulopathy

first_line_drugs.notes: IV pulse 30 mg/kg/day (max 1 g) for 3 consecutive days; then oral pred 1-2 mg/kg/day taper
IV pulse 500-750 mg/m2 monthly x6 or PO 2 mg/kg/day x8-12 wk; for induction

second_line_drugs.notes: 375 mg/m2 IV weekly x4 for ANCA-associated RPGN; non-inferior to cyclophosphamide
5-7 exchanges over 7-14 days for anti-GBM disease or severe ANCA vasculitis with DAH
1200 mg/m2/day PO divided BID for maintenance after induction; alternative to azathioprine
1-2 mg/kg/day PO for maintenance therapy after induction remission achieved

notes: Urgent renal biopsy is essential to guide therapy. Aggressive induction immunosuppression has markedly improved outcomes; dialysis-dependent AKI predicts poor prognosis.
guideline_changes: Rituximab increasingly used in pediatric ANCA-associated RPGN
counselling: Child has rapidly worsening kidney disease that needs urgent treatment
Strong medicines to suppress the immune system are needed to stop kidney damage
Kidney biopsy (taking a small piece of kidney with a needle) is needed to guide treatment
Treatment requires close monitoring with frequent blood tests
With early and aggressive treatment, kidney function can be preserved in many cases
referral_criteria: Urgent referral to pediatric nephrologist; renal biopsy within 48 hours
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require admission for urgent renal biopsy, pulse immunosuppression, and monitoring

investigations.name: Renal biopsy
investigations.indication: Urgent; crescentic GN classification guides therapy

investigations.name: Serum creatinine (serial)
investigations.indication: Monitor rate of renal function decline

first_line_drugs.notes: Serial urinalysis q3-6mo; monitor BP, proteinuria, serum creatinine; reassurance if isolated
Enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO if proteinuria >0.5 g/day develops; renoprotective

second_line_drugs.notes: Losartan 0.7-1.4 mg/kg/day PO if ACEi not tolerated; for proteinuric hematuria
Adjunctive therapy for IgA nephropathy with proteinuria >1 g/day; per KDIGO GN guidelines

notes: Isolated microscopic hematuria without proteinuria has good prognosis. Refer to nephrology if proteinuria, hypertension, or family history of renal disease.
counselling: Blood in urine is being investigated; most causes in children are not dangerous
Regular check-ups with urine tests every 3-6 months are needed
If protein starts appearing in urine or blood pressure rises, medicine may be needed
Vigorous exercise may temporarily worsen visible blood in urine; this is usually harmless
Report any swelling of face/legs, tea-colored urine, or high BP immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist if proteinuria develops, hypertension, family history of renal disease, or hearing loss (Alport syndrome)
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if gross hematuria with clots causing retention, or associated nephritic syndrome features

investigations.name: Urine calcium/creatinine ratio
investigations.indication: Hypercalciuria is common cause of isolated hematuria

investigations.name: Serum creatinine, C3, C4
investigations.indication: Assess renal function and complement levels

investigations.name: Family urine screening
investigations.indication: Thin basement membrane disease and Alport syndrome

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg IV/PO q6-12h for edema and volume overload; monitor electrolytes
0.06-0.3 mg/kg/day PO once daily for hypertension; max 5-10 mg/day
Fluid and sodium restriction; monitor renal function, electrolytes, complement levels

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO for persistent proteinuria or hypertension; renoprotective
1-2 mg/kg/day PO if biopsy shows crescentic or proliferative GN requiring immunosuppression

notes: Establish specific diagnosis via serology and biopsy when possible. Treatment is primarily supportive unless biopsy reveals disease requiring immunosuppression.
counselling: Child's kidneys are inflamed; the exact cause is being investigated
Restrict salt and water during the swelling phase
Blood pressure and kidney function will be monitored closely
Additional tests including kidney biopsy may be needed to find the exact cause
Most cases in children respond well to treatment once the cause is identified
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist for full evaluation; renal biopsy if not typical APSGN
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if hypertensive emergency, significant fluid overload, or rapidly deteriorating renal function

investigations.name: Urinalysis with microscopy
investigations.indication: RBC casts, dysmorphic RBCs confirm glomerular disease

investigations.name: Renal biopsy
investigations.indication: If atypical features or not improving as expected

first_line_drugs.notes: IV NS bolus then maintenance fluids; ketorolac 0.5 mg/kg IV (max 30 mg) for acute colic
Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy for stones <2 cm; preferred first-line for most pediatric stones

second_line_drugs.notes: Flexible URS with laser lithotripsy for ureteral or lower pole stones; holmium laser preferred
0.4 mg PO daily (off-label) for medical expulsive therapy of distal ureteral stones 5-10 mm
1-2 mEq/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for hypocitraturia prevention; adjust per urine citrate
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO for hypercalciuria prevention; monitor electrolytes

notes: Metabolic evaluation with stone analysis is mandatory in all pediatric stone formers. Tailor prevention to stone composition; dietary modifications are first-line prevention.
guideline_changes: AUA 2025 updated surgical stones guideline with pediatric-specific sections
counselling: Child has a kidney or ureter stone causing pain
Increase water intake to at least 2 liters per day to prevent new stones
Pain medicine will be given; most small stones pass on their own
Stone should be collected and sent for analysis to find the cause
Diet changes (less salt, less animal protein) help prevent recurrence
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist if stone >5mm, obstruction, infection, or not passing spontaneously
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if infected obstructed stone (surgical emergency), intractable pain, or AKI

investigations.name: Non-contrast CT abdomen (low-dose)
investigations.indication: Gold standard for stone detection; size and location

investigations.name: Renal and bladder ultrasound
investigations.indication: First-line in children; avoid radiation when possible

investigations.name: 24-hour urine metabolic profile
investigations.indication: Calcium, oxalate, citrate, uric acid, cystine

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes, calcium, uric acid, creatinine

first_line_drugs.notes: Serial renal US q3-6mo; ~50% resolve spontaneously; SFU grade 1-2 typically managed conservatively
Amoxicillin 10-15 mg/kg PO daily or TMP-SMX 2 mg/kg PO daily for high-grade HN to prevent UTI

second_line_drugs.notes: Open or laparoscopic repair for UPJ obstruction with declining function or SFU grade 3-4
Diuretic renography to assess drainage and differential renal function; guides surgical decision

notes: About half of hydronephrosis cases resolve spontaneously. Surgery indicated for febrile UTIs, deteriorating hydronephrosis, or declining renal function on renogram.
counselling: Baby's kidney is swollen with urine because of slow drainage; this was found on ultrasound
Many cases improve on their own without surgery; regular ultrasound monitoring is needed
Antibiotic medicine may be given daily to prevent urinary infections
Surgery is only needed if the kidney function is getting worse
Keep all follow-up ultrasound appointments every 3-6 months
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist if SFU grade 3-4, declining function on renogram, or febrile UTI
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for febrile UTI with hydronephrosis or postoperatively after pyeloplasty

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound (serial)
investigations.indication: Monitor APD and SFU grade q3-6 months

investigations.name: MAG3 diuretic renogram
investigations.indication: Assess drainage and differential renal function for SFU 3-4

investigations.name: VCUG
investigations.indication: If UTI occurs to rule out vesicoureteral reflux

investigations.name: Urine culture
investigations.indication: If febrile illness to detect UTI early

first_line_drugs.notes: TMP-SMX 2 mg/kg PO daily or nitrofurantoin 1-2 mg/kg PO daily for VUR grade III-V or post-UTI
Treat bladder-bowel dysfunction with timed voiding, constipation management before surgical intervention

second_line_drugs.notes: Deflux (dextranomer/HA) subureteral injection for VUR grade III-IV; 70-80% success per injection
Open or robotic-assisted surgical correction for high-grade VUR refractory to conservative management
0.1-0.2 mg/kg PO BID-TID if associated overactive bladder; treat BBD concomitantly

notes: Risk-based approach: low-grade VUR often resolves spontaneously. Treat BBD before considering surgery; antibiotic prophylaxis for recurrent febrile UTIs.
guideline_changes: 2024 EAU/ESPU VUR chapter updated with risk-based management approach
counselling: Child's urine flows backward from the bladder to the kidneys which can cause infections
Daily low-dose antibiotic prevents urinary infections while waiting for reflux to resolve
Treat constipation and ensure regular toilet habits as these affect reflux
Many children outgrow reflux by age 5-6 years; surgery is only for severe cases
Bring child for urine test if there is fever without obvious source
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist for high-grade VUR (IV-V), recurrent febrile UTIs despite prophylaxis, or renal scarring
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for febrile UTI, post-surgical management, or acute pyelonephritis

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Assess for hydronephrosis and renal size

first_line_drugs.notes: Assess volume status; restrict fluids to insensible losses + urine output if oliguric; avoid fluid overload
1-2 mg/kg IV bolus or 0.1-0.4 mg/kg/h infusion; convert oliguric to non-oliguric AKI if euvolemic
Calcium gluconate 100 mg/kg IV, insulin-glucose, kayexalate for hyperkalemia; bicarb for acidosis

second_line_drugs.notes: Continuous renal replacement therapy for fluid overload >10%, refractory electrolyte/acid-base derangement
Preferred RRT modality in small children; for AKI refractory to conservative measures
0.2 mg/kg IV once for tumor lysis syndrome-associated AKI; prevents uric acid nephropathy

notes: Identify and treat underlying cause promptly. Avoid nephrotoxins; adjust all renally-cleared drug doses; fluid overload >10% associated with poor outcomes.
guideline_changes: Pediatric-specific AKI biomarkers (NGAL, KIM-1) increasingly used for early detection
counselling: Child's kidneys have suddenly stopped working properly; this needs urgent treatment
Fluid intake and urine output will be carefully measured
Blood tests will be checked frequently; some may show high potassium which needs immediate treatment
Dialysis (blood cleaning machine) may be needed if kidneys do not recover quickly
Most children recover kidney function if the cause is treated early
referral_criteria: Immediate referral to pediatric nephrologist; ICU if hemodynamically unstable or needing dialysis
hospitalisation_criteria: All AKI cases require admission; ICU for dialysis, hyperkalemia, fluid overload >10%, or metabolic acidosis

investigations.name: Serum creatinine (serial)
investigations.indication: Monitor trend; staging per pRIFLE criteria

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes (K, Na, Ca, PO4)
investigations.indication: Hyperkalemia is life-threatening; frequent monitoring needed

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Assess kidney size and rule out obstruction

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO daily; ACEi for proteinuria and BP control; target BP <50th percentile
50 U/kg SC/IV 3x/wk for CKD anemia; target Hb 11-12 g/dL; supplement iron first
Phosphate binder; dose titrated to normalize serum phosphorus; give with meals

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05 mg/kg/day SC for CKD-related growth failure; per KDOQI; continue until target height or transplant
Vitamin D 1000-2000 IU/day PO; calcitriol 0.01-0.05 mcg/kg/day if secondary hyperparathyroidism
Non-calcium phosphate binder; 400-800 mg PO TID with meals if calcium-based binders insufficient
0.45 mcg/kg SC/IV q2-4wk; longer-acting ESA alternative to erythropoietin

notes: Multidisciplinary CKD clinic follow-up essential. Preterm and SGA infants at increased CKD risk; early RAS blockade for proteinuria slows progression.
guideline_changes: KDIGO 2024 major update with dedicated pediatric chapter including growth and development focus
counselling: Child's kidneys are not working fully; this is a long-term condition needing regular care
Blood pressure medicine (ACE inhibitor) protects the remaining kidney function
Follow the dietary advice: reduce salt, ensure adequate protein (not too much or too little)
Growth may be slow; growth hormone injection may help
Keep all follow-up appointments; blood and urine tests are needed every 3-6 months
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist at diagnosis; dietitian, and transplant team for CKD stage 4-5
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for hyperkalemia, severe fluid overload, uremic symptoms, or dialysis initiation

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Anemia of CKD; guide EPO therapy

first_line_drugs.notes: Urgent workup: renal US, electrolytes, urinalysis, serology; classify as AKI vs CKD to guide therapy
1-2 mg/kg IV/PO for fluid overload; dose adjust based on renal function response
Correct hyperkalemia, acidosis, hyperphosphatemia; restrict potassium and phosphorus intake

second_line_drugs.notes: PD or HD if conservative measures fail; PD preferred in young children; preemptive transplant evaluation
0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO if CKD with proteinuria/HTN confirmed; avoid in AKI with hemodynamic instability

notes: Priority is distinguishing AKI from CKD via renal US (kidney size) and history. Multidisciplinary team approach with nephrology, dietitian, and social work.
counselling: Child's kidneys are not working well; the doctor is investigating whether this is sudden or long-standing
Blood tests and urine tests will help determine the cause and best treatment
Fluid and diet may need to be restricted temporarily
If kidney function does not improve, dialysis or transplant may be discussed
Regular follow-up with the kidney specialist is essential
referral_criteria: Urgent referral to pediatric nephrologist for workup and classification
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for electrolyte derangements, fluid overload, uremic symptoms, or need for dialysis

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Kidney size differentiates AKI (normal/enlarged) from CKD (small)

investigations.name: Urinalysis
investigations.indication: Casts, protein, and blood guide etiology

first_line_drugs.notes: Watch and wait for noncommunicating hydrocele in infants; most resolve by 12-24 months of age
Inguinal approach hydrocelectomy if persists >12-24 mo, communicating type, or symptomatic

second_line_drugs.notes: Laparoscopic extraperitoneal closure; safe and effective in children; shorter recovery
10 mg/kg PO q6h post-op for pain control; acetaminophen 15 mg/kg q6h alternative; avoid narcotics in infants

notes: Noncommunicating hydroceles in infants usually resolve spontaneously. Spermatoceles are benign, asymptomatic, and rarely require intervention in children.
guideline_changes: Laparoscopic hydrocele repair increasingly adopted as safe alternative
counselling: Baby has fluid around the testicle causing swelling; this is usually harmless
In infants, most hydroceles resolve on their own by 12-24 months
Surgery is only needed if the swelling persists beyond 1-2 years or increases in size
Surgery is a minor procedure; baby usually goes home the same day
If swelling suddenly becomes painful or red, come to hospital immediately (may be hernia)
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric surgeon if hydrocele persists beyond 12-24 months, communicating type, or rapidly enlarging
hospitalisation_criteria: Day-care surgery; overnight admission rarely needed unless complications

investigations.name: Clinical examination with transillumination
investigations.indication: Transillumination positive for hydrocele; confirms fluid

investigations.name: Scrotal ultrasound
investigations.indication: If diagnosis uncertain; differentiate from inguinal hernia or testicular pathology

first_line_drugs.notes: Bed rest, scrotal elevation, ice packs, ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO q6-8h or acetaminophen 15 mg/kg q6h
Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM x1 + doxycycline 2 mg/kg PO BID x10 days; for sexually active adolescents only

second_line_drugs.notes: 4-6 mg TMP/kg/day PO divided BID for 10-14 days if UTI-associated epididymitis with positive culture
25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided QID for 10-14 days for culture-positive bacterial epididymitis

notes: In prepubertal boys, antibiotics reserved for those with pyuria or positive urine culture. Refer to urology to evaluate for anatomic abnormalities.
guideline_changes: Evidence supports withholding antibiotics in prepubertal boys without pyuria
counselling: Child has swelling and pain in the testicle area; this is usually due to infection
Rest, scrotal support, and pain medicine will help
In prepubertal boys, antibiotics are only needed if urine test shows infection
It is important to rule out testicular torsion (twisted testicle) which is an emergency
Complete the antibiotic course if prescribed; follow up if not improving in 3-5 days
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric urologist to evaluate for anatomic abnormalities; emergency referral if torsion cannot be ruled out
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if testicular torsion cannot be excluded clinically or if systemically unwell

investigations.name: Scrotal ultrasound with Doppler
investigations.indication: Differentiate from testicular torsion; assess blood flow

investigations.name: VCUG
investigations.indication: Consider if UTI-associated; rule out structural abnormality

first_line_drugs.notes: Scrotal exploration and detorsion ASAP; >90% salvage if within 6h; do not delay for imaging
Fix both testes to dartos fascia at time of surgery; prevents recurrence and contralateral torsion

second_line_drugs.notes: Outward rotation (open book) at bedside as temporizing measure; does not replace surgery
Removal of non-viable testis if necrotic on exploration; consider prosthesis discussion later

notes: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency; salvage rate drops rapidly after 6 hours. Clinical diagnosis should not be delayed by imaging when suspicion is high.
counselling: Child's testicle has twisted, cutting off blood supply; this is a surgical emergency
Surgery must happen within 6 hours for the best chance of saving the testicle
Both testicles will be fixed during surgery to prevent future torsion
After surgery, some swelling and bruising is normal; ice packs and pain medicine help
If the testicle could not be saved, discuss prosthesis options when child is older
referral_criteria: IMMEDIATE surgical referral; do not delay for imaging when clinical suspicion is high
hospitalisation_criteria: Emergency admission for immediate scrotal exploration; no delay for imaging

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (no delay for imaging)
investigations.indication: Surgery should not be delayed for ultrasound if clinical suspicion is high

investigations.name: Scrotal ultrasound with Doppler
investigations.indication: Only if diagnosis uncertain and will not delay surgery; absent blood flow

first_line_drugs.notes: Urinalysis with microscopy, urine Ca/Cr ratio, serum creatinine, C3/C4, renal US; repeat UA x3 to confirm
Reassurance and serial monitoring q3-6mo if isolated microscopic hematuria without proteinuria or HTN

second_line_drugs.notes: Enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO if concurrent proteinuria >0.5 g/day develops
Treat underlying UTI if identified; cephalexin 25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided TID-QID for 7-10 days

notes: Most isolated hematuria is benign; confirm persistence with 3 samples 2 weeks apart. Refer to nephrology if proteinuria, HTN, decreased C3, or family history of renal disease.
counselling: Blood has been found in child's urine; most causes in children are not serious
Several tests are needed to find the cause; this may take a few visits
If urine is tea-colored, face is puffy, or blood pressure is high, come immediately
Avoid dehydration; encourage child to drink adequate water
Follow up regularly for urine tests until the cause is identified and resolved
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist if proteinuria present, hypertension, low complement, family history of renal disease, or persistent gross hematuria
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if gross hematuria with clots, associated nephritic syndrome, or significant proteinuria

investigations.name: Urinalysis with microscopy (x3)
investigations.indication: Confirm persistence; dysmorphic RBCs suggest glomerular origin

first_line_drugs.notes: First morning urine protein/creatinine ratio; repeat x3 to exclude orthostatic proteinuria; renal function panel
Enalapril 0.08-0.6 mg/kg/day PO for persistent proteinuria >0.5 g/day; uptitrate q4-8 wk to effect

second_line_drugs.notes: Losartan 0.7-1.4 mg/kg/day PO once daily if ACEi not tolerated; antiproteinuric
Sodium restriction <2 g/day enhances ACEi/ARB antiproteinuric effect; dietary counseling

notes: Exclude orthostatic proteinuria (benign) with first morning sample. Persistent non-orthostatic proteinuria warrants nephrology referral and consideration of biopsy.
counselling: Protein is leaking into child's urine; this needs investigation to find the cause
First morning urine sample is important for accurate testing
Orthostatic proteinuria (only when standing) is harmless and common in adolescents
Low-salt diet helps reduce proteinuria along with medicine if needed
Regular follow-up urine tests are needed every 1-3 months
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist if persistent non-orthostatic proteinuria >0.5 g/day, associated hematuria, or declining renal function
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if nephrotic-range proteinuria with edema or associated renal dysfunction

first_line_drugs.notes: 50-75 mg/kg IV/IM daily (max 2 g) for initial empiric therapy; switch to oral when afebrile x24-48h
8 mg/kg PO daily (max 400 mg) for outpatient oral therapy; 10-14 day total course
25-45 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for 10-14 days; oral alternative for outpatient treatment

second_line_drugs.notes: Ampicillin 100 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h + gentamicin 5-7.5 mg/kg/day IV; for neonates/young infants
10-20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 750 mg/dose); reserve for resistant organisms in older children

notes: Oral therapy is equivalent to IV for non-toxic children >2 months. Obtain urine culture before antibiotics; VCUG if febrile UTI in child <2 years.
guideline_changes: AAP 2024 Redbook supports shorter 5-10 day courses for uncomplicated cases
counselling: Child has a kidney infection with fever that needs antibiotic treatment for 10-14 days
Complete the full antibiotic course even if child feels better
Ensure child drinks plenty of fluids
If child has recurrent infections, further tests will be done to check for kidney problems
Toilet training and regular voiding habits are important to prevent future infections
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist/urologist for first febrile UTI in child <2 years; recurrent pyelonephritis; or abnormal imaging
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if age <2 months, toxic-appearing, unable to tolerate oral medication, or immunocompromised

investigations.name: CBC, CRP
investigations.indication: Elevated WBC and CRP support pyelonephritis

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Rule out obstruction, abscess; assess kidney size

investigations.name: VCUG
investigations.indication: After first febrile UTI in child <2 years per AAP

investigations.name: DMSA scan
investigations.indication: Detect renal scarring 4-6 months after acute infection

diagnosis_name: Tubulo-interstitial nephritis, not specified as acute or chronic

first_line_drugs.notes: Identify and discontinue causative drug (NSAIDs, antibiotics, PPIs); treat underlying infection if present
1 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) for 4-6 wk then gradual taper; for drug-induced or immune-mediated ATIN

second_line_drugs.notes: 600-1200 mg/m2/day PO divided BID for steroid-refractory TIN; emerging evidence
BP control, fluid/electrolyte management; monitor renal function for recovery; low-salt diet

notes: Removing the offending agent is most important step; most cases recover spontaneously. Steroids may accelerate recovery but evidence from RCTs is limited.
guideline_changes: Role of mycophenolate mofetil emerging for steroid-refractory TIN
counselling: Child's kidney is inflamed, most likely from a medicine; the causative medicine must be stopped
Kidney function usually recovers once the medicine is stopped
Steroid medicine may be given to help the kidney heal faster
Blood tests will be repeated regularly to ensure kidney function is improving
Always inform doctors about this drug reaction to avoid the medicine in future
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist if renal function not recovering after stopping offending agent, or renal biopsy needed
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if AKI, significant electrolyte disturbance, or need for renal biopsy

investigations.name: Renal biopsy
investigations.indication: If diagnosis uncertain or not improving with drug withdrawal

investigations.name: Renal ultrasound
investigations.indication: Assess kidney size; enlarged in acute TIN

diagnosis_name: Urinary tract infection, site not specified

first_line_drugs.notes: 25-50 mg/kg/day PO divided TID-QID for 7-10 days; first-line for uncomplicated lower UTI
25-45 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for 7-10 days; alternative oral first-line
8 mg/kg PO daily (max 400 mg) for 7-10 days; once-daily dosing improves adherence

second_line_drugs.notes: 6-12 mg TMP/kg/day PO divided BID for 7-10 days; avoid if local resistance >20%
5-7 mg/kg/day PO divided QID for 7 days; for lower UTI only; not for pyelonephritis
50-75 mg/kg IV/IM daily (max 2 g) for toxic-appearing children or infants <2 months

notes: Obtain urine culture before starting empiric antibiotics. Screen toilet-trained children for bladder-bowel dysfunction; shorter courses may suffice for afebrile UTI.
guideline_changes: AAP 2024 supports shorter antibiotic courses (5-10 days) for uncomplicated UTI
counselling: Child has a urine infection that needs antibiotic treatment
Complete the full antibiotic course; do not stop early even if symptoms improve
Encourage child to drink plenty of water throughout the day
Toilet habits are important: do not hold urine, empty bladder completely, wipe front to back
Treat constipation as it contributes to urinary infections
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric nephrologist/urologist for first febrile UTI <2 years, recurrent UTIs (>3/year), or abnormal imaging
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if age <2 months, toxic-appearing, unable to tolerate oral medication, or complicated UTI with obstruction

investigations.name: Urine culture (clean catch or catheter)
investigations.indication: Gold standard for diagnosis; must be collected before antibiotics

investigations.name: VCUG
investigations.indication: Recurrent febrile UTI or abnormal renal US

first_line_drugs.notes: Early intervention PT/OT/Speech starting from infancy; individualized goals
10-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for associated seizures; titrate over 2-4 wks
Ongoing developmental support; fine motor and adaptive skill training

second_line_drugs.notes: 3-5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for seizures in infants
Surgical placement if oral feeding unsafe; for severe cases with dysphagia

notes: No treatment reverses microcephaly; management is supportive with early intervention services. Seizure control and nutritional support are key priorities.
guideline_changes: CDC/AAP updated developmental milestone surveillance tools in 2022
counselling: Child's head is smaller than normal which may affect development
Early intervention (physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy) is the most important treatment
There is no medicine to grow the head, but therapy helps the child reach their best potential
Seizures may develop; if child has jerking movements or staring spells, report immediately
Join a support group for families of children with developmental conditions; many resources are available
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist and genetics at diagnosis; early intervention program
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for seizure control, feeding difficulties requiring tube placement, or acute illness in compromised child

investigations.name: TORCH screening
investigations.indication: Rule out congenital infection (CMV, Zika, toxoplasmosis)

first_line_drugs.notes: Individualized surgical approach based on malformation type; e.g. Chiari decompression with duraplasty
10-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for associated seizures
Early intervention PT/OT/Speech to maximize cognitive and motor function

second_line_drugs.notes: For associated hydrocephalus; programmable valve preferred
15-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for seizures; monitor LFTs

notes: Management is highly individualized based on specific malformation and severity. Multidisciplinary team including neurosurgery, neurology, genetics, and rehabilitation is essential.
counselling: Child has a brain structure abnormality present from birth
Management depends on the specific type; the neurologist will explain the treatment plan
Early intervention therapies are essential to maximize development
Some children may need surgery (shunt for fluid, decompression)
Long-term follow-up with multiple specialists is needed; keep all appointments
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurology and neurosurgery at diagnosis; genetics for syndromic evaluation
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for raised intracranial pressure, hydrocephalus, intractable seizures, or surgical intervention

investigations.name: Brain MRI
investigations.indication: Detailed structural assessment of the malformation

investigations.name: Genetic testing
investigations.indication: Identify underlying genetic cause; guide counseling

first_line_drugs.notes: Multidisciplinary PT/OT/Speech therapy; federally mandated for ages 0-3 yrs
1-3 sessions/wk targeting expressive/receptive language goals
Fine motor, sensory integration, adaptive skills training; 1-3 sessions/wk

second_line_drugs.notes: 3-6 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO if iron-deficiency anemia contributing to delay
Levothyroxine 10-15 mcg/kg/day PO if hypothyroidism identified

notes: Earlier identification leads to better outcomes; screen at 9, 18, and 30 months per AAP. Treat underlying causes (metabolic, genetic, nutritional) while providing therapy.
source: AAP 2022, CDC Learn the Signs Act Early
guideline_changes: AAP/CDC updated milestone checklists in 2022 raising threshold from 50% to 75%
counselling: Child is not reaching milestones at the expected age; early therapy makes a big difference
Start physiotherapy, speech therapy, and occupational therapy as recommended
Practice exercises at home daily as taught by the therapist
Screen hearing and vision as they can contribute to developmental delay
Track milestones using a milestone diary; share progress with the doctor at each visit
referral_criteria: Refer to developmental pediatrician, early intervention program; audiology and ophthalmology screening
hospitalisation_criteria: Admission usually not required unless associated with acute seizures, severe malnutrition, or metabolic crisis

investigations.name: Developmental screening (ASQ/DASII)
investigations.indication: Formal developmental assessment to quantify delay

investigations.name: Thyroid function test
investigations.indication: Rule out hypothyroidism as treatable cause

investigations.name: Hearing assessment (OAE/BERA)
investigations.indication: Hearing loss is common treatable cause of speech delay

investigations.name: Vision screening
investigations.indication: Visual impairment contributes to global delay

investigations.name: Genetic testing (microarray)
investigations.indication: If etiology unclear or dysmorphic features

first_line_drugs.notes: Physical therapy with task-specific motor training; evidence-based per APTA CPG; 1-3x/wk
Activity-based intervention targeting daily living skills and fine motor tasks
Structured home exercises and environmental modifications per therapist guidance

second_line_drugs.notes: Cognitive Orientation to Occupational Performance; child-directed problem solving for motor tasks
0.3-1 mg/kg/day PO for comorbid ADHD if present; start low and titrate

notes: Screen for comorbid ADHD, ASD, and language disorders which are common. No pharmacotherapy for DCD itself; intervention focuses on task-oriented motor skill training.
counselling: Child has difficulty with coordination and motor skills; this is called DCD (developmental coordination disorder)
Occupational therapy and physical therapy are the main treatments
Practice motor skills through fun activities and play at home
This is not laziness; child is trying hard but their brain processes movement differently
Many children improve significantly with consistent therapy; be patient and encouraging
referral_criteria: Refer to occupational therapist and physiotherapist; screen for ADHD and learning disabilities
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalization not typically required

investigations.name: Movement ABC-2
investigations.indication: Standardized motor assessment for DCD diagnosis

investigations.name: Vision screening
investigations.indication: Rule out visual problems affecting coordination

investigations.name: Cognitive assessment
investigations.indication: Rule out intellectual disability; identify learning needs

diagnosis_name: Specific developmental disorders of speech and language

first_line_drugs.notes: Structured parent training (e.g. Heidelberg model) for expressive language delay; start by age 2-3
Direct child-centered therapy by SLP; 1-3 sessions/wk; phonological and language-focused
Targeted phonological or integrated phonological treatment for speech sound disorders

second_line_drugs.notes: Picture exchange, speech-generating devices for severe expressive impairment
Motor approach for isolated phonetic speech sound disorders

notes: Begin intervention by age 2-3 as only ~1/3 of late talkers catch up spontaneously. For multilingual children, provide therapy in all languages when possible.
guideline_changes: 2024 interdisciplinary CPG published with updated intervention recommendations for DLD
counselling: Child has delayed speech or language development; early therapy gives the best results
Talk to your child frequently; narrate daily activities in simple language
Reduce screen time; face-to-face interaction is best for language learning
Read picture books together daily; let child point and name objects
Speech therapy should be regular (1-3 times per week); practice exercises at home too
referral_criteria: Refer to speech-language pathologist by age 2 if not using words; audiology for all speech-delayed children
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalization not required for speech/language disorders

investigations.name: Hearing assessment (OAE/BERA)
investigations.indication: Mandatory for all children with speech delay; hearing loss is leading treatable cause

investigations.name: Developmental assessment
investigations.indication: Evaluate for global developmental delay vs isolated language delay

investigations.name: Autism screening (M-CHAT)
investigations.indication: Rule out ASD which commonly presents with speech delay

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO once at headache onset; max 400 mg/dose; 68% response rate in trials
15 mg/kg PO once at headache onset; max 1000 mg/dose; 54% response rate

second_line_drugs.notes: 5 mg PO if <40 kg, 10 mg PO if >=40 kg; for moderate-severe headache
5-20 mg intranasal once; FDA-approved for adolescents >=12 yrs

notes: Rule out secondary causes with red flag screening before treating as primary headache. NSAIDs are first-line; triptans reserved for moderate-severe or NSAID-refractory cases.
guideline_changes: AAN guideline reaffirmed Oct 2025; IHS global acute migraine recs published 2024
counselling: Give pain medicine (ibuprofen/paracetamol) early at the start of headache for best effect
Maintain regular sleep schedule, adequate hydration, and regular meals
Limit screen time; take regular breaks from computer/phone
Keep a headache diary noting triggers, duration, and what helped
Stress and anxiety are common triggers in school-going children; address them
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist if headaches are progressive, waking child from sleep, associated with vomiting, vision changes, or focal neurological signs
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for thunderclap headache, signs of raised ICP, meningism, or new focal neurological deficits

investigations.name: Brain MRI
investigations.indication: Only if red flags present (progressive, focal signs, early morning vomiting)

investigations.name: Vision screening
investigations.indication: Refractive errors contribute to headaches in children

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO at onset; max 400 mg; first-line acute treatment
5 mg PO if <40 kg, 10 mg if >=40 kg; FDA-approved for pediatric migraine age >=6
5-20 mg intranasal; FDA-approved >=12 yrs for acute migraine
1-3 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for prophylaxis; only FDA-approved preventive in children 12-17

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.25-1 mg/kg PO qHS for prophylaxis; max 1 mg/kg/day; limited evidence vs placebo
1-4 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for prophylaxis; monitor HR and BP
6.25-12.5 mg PO once; FDA-approved >=12 yrs for acute migraine
Combined cognitive behavioral therapy with amitriptyline shown beneficial in CHAMP trial

notes: Acute treatment should be given early at headache onset for best effect. Evidence for preventive medications is limited; CBT combined with medication may be superior to medication alone.
guideline_changes: AAN acute and preventive guidelines reaffirmed Oct 2025
counselling: Migraine is a recurring headache condition; it can be managed but not cured
Take pain medicine within 30 minutes of headache onset for best effect
Identify and avoid triggers: skipped meals, poor sleep, stress, bright lights, certain foods
If headaches are frequent (>4/month), preventive medicine may be needed
Relaxation techniques and regular exercise help reduce migraine frequency
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist if >4 headaches/month, not responding to acute treatment, or atypical features
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for status migrainosus (>72h), associated stroke-like symptoms, or intractable vomiting

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (ICHD-3 criteria)
investigations.indication: Migraine is a clinical diagnosis; no specific test needed

investigations.name: Brain MRI
investigations.indication: Only if atypical features, aura prolonged >60 min, or focal deficits

investigations.name: Vision screening
investigations.indication: Rule out refractive error contributing to headaches

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg IV day 1 then 50 mg/kg IV q12h; max 4 g/day; 10-14 days
15 mg/kg IV q6h; target trough 15-20 mcg/mL; add empirically for DRSP coverage
0.15 mg/kg IV q6h x 2-4 days; give 10-20 min before or with first antibiotic dose

second_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg IV q6h; max 12 g/day; alternative to ceftriaxone
40 mg/kg IV q8h; max 6 g/day; for resistant organisms or penicillin allergy

notes: Do not delay antibiotics for LP; start empiric therapy immediately if meningitis suspected. Adjunctive dexamethasone reduces hearing loss especially in H. influenzae meningitis.
guideline_changes: NICE updated meningitis guidelines March 2024 with revised empiric antibiotic recommendations
counselling: Child has a serious bacterial infection of the brain lining; strong IV antibiotics are needed immediately
Treatment requires at least 10-14 days of IV antibiotics in hospital
Steroid medicine is given to reduce brain swelling and protect hearing
Hearing test must be done before discharge as hearing loss can occur
Vaccinations (pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib) are the best prevention for close contacts and future protection
referral_criteria: Immediate referral to pediatric ICU; neurology consultation; audiology before discharge
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require emergency admission; ICU if altered consciousness, shock, or seizures

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis
investigations.indication: Cell count, protein, glucose, gram stain, culture; do not delay antibiotics if LP delayed

investigations.name: Brain CT/MRI
investigations.indication: If focal signs, papilledema, or clinical deterioration

first_line_drugs.notes: Neonates: 20 mg/kg IV q8h x 21 days; Children >3mo: 10 mg/kg IV q8h x 14-21 days
100 mg/kg IV day 1 then 50 mg/kg IV q12h empirically until bacterial meningitis excluded

second_line_drugs.notes: 20-30 mg/kg/day IV x 3-5 days for autoimmune/ADEM; max 1 g/day
2 g/kg IV over 2-5 days for autoimmune encephalitis or ADEM refractory to steroids
5 mg/kg IV q12h for CMV encephalitis in immunocompromised

notes: Start acyclovir empirically in all suspected encephalitis; do not wait for HSV PCR results. Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDAR) increasingly recognized and requires immunotherapy.
counselling: Child has inflammation of the brain which can be caused by virus or immune system problems
IV antiviral medicine (acyclovir) is started immediately and continued until tests confirm the cause
Child may be confused, have seizures, or behave differently during the illness
Recovery can take weeks to months; rehabilitation may be needed
Follow-up with neurologist is important for long-term cognitive and developmental monitoring
referral_criteria: Immediate referral to pediatric neurology and infectious disease; ICU if altered consciousness
hospitalisation_criteria: All cases require admission; ICU for seizures, altered consciousness, or need for intubation

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis
investigations.indication: Lymphocytic pleocytosis; HSV PCR, enterovirus PCR

investigations.name: Brain MRI
investigations.indication: Temporal lobe involvement suggests HSV; diffuse ADEM pattern

investigations.name: EEG
investigations.indication: Seizure detection; periodic lateralized discharges in HSV

investigations.name: Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
investigations.indication: Autoimmune encephalitis panel if viral causes excluded

diagnosis_name: Meningitis due to other and unspecified causes

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg IV day 1 then 50 mg/kg IV q12h; empiric until etiology clarified; max 4 g/day
15 mg/kg IV q6h empirically; de-escalate once organism identified
10 mg/kg IV q8h empirically if viral (HSV) etiology possible; pending PCR

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg IV q6h x 2-4 days if bacterial etiology suspected
50 mg/kg IV q6h; add for Listeria coverage in neonates and immunocompromised

notes: Start empiric antibiotics plus acyclovir until CSF culture, PCR, and gram stain clarify etiology. Up to 92% of aseptic meningitis cases receive empirical antibiotics pending workup.
guideline_changes: NICE 2024 guidelines updated empiric treatment recommendations
counselling: Child has an infection of the brain covering; cause is being investigated
Antibiotics and antiviral medicines are started until the exact cause is known
Blood and spinal fluid tests will identify whether bacteria, virus, or other cause is responsible
If it is viral meningitis, it usually gets better on its own in 1-2 weeks
Watch for worsening headache, neck stiffness, drowsiness, or seizures and come immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist and infectious disease; ICU if clinical deterioration
hospitalisation_criteria: All suspected meningitis cases require admission until etiology is established

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis
investigations.indication: Cell count, protein, glucose, gram stain, culture, viral PCR

investigations.name: Brain MRI
investigations.indication: If atypical course or focal signs

first_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO q6-8h for headache and fever; max 40 mg/kg/day
15 mg/kg PO q4-6h for analgesia/antipyresis; max 75 mg/kg/day
Maintenance fluids; monitor for SIADH; restrict if serum Na <135 mEq/L

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO q8h; max 4 mg/dose; for nausea/vomiting
10 mg/kg IV q8h if HSV-2 meningitis confirmed or immunocompromised patient

notes: Most cases are self-limited and resolve in 1-2 weeks with supportive care. Admit neonates, immunocompromised, or those with altered mental status or seizures for observation.
guideline_changes: WHO launched first-ever meningitis diagnosis/treatment/care guidelines April 2025
counselling: Child has a viral infection of the brain covering; this is usually milder than bacterial meningitis
Treatment is mainly supportive: rest, fluids, and pain medicine
Most children recover completely within 1-2 weeks
Keep child comfortable; give pain medicine regularly for headache
Return to hospital if child becomes more drowsy, has seizures, or symptoms worsen
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist if altered consciousness, seizures, or not improving within expected timeframe
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit neonates, immunocompromised, altered mental status, or if unable to differentiate from bacterial meningitis

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis
investigations.indication: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, mildly elevated protein

investigations.name: CSF enterovirus PCR
investigations.indication: Most common cause of viral meningitis in children

investigations.name: CBC, CRP
investigations.indication: Normal/mildly elevated CRP helps distinguish from bacterial

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.3 mg/kg/day PO divided TID; titrate weekly to 0.75-2 mg/kg/day; max 40 mg/day <8 yrs
OnabotulinumtoxinA 10-15 U/kg per session for focal spasticity; max 340 U total per session
Ongoing PT/OT; goal-directed task-specific training; minimum 2-3 sessions/wk
0.12-0.8 mg/kg/day PO divided TID-QID for generalized spasticity; short-term use preferred

second_line_drugs.notes: ITB pump: test dose 25-100 mcg intrathecal; for severe generalized spasticity refractory to oral meds
0.05 mg/kg PO qHS; titrate to 0.05 mg/kg TID; max 0.1 mg/kg TID
0.5 mg/kg PO BID; titrate to max 3 mg/kg QID or 400 mg/day; monitor LFTs
Surgical option for children with spastic diplegia age 3-8 yrs with good selective motor control

notes: Multimodal approach combining physical therapy, orthotics, and pharmacology is standard. BoNT-A is preferred for focal/segmental spasticity; oral agents for generalized spasticity.
guideline_changes: 2024 China neurorestorative therapy CPG published with updated rehabilitation recommendations
counselling: Cerebral palsy is a lifelong condition but therapy can greatly improve your child's abilities
Regular physiotherapy and occupational therapy at home is as important as hospital sessions
Medicines for stiffness need to be given on time every day and dose changes should only be done by the doctor
Watch for difficulty swallowing or frequent choking during meals and report to doctor
Your child can attend school with appropriate support - ask about inclusive education options
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist for classification, botulinum toxin planning, or if seizures develop; orthopedic referral for progressive contractures or hip surveillance
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe aspiration pneumonia, status epilepticus, post-surgical care (SDR, ITB pump placement), or acute hip dislocation

investigations.name: MRI brain
investigations.indication: Determine etiology and extent of brain injury

investigations.name: Swallowing assessment (VFSS)
investigations.indication: If feeding difficulties or recurrent pneumonia

first_line_drugs.notes: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt with programmable valve; antibiotic-impregnated catheter recommended (Level I)
Endoscopic third ventriculostomy; preferred in obstructive hydrocephalus >6 months age

second_line_drugs.notes: ETV with choroid plexus cauterization; lower shunt dependency rates in selected patients
25-100 mg/kg/day PO divided TID; temporary CSF reduction; NOT recommended for post-hemorrhagic

notes: Antibiotic-impregnated shunt tubing reduces infection risk (Level I evidence). ETV success depends on age and etiology; higher failure rates in premature and very young infants.
guideline_changes: 2024 narrative review supports increasing ETV+CPC use over VP shunt in select patients
counselling: Watch for signs of increased head pressure: vomiting, excessive sleepiness, bulging fontanelle, sunset eyes
Keep regular follow-up appointments for head circumference measurement
If a shunt is placed, learn the signs of shunt blockage: headache, vomiting, drowsiness
Avoid rough play or contact sports that could damage the shunt
Development may be delayed; early intervention therapy helps your child reach their potential
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurosurgeon at diagnosis for surgical planning; ophthalmology for papilledema screening
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for acute raised ICP symptoms, shunt malfunction or infection, pre-operative preparation for VP shunt or ETV

investigations.name: MRI brain with CSF flow study
investigations.indication: Determine type and severity of hydrocephalus

first_line_drugs.notes: OnabotulinumtoxinA 10-15 U/kg per session into affected limb muscles; repeat q3-6 months
CIMT: restrain unaffected limb 2-6 hrs/day for 2-8 wks to promote affected side use
Task-specific upper and lower limb rehabilitation; 3-5 sessions/wk during intensive programs

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.3 mg/kg/day PO divided TID; titrate to 0.75-2 mg/kg/day for associated spasticity
Short leg cast changed weekly x 4-6 wks for ankle equinus contracture after BoNT-A
ITB pump for severe refractory spasticity; test dose 25-100 mcg intrathecal

notes: BoNT-A combined with intensive PT or CIMT gives best functional outcomes for hemiplegic CP. Address both upper and lower limb involvement for optimal rehabilitation.
counselling: Encourage your child to use the affected hand/leg during daily activities like eating and dressing
Home exercises prescribed by the therapist should be done daily for best results
Botulinum toxin injections help reduce stiffness and need to be repeated every 3-6 months
Use proper footwear and ankle braces as recommended to prevent falls
Your child can participate in most activities with appropriate adaptations
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist for etiology workup; orthopedic surgeon for progressive contractures or gait abnormalities
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for intensive rehabilitation programs, post-surgical recovery (tendon transfers, SDR), or acute neurological deterioration

first_line_drugs.notes: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt; use antibiotic-impregnated catheter (Level I); programmable valve preferred
Endoscopic third ventriculostomy for obstructive hydrocephalus; lower infection/reoperation rates

second_line_drugs.notes: ETV with choroid plexus cauterization; reduces CSF production; emerging evidence in infants
25-100 mg/kg/day PO divided TID as temporizing measure; max 2 g/day; monitor electrolytes
For post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus; removes intraventricular clots; may reduce shunt need (Level III)

notes: Both CSF shunts and ETV are acceptable options (Level II evidence). Shunt infection prevention with antibiotic catheters and strict protocols is a priority.
guideline_changes: 2021 CNS systematic review update reinforced antibiotic-impregnated shunt use
counselling: Learn the danger signs of shunt blockage: sudden headache, vomiting, drowsiness, vision changes
Bring the child immediately to hospital if these signs appear at any time
Regular follow-up with neurosurgeon is essential even if the child seems well
Keep the shunt identification card with you at all times
Inform any doctor treating your child about the VP shunt before procedures or medications
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurosurgeon for all new diagnoses; ophthalmology for papilledema monitoring
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for acute raised ICP, shunt malfunction or infection, new-onset hydrocephalus, or pre-surgical workup

investigations.name: Shunt series X-ray
investigations.indication: Assess shunt continuity if malfunction suspected

snomed_display: (Child cerebral degeneration) or (hydrocephalus) or (senile brain degeneration)

first_line_drugs.notes: Balance and coordination training; gait rehab; 4-wk inpatient rehab improves ataxia scores
5 mg PO BID for episodic ataxia type 2; reduces attack frequency (Level B AAN)
50 mg PO BID for mixed/Friedreich/SCA ataxias; probably improves ataxia signs

second_line_drugs.notes: 150 mg PO daily for Friedreich ataxia age >=16 yrs; FDA-approved 2023; NRF2 activator
PO for Niemann-Pick type C; FDA-approved 2024; dose per weight-based schedule
2 g/kg IV over 2-5 days for acute post-infectious cerebellar ataxia; for refractory cases
5-10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID for episodic ataxia type 2 if 4-AP not tolerated

notes: Treatment is etiology-specific; identify underlying cause before selecting therapy. New disease-modifying agents for Friedreich ataxia and NPC represent major recent advances.
guideline_changes: FDA approved omaveloxolone (Skyclarys) 2023 for Friedreich ataxia; levacetylleucine (Aqneursa) 2024 for NPC
counselling: Ataxia means your child has difficulty with balance and coordination - this has many possible causes
Keep the home environment safe: remove loose rugs, install grab bars, pad sharp furniture corners
Some causes of ataxia are treatable, so completing all recommended tests is very important
If ataxia came on suddenly, bring the child to the hospital immediately
Physical therapy exercises at home help improve balance and should be practiced daily
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist for all new-onset ataxia for etiology workup; urgent referral if acute onset suggesting stroke or posterior fossa lesion
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for acute-onset ataxia (rule out posterior fossa pathology, stroke, intoxication), progressive ataxia with raised ICP signs, or inability to walk/stand safely

investigations.name: MRI brain with posterior fossa views
investigations.indication: Rule out posterior fossa tumor, stroke, demyelination

investigations.name: Urine toxicology screen
investigations.indication: Rule out drug ingestion in acute ataxia

first_line_drugs.notes: Start 1 mg/day PO; increase 1 mg q3-5 days to 2 mg TID; max 30 mg TID; 51% response rate
1-5 mg/kg/day levodopa PO divided TID; diagnostic trial for dopa-responsive dystonia first
Focal injection into dystonic muscles; dose varies by muscle group; repeat q3 months

second_line_drugs.notes: Start 5 mg/day PO; increase 5 mg q3-5 days; max 40-80 mg/day divided TID; 10% response rate
GPi DBS for severe generalized dystonia refractory to medical therapy; best for DYT1+
ITB pump for severe dystonia-spasticity; test dose 25-100 mcg intrathecal
0.01-0.03 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID; adjunctive for dystonia with myoclonus

notes: Always trial levodopa first to rule out dopa-responsive dystonia (Segawa disease). Anticholinergics (trihexyphenidyl) are most effective first-line oral agents in children.
