counselling: Dystonia causes involuntary muscle contractions - your child is not doing this on purpose
Medicines take several weeks to show full effect; do not stop them suddenly
A trial of levodopa is important because some types of dystonia respond dramatically to it
Stress and fatigue can worsen symptoms; ensure adequate rest and a calm environment
Regular follow-up is needed to adjust medicine doses as your child grows
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric movement disorder specialist for classification and genetic testing; neurosurgery referral for DBS evaluation if refractory to medications
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for status dystonicus (life-threatening), acute dystonic storm with respiratory compromise, or DBS implantation

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.02-0.05 mg/kg/dose IM/IV for acute dystonic reaction; then 1-2 mg PO BID maintenance
1.25 mg/kg IV/IM for acute EPS; then 5 mg/kg/day PO divided QID; max 300 mg/day
Start 1 mg PO daily; titrate by 1 mg q3-5 days; for chronic movement disorders

second_line_drugs.notes: VMAT2 inhibitor for tardive dyskinesia; limited pediatric data; FDA-approved adults only
12.5 mg PO daily; titrate to 12.5 mg TID; for chorea/hyperkinetic disorders; monitor for depression
1-4 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for essential/action tremor

notes: Identify and discontinue offending medication for drug-induced movement disorders first. Treatment depends on specific movement disorder type; anticholinergics for acute EPS, VMAT2 inhibitors for tardive syndromes.
guideline_changes: VMAT2 inhibitors emerging for tardive dyskinesia; novel gene therapies under investigation
counselling: If your child developed these movements after starting a new medicine, inform the doctor immediately
Do not stop any prescribed medicine without consulting the doctor first
These movements are often treatable once the cause is identified
Keep a diary of when movements are worst and what triggers them
Ensure adequate sleep as tiredness can worsen movement problems
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist if movement disorder persists beyond 2 weeks, etiology unclear, or not responding to initial treatment
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe acute dystonic reaction with airway compromise, tardive dyskinesia with functional impairment, or diagnostic workup requiring prolonged monitoring
snomed_display: Other extrapyramidal disease and abnormal movement disorders

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.4 g/kg/day IV x 5 days (total 2 g/kg); preferred in children; can also give 1 g/kg/day x 2 days
Monitor respiratory function q4h; intubate if FVC <20 mL/kg; DVT prophylaxis; pain management

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-1.5 plasma volumes x 5-6 sessions over 10-14 days; for children >10-15 kg; requires central line
5-10 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for neuropathic pain; titrate to effect

notes: IVIG preferred over plasmapheresis in children due to easier administration and fewer side effects. Monitor for respiratory failure and autonomic instability closely; ICU admission for severe cases.
counselling: GBS causes weakness that usually starts in the legs and moves upward - this is expected
Most children recover fully but recovery can take weeks to months
If your child has difficulty breathing or swallowing, rush to the hospital immediately
Physiotherapy during and after recovery is very important to regain strength
Pain is common in GBS and should be reported to the doctor for treatment
referral_criteria: All GBS cases should be managed by or in consultation with pediatric neurologist; ICU referral if respiratory function declining
hospitalisation_criteria: All suspected GBS cases require hospitalization for monitoring; ICU admission if FVC <20 mL/kg, bulbar weakness, rapid progression, or autonomic instability

investigations.name: Nerve conduction study/EMG
investigations.indication: Confirm diagnosis and classify subtype (AIDP vs AMAN)

diagnosis_name: Myasthenia gravis and other myoneural disorders

first_line_drugs.notes: 1 mg/kg PO q4-6h; start 30 mg TID then titrate to max 120 mg QID based on symptoms
1-2 mg/kg/day PO; start low and increase slowly to minimize transient worsening; taper once stable

second_line_drugs.notes: 2-3 mg/kg/day PO as steroid-sparing agent; check TPMT before starting; onset 3-6 months
600 mg/m2 PO BID; max 2 g/day; steroid-sparing; onset 2-6 months
2 g/kg IV over 2-5 days for exacerbations or pre-thymectomy
Weight-based IV q2wk for AChR-Ab+ generalized MG refractory to standard therapy; FDA-approved peds

notes: Children with ocular MG may achieve spontaneous remission; start with pyridostigmine alone initially. Thymectomy should be considered for generalized MG in children; vaccinate against meningococcus before eculizumab.
guideline_changes: FDA approved eculizumab expanded indication for pediatric generalized MG
counselling: Myasthenia gravis causes muscle weakness that worsens with activity and improves with rest
Give medicines exactly on time as even small delays can increase weakness
Learn the emergency signs: difficulty breathing, unable to swallow, or very weak voice
Avoid very hot weather and overexertion as these worsen symptoms
Inform any doctor or dentist about myasthenia before any procedure or new medicine
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist for all new diagnoses; thoracic surgery referral for thymectomy evaluation in generalized MG
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure, severe dysphagia), cholinergic crisis, or pre-thymectomy preparation

investigations.name: CT chest
investigations.indication: Evaluate for thymoma or thymic hyperplasia

investigations.name: Repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS)
investigations.indication: Electrophysiological confirmation of neuromuscular junction disorder

investigations.name: Pulmonary function tests
investigations.indication: Baseline and serial monitoring of respiratory muscle strength

first_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg); taper over 6-12 months once response achieved
15 mg/m2/wk PO or SC (max 25 mg/wk); preferred steroid-sparing agent; give with folic acid

second_line_drugs.notes: 2 g/kg IV monthly for refractory disease or severe dysphagia; as adjunct to immunosuppression
375 mg/m2 IV x 4 weekly doses for treatment-resistant IIM
600 mg/m2 PO BID; max 2 g/day; for skin-predominant or steroid-intolerant disease
500 mg/m2 IV monthly x 6 for severe refractory IIM with lung involvement

notes: Juvenile IIM should be managed by pediatric rheumatology specialist. Exercise and physiotherapy across all disease activity levels improves outcomes and quality of life.
guideline_changes: 2025 PRES/CARRA treat-to-target recommendations published for juvenile dermatomyositis
counselling: Your child has muscle inflammation that needs long-term treatment to control
Steroid medicines are essential but have side effects - do not stop them without doctor advice
Protect your child from sun exposure with sunscreen and clothing as skin is very sensitive
Regular gentle exercise and physiotherapy help maintain muscle strength
Blood tests are needed regularly to monitor disease activity and medicine side effects
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric rheumatologist for all inflammatory myopathies; dermatology for skin-predominant disease
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe muscle weakness affecting swallowing or breathing, pulse IV steroid therapy, or disease flare not responding to outpatient treatment

investigations.name: CK (creatine kinase)
investigations.indication: Marker of muscle damage and disease activity

investigations.name: MRI muscles (thighs)
investigations.indication: Assess extent of muscle inflammation and guide biopsy

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.75 mg/kg/day PO for Duchenne MD; reduces strength loss; delays cardiomyopathy; long-term use
0.9 mg/kg/day PO for DMD; FDA-approved; possibly less weight gain than prednisone
Regular stretching, strengthening, and cardiopulmonary rehab; prevent contractures; ongoing

second_line_drugs.notes: 40 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for DMD with nonsense mutations; EMA-approved
30 mg/kg IV weekly for DMD amenable to exon 51 skipping; FDA-approved
ACE inhibitor (enalapril 0.1 mg/kg/day) or beta-blocker started by age 10 for cardiomyopathy prevention
Gene therapy for DMD age 4-5; single IV dose 1.33x10^14 vg/kg; FDA-approved 2023

notes: Corticosteroids remain the cornerstone of DMD treatment and should be initiated when motor function plateaus. Gene therapies are emerging; specific genetic testing guides exon-skipping and gene therapy eligibility.
source: AAN 2016 (DMD corticosteroids), French Soc Ped Neuro 2024
guideline_changes: 2024 French guidelines on corticosteroid compound choice and side effect management in DMD
counselling: Muscular dystrophy is a genetic condition - steroid medicine slows the progression significantly
Daily steroid medicine must not be stopped suddenly; your doctor will adjust the dose
Regular physiotherapy and stretching prevents stiff joints and maintains mobility longer
Watch for signs of heart or breathing problems: getting tired easily, cough at night, swollen feet
Genetic counseling is available for family planning and testing other family members
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist and geneticist at diagnosis; cardiology referral by age 10 or at diagnosis for cardiac monitoring
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for acute respiratory failure, pneumonia, cardiac decompensation, fractures in osteoporotic bone, or post-surgical care

investigations.name: Genetic testing (dystrophin gene)
investigations.indication: Confirm specific mutation for treatment eligibility

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: Baseline cardiac function; annual from age 10

diagnosis_name: Anoxic brain damage, not elsewhere classified

first_line_drugs.notes: Cool to 33.5-34.5°C within 6h of birth x 72h then rewarm over 6-12h; for mod-severe HIE >=36wk GA
20 mg/kg IV loading dose for seizures; then 3-5 mg/kg/day maintenance; first-line neonatal ASM
Maintain normoglycemia, normocarbia, avoid hypotension; target MAP for gestational age

second_line_drugs.notes: 40-60 mg/kg IV loading then 20-30 mg/kg/day divided BID for neonatal seizures; preferred if cardiac
1000 U/kg IV x 3-5 doses adjunct to cooling; investigational neuroprotectant; mixed trial results

notes: Therapeutic hypothermia is standard of care for moderate-severe neonatal HIE within 6 hours of birth. Late cooling (6-24h) may still provide modest benefit per updated 2026 AAP guidance.
guideline_changes: AAP released updated clinical report on therapeutic hypothermia for neonatal HIE Feb 2026
counselling: Your baby's brain was affected by low oxygen - cooling treatment helps protect the brain
Your baby will be closely monitored in the NICU for several days
Seizures are common after brain injury and will be treated with medicines
Long-term developmental follow-up is very important even if your baby seems to recover well
Early intervention therapy (physiotherapy, occupational therapy) should start as soon as recommended
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist for all moderate-severe HIE; developmental pediatrician for long-term follow-up
hospitalisation_criteria: All moderate-severe HIE requires NICU admission for therapeutic hypothermia, seizure management, and multiorgan support

first_line_drugs.notes: 15-25 mg/kg/day PO divided TID; titrate up to max 100 mg/kg/day (max 2 g/day); monitor electrolytes
Target 5-10% weight loss if overweight/obese; dietary counseling; primary intervention in obese teens

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-3 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; has carbonic anhydrase activity; also aids weight loss
1-2 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; adjunct to acetazolamide if insufficient response
Surgical; for progressive visual loss refractory to medical therapy
Lumboperitoneal or VP shunt for refractory headache with visual loss despite medical therapy

notes: Acetazolamide is first-line pharmacotherapy; monitor for metabolic acidosis and electrolyte abnormalities. Ophthalmology follow-up is essential to monitor papilledema and visual fields.
source: Alder Hey IIH Guideline 2024, IIH Consensus 2018
guideline_changes: 2024 Alder Hey hospital published updated pediatric IIH clinical guideline
counselling: Your child has increased pressure inside the head that can affect vision if not treated
Take the medicine regularly even if headaches improve - stopping too soon causes relapse
Regular eye checkups are very important to protect your child's vision
If your child is overweight, losing weight is one of the most important treatments
Report any worsening headache, vision changes, or double vision immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to ophthalmologist urgently for visual field and fundus assessment; neurosurgeon if vision deteriorating despite medical therapy
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for acute vision loss, severe papilledema with visual field loss, need for therapeutic lumbar puncture, or surgical intervention

investigations.name: MRI brain with MRV
investigations.indication: Rule out cerebral venous sinus thrombosis and mass lesion

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture with opening pressure
investigations.indication: Confirm elevated CSF pressure (>28 cmH2O in children)

investigations.name: Visual field perimetry
investigations.indication: Baseline and serial monitoring of visual field loss

first_line_drugs.notes: Maintain airway, normoglycemia, normothermia; treat raised ICP; target MAP for age; GCS monitoring
0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4 mg) for seizures; may repeat x1 in 5 min; first-line anticonvulsant
10 mg/kg IV q8h empirically pending HSV PCR if infectious etiology suspected

second_line_drugs.notes: 20-30 mg/kg/day IV x 3-5 days for suspected autoimmune/inflammatory encephalopathy; max 1 g/day
2 g/kg IV over 2-5 days for autoimmune encephalopathy refractory to steroids
0.25-1 g/kg IV over 20 min for raised ICP; can repeat q4-6h; monitor serum osmolality

notes: Identify and treat the underlying cause (metabolic, infectious, autoimmune, toxic). PICU admission required for GCS <8, raised ICP, or hemodynamic instability.
counselling: Your child's brain is not functioning normally - the cause needs to be found and treated
Your child will need close monitoring in the hospital, possibly in the ICU
Many causes of encephalopathy are treatable and children can recover well
Report any seizures, change in alertness, or breathing difficulty to the nursing staff immediately
After recovery, developmental follow-up may be needed to check for any lasting effects
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist urgently for all encephalopathy cases; infectious disease if infection suspected; metabolic genetics if metabolic cause suspected
hospitalisation_criteria: All children with encephalopathy require hospitalization; ICU admission for GCS <8, seizures, hemodynamic instability, or need for airway protection

investigations.name: CT brain (urgent)
investigations.indication: Rapid screen for mass lesion, hemorrhage, edema

investigations.name: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis
investigations.indication: Rule out meningitis/encephalitis (cell count, protein, glucose, HSV PCR)

investigations.name: EEG
investigations.indication: Assess for subclinical seizures and encephalopathy severity

first_line_drugs.notes: 100 mg/kg/day IV divided q12h; max 4 g/day; for aerobic gram-negative and streptococcal coverage
30 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h; max 4 g/day; excellent anaerobic coverage and CNS penetration
60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h; target trough 15-20 mcg/mL; for MRSA/resistant strep coverage

second_line_drugs.notes: 120 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h; max 6 g/day; for resistant organisms or Pseudomonas coverage
Stereotactic aspiration for abscess >2.5 cm or causing mass effect; culture-directed therapy
0.5-1 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h short-term for significant perilesional edema and mass effect

notes: Empiric triple therapy (cephalosporin + metronidazole + vancomycin) covers most pathogens. Duration is 4-6 weeks post-surgery or 6-8 weeks for medically managed abscesses.
guideline_changes: ESCMID 2023 published first comprehensive brain abscess guidelines for children and adults
counselling: Your child has a serious infection inside the brain that needs long-term antibiotics through IV
Treatment usually takes 4-6 weeks or longer - completing the full course is critical
Surgery may be needed to drain the infection if medicines alone are not enough
Regular CT or MRI scans will be done to check if the infection is shrinking
After treatment, your child will need follow-up to watch for seizures or other complications
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurosurgeon for all brain abscesses; infectious disease specialist for antibiotic guidance; ENT if sinogenic or otogenic source
hospitalisation_criteria: All intracranial abscesses require hospitalization for IV antibiotics; urgent neurosurgical intervention for abscess >2.5 cm, mass effect, or clinical deterioration

investigations.name: MRI brain with contrast
investigations.indication: Confirm abscess, assess size, ring enhancement, edema

investigations.name: Stereotactic aspiration culture
investigations.indication: Culture-directed antibiotic therapy if surgical drainage done

first_line_drugs.notes: 15-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID; first-line for generalized epilepsy; monitor LFTs; avoid in pregnancy
10-30 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; titrate over 2-4 wks; first-line for focal and generalized seizures
10-20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID; first-line for focal seizures; check HLA-B*1502 in at-risk groups
20 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; first-line for childhood absence epilepsy; max 40 mg/kg/day

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.3-1 mg/kg/day PO; titrate slowly over 8 wks to 5-15 mg/kg/day; first-line for focal/generalized
8-10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; titrate to 30-46 mg/kg/day; for focal seizures
1-3 mg/kg/day PO; titrate to 5-9 mg/kg/day; adjunctive or monotherapy for focal/generalized
Starting dose 12.5 mg/day PO; titrate slowly; for drug-resistant focal epilepsy >=18 yrs; peds trials ongoing

notes: Choice of ASM depends on seizure type and epilepsy syndrome; monotherapy is preferred. Avoid valproate in females of childbearing potential due to teratogenicity per ILAE/NICE guidance.
guideline_changes: ILAE updated initial monotherapy recommendations 2022; cenobamate emerging for refractory epilepsy
counselling: Epilepsy medicine must be taken every day at the same time - never skip doses
Do not stop the medicine suddenly even if seizures have stopped - this is dangerous
Keep a seizure diary noting date, time, duration, and description of each episode
Your child can attend school normally but inform the teacher about the condition and first aid
Avoid swimming alone and heights; ensure adequate sleep as lack of sleep triggers seizures
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric neurologist if seizures not controlled with first medication, diagnostic uncertainty, or consideration of epilepsy surgery
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for status epilepticus, cluster seizures, new-onset seizures with altered consciousness, or for EEG monitoring

investigations.name: EEG (awake and sleep)
investigations.indication: Classify epilepsy syndrome and guide medication choice

investigations.name: Drug levels
investigations.indication: Therapeutic drug monitoring for valproate, carbamazepine, phenytoin

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.1 mg/kg IV over 2 min; max 4 mg; repeat x1 after 5 min if seizure persists; first-line
0.2 mg/kg IM/IN (max 10 mg) if no IV access; or 0.1 mg/kg IV; faster onset via IM than IV diazepam
0.2-0.3 mg/kg IV (max 10 mg) or 0.5 mg/kg PR (max 20 mg); repeat x1 after 5 min

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg PE/kg IV at 3 mg PE/kg/min; max 1500 mg PE; 2nd-line if benzodiazepine fails
60 mg/kg IV over 15 min; max 4500 mg; 2nd-line per ESETT trial; equivalent efficacy to fosphenytoin
20-40 mg/kg IV over 5-10 min; max 3000 mg; 2nd-line; avoid in <2 yrs or liver disease
Load 0.2 mg/kg IV then 0.05-2 mg/kg/hr for refractory SE; titrate to EEG burst suppression

notes: Benzodiazepines must be given within 5 minutes of seizure onset; underdosing is common and increases morbidity. ESETT trial showed levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, and valproate equally effective as second-line agents.
guideline_changes: ESETT trial confirmed equivalent efficacy of levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, and valproate as 2nd-line
counselling: Status epilepticus is a medical emergency - your child needed emergency treatment for prolonged seizures
If seizure lasts more than 5 minutes at home, give the emergency medicine and call an ambulance
Keep rescue medicine (midazolam nasal spray or diazepam rectal) with you at all times
After discharge, regular follow-up with the neurologist is essential to prevent recurrence
Ensure your child takes daily seizure medicines on time and never misses a dose
referral_criteria: All status epilepticus cases should be managed by or in consultation with pediatric neurologist; refer for refractory SE or if underlying cause unclear
hospitalisation_criteria: All status epilepticus requires emergency hospitalization; ICU admission for refractory status epilepticus, respiratory compromise, or need for continuous infusions

investigations.name: Continuous EEG
investigations.indication: Monitor for ongoing subclinical seizures in refractory SE

investigations.name: CT brain
investigations.indication: If first presentation or focal signs suggesting structural cause

investigations.name: Antiepileptic drug levels
investigations.indication: Check therapeutic levels in known epilepsy patients

first_line_drugs.notes: Consistent bedtime routine, limit screens 1h before bed; graduated extinction; first-line always
Start 0.5 mg PO 30-60 min before bedtime; titrate weekly to max 5 mg; for sleep-onset insomnia

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.05-0.1 mg PO qHS for sleep-onset difficulty with comorbid ADHD; max 0.4 mg/day
3-6 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO if ferritin <50 ng/mL and restless legs/PLMD suspected
1-2 mg/kg PO qHS for sleep-maintenance insomnia; limited pediatric data; off-label use

notes: Behavioral interventions should always precede and accompany any pharmacotherapy for pediatric insomnia. Melatonin not recommended under age 2; avoid in typically developing children without clear clinical indication.
guideline_changes: 2025 IPSA expert consensus on melatonin use in typically developing children published
counselling: Good sleep habits are the most important treatment - set a fixed bedtime and wake time every day
Remove all screens (TV, tablet, phone) from the bedroom and stop screen use 1 hour before bed
Create a calm bedtime routine: bath, story, dim lights, then sleep
Avoid caffeinated drinks (cola, tea, coffee, chocolate) especially after lunch
If using melatonin, give it 30-60 minutes before the desired bedtime
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric sleep specialist if behavioral measures and melatonin fail after 4-6 weeks, suspected sleep-disordered breathing, or narcolepsy
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalization rarely needed; consider admission for polysomnography if severe obstructive symptoms or for safety if parasomnia causing injury

investigations.name: Serum ferritin
investigations.indication: Low ferritin (<50 ng/mL) associated with restless legs/PLMD

investigations.name: Polysomnography
investigations.indication: If sleep-disordered breathing or periodic limb movements suspected

first_line_drugs.notes: First-line surgical treatment for pediatric OSA; refer to ENT for evaluation
Titrated via polysomnography; for children not candidates for surgery or with persistent OSA post-surgery
Fluticasone 1 spray/nostril daily or mometasone 1 spray/nostril daily for mild OSA

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-5 yr: 4 mg PO daily; 6-14 yr: 5 mg PO daily; for mild residual OSA
Dietary and lifestyle interventions for obese children with OSA
Orthodontic device for children with maxillary constriction and OSA

notes: Up to 40% of children have persistent OSA after adenotonsillectomy. Polysomnography is recommended for diagnosis and post-surgical reassessment.
guideline_changes: ATS 2024 guideline on persistent post-adenotonsillectomy OSA management
counselling: Your child stops breathing briefly during sleep which reduces oxygen to the brain and body
Enlarged tonsils and adenoids are the most common cause in children and surgery usually cures it
If your child snores loudly, breathes through the mouth, or is restless during sleep, these are important signs
Overweight children should be encouraged to eat healthy and exercise to help reduce symptoms
After surgery, a follow-up sleep study may be needed to confirm the problem is resolved
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT for adenotonsillectomy evaluation; sleep medicine if OSA persists post-surgery or child not a surgical candidate
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for adenotonsillectomy (day surgery or overnight for high-risk); severe OSA in young children (<3 yrs) or those with comorbidities may need overnight observation post-op

investigations.name: Polysomnography (sleep study)
investigations.indication: Gold standard for diagnosing and grading OSA severity

investigations.name: Lateral neck X-ray
investigations.indication: Assess adenoid size if PSG not available

investigations.name: Echocardiography
investigations.indication: If severe OSA to rule out cor pulmonale

first_line_drugs.notes: Fluticasone 1-2 sprays/nostril daily or mometasone 1 spray/nostril daily; age >=2 yr
Isotonic or hypertonic saline rinse 1-2 times daily via squeeze bottle or neti pot
45 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin) PO divided BID for acute exacerbations; max 875 mg/dose

second_line_drugs.notes: Surgical removal of adenoids; first-line surgical option when medical therapy fails
Reserved for refractory CRS after failed medical therapy and adenoidectomy
30-40 mg/kg/day PO divided TID for 21 days if anaerobes suspected; max 1.8 g/day

notes: Chronic sinusitis requires symptoms >12 weeks without resolution. Routine systemic antibiotics are not recommended for uncomplicated chronic sinusitis in children.
guideline_changes: Emerging biologic therapies under investigation for pediatric CRS with nasal polyps
counselling: Chronic sinusitis means long-standing sinus infection lasting more than 12 weeks
Daily nasal saline rinse is very helpful - teach your child to do it morning and evening
Use the nasal spray medicine regularly as prescribed even when symptoms improve
Avoid exposure to cigarette smoke and dust as these worsen sinus problems
If your child has nasal polyps or does not improve with medicines, surgery may be needed
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if symptoms persist after 3 months of medical therapy, nasal polyps identified, or recurrent acute exacerbations
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for orbital or intracranial complications of sinusitis (orbital cellulitis, epidural abscess), or for endoscopic sinus surgery

investigations.name: CT sinuses (non-contrast)
investigations.indication: Confirm chronic sinusitis and assess extent

investigations.name: Nasal endoscopy
investigations.indication: Visualize middle meatus, polyps, adenoid hypertrophy

investigations.name: Allergy testing (skin prick or specific IgE)
investigations.indication: Identify allergic component contributing to CRS

first_line_drugs.notes: Neb: 0.15 mg/kg (min 2.5 mg) q20min x3 doses; MDI: 4-8 puffs via spacer q20min
Neb: 250 mcg (<20 kg) or 500 mcg (>=20 kg) q20min x3 doses with albuterol
1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) for 3-5 days for moderate-severe episodes

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.6 mg/kg PO/IM x1 dose (max 16 mg); may repeat x1 in 24-48h as alternative to pred
0.01 mg/kg IM (1:1000, max 0.5 mg) q15-20min x3 for severe bronchospasm
25-50 mg/kg IV over 20 min (max 2 g) for refractory bronchospasm

notes: Inhaled SABA via MDI with spacer is preferred over nebulizer in cooperative children. Assess response after each treatment and escalate as needed.
counselling: Bronchospasm means the airways in the lungs have tightened making it hard to breathe
Give the inhaler medicine with a spacer as shown by the doctor - this is the most effective way
If your child is not improving after 3 puffs of the blue inhaler, come to the hospital immediately
Keep the rescue inhaler available at all times including at school
Identify and avoid triggers like dust, smoke, cold air, and strong smells
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if recurrent bronchospasm suggesting asthma, poor response to standard bronchodilators, or diagnostic uncertainty
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe bronchospasm not responding to initial bronchodilator therapy, oxygen saturation <92% despite treatment, or respiratory distress

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Rule out pneumonia, foreign body, or pneumothorax

first_line_drugs.notes: 5-10 kg: 7.5 mg/hr; 10-20 kg: 11.25 mg/hr; >20 kg: 15 mg/hr via continuous neb
Neb: 250-500 mcg q4-6h; give with albuterol in first hour q20min x3
1 mg/kg IV q6h (max 30 mg/dose) or 2 mg/kg load then 0.5 mg/kg q6h
50 mg/kg IV over 20-30 min (max 2 g); single dose for severe/refractory cases

second_line_drugs.notes: Load: 10 mcg/kg (max 400 mcg); infusion: 0.1-10 mcg/kg/min titrated to effect
0.01 mg/kg IM (1:1000, max 0.5 mg) q20min x3; or IV infusion 0.1-1 mcg/kg/min
0.5-2 mg/kg IV bolus then 0.5-2 mg/kg/hr infusion for bronchodilation and sedation
Load: 6 mg/kg IV over 20 min; maintenance: 0.5-1 mg/kg/hr; monitor levels

notes: Status asthmaticus is refractory to standard bronchodilator therapy and requires ICU admission. Monitor for cardiac arrhythmias with continuous beta-agonist use.
source: GINA 2024, NAEPP EPR-3, CHOP PICU Pathway
counselling: Your child had a very severe asthma attack that needed intensive hospital treatment
After discharge, take all prescribed medicines regularly especially the preventer inhaler daily
Get an asthma action plan from your doctor and keep it accessible at home and school
Avoid known triggers: dust, smoke, strong perfumes, cold air, and pets if allergic
If the reliever inhaler is not working or your child is struggling to breathe, come to hospital immediately
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist/allergist for all cases; ICU consultation for refractory cases
hospitalisation_criteria: All status asthmaticus requires hospitalization, typically in PICU; admit if not responding to repeated bronchodilators, SpO2 <90%, altered consciousness, or silent chest

investigations.name: Arterial blood gas
investigations.indication: Assess severity of respiratory failure and CO2 retention

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Rule out pneumothorax, pneumonia, foreign body

investigations.name: Serum electrolytes
investigations.indication: Monitor for hypokalemia from beta-agonist use

investigations.name: Peak flow (if cooperative)
investigations.indication: Objective measure of airflow obstruction and response to treatment

snomed_display: Acute severe exacerbation of severe persistent asthma co-occurrent with allergic rhinitis

first_line_drugs.notes: MDI: 2-4 puffs via spacer q4-6h PRN; Neb: 0.15 mg/kg (min 2.5 mg) q4-6h PRN
1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 40 mg) for 3-5 days for acute wheezing exacerbation
Fluticasone 44-88 mcg 2 puffs BID or budesonide neb 0.25-0.5 mg BID for MTW

second_line_drugs.notes: 6 mo-5 yr: 4 mg PO daily; 6-14 yr: 5 mg PO daily; for recurrent viral wheeze
Budesonide 1 mg neb BID or fluticasone 250 mcg BID at onset of viral illness x7-10d
Neb: 250-500 mcg q6-8h as add-on bronchodilator for acute episodes

notes: Classify as episodic viral wheeze or multiple-trigger wheeze to guide maintenance therapy. Daily ICS is most effective for multiple-trigger wheeze phenotype.
guideline_changes: ERS updated phenotype-based approach distinguishing episodic viral vs multiple-trigger wheeze
counselling: Wheezing means there is a whistling sound when your child breathes out, caused by narrowed airways
Many young children wheeze only with viral infections and outgrow it by age 5-6
Give the inhaler with spacer and mask as demonstrated; this is more effective than nebulizer
If wheezing happens frequently (more than once a month), a daily preventer medicine may be needed
Keep your home smoke-free and reduce dust exposure to minimize episodes
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if wheezing is recurrent (>3 episodes), persistent between episodes, or not responding to standard treatment
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, severe respiratory distress, poor feeding in infants, or not improving with bronchodilator therapy

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: If first episode, atypical presentation, or not responding to treatment

first_line_drugs.notes: Titrate to SpO2 92-97%; via nasal cannula, face mask, or high-flow nasal cannula (HFNC)
CPAP 5-10 cmH2O or BiPAP via nasal/face mask; trial before intubation if able
Tidal volume 6-8 mL/kg IBW; PEEP per ARDSNet table; plateau pressure <28 cmH2O

second_line_drugs.notes: Flow rate 2 L/kg/min (max ~60 L/min); FiO2 titrated to SpO2 target
Calfactant 3 mL/kg or beractant 4 mL/kg via ETT for severe ARDS in young children
12-16 hr/day in mechanically ventilated patients with severe hypoxemia (P/F <150)
Start 20 ppm; titrate down; for refractory hypoxemia; assess response at 30 min

notes: Treat underlying cause aggressively. Lung-protective ventilation with low tidal volumes is the cornerstone of management in pediatric acute respiratory failure.
guideline_changes: PALICC-2 (2023) updated ventilation and oxygenation targets for pediatric respiratory failure
counselling: Your child is having difficulty breathing and needs oxygen and close monitoring in the hospital
The medical team may need to use a breathing machine if your child is not improving
This is a serious condition but most children recover well with proper treatment
After discharge, follow-up with the doctor to ensure lungs have recovered fully
If your child was on a ventilator, some cough and tiredness is normal for a few weeks after
referral_criteria: Manage in PICU; consult pediatric pulmonologist and intensivist for all cases
hospitalisation_criteria: All acute respiratory failure requires hospitalization; PICU admission for need for mechanical ventilation, severe hypoxemia (P/F <200), or hemodynamic instability

investigations.name: Arterial blood gas
investigations.indication: Confirm type of respiratory failure (hypoxemic vs hypercapnic)

first_line_drugs.notes: Tidal volume 6-8 mL/kg IBW; PEEP >=5 cmH2O per FiO2 table; Pplat <28 cmH2O
Target SpO2 92-97% (mild-moderate); SpO2 >=88% acceptable in severe PARDS after PEEP optimization
Target negative or neutral fluid balance after initial resuscitation; furosemide 1 mg/kg IV PRN

second_line_drugs.notes: >=12 hr/day for moderate-severe PARDS; improves V/Q matching and oxygenation
5-20 ppm for refractory hypoxemia; discontinue if no response in 30-60 min
Cisatracurium 1-2 mcg/kg/min or vecuronium 1-2 mcg/kg/min for severe PARDS; <=48h
Consider VV-ECMO for refractory hypoxemia despite optimized ventilation; OI >40

notes: No specific pharmacotherapy for PARDS; management is supportive with lung-protective ventilation. Non-adherence to PALICC recommendations is associated with higher mortality.
guideline_changes: PALICC-2 replaced PALICC-1 (2015) with updated severity stratification and ventilation targets
counselling: Your child has a serious lung condition called ARDS that needs intensive care treatment
A breathing machine (ventilator) is necessary to help the lungs heal
Recovery takes time - lungs need days to weeks to recover
The medical team will keep you informed about your child's progress daily
After recovery, some children may need follow-up lung function tests
referral_criteria: Manage in PICU; pediatric intensivist leads care; consult pulmonology and infectious disease as needed
hospitalisation_criteria: All PARDS cases require PICU admission for mechanical ventilation, close monitoring, and potentially ECMO for refractory cases

investigations.name: Arterial blood gas
investigations.indication: Calculate P/F ratio for PARDS severity classification

first_line_drugs.notes: Definitive removal under general anesthesia; procedure of choice in pediatric FB aspiration
Infant <1 yr: 5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts; for acute choking with complete obstruction
Child >1 yr: subdiaphragmatic thrusts until object expelled or child becomes unresponsive

second_line_drugs.notes: Diagnostic or therapeutic in select cases; smaller caliber but limited extraction capability
0.5-0.6 mg/kg IV/PO (max 10 mg) pre/post bronchoscopy to reduce airway edema
Amoxicillin-clavulanate 45 mg/kg/day PO BID if secondary infection from prolonged impaction

notes: Complications increase significantly after 24-48 hours of impaction. Urgent rigid bronchoscopy should not be delayed for imaging if clinical suspicion is high.
counselling: Your child had a foreign body in the airway which has been removed
Keep small objects (nuts, coins, button batteries, small toys) away from children under 3 years
Cut food into small pieces for young children - avoid whole grapes, popcorn, raw carrots
Teach older children to chew food properly and not to talk or laugh while eating
If your child suddenly starts coughing, choking, or turns blue, learn basic choking first aid
referral_criteria: Urgent ENT/pediatric surgery referral for bronchoscopic removal; immediate referral for complete airway obstruction
hospitalisation_criteria: All confirmed or suspected airway foreign bodies require hospital admission for bronchoscopy; observe 24h post-removal for airway edema

investigations.name: Chest X-ray (inspiratory and expiratory)
investigations.indication: Look for air trapping, radio-opaque foreign body

investigations.name: CT chest
investigations.indication: If X-ray negative but clinical suspicion high

first_line_drugs.notes: Via nasal cannula if SpO2 persistently <90%; titrate to maintain SpO2 >=90%
Gentle superficial suctioning of nares PRN; avoid deep suctioning
If unable to maintain oral hydration; NS or D5-NS at maintenance rate

second_line_drugs.notes: 2 L/kg/min (max 8-10 L/min in infants) if worsening on standard O2; FiO2 titrated
3% NaCl 4 mL neb q4-8h; may reduce hospital stay in admitted patients; not for ED
0.15 mg/kg neb x1 dose; consider in >12 mo with prior wheeze/atopy; stop if no response

notes: Bronchodilators, corticosteroids, and antibiotics are NOT routinely recommended. Supportive care with oxygen and hydration is the mainstay of treatment.
counselling: Bronchiolitis is a viral chest infection very common in babies during winter months
Antibiotics do not help as this is a viral infection - supportive care is the main treatment
Keep your baby's nose clear by gentle suctioning before feeds
Feed small amounts frequently as babies tire easily while breathing fast
Come back to hospital if your baby stops feeding, breathes very fast, or turns blue
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if atypical course, recurrent bronchiolitis, or underlying lung/heart disease
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 persistently <90%, unable to maintain oral feeding, marked respiratory distress, age <3 months, or significant comorbidity (CHD, prematurity, immunodeficiency)

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Only if diagnostic uncertainty or clinical deterioration (not routine)

first_line_drugs.notes: O2 if SpO2 <90%, nasal suctioning PRN, IV/NG fluids if poor oral intake; no routine meds
<5 kg: 50 mg IM x1; >=5 kg: 100 mg IM x1; before first RSV season; high-risk 2nd season: 200 mg
2 L/kg/min for infants with worsening respiratory distress despite standard O2

second_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg IM monthly x5 doses during RSV season; being phased out by Dec 2025
3% NaCl 4 mL neb q4-8h; for hospitalized patients; may reduce LOS
Aerosolized ribavirin reserved for severely immunocompromised with life-threatening RSV

notes: Nirsevimab is now the preferred single-dose prophylaxis for RSV in infants entering their first RSV season. Treatment remains supportive; no antiviral has proven benefit in routine RSV bronchiolitis.
guideline_changes: Nirsevimab (2023) approved as single-dose RSV prophylaxis replacing monthly palivizumab for most infants
counselling: Your baby has an RSV infection which is the most common cause of bronchiolitis
Treatment is mainly supportive - oxygen and fluids if needed; medicines do not help much
Keep your baby away from other sick children and crowded places during illness
Handwashing is the most important way to prevent spreading the infection
Ask your doctor about nirsevimab injection to protect your baby before the next RSV season
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if prolonged course, underlying chronic lung disease, or recurrent RSV infections
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <90%, unable to feed, marked respiratory distress, age <6 weeks, or high-risk (preterm, CHD, immunodeficiency)

investigations.name: RSV rapid antigen test or PCR
investigations.indication: Confirm RSV for cohorting and epidemiology

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Only if clinical deterioration or suspected complication

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg/dose PO q4-6h PRN (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4 g/day) for fever and discomfort
10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h PRN (max 40 mg/kg/day); age >=6 months for fever/pain
2.5-10 mL PO at bedtime for cough in children >1 yr; NOT for infants <1 yr (botulism risk)

second_line_drugs.notes: Bedside use at night for cough relief; keep clean to prevent mold
1-2 mg/kg/day divided q6-8h PO; age >4 yr only; limited evidence of efficacy
40-50 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID for 2-4 wk if chronic wet cough >4 wk (PBB)

notes: Antibiotics should NOT be prescribed for acute bronchitis as etiology is almost always viral. Consider protracted bacterial bronchitis only if wet cough persists >4 weeks.
counselling: Acute bronchitis is usually caused by a virus and antibiotics are NOT needed
Cough may last 2-3 weeks even after the child is better - this is normal
Give plenty of warm fluids; honey at bedtime helps cough in children over 1 year
Avoid cough syrups in children under 4 years as they do not work and can be harmful
Return if fever persists beyond 5 days, or if your child develops difficulty breathing
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if wet cough persists >4 weeks (suspected protracted bacterial bronchitis) or recurrent bronchitis
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalization rarely needed; admit only if respiratory distress, inability to maintain hydration, or suspected complications (pneumonia)

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Only if pneumonia or complications suspected

first_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 4 g/day) for 5 days if bacterial cause suspected
Supplemental O2 if SpO2 <92%; oral rehydration; antipyretics PRN

second_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin) PO divided BID (max 4 g/day) if resistant organisms suspected
10 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg) then 5 mg/kg/day days 2-5 if atypical pathogen suspected
50-100 mg/kg/day IV/IM once daily (max 2 g) if hospitalized or unable to take PO

notes: Most pediatric LRTIs are viral and require only supportive care. Reserve antibiotics for suspected bacterial infection based on clinical features and inflammatory markers.
counselling: Your child has a lower respiratory infection - it may be viral or bacterial
If antibiotics are prescribed, complete the full course even if your child feels better
Give plenty of fluids and rest; paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever
Watch for danger signs: fast breathing, chest indrawing, not feeding, becoming drowsy
Vaccination (pneumococcal, Hib, influenza) helps prevent many chest infections
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if recurrent LRTI, poor response to antibiotics, or underlying chronic condition
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, severe respiratory distress, unable to feed, danger signs per WHO criteria, or failed outpatient treatment

investigations.name: CRP
investigations.indication: Inflammatory marker to guide antibiotic use

first_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID (max 4 g/day) for 5-7 days; first-line outpatient
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 12 g/day) for hospitalized uncomplicated CAP
50-100 mg/kg/day IV/IM once daily (max 4 g) if not fully immunized or resistant organism

second_line_drugs.notes: 10 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg) then 5 mg/kg/day x4 days for atypical coverage
30-40 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 2.7 g/day) if MRSA or anaerobe suspected
6 mo-4 yr: 16-20 mg/kg/day IV/PO BID; >=5 yr: 8-10 mg/kg/day daily (max 750 mg)
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 12 g/day) for hospitalized patients

notes: Amoxicillin is first-line for outpatient CAP in immunized children. Viral pathogens cause most pneumonia in preschoolers; antibiotics not always needed.
guideline_changes: IDSA/PIDS 2026 updated CAP guidelines released Feb 2026 replacing 2011 version
counselling: Your child has a bacterial lung infection (pneumonia) that needs antibiotics
Complete the full antibiotic course even if your child feels better after a few days
Give plenty of fluids and rest; keep your child in a well-ventilated room
Return to hospital if fever does not come down after 48 hours of antibiotics or breathing worsens
Make sure your child is up to date on pneumococcal and flu vaccines to prevent future pneumonia
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if complicated pneumonia (effusion, empyema, necrotizing), recurrent pneumonia, or immunocompromised host
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, moderate-severe respiratory distress, unable to tolerate oral fluids/medicines, age <6 months, or complicated pneumonia

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Confirm pneumonia and assess for complications

investigations.name: Blood culture
investigations.indication: Identify pathogen; obtain before antibiotics if possible

investigations.name: CRP/Procalcitonin
investigations.indication: Guide antibiotic therapy and monitor response

first_line_drugs.notes: 200-300 mg/kg/day (ampicillin) IV divided q6h (max 12 g amp/day); empiric first-line
Ceftriaxone 50-100 mg/kg/day IV daily + clindamycin 30-40 mg/kg/day IV q6-8h
10-14 Fr tube; alteplase 0.1 mg/kg (max 6 mg) q12h x4-6 doses with 1h dwell time

second_line_drugs.notes: For multiloculated empyema or failure of chest tube + fibrinolytics after 48-72h
40-60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 4 g/day) if MRSA suspected; trough 15-20 mcg/mL
60 mg/kg/day IV divided q8h (max 6 g/day) for polymicrobial or resistant infection

notes: Total antibiotic duration (IV + oral) is typically 3-4 weeks. Intrapleural fibrinolytics with chest tube drainage are superior to drainage alone.
counselling: Your child has pus collection around the lung which needs long-term antibiotic treatment
A chest tube may be placed to drain the fluid - this is necessary for recovery
IV antibiotics will be given for 1-2 weeks followed by oral antibiotics for 2-4 more weeks
Your child may need to stay in hospital for 1-2 weeks or longer
Follow-up chest X-ray will be done to ensure the infection has cleared completely
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric surgeon/thoracic surgeon for chest tube placement and VATS if needed; infectious disease for antibiotic guidance
hospitalisation_criteria: All empyema cases require hospitalization for IV antibiotics and drainage; surgical referral for multiloculated collections

investigations.name: Chest ultrasound
investigations.indication: Characterize effusion (simple vs loculated) and guide drainage

investigations.name: CT chest with contrast
investigations.indication: Assess for loculations, lung parenchyma, and guide intervention

investigations.name: Pleural fluid analysis (cell count, pH, glucose, LDH, culture)

diagnosis_name: Lung abscess and necrosis of lung

first_line_drugs.notes: 30-40 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 2.7 g/day); covers S. aureus and anaerobes
50-100 mg/kg/day IV once daily (max 4 g); combined with clindamycin for broad coverage
200-300 mg/kg/day (ampicillin) IV divided q6h (max 12 g/day); alternative empiric

second_line_drugs.notes: 300 mg/kg/day (piperacillin) IV divided q6-8h (max 16 g/day) for polymicrobial
40-60 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 4 g/day) if MRSA confirmed; trough 15-20
For large (>4 cm) peripheral abscess not responding to 7-10 days of antibiotics
10 mg/kg IV/PO q8h (max 600 mg/dose); for MRSA if vancomycin not tolerated

notes: Prolonged IV antibiotics (2-4 weeks) followed by oral step-down (total 4-6 weeks) is standard. Surgical drainage is rarely needed if antibiotics started early.
counselling: Your child has a serious lung infection with abscess formation that needs prolonged antibiotics
IV antibiotics will be given for 2-4 weeks followed by oral antibiotics to complete 4-6 weeks total
Your child will need to stay in hospital for several weeks
Regular CT scans will monitor if the abscess is shrinking
Most children recover completely but complete the full antibiotic course to prevent relapse
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist and infectious disease; interventional radiology or surgery if abscess >4 cm not responding to 7-10 days of antibiotics
hospitalisation_criteria: All lung abscesses require hospitalization for prolonged IV antibiotics; ICU admission if sepsis or respiratory failure

investigations.name: CT chest with contrast
investigations.indication: Confirm abscess, assess size and guide intervention

first_line_drugs.notes: 18-20G needle; send fluid for cell count, glucose, LDH, pH, Gram stain, culture
Ceftriaxone 50-100 mg/kg/day IV daily + clindamycin 30-40 mg/kg/day IV q6-8h
10-14 Fr pigtail catheter for complicated effusion (pH <7.0, glucose <40, LDH >1000, pus)

second_line_drugs.notes: 40,000 IU in 40 mL NS (>=10 kg) BID x3 days via chest tube; dwell 4h then drain
Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery for multiloculated effusion or fibrinolytic failure
Furosemide 1-2 mg/kg IV/PO q6-12h for transudative effusion (e.g., nephrotic, CHF)

notes: Determine if effusion is transudative or exudative using Light criteria to guide management. Parapneumonic effusion requires antibiotics and may need drainage if complicated.
counselling: There is fluid collection around your child's lung that needs to be investigated and treated
A needle may be used to take a sample of the fluid to find the cause
If the fluid is infected, antibiotics and a drainage tube may be needed
Some effusions resolve with treating the underlying cause without needing drainage
Regular follow-up and repeat imaging will ensure the fluid is clearing
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist for all significant effusions; surgeon if drainage needed; nephrology/cardiology if transudative
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for symptomatic effusion causing respiratory distress, complicated parapneumonic effusion, or need for thoracentesis/chest tube

investigations.name: Serum albumin and LDH
investigations.indication: Compare with pleural fluid for Light criteria

first_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 4 g/day) for 5-7 days; first-line oral therapy
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 12 g/day) for hospitalized uncomplicated cases
200,000-250,000 U/kg/day IV divided q4-6h (max 24 MU/day) if penicillin-susceptible

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg/kg/day IV/IM once daily (max 4 g) for penicillin-resistant strains (MIC >=2)
30-40 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 2.7 g/day) for penicillin-allergic patients
>=5 yr: 8-10 mg/kg/day PO/IV daily (max 750 mg) for beta-lactam allergy
40-60 mg/kg/day IV q6-8h (max 4 g/day) add if meningitis suspected or highly resistant

notes: High-dose amoxicillin (90 mg/kg/day) overcomes intermediate penicillin resistance in S. pneumoniae. Ensure PCV13/15/20 vaccination is up to date.
counselling: Your child has pneumonia caused by pneumococcus bacteria which responds well to antibiotics
Complete the full course of antibiotics even after your child starts feeling better
Ensure your child has received all pneumococcal vaccine doses (PCV) as per schedule
Give adequate fluids and rest; paracetamol for fever
Return if fever persists after 48 hours of antibiotics or breathing worsens
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if complicated pneumonia (empyema, necrotizing) or recurrent pneumococcal infections suggesting immunodeficiency
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, unable to take oral medicines, moderate-severe respiratory distress, complicated pneumonia, or age <6 months

investigations.name: Chest X-ray
investigations.indication: Confirm pneumonia and check for complications

first_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin) PO divided BID (max 4 g/day) for 7 days; covers beta-lactamase
50-100 mg/kg/day IV/IM once daily (max 4 g) for hospitalized patients

second_line_drugs.notes: 150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6-8h (max 12 g/day) alternative to ceftriaxone
10 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg) then 5 mg/kg/day x4 days for beta-lactam allergy
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h; only if beta-lactamase negative confirmed
8-10 mg/kg/day (TMP) PO divided BID for 7-10 days as oral step-down alternative

notes: 25-50% of H. influenzae produce beta-lactamase; do not use ampicillin empirically. Use amoxicillin-clavulanate or a cephalosporin for empiric coverage.
counselling: Your child has pneumonia caused by Haemophilus bacteria - antibiotics will treat it
Complete the full antibiotic course as prescribed even if your child improves early
Ensure your child has received Hib vaccine doses as per schedule
Give plenty of fluids and paracetamol for fever
Return if fever does not improve in 48 hours or breathing worsens
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if complicated or recurrent pneumonia; immunology if recurrent H. influenzae infections
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, severe respiratory distress, unable to take oral medicines, or complicated pneumonia

investigations.name: Blood culture
investigations.indication: Confirm H. influenzae and susceptibility testing

first_line_drugs.notes: 90 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 4 g/day) for 5-7 days; outpatient empiric first-line
150-200 mg/kg/day IV divided q6h (max 12 g/day) for hospitalized children
O2 if SpO2 <92%; IV fluids if dehydrated; antipyretics (acetaminophen 15 mg/kg q4-6h)

second_line_drugs.notes: 50-100 mg/kg/day IV/IM once daily (max 4 g) for under-immunized or severe illness
10 mg/kg PO day 1 then 5 mg/kg/day x4 days for school-age if atypical suspected
<=15 kg: 30 mg BID; 15-23 kg: 45 mg; 23-40 kg: 60 mg; >40 kg: 75 mg BID x5d if influenza
30-40 mg/kg/day IV q6-8h (max 2.7 g/day) add if necrotizing/MRSA suspected

notes: Viral causes predominate in children <5 years; antibiotics are not always required. Obtain CXR and labs to help differentiate bacterial vs viral etiology.
guideline_changes: IDSA/PIDS 2026 updated empiric CAP therapy recommendations
counselling: Your child has a lung infection (pneumonia) - most cases in young children are caused by viruses
If antibiotics are prescribed, complete the full course; do not stop early
Give plenty of warm fluids, rest, and paracetamol/ibuprofen for fever
Watch for danger signs: very fast breathing, chest indrawing, refusing to eat, becoming very sleepy
Pneumococcal and influenza vaccines help prevent many types of pneumonia
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if complicated pneumonia, recurrent pneumonia (>2 episodes in 1 year), or underlying risk factors
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, severe respiratory distress, unable to feed/drink, toxic appearance, or age <6 months with suspected bacterial pneumonia

first_line_drugs.notes: 100% O2 via NRB mask for small (<20%) stable primary spontaneous PTX; monitor q4-6h
14-16G needle at 4th ICS mid-axillary line (preferred in peds); for tension PTX or large PTX
10-14 Fr pigtail catheter; connect to water seal or -20 cmH2O suction; 4th-5th ICS MAL

second_line_drugs.notes: 16-18G over-needle catheter with 3-way stopcock; for stable patients with large 1st episode PSP
One-way flutter valve attached to pigtail catheter for ambulatory management of stable PTX
For recurrent PTX (>=2 episodes); bleb resection + mechanical or chemical pleurodesis

notes: Tension pneumothorax requires immediate needle decompression followed by chest tube placement. The 4th ICS at the anterior axillary line is the preferred site in pediatric patients.
counselling: Your child has air leaking from the lung into the space around it
A small pneumothorax may heal on its own with rest and oxygen; larger ones need a chest tube
Your child should avoid vigorous exercise and air travel until fully healed
If sudden chest pain or breathlessness occurs again, come to hospital immediately
Recurrence is possible; discuss with doctor about preventive surgery if it happens again
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric surgeon/thoracic surgeon for chest tube placement; VATS referral for recurrent pneumothorax
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit all symptomatic pneumothorax; immediate intervention for tension pneumothorax; observation for small (<20%) asymptomatic primary spontaneous PTX

investigations.name: CT chest
investigations.indication: If recurrent PTX or to plan surgical intervention

investigations.name: ABG
investigations.indication: If respiratory distress or large PTX

first_line_drugs.notes: O2 if SpO2 <92%; IV fluids if dehydrated; antipyretics PRN; humidified air
<1 yr: 3 mg/kg PO BID; 1-12 yr: weight-based (30-75 mg BID); start within 48h x5 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 5-<12 yr, >=20 kg: weight-based single dose PO; >=12 yr & 40-80 kg: 40 mg x1; >80 kg: 80 mg
>=3.5 kg: 5 mg/kg IV day 1, then 2.5 mg/kg IV daily for 5 days; for hospitalized pts
Aerosolized: 6 g reconstituted in 300 mL via SPAG-2 x12-18 hr/day; severe immunocompromised RSV

notes: Most viral pneumonia in children is self-limiting and requires only supportive care. Oseltamivir should be started within 48 hours for confirmed or suspected influenza.
counselling: Your child has a viral lung infection - antibiotics will NOT help as it is caused by a virus
Most viral pneumonia gets better on its own in 1-2 weeks with rest and fluids
Give paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever; ensure your child drinks plenty of fluids
If your child has influenza, antiviral medicine (oseltamivir) works best if started within 48 hours
Return if breathing worsens, fever persists beyond 5 days, or child stops eating/drinking
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric pulmonologist if severe course, not improving as expected, or underlying lung/immune condition
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if SpO2 <92%, severe respiratory distress, dehydration, or high-risk child (immunocompromised, chronic lung disease)

diagnosis_name: Respiratory tuberculosis, bacteriologically and histologically confirmed

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 300 mg); give with pyridoxine 1-2 mg/kg/day
10-20 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 600 mg); take on empty stomach
30-40 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 2 g); used in intensive phase (first 2 months)
15-25 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 2.5 g); used in intensive phase; monitor visual acuity

second_line_drugs.notes: Isoniazid + rifapentine + pyrazinamide + moxifloxacin daily x4 months; for adolescents >=12 yr
15-20 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 750 mg); for drug-resistant TB or ethambutol intolerance
15-30 mg/kg/day IV/IM daily (max 1 g); for MDR-TB regimens; monitor audiology

notes: Standard regimen: 2 months HRZE then 2-4 months HR (4-month total for non-severe, 6-month for severe/cavitary). Directly Observed Therapy (DOT) is recommended.
guideline_changes: ATS/CDC/ERS/IDSA 2025 update: 4-month regimen (2HRZE/2HR) now standard for non-severe pediatric TB
counselling: Tuberculosis is curable but treatment takes 4-6 months - completing all medicines is critical
All medicines must be taken daily without missing any dose; directly observed therapy is recommended
Common side effects include orange urine (rifampicin) - this is normal and harmless
Contact screening: all family members especially children should be tested for TB
Your child can return to school after 2 weeks of treatment if feeling well and cough improving
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric TB specialist if drug-resistant TB, extrapulmonary TB, HIV co-infection, or treatment failure
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe or miliary TB, TB meningitis, respiratory distress, or when directly observed therapy initiation is needed

investigations.name: Sputum/gastric aspirate for AFB and GeneXpert
investigations.indication: Confirm TB and detect rifampicin resistance

investigations.name: LFT baseline
investigations.indication: Before starting hepatotoxic drugs (INH, RIF, PZA)

investigations.name: HIV test
investigations.indication: TB-HIV coinfection screening per NACO guidelines

snomed_display: Respiratory tuberculosis, bacteriologically and histologically confirmed

diagnosis_name: Respiratory tuberculosis, not confirmed bacteriologically or histologically

first_line_drugs.notes: 10-15 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 300 mg); give with pyridoxine 1-2 mg/kg/day
10-20 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 600 mg); take on empty stomach
30-40 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 2 g); intensive phase (first 2 months)

second_line_drugs.notes: 15-25 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 2.5 g); add to intensive phase if drug resistance suspected
Isoniazid + rifapentine + pyrazinamide + moxifloxacin daily x4 months; adolescents >=12 yr
15-20 mg/kg/day PO daily (max 750 mg); for drug-resistant TB or intolerance

notes: Clinically diagnosed TB (smear/culture negative) in children is treated identically to confirmed TB. The 4-month regimen is non-inferior for non-severe disease per SHINE trial.
guideline_changes: 4-month regimen (2HRZ(E)/2HR) validated for clinically diagnosed non-severe pediatric TB per SHINE trial
counselling: Your child is being treated for TB even though the test did not grow the bacteria - this is common in children
Treatment is the same and just as important; complete all 4-6 months of medicines
Give medicines on empty stomach in the morning as directed
Watch for side effects: jaundice (yellow eyes), persistent vomiting, severe itching - report immediately
All close contacts should be screened for TB; keep follow-up appointments
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric TB specialist if not improving after 2 months of treatment, drug-resistant contacts, or extrapulmonary involvement
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe disease, miliary/meningeal TB, respiratory distress, or for treatment initiation in unreliable home setting

investigations.name: Gastric aspirate or induced sputum for GeneXpert

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.6 mg/kg PO/IM x1 dose (max 16 mg); onset 2-3h, lasts 24-48h; effective for all severity
L-epi 1:1000: 0.5 mL/kg (max 5 mL) or racemic epi 2.25%: 0.05 mL/kg (max 0.5 mL) neb
Cool mist therapy; limited evidence but commonly used for comfort; avoid steam (burn risk)

second_line_drugs.notes: 1-2 mg/kg/day PO (max 60 mg) for 2-3 days; if dexamethasone unavailable
2 mg neb x1 dose; for patients unable to take PO dexamethasone or with persistent symptoms
70:30 or 80:20 helium-oxygen mixture via face mask; for severe croup with stridor at rest

notes: A single dose of oral dexamethasone is effective for all croup severities including mild. Observe for 2-4 hours after nebulized epinephrine due to rebound potential.
counselling: Croup causes a barking cough and noisy breathing which sounds worse at night
A single dose of dexamethasone steroid medicine usually helps a lot within a few hours
Cool night air or cool mist can help ease the breathing - take the child near an open window
Most croup gets better in 3-5 days; the barking cough may last a bit longer
Rush to hospital if your child has noisy breathing at rest, drooling, or turns blue
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if recurrent croup (>3 episodes) to rule out subglottic stenosis or other airway abnormality
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if stridor at rest persists after nebulized epinephrine, severe respiratory distress, age <6 months, or toxic appearance (suspect bacterial tracheitis)

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (no routine investigations)
investigations.indication: Croup is diagnosed clinically by barking cough and stridor

investigations.name: Neck X-ray (AP view)
investigations.indication: Steeple sign if diagnosis uncertain (not routine)

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg/dose PO q4-6h PRN (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4 g/day) for fever and discomfort
10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h PRN (max 40 mg/kg/day); age >=6 months; for fever/pain
1-2 drops/sprays per nostril PRN for nasal congestion; safe for all ages including neonates

second_line_drugs.notes: 2.5-10 mL PO at bedtime; age >1 yr only; as effective as dextromethorphan for cough
Bulb suction or NoseFrida for infants before feeds and sleep; after saline drops

notes: Antibiotics are NOT indicated for the common cold. Cough/cold medicines should be avoided in children <4 years per AAP Choosing Wisely recommendation.
counselling: Common cold is caused by a virus and gets better on its own in 5-7 days
Antibiotics do NOT help for common cold and should not be used
Give plenty of warm fluids (dal paani, soup); paracetamol for fever
Do not give cough syrup to children under 4 years - they are not effective and can be harmful
Teach your child to wash hands frequently and cover coughs to prevent spreading
referral_criteria: Referral not usually needed; refer to ENT if symptoms persist beyond 10-14 days suggesting secondary bacterial sinusitis
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalization not needed for uncomplicated common cold; admit only if complications develop (dehydration in infants, febrile seizures)

first_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg PO once daily (max 1000 mg) or 25 mg/kg BID (max 500 mg/dose) for 10 days
250 mg PO BID-TID (<27 kg) or 500 mg PO BID (>=27 kg) for 10 days for confirmed GAS
<27 kg: 600,000 U IM x1; >=27 kg: 1.2 MU IM x1; for adherence concerns

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/dose PO BID (max 500 mg/dose) for 10 days; non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy
12 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg) then 6 mg/kg/day x4 days; for anaphylactic PCN allergy
7 mg/kg/dose PO TID (max 300 mg/dose) for 10 days; for type 1 PCN allergy

notes: Rapid strep test or throat culture should confirm GAS before starting antibiotics. Treatment prevents rheumatic fever and reduces symptom duration by 1-2 days.
counselling: Sore throat can be caused by virus or bacteria; a throat swab test will tell which one
If it is strep throat (bacterial), antibiotics for full 10 days are needed to prevent rheumatic fever
Give soft, warm foods and plenty of fluids; cold items like ice cream also help soothe the throat
Paracetamol or ibuprofen can be given for pain and fever
Your child can return to school 24 hours after starting antibiotics if feeling better
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if recurrent strep pharyngitis (>=7/year or >=5/year for 2 years) for tonsillectomy consideration; rheumatology if rheumatic fever develops
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for peritonsillar abscess, inability to swallow fluids, dehydration, or airway compromise

investigations.name: Rapid strep test (throat swab)
investigations.indication: Detect Group A Streptococcus before prescribing antibiotics

investigations.name: Throat culture
investigations.indication: If rapid test negative but clinical suspicion high

first_line_drugs.notes: 45-90 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 2 g/dose) for 10-14 days; standard first-line
90 mg/kg/day (amoxicillin) PO divided BID (max 2 g/dose) for 10-14d; severe or risk factors

second_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg IM/IV x1 dose (max 1 g); for vomiting or unable to tolerate oral; then switch to PO
30-40 mg/kg/day PO divided TID (max 1.8 g/day) for penicillin-allergic patients
Fluticasone 1 spray/nostril daily as adjunct to antibiotics; age >=2 yr

notes: Diagnose bacterial sinusitis by clinical criteria (persistent >10d, worsening, or severe onset). Watchful waiting for 3 days may be appropriate for mild uncomplicated cases.
counselling: Sinusitis means infection of the sinuses (air spaces in the face bones)
Antibiotics need to be taken for the full 10-14 days even if your child feels better earlier
Saline nose drops or spray help clear the sinuses - use before meals and bedtime
Avoid smoke exposure and keep the house dust-free
Return if symptoms worsen after 48-72 hours of antibiotics or if high fever develops
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT if complications (orbital/intracranial), recurrent acute sinusitis (>4 episodes/year), or progression to chronic sinusitis
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for orbital complications (periorbital/orbital cellulitis), intracranial complications, or toxic appearance

investigations.name: Clinical diagnosis (no routine imaging)
investigations.indication: Diagnosis based on clinical criteria per AAP

investigations.name: CT sinuses
investigations.indication: Only if orbital or intracranial complications suspected

first_line_drugs.notes: 50 mg/kg PO once daily (max 1000 mg) or 25 mg/kg BID (max 500 mg/dose) for 10 days
250 mg PO BID-TID (<27 kg) or 500 mg PO BID (>=27 kg) for 10 days for confirmed GAS
<27 kg: 600,000 U IM x1; >=27 kg: 1.2 MU IM x1; for adherence concerns

second_line_drugs.notes: 20 mg/kg/dose PO BID (max 500 mg/dose) for 10 days; for non-anaphylactic PCN allergy
12 mg/kg PO day 1 (max 500 mg) then 6 mg/kg/day x4 days; for type 1 PCN allergy
7 mg/kg/dose PO TID (max 300 mg/dose) for 10 days; penicillin allergy alternative
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg q4-6h or ibuprofen 10 mg/kg q6-8h PRN for pain/fever

notes: Confirm GAS with rapid strep test or culture before prescribing antibiotics. Tonsillectomy is considered for recurrent GAS tonsillitis (>=7 episodes/yr).
counselling: Tonsillitis is usually caused by a virus but strep bacteria also cause it
If strep test is positive, complete 10 days of antibiotics to prevent rheumatic fever and kidney problems
Warm salt water gargling (for children >6 years) and cold fluids help with pain
Your child can go back to school 24 hours after starting antibiotics
If tonsillitis keeps recurring (>7 times a year), surgery to remove tonsils may be recommended
referral_criteria: Refer to ENT for recurrent tonsillitis (>=7/yr, >=5/yr x2 yrs, or >=3/yr x3 yrs) or peritonsillar abscess
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for peritonsillar abscess drainage, airway compromise from severe tonsillar enlargement, inability to swallow, or dehydration

investigations.name: Throat culture
investigations.indication: If rapid test negative but GAS clinically suspected

investigations.name: Monospot test
investigations.indication: If EBV mononucleosis suspected (bilateral tonsillar enlargement, lymphadenopathy)

first_line_drugs.notes: 15 mg/kg/dose PO q4-6h PRN (max 75 mg/kg/day or 4 g/day) for fever and discomfort
10 mg/kg/dose PO q6-8h PRN (max 40 mg/kg/day); age >=6 months for fever/pain
Saline drops (infants) or spray/rinse (older children) PRN for nasal congestion

second_line_drugs.notes: 2.5-10 mL PO at bedtime for nocturnal cough; age >1 yr only (botulism risk in infants)
Bedside humidifier at night for cough/congestion relief; keep device clean
0.6 mg/kg PO x1 (max 16 mg) if laryngotracheitis/croup component identified

notes: Antibiotics are NOT indicated for viral URI. Avoid OTC cough/cold medications in children <4 years per AAP recommendation due to lack of efficacy and safety concerns.
counselling: Upper respiratory infection (URI) is very common in children and caused by viruses
Antibiotics are NOT helpful and should not be used for viral URI
Keep your child hydrated with warm fluids; honey at bedtime for cough (>1 year only)
Do not give OTC cough and cold medicines to children under 4 years
See a doctor if fever lasts >5 days, breathing becomes fast, or ear pain develops
referral_criteria: Referral not usually needed; refer to ENT if recurrent URI with complications or suspected anatomical abnormality
hospitalisation_criteria: Hospitalization rarely needed; admit if febrile infant <3 months, severe dehydration, or complications develop

investigations.name: CBC
investigations.indication: Only if fever prolonged >5 days to rule out bacterial infection

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.25 mg/kg/dose PO daily; 6-23mo: 2.5mg, 2-5yr: 5mg, >=6yr: 10mg daily
2-5yr: 5mg PO daily; >=6yr: 10mg PO daily
1-2 mg/kg/day PO for acute allergic reactions, max 60mg, short course 3-5 days

second_line_drugs.notes: 75-375mg SC q2-4wks based on IgE level and weight; age >=1yr for food allergy
6mo-5yr: 4mg PO daily; 6-14yr: 5mg PO daily; >=15yr: 10mg PO daily
2-11yr: 30mg PO BID; >=12yr: 60mg PO BID or 180mg daily

notes: Second-generation antihistamines are first-line; avoid first-gen due to sedation and CNS effects. Identify and eliminate allergen triggers before escalating pharmacotherapy.
guideline_changes: Omalizumab approved Feb 2024 for IgE-mediated food allergy age >=1yr; Neffy nasal epinephrine approved Aug 2024
counselling: Identify and strictly avoid the allergen that caused the reaction
Always carry the allergy card listing your child's allergens
Second-generation antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine) are preferred over older ones that cause drowsiness
If your child has severe food allergy, read all food labels carefully
Ask your doctor if your child needs to carry an emergency epinephrine auto-injector
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric allergist for skin prick testing, specific IgE testing, and allergen identification; refer urgently if anaphylaxis history
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit for severe allergic reactions with systemic involvement, anaphylaxis, or for supervised oral food challenge

investigations.name: Skin prick testing
investigations.indication: Gold standard for IgE-mediated allergy identification

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.01 mg/kg IM anterolateral thigh (1:1000), max 0.5mg; repeat q5-15min PRN
20 mL/kg IV bolus, repeat PRN for hypotension; max 1L/dose
1-2 mg/kg IV/IM/PO q6h, max 50mg/dose; adjunct only, not substitute for epi
1-2 mg/kg IV, max 125mg; for biphasic reaction prevention

second_line_drugs.notes: 0.1-1 mcg/kg/min IV continuous for refractory anaphylaxis; max 10 mcg/min
20-30 mcg/kg IV bolus, max 1mg; for beta-blocker-associated refractory hypotension
2mg intranasal single spray; for patients >=30kg as alternative to IM epi

notes: Epinephrine IM is the ONLY first-line treatment; delays increase mortality. Observe 4-6h minimum post-reaction for biphasic anaphylaxis risk.
guideline_changes: Neffy (epinephrine nasal spray) approved Aug 2024 for patients >=30kg
counselling: Anaphylaxis is a life-threatening allergic reaction - your child must always carry emergency epinephrine
Learn how to use the epinephrine auto-injector and practice with the trainer device
Give epinephrine FIRST if severe reaction occurs, then call ambulance - do not delay
Get a medical alert bracelet for your child listing their allergy
Strictly avoid the identified trigger; inform school, relatives, and caretakers about the allergy
referral_criteria: Urgent referral to pediatric allergist for all anaphylaxis cases for trigger identification and management plan
hospitalisation_criteria: All anaphylaxis requires emergency department evaluation; observe minimum 4-6 hours for biphasic reaction; admit if severe, required multiple epinephrine doses, or has asthma

investigations.name: Serum tryptase (within 1-4 hours)
investigations.indication: Confirm mast cell degranulation in anaphylaxis

investigations.name: Specific IgE panel
investigations.indication: Identify trigger allergen (after acute episode resolves)

investigations.name: Skin prick testing
investigations.indication: Comprehensive allergen identification (4-6 weeks after episode)

first_line_drugs.notes: 0.25 mg/kg/dose PO daily; can updose to 2-4x standard dose if uncontrolled
2-5yr: 5mg PO daily; >=6yr: 10mg daily; updose to 2x if needed
6-11yr: 30mg PO BID; >=12yr: 180mg PO daily; updose up to 4x if needed

second_line_drugs.notes: 150-300mg SC q4wks for chronic spontaneous urticaria; age >=12yr
6mo-5yr: 4mg PO daily; 6-14yr: 5mg; add-on to antihistamine
3-5 mg/kg/day PO divided BID; for refractory CSU, short-term use
0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO short course 3-7 days for acute flares only

notes: Updose second-gen antihistamines up to 4x before adding biologics. Avoid first-generation antihistamines per international guidelines.
guideline_changes: 2024 global update recommends same algorithm for children as adults with weight-based dosing
counselling: Hives (urticaria) are itchy raised welts that can appear anywhere and usually resolve within 24 hours per lesion
Antihistamine medicine (cetirizine or loratadine) should be taken daily, not just when itchy
If hives last more than 6 weeks, it is called chronic urticaria and may need longer treatment
Cool compresses and loose cotton clothing help reduce itching
If hives come with swelling of lips/tongue or difficulty breathing, use epinephrine and go to hospital
referral_criteria: Refer to pediatric allergist/dermatologist for chronic urticaria (>6 weeks), angioedema, or if standard antihistamines at 4x dose are ineffective
hospitalisation_criteria: Admit if associated angioedema with airway compromise, anaphylaxis, or systemic vasculitis
